<img src="2604.23301_figures/2604.23301_fig8.jpg" alt="Figure 8">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 8.</b> FIG. 7. (a,c) The imaginary-time correlation functions G↑↑(τ) (a) and G↑↓(τ) (c) as functions of the imaginary time τ for the SAIM with β = 10 during 10 DMFT iterations, where the dashed lines in different colors are the Nambu-GTEMPO results calculated with χ = 200 and δτ = 0.1 at the selected iterations, and the solid lines with the same colors are the cor- responding CTQMC results. (b,d) similar to (a, c), but for the SAIM with β = 100, where the Nambu-GTEMPO results are calculated with χ = 300 and δτ = 0.5 instead. The insets show the errors between Nambu-GTEMPO and CTQMC at the selected iterations, in correspondence with the main pan- els. We have used U = −1 and εd = −U/2 in these...</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23301_figures/2604.23301_fig9.jpg" alt="Figure 9">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 9.</b> FIG. 8. Time evolution of the probability of the four impurity states ∣0⟩(a), ∣↑⟩(b), ∣↓⟩(c) and ∣↑↓⟩(d), for the SAIM. The orange solid lines from lighter to darker are the Nambu- GTEMPO results calculated with different χs from small to large, with fixed δt = 0.05. In the insets, we show the errors between the Nambu-GTEMPO results calculated using δt = 0.05 and δt = 0.05, with fixed χ = 400. We have used U = −1, εd = −U/2 and β = 10 in these simulations.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** Extending the Grassmann time-evolving matrix product operator (GTEMPO) method to solve fermionic impurity problems where the impurity is coupled to a superconducting bath, specifically addressing the Nambu formalism.

**Main result.** The authors successfully developed the Nambu-GTEMPO method, demonstrating its accuracy against exact diagonalization and CTQMC, and showing it can handle real-time non-equilibrium dynamics without the dynamical sign problem.

**Method.** The method uses a Bogoliubov transformation to map the superconducting bath to a normal bath form, representing the Feynman-Vernon influence functional as a Grassmann matrix product state (GMPS).

**Summary.** This paper introduces Nambu-GTEMPO, a numerical method for studying fermionic impurities in superconducting baths. By using a Bogoliubov transformation, the authors map the complex superconducting coupling into a form compatible with existing Grassmann matrix product operator techniques. The method is validated against exact methods and shown to be effective for both equilibrium and non-equilibrium real-time dynamics. Crucially, it avoids the dynamical sign problem that plagues Monte Carlo simulations in the Keldysh regime. This makes it a powerful tool for investigating the physics of the Superconducting Anderson Impurity Model.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The Superconducting Anderson Impurity Model (SAIM), featuring a localized fermionic impurity with on-site energy and interaction strength coupled to an s-wave superconducting bath.

**Key observables.** Imaginary-time and real-time correlation functions (G_up-up, G_up-down, etc.) and impurity state probabilities (vacuum, single, and double occupation).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Interaction strength (U), impurity energy (epsilon_d), superconducting gap (Delta_sc), hybridization function, temperature (beta), bond dimension (chi), and time discretization step (delta_t).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The bath is assumed to be non-interacting, and the hybridization is linear in terms of bath creation and annihilation operators.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the partial IF algorithm and correlation function calculation; Figure 2 shows imaginary-time correlation functions for a toy model; Figure 4 and 5 show error scaling with respect to time step and bond dimension; Figure 8 shows the time evolution of impurity state probabilities.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the Nambu-GTEMPO extension, describes the Bogoliubov transformation technique, validates the method using exact diagonalization for simple cases, compares it to CTQMC for interacting cases in DMFT, and performs error analysis and real-time dynamics demonstrations.

**Why it may be interesting.** This is highly relevant for many-body dynamics and open quantum systems because it provides a sign-problem-free tensor network approach to studying non-equilibrium dynamics in superconducting environments, a regime where traditional Monte Carlo methods fail.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

The Grassmann time-evolving matrix product operator (GTEMPO) method, which represents the Feynman-Vernon influence functional as a temporal matrix product state, has been shown to be a flexible and potentially scalable solution for fermionic quantum impurity problems. In this work, we extend GTEMPO to solve fermionic impurity problems in the Nambu formalism, in which the impurity is coupled to a superconducting bath. A key insight is that by employing the Bogoliubov transformation for the superconducting bath, one could obtain the analytic expression of the Feynman-Vernon influence functional in a similar form to the case of a normal bath, after which the core algorithms of GTEMPO can be straightforwardly adapted. We demonstrate the accuracy of our method by benchmarking it against exact diagonalization in several exactly solvable cases, and against the continuous-time quantum Monte Carlo method using converged dynamical mean field theory (DMFT) iterations on the imaginary contour in the non-integrable case. In all cases, we perform both imaginary- and real-time calculations to illustrate the flexibility of our method. These results illustrate that our method could be potentially useful as an impurity solver in DMFT as well as its non-equilibrium extension for fermionic impurity problems in the Nambu formalism.

</details>

### [Boundary-Robust Transmission Asymmetry as a Topological Signature in Open Floquet Lattices](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23420v1)

**Authors:** Ren Zhang, Xiao-Yu Ouyang, Xu-Dong Dai, Xi Dai  
**Type:** both · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.23420v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `non-equilibrium dynamics` **4/5** · `analog quantum simulation` **3/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **3/5** · `driven-dissipative phase transition` **2/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23420_figures/2604.23420_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> FIG. 1. Construction of the smoothed transmission spectrum. Raw transmission curves for sample lengths L = 4, 8, 16 exhibit dense Fabry–P´erot oscillations, whereas the energy-window smoothed curves rapidly converge. In the L →∞limit, the smoothed transmission becomes a well- defined local coarse-grained observable that effectively filters out finite-size interference.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23420_figures/2604.23420_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> FIG. 2. Boundary robustness of the integrated spec- tral asymmetry. For an open Floquet lattice with V (x, t) = ξ(x)Vbulk(x, t) and Vbulk(x, t) = 0.8ER cos(2πx−ωt)+0.2ER, the boundary envelope ξ(x) implements a linear ramp from 0 to 1 over a length Lramp. While varying Lramp strongly distorts the smoothed transmission spectra ¯T L/R(E), the in- tegrated asymmetry A(E0) remains invariant and saturates to a stable plateau. This plateau height equals the rigid spec- tral shift dictated by the bulk winding number in the adia- batic limit, demonstrating that the topological signature is immune to microscopic boundary details.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23420_figures/2604.23420_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> FIG. 3. Schematic of the cold-atom Bragg-scattering setup. Two counter-propagating laser beams with a con- trolled frequency detuning, implemented via an acousto-optic modulator (AOM), generate a moving optical lattice. A Bose– Einstein condensate (BEC) serves as the scattering medium, confined by a quasi-1D red-detuned optical waveguide to re- alize a strictly one-dimensional scattering geometry.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23420_figures/2604.23420_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> FIG. 5. Zero-bias current versus SAW drive strength and chemical potential within the coherent-scattering prescription. Numerical results use hard-wall boundary conditions—a conservative estimate for which realistic soft boundaries should yield sharper quantization. A near-2ef re- sponse emerges over a broad parameter regime within this prescription, illustrating a possible electrical readout of the boundary-robust integrated transmission asymmetry.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23420_figures/2604.23420_fig5.jpg" alt="Figure 5">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> FIG. 4. Schematic of the SAW-driven zero-bias trans- port device. An interdigitated transducer (IDT) on a piezo- electric substrate (e.g., LiNbO3) launches a unidirectional sur- face acoustic wave, terminated by an absorber to suppress re- flections. The traveling strain field induces a moving potential in a proximal nanowire (e.g., InSb). By tuning the chemical potential via a back-gate and maintaining zero source–drain bias, the resulting SAW-driven transport current is measured, providing a possible electrical readout of the integrated spec- tral asymmetry within the coherent-scattering interpretation.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23420_figures/2604.23420_fig6.jpg" alt="Figure 6">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 6.</b> FIG. 6. Experimentally distinguishable transport predictions. Left: zero-bias current from the coher- ent prescription [Eq. (12)], showing a weakly temperature- dependent near-2ef plateau. Right: zero-bias current from the blocking-factor prescription [Eq. (14)], obtained from the same sideband-resolved transmission data [Eq. (13)]. The plateau is suppressed and the temperature dependence is much stronger. The driving amplitude is fixed at V = 0.8ER, with all other parameters the same as in Fig. 5.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** Determining whether bulk Floquet topology can be extracted from transmission observables in open systems, given that non-adiabatic boundaries distort pointwise transmission profiles.

**Main result.** The integrated left-right transmission asymmetry is a robust topological signature that saturates to a plateau determined by the bulk Floquet winding number, due to the generic openness of propagating branches.

**Method.** The authors use a theoretical framework based on the deep-bulk branch-population principle and asymptotic analysis, supplemented by numerical Monte Carlo sampling and proposed experimental setups in cold atoms and SAW devices.

**Summary.** This paper identifies a boundary-robust topological signature in open Floquet lattices: the integrated transmission asymmetry. While realistic, non-adiabatic boundaries distort the detailed transmission spectrum, the cumulative asymmetry remains locked to the bulk winding number. This robustness arises because propagating Floquet-Bloch branches are generically fully populated. The authors propose detecting this via cold-atom spectroscopy and electronic transport in SAW-driven devices.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** Open Floquet lattices (periodically driven lattices) coupled to asymptotic leads, including models for cold-atom transmission spectroscopy and electronic transport in SAW-driven piezoelectric nanowires.

**Key observables.** Integrated left-right transmission asymmetry, smoothed long-sample transmittance, and zero-bias electrical current.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Bulk Floquet winding number, quasienergy, lattice length (long-sample limit), boundary ramp length, and driving frequency.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The results rely on the long-sample limit (L approaching infinity) and the assumption of a regular quasienergy window with non-zero group velocities.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the convergence of smoothed transmission spectra; Figure 2 demonstrates the robustness of integrated asymmetry against boundary changes; Figure 3 and 4 illustrate the proposed cold-atom and SAW experimental setups; Figure 5 and 6 compare electrical transport predictions under different contact models.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of boundary-induced distortion, develops the deep-bulk branch-population principle to prove robustness, provides a mathematical proof of generic openness, and concludes with proposed experimental implementations in cold atoms and electronic transport.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a new, robust way to detect topological invariants in driven, open systems where traditional pointwise signatures are destroyed by boundary effects, which is highly relevant for experimentalists working with Floquet engineering.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We identify a boundary-robust topological signature of open Floquet lattices: although nonadiabatic boundaries strongly reshape the transmission lineshape, the integrated left--right transmission asymmetry saturates to a plateau set by the bulk Floquet winding number. Its origin is a deep-bulk branch-population principle: in the long-sample limit, each propagating Floquet--Bloch branch is generically populated with unit weight, since true Floquet bound states are nongeneric. The robust observable is therefore the cumulative transmission imbalance rather than the boundary-sensitive transmission profile. We propose direct detection by cold-atom transmission spectroscopy. For electronic transport, the same asymmetry admits contact-model-dependent electrical readouts: a coherent Floquet--Landauer--Büttiker interpretation predicts a near-\(2ef\) response in weak SAW devices, whereas a blocking-factor post-processing yields a qualitatively different signal.

</details>

### [Fiber-integrated Quantum Frequency Conversion for Long-distance Quantum Networking](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23444v1)

**Authors:** Zhichuan Liao, Ao Shen, Lai Zhou, Nan Jiang, Zhiliang Yuan  
**Type:** both · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.23444v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

The attenuated signal pulses are subsequently combined with a 1064.1 nm pump laser via dense wavelength division multiplexing (DWDM), then coupled into a periodically poled lithium niobate (PPLN) waveguide for fre- quency conversion. The converted photons are spectrally filtered using a combination of DWDM, fiber Bragg gratings (FBG), and a ultra-narrowband tunable optical filter (UNTF). A clock source, time...</figcaption>
</figure>

<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> Fig. 4. Simulated entanglement fidelity as a function of fiber length. The circle and diamond are calculated using the measured SNRs. The red solid line and circle correspond to a photon count rate of RS = 32.7 kHz (our work), and the red dashed line and di- amond correspond to RS = 35.5 kHz (Ref. [34]). The circle marks an equivalent fiber length of 7.5 km, corresponding to 1.2 dB transmission loss between the two laboratories.</figcaption>

**Main problem.** Signal photons from quantum nodes like NV centers often emit at wavelengths incompatible with low-loss telecom fibers, causing high transmission loss in long-distance networks.

**Main result.** The authors demonstrated a fiber-integrated quantum frequency conversion system with 9% efficiency and extremely low noise (154 Hz), achieving an expected entanglement fidelity exceeding 52% over 100 km.

**Method.** The researchers used a PPLN waveguide for down-conversion, implemented a multi-stage filtering module (DWDM, FBG, and UNTF) for noise suppression, and developed a theoretical model to simulate spin-photon entanglement fidelity.

**Summary.** This paper presents a fiber-integrated quantum frequency conversion system designed to bridge the gap between quantum nodes and telecom-band channels. By using a PPLN waveguide and a multi-stage filtering system, the authors achieved high-efficiency conversion with very low noise. They demonstrated that this setup can maintain high entanglement fidelity over 100 km of fiber. This advancement is crucial for building scalable, long-distance quantum communication networks.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The experimental platform is a fiber-integrated system using a periodically poled lithium niobate (PPLN) waveguide to convert 637.2 nm photons to 1588.3 nm. The quantum node model involves nitrogen-vacancy (NV) center electron spins using time-bin encoding.

**Key observables.** Conversion efficiency, pump-induced noise rate, signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), and entanglement fidelity.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Conversion efficiency of ~9%, noise suppression of >97 dB, 100 km transmission distance, and input photon rates ranging from 32.7 kHz to 327.7 kHz.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The model assumes the NV center electron spin is initially prepared in a specific state and uses a specific sequence for generating time-bin entanglement; filtering performance is limited by the trade-off between noise suppression and insertion loss.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1 shows the experimental setup; Fig 2 plots conversion efficiency and noise rate vs pump power; Fig 3 shows SNR vs pump power for different input rates; Fig 4 shows simulated entanglement fidelity vs fiber length.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the challenge of long-distance networking, describes the experimental setup and filtering architecture, presents experimental results for efficiency and noise, provides a theoretical model for fidelity, and concludes with the system's potential for scalability.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and quantum networks as it addresses the practical engineering challenges of frequency conversion, noise mitigation, and maintaining entanglement over long-distance fiber links.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Signal photons emitted by quantum nodes typically fall outside the low-loss telecom window of optical fibers, leading to severe transmission losses. Quantum frequency conversion (QFC) offers an effective optical interface that bridges quantum nodes with telecom-band channels, enabling long-distance quantum communication. In this work, we demonstrate a compact, fiber-integrated QFC system with low noise and a high signal-to-noise ratio (SNR). Using a periodically poled lithium niobate (PPLN) waveguide, input photons at 637.2 nm are down-converted to telecom photons at 1588.3 nm. Our system achieves a total conversion efficiency of approximately 9%, with pump-induced noise suppressed to 154 Hz. For input photon rates of 32.7, 118.0, and 327.7 kHz, the corresponding SNRs are 12.3, 43.9, and 117.8, respectively. We further develop a theoretical model to simulate the entanglement fidelity between nitrogen-vacancy (NV) center spins and the frequency-converted telecom photons. At the emission rate of an NV center, our QFC system maintains an expected fidelity exceeding 52% over a transmission distance of 100 km. These findings highlight the potential of our QFC system for scalable, long-distance quantum networking.

</details>

**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.24735v1>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates how Kochen-Specker contextuality disappears in the classical limit, specifically resolving the paradox of why state-independent contextuality vanishes despite persisting in the maximally mixed state.

**Main result.** The authors demonstrate that while state-dependent contextuality is erased by state degradation, state-independent contextuality is suppressed by noise acting between sequential measurements, which shrinks the observable correlations.

**Method.** The study uses open-system dynamics, analyzing sequential measurement implementations under depolarizing channels using both Schrödinger and Heisenberg pictures.

**Summary.** This paper explores the mechanism by which quantum contextuality vanishes as a system interacts with its environment. It addresses the apparent paradox where state-independent contextuality seems to resist classicalization because it persists even in maximally mixed states. By modeling noise via depolarizing channels in sequential measurements, the authors show that decoherence suppresses the specific correlations needed to witness contextuality. Ultimately, they prove that environmental interaction effectively drives the system into a classical, noncontextual regime.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The framework involves open quantum systems modeled via depolarizing channels and the GKSL master equation, applied to the KCBS and Peres-Mermin (PM) square scenarios.

**Key observables.** Sequential correlators and noncontextuality inequalities (KCBS and Peres-Mermin bounds).

**Important parameters / regimes.** The depolarizing parameter (p), representing the level of noise/decoherence, and the critical threshold (p_crit) for the disappearance of contextuality.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes Markovian dynamics, isotropic noise (unital noise), and that decoherence acts both on state preparation and during the measurement sequence.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by defining classicality and contextuality, introduces the paradox of state-independent contextuality, applies depolarizing channels to the KCBS and PM scenarios, and concludes by showing how noise-induced suppression of correlations leads to the classical limit.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a fundamental link between the emergence of classicality through decoherence and the erasure of foundational quantum properties like Kochen-Specker contextuality, which is highly relevant to the study of open quantum systems and quantum foundations.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

The emergence of classicality is fundamentally driven by the interaction between a quantum system and its environment. Foundational open-system approaches, notably the Caldeira-Leggett model, successfully captured how these interactions lead to macroscopic effects like quantum dissipation and decoherence. However, these approaches often leave the precise definitions of classicality and quantumness ambiguous. In quantum information theory, this boundary is a heavily scrutinized question, and Kochen-Specker contextuality emerges as a hallmark of nonclassicality. It is therefore natural to investigate whether decoherence can actually suppress this property. Taking this path creates an apparent conundrum, once there exist two distinct manifestations of quantum contextuality: state-dependent and state-independent ones. While state-dependent contextuality naturally vanishes under state degradation, state-independent contextuality could persist for any quantum state, since it shows up even for the maximally mixed state! In this paper, we resolve this apparent paradox by analyzing sequential measurement implementations of the paradigmatic Klyachko, Can, Binicioğlu, and Shumovsky (KCBS) and Peres-Mermin prepare-and-measure scenarios under the influence of depolarizing channels. By introducing noise both prior to and in between measurements, and by analyzing the resulting sequential correlators in both the Schrödinger and Heisenberg pictures, we show how open-system dynamics suppress the correlations required to witness contextuality, leading to classicalization.

</details>

### [Dynamical generation of stable optical-microwave squeezing in structured reservoirs](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.24215v1)

[(a), (b)] The squeezing level (SL) S(τ) at ˜ωbτ = 300 is plotted in the parameter space spanned by the coupling strength g and the MPA parameter r for both Markovian and structured envi- ronments, respectively. [(c),(d)] The SL S(τ) is shown as a function of the global dissipation rate γa and spectrum width λa for vari- ous coupling strengths, respectively. In panels (c) and (d), we take g = G = 0.15˜ωb and r = 0.2. The other parameters are identical to those in Fig. 2.</figcaption>
</figure>

[(a), (b)] The time evolutions of the variances ∆X(t) and ∆Y (t) are shown for both the generation (teal areas) and persistence (beige areas) processes under different environmental noises, respec- tively. Here, the spectral-density parameters satisfy λc/λa = γc/γa, and all other parameters are identical to those in Figs. 2(b), except for γc and λc.</figcaption>
</figure>

The squeezing level S(T ) at a uniformly chosen prolonged duration T (with ˜ωbT = 1000) is plotted as a function of the driving- switch-off time τ. In panel (a), g = G, and all other parameters are identical to those in Fig. 2(b). In panel (b), g = G = 0.15˜ωb, γa = 10−3˜ωb, λa = 10−2˜ωb, and the ratio λc/λa = γc/γa. [(b), (d)] Analytical results for the energy shift δ in Eq. (6) (lines) are similarly compared with the numeri- cally obtained values (points) as functions of g and G, respectively. G = 0.15˜ωb for panels (a) and (b), and g = 0.15˜ωb for panels (c) and (d). Additionally, ∆c = 3.5˜ωb is ﬁxed for all panels.</figcaption>

</figure>

<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 9.</b> FIG. 9. The dynamics of the variance ∆X(t) are presented using the effective Hamiltonian in Eq. (5) and the full system Hamiltonian in Eq. (4). The inset depicts the evolution of the corresponding CM element under the effective and full Hamiltonians. The parameters are set as κb = 10−5˜ωb, κa = 0.002˜ωb, κc = 10−3 ˜ωb, and the thermal numbers Na = Nc = 0 and Nb = 10. Other parameters are the same as Fig. 7.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** The challenge of generating and maintaining stable, high-level optical-microwave two-mode squeezed states (TMSS) in the presence of noise and the limitations of Markovian environments.

**Main result.** Non-Markovian (structured) environments can significantly enhance squeezing levels and allow TMS to persist even after external driving is switched off, especially when the spectral densities of the two modes are matched.

**Method.** The authors use second-order perturbation theory to derive an effective Hamiltonian and employ the Non-Markovian Hierarchical Equation of Motion (NMHL) to analyze the system's dynamical evolution.

**Summary.** This paper investigates how structured, non-Markovian environments can be used to generate and stabilize two-mode squeezing between optical and microwave modes in a hybrid electromechanical system. The authors demonstrate that non-Markovian noise can enhance squeezing levels compared to standard Markovian noise and even allow the squeezed state to persist after the external driving fields are turned off. They also find that matching the spectral densities of the optical and microwave reservoirs is key to preventing the degradation of the squeezed state. This work offers a way to mitigate the detrimental effects of anti-squeezing in quantum transducers.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** A hybrid electro-optomechanical (EOM) system consisting of an optical cavity mode, a microwave LC resonator, and a mechanical oscillator, all coupled to structured (Lorentzian) reservoirs.

**Key observables.** Two-mode squeezing level (SL), squeezing variance (Delta X), and anti-squeezing variance (Delta Y).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Effective coupling strength (g_eff), mechanical parametric amplification (r), spectral density width (lambda), and detunings.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The system operates in a large detuning regime where the effective Hamiltonian is valid, and the use of the Rotating Wave Approximation (RWA) for system-reservoir coupling.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1: Schematic of the EOM system; Fig 2: Time evolution of squeezing and anti-squeezing; Fig 3: Parameter space analysis of squeezing level; Fig 4: Squeezing persistence during drive switch-off; Fig 7-9: Validation of the effective Hamiltonian and coupling strength.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins with a system schematic and effective Hamiltonian derivation, followed by a numerical validation of the model, an analysis of squeezing dynamics in Markovian vs. non-Markovian environments, an investigation of squeezing persistence after driving is removed, and concludes with a comparison of matched vs. mismatched spectral densities.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a theoretical framework for using reservoir engineering and non-Markovian memory effects to enhance and stabilize entanglement, which is crucial for quantum transduction and continuous-variable quantum information processing.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Two-mode squeezed states as paradigmatic entangled resources have broad applications in quantum information processing. Here, we study the generation of stable optical-microwave squeezing in structured environments within a hybrid electro-optomechanical system, where a mechanical oscillator is simultaneously coupled to an optical cavity mode and a microwave mode of an LC resonator. Specifically, an effective Hamiltonian that captures the optical-microwave squeezing interaction is constructed by combining strongly modulated driving fields applied to both photonic modes with a mechanical parametric amplifier. Based on this effective model, the dynamical evolution of two-mode squeezing in structured environments is analyzed. It is remarkably shown that the non-Markovian noise can substantially enhance the squeezing level in comparison to the Markovian case, and that two-mode squeezing can persist even in the absence of external driving fields under non-Markovian conditions, thereby mitigating the detrimental effects of anti-squeezing. Furthermore, the persistence of the two-mode squeezed state is enhanced when the environmental spectral densities of the microwave and optical modes are identical. Our work provides a theoretical framework for generating and persisting two-mode squeezing in structured environments.

</details>

### [Impact of thermal and dissipative effects in a periodically-kicked quantum battery](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.24409v1)

**Authors:** Sebastián V. Romero, Xi Chen, Yue Ban  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.24409v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `non-equilibrium dynamics` **4/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **3/5** · `driven-dissipative phase transition` **2/5** · `entanglement & information structure` **2/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">

<img src="2604.24409_figures/2604.24409_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> FIG. 1. Schematic of a dissipative kicked-Ising QB. Start- ing from a passive Gibbs state at temperature T = 1/β, the battery undergoes periodic driving in the presence of dissipa- tion. Decoherence mechanisms include pure dephasing, which suppresses quantum coherences at a rate γz, and excitation- relaxation processes, which induce transitions between energy levels at rates γ±, respectively.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24409_figures/2604.24409_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> FIG. 2. Normalized injected energies using (Jth, hth) = (1/2, 1) for the initial TFIM and evolving at the self-dual point. Dashed (solid) lines correspond to dynamics in the presence (absence) of dephasing. (a) For N = 100 spins, evolution for different β up to m = 100 kicks with γz = 0.005. (b) Normalized injected energies at m = N/2 for different system sizes across varying temperatures, with γz = 1/(10N). (c) Normalized injected energy in the presence of dephasing under different (β, γz) pairs. All data are obtained analytically [12].</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24409_figures/2604.24409_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> FIG. 3. (a) Normalized injected energies (solid) and ergotropies (dash-dotted) in the presence of dephasing using (Jth, hth) = (1/2, 1) for the initial TFIM Hamiltonian and evolving at the self-dual point (J, b) = (π/4, −π/4) using N = 10 spins up to m = 10 kicks with γz = 0.01. (b) Same analysis as in (a), but for different system sizes N and temperatures β at kick m = N/2. (c) Normalized injected energy at the kick m = N/2 of a KIC QB with N = 10 in the presence of dephasing under various (β, γz) pairs. We set (Jth, hth) = (1/2, 1) for the TFIM Hamiltonian. (d) Same study as (c), but computing the ergotropy.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24409_figures/2604.24409_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> FIG. 4. (a) Normalized injected energies (solid) and ergotropies (dash-dotted) in the presence of thermal dissipation using (Jth, hth) = (1/2, 1) for the initial TFIM Hamiltonian and evolving at the self-dual point (J, b) = (π/4, −π/4). Results are shown for N = 10 spins, up to m = 10 kicks with γ = 0.01. (b) Same analysis as (a) but for different system sizes N across different temperatures β at the kick m = N/2. (c) Normalized injected energy at the kick m = N/2 of a KIC QB with N = 10 in the presence of thermal dissipation under various (β, γ) pairs. We set (Jth, hth) = (1/2, 1) for the TFIM Hamiltonian. (d) Same study as (c), but computing the ergotropy.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** Investigating the impact of thermal fluctuations and environmental dissipation (dephasing and relaxation) on the performance and robustness of periodically-kicked quantum batteries.

**Main result.** The study establishes that while temperature and dissipation reduce energy injection and ergotropy, the charging protocol remains robust under certain regimes, and provides an analytical framework to quantify these effects.

**Method.** The authors use the Jordan-Wigner and Fourier transformations to map the system to free fermions, solving the Lindblad master equation via a system of coupled linear ordinary differential equations and applying the Cayley-Hamilton theorem.

**Summary.** This paper explores how realistic environmental factors, such as temperature and decoherence, affect the charging of a quantum battery based on a kicked-Ising model. By using a mathematical framework involving Jordan-Wigner transformations, the authors show how energy injection and extractable work (ergotropy) decay under dissipation. They identify specific regimes where the battery's performance remains robust despite these effects. The work provides a systematic way to assess the viability of quantum batteries in noisy, real-world environments.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** A Kicked-Ising Chain (KIC) acting as a quantum battery, modeled by a Transverse-Field Ising Model (TFIM) Hamiltonian subject to periodic kicks and open-system dynamics via the Lindblad master equation (including pure dephasing and thermal dissipation).

**Key observables.** Ergotropy (extractable work), injected energy, mean energy stored, fermion occupation number, and parity weights.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Inverse temperature (beta), dephasing rate (gamma_z), dissipation rates (gamma_plus/minus), system size (N), and Ising interaction strength (J).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The dynamics are modeled using the Lindblad master equation, and the dissipation is assumed to follow specific local-spin operators.

**Figures summary.** Figure 2 shows the evolution of normalized injected energy over time for different temperatures, the scaling of energy injection with system size, and a parameter map of energy injection across temperature and dephasing rate space.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by defining the Kicked-Ising battery model, moves to the analytical derivation of the open-system dynamics using fermionization, presents the results for energy injection and ergotropy under thermal and dissipative effects, and concludes with a discussion on robustness and scaling.

**Why it may be interesting.** This paper is highly relevant for researchers in open quantum systems and many-body dynamics as it provides an exact analytical framework for studying how environmental noise and finite temperature degrade the performance of non-equilibrium quantum energy storage devices.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Quantum batteries (QBs) have emerged as a promising route for fast energy storage and on-chip power supply in quantum devices. Given the limited analytical understanding of open Floquet QBs, we employ the kicked-Ising model as a tractable platform to systematically study its performance under realistic conditions, including finite temperature effects and environmental dissipation. Starting from Gibbs states of the transverse-field Ising model, we incorporate thermal and decoherence effects along the evolution, using both analytical and numerical approaches. Taking ergotropy as a central figure of merit, we characterize the injected and extractable energy, and identify regimes where charging remains robust despite environmental effects. Our results provide a systematic framework for assessing QB performance under thermal and dissipative effects.

</details>

### [Entropy Signatures of Collective Modes and Vortex Dynamics in Rotating Two--Dimensional Bose--Einstein Condensates](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.24557v1)

**Authors:** L. A. Machado, N. D. Chavda, B. Chatterjee, M. A. Caracanhas, B. Chakrabarti, A. Gammal, R. P. Sagar  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum gases · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.24557v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `entanglement & information structure` **4/5** · 🔥 `non-equilibrium dynamics` **4/5** · `driven-dissipative phase transition` **2/5**

<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> FIG. 1. Initial condensate states at selected rotation frequen- cies Ω. Top panels show the density n(x, y; 0), and bottom panels display the corresponding phase. For Ω≤1.2, the condensate forms a uniform central cloud with no vortex, and the phase is essentially uniform. At Ω= 1.3, a central vortex emerges, while Ω= 1.5 and Ω= 2.0 show increasing vortex charge, evolving into a giant vortex with annular density and a phase indicating total charges of 4 and 8, respectively.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24557_figures/2604.24557_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> FIG. 2. Shannon information entropies and mutual informa- tion as a function of rotation frequency Ω. All four quantities, Sx, Sy, Sxy, and mutual information I, remain constant for Ω≤1.2. Beyond Ω= 1.2, each measure exhibits a kink and increases in a nonlinear manner, signaling the onset of cen- tral vortex formation and the emergence of correlations not directly visible in the density or phase profiles.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24557_figures/2604.24557_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> FIG. 3. Density evolution of the condensate for Ω= 0.5 fol- lowing an weak interaction quench. Snapshots at selected time points show the periodic expansion and contraction of the central bright cloud, characteristic of the monopole (breathing) mode.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24557_figures/2604.24557_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> FIG. 4. Time evolution of information-theoretic measures for Ω= 0.5 during the monopole dynamics. We follow the same quench protocol as in Fig. 3. Panels show (a) Sx, (b) Sy, (c) Sxy, and (d) mutual information I as functions of time.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24557_figures/2604.24557_fig5.jpg" alt="Figure 5">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> FIG. 5. Time evolution of the density for the Ω= 2.0 conden- sate following a stronger interaction quench (g′ ≈5g). The central giant vortex (dark circular region) remains largely cir- cular, while the bright outer ring develops a fourfold modula- tion, splitting into four partially connected high-density seg- ments. The snapshots illustrate the combined effects of radial breathing and angular symmetry breaking at selected times, highlighting the dynamic fragmentation of the outer annular region.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24557_figures/2604.24557_fig6.jpg" alt="Figure 6">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 6.</b> FIG. 6. Time evolution of angular observables along the outer ring. We follow the same quench protocol as in Fig. 5. Ob- servables: (a) Radially weighted fourfold operator O4(t). (b) Angular Fourier components A4(t) and A2(t). (c) Angular Kullback–Leibler divergence Dθ KL(t) and (d) mutual infor- mation I(t).</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24557_figures/2604.24557_fig7.jpg" alt="Figure 7">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 7.</b> FIG. 8. Time evolution of quadrupole and information- theoretic observables for Ω= 0.5 following the trap quench. (a) Quadrupole operator OQ(t), (b) marginal entropies Sx(t) (red curve) and Sy(t) (blue curve), (c) joint entropy Sxy(t), and (d) mutual information I(t)</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24557_figures/2604.24557_fig8.jpg" alt="Figure 8">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 8.</b> FIG. 7. Density snapshots at selected times for Ω= 0.5 fol- lowing the trap quench. The initially circular, vortex-free cloud oscillates between prolate and oblate shapes along x and y axes. The density remains compact with no fragmentation, and the center of mass stays near the trap origin, indicating a nearly pure quadrupole oscillation.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24557_figures/2604.24557_fig9.jpg" alt="Figure 9">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 9.</b> FIG. 9. One-body density snapshots at selected times for the giant vortex with initial Ω= 2.0 following the trap quench along the x-axis. The initial giant vortex at the center and the thin outer ring exhibit strong deformation and fragmentation over time, leading to irregular, chaotic dynamics.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24557_figures/2604.24557_fig10.jpg" alt="Figure 10">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 10.</b> FIG. 10. Time evolution of collective and angular observables for Ω= 2.0 following the trap quench. Panels (a)–(c) show the monopole OM(t), quadrupole OQ(t), and fourfold mode O4(t), respectively. Panel (d) displays the angular Fourier modes A4(t) ,A2(t) and A1(t). All observables exhibit irregu- lar, large-amplitude oscillations indicative of chaotic dynam- ics.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** Investigating the nonequilibrium dynamics, stability, and symmetry breaking of rotating 2D Bose-Einstein condensates (BECs) following interaction and trap quenches.

**Main result.** The study identifies a transition from regular, periodic oscillations in vortex-free states to irregular, chaotic dynamics in giant vortices, using mutual information to detect a clear dynamical phase transition at a specific rotation frequency.

**Method.** The Multiconfigurational Time-Dependent Hartree method for bosons (MCTDHB) is used to solve the time-dependent Schrödinger equation for a fully correlated many-body system.

**Summary.** This paper explores how rotating 2D Bose-Einstein condensates respond to sudden changes in their environment. It demonstrates that while low-rotation states undergo regular oscillations, high-rotation 'giant' vortices exhibit chaotic splitting and fragmentation. By using information-theoretic tools like mutual information, the authors can precisely identify the transition between regular and chaotic dynamical regimes. This work establishes entropy-based measures as sensitive probes for many-body correlations in quantum gases.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** A 2D rotating Bose gas of N=8 identical bosons in a symmetric anharmonic (quartic) trap with a finite-range Gaussian interaction potential.

**Key observables.** One-body density and phase, Shannon entropies (marginal and joint), mutual information, Kullback-Leibler divergence (including angular-resolved), and angular Fourier components.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Rotation frequency (Omega), interaction strength (g), trap anisotropy, and number of bosons (N=8).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The system is treated in a 2D plane with a finite-range Gaussian potential instead of a pure delta-function contact interaction; the number of orbitals (M) is finite.

**Figures summary.** Figures show density snapshots of vortex formation and fragmentation, time evolution of entropy and mutual information, and a 3D dynamical phase diagram of correlations.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the physical system and Hamiltonian, describes the MCTDHB method, analyzes initial state characterization, explores dynamics under interaction quenches, examines dynamics under trap quenches, and concludes with a phase diagram of correlations.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a powerful framework for using information-theoretic measures to quantify many-body complexity, fragmentation, and the onset of chaos in ultracold atomic systems.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We investigate the nonequilibrium dynamics of a two-dimensional rotating Bose gas confined in a symmetric anharmonic trap, employing the multiconfigurational time-dependent Hartree method for bosons (MCTDHB). We study states ranging from vortex-free configurations to multicharged (giant) vortices, prepared by tuning the rotation frequency, and analyze their response to sudden interaction and trap quenches. In vortex-free states, interaction quenches induce regular breathing--like dynamics, whereas in the presence of giant vortices they lead to symmetry-breaking surface excitations. In contrast, trap deformations that excite quadrupole-like modes produce stable oscillations in vortex-free condensates but trigger rapid, irregular, and effectively chaotic splitting dynamics in multicharged vortices. To characterize these processes beyond conventional density and phase observables, we employ information-theoretic measures, including marginal and joint entropies, mutual information, and Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence, supplemented by an angular-resolved KL measure that captures symmetry breaking and azimuthal localization. We find that chaotic splitting is accompanied by a pronounced growth of information-theoretic indicators, signaling the buildup of many-body correlations and increasing complexity in the system dynamics. Our results demonstrate the extreme sensitivity of giant vortices to excitation protocols and establish information-theoretic measures as a powerful framework to quantify correlations and complexity in rotating quantum gases.

</details>

**Main problem.** Developing a programmable method for Floquet engineering of tight-binding Hamiltonians in momentum-space lattices by independently tuning on-site energies and tunneling amplitudes.

**Main result.** The authors demonstrate that quantum resonances in a shaken rotor can be used to engineer various models, including the Rice-Mele model, and show that optimal control can significantly enhance multi-period Floquet fidelity.

**Method.** The study uses Floquet theory, first-order time-dependent perturbation theory, and a kernel-based inversion method, supplemented by gradient-ascent optimal control techniques.

**Summary.** This paper presents a method to engineer programmable tight-binding Hamiltonians in momentum space using a shaken rotor of ultracold atoms. By exploiting quantum resonances and optimal control, the researchers can independently tune tunneling amplitudes and on-site energies. They experimentally demonstrate the simulation of the Rice-Mele model, topological edge states, and Bloch oscillations. This approach turns quantum resonances into a versatile resource for quantum simulation.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** A Bose-Einstein condensate of Rubidium-87 atoms in a dynamically modulated (shaken) optical lattice, modeled by a generalized 'shaken rotor' Hamiltonian.

**Key observables.** Momentum distribution, site populations, momentum width, average momentum, and Floquet operator fidelity.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Effective Planck constant (hbar_eff), kick period (T), lattice depth (s0), and modulation functions (f1, f2).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The framework assumes a perturbative regime (weak driving) and relies on quantum resonance conditions to maintain discrete translational symmetry.

**Figures summary.** Figures show the real and imaginary parts of modulation functions, comparison between perturbative and optimal control solutions, and experimental observations of momentum-space Bloch oscillations, topological edge states, and wave packet expansion in superlattices.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses from theoretical derivation of the Floquet framework and analytical relations to the implementation of optimal control, followed by experimental verification of various tight-binding models and topological phenomena.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a powerful toolkit for simulating complex condensed-matter models like the Rice-Mele model and topological junctions using the highly controllable platform of momentum-space lattices in ultracold atoms.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Quantum simulation with ultracold atoms provides a versatile platform to emulate condensed-matter models. In particular, momentum-space lattices enable the realization of programmable tight-binding Hamiltonians. Here, we generalize this approach by exploiting quantum resonances of a periodically driven (shaken) rotor within the Floquet framework. Using first-order time-dependent perturbation theory, we derive analytical relations between the lattice modulation and the effective tight-binding parameters, and identify explicit solutions for several resonances. We further apply optimal-control techniques to enhance the multi-period Floquet fidelity and extend the accessible parameter regimes. Experimentally, we implement this scheme with a Bose-Einstein condensate of rubidium-87 atoms in a dynamically modulated optical lattice. We demonstrate the simulation of the Rice-Mele model, including band-structure measurements and topological edge states, as well as momentum Bloch oscillations, and superlattice configurations with controlled periodicity. Our results establish quantum resonances as a powerful resource for Floquet engineering of tight-binding models in momentum space.

</details>

**Authors:** Asliddin Khudoyberdiev, Dag-Björn Hering, Vanessa Sulaiman, Götz S. Uhrig

In panel b), the field is turned off after the switching at t1, where t1 is the time at which m(t) reaches its first minimum in the m(t) &lt; 0 region. The minimum value of the external field for switching is labeled by the superscript “thr” for threshold. For all anisotropies, the field strength is chosen 5% higher than the threshold value to ensure reliable switching.</figcaption>

<img src="2604.24695_figures/2604.24695_fig8.jpg" alt="Figure 8">

The initial state, with two linked red and blue sublat- tices, is shown on the left. The arrows inside the circles indicate the direction of the sublattice magnetization. Subsequent to the ap- plication of the staggered magnetic fields on the sublattices (gray arrows), the spins exhibit a slight deflection, concurrently forming a robust effective field (black arrow). Consequently, the spins ro- tate around the resulting effective field (shown by the green curved arrows) and the final switched state is obtained (last two circles on the right).</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24695_figures/2604.24695_fig9.jpg" alt="Figure 9">

The triangle markers correspond to the hthr s values while the solid lines are linear fits: hthr,α s,ﬁt = zαdα(1 −χ), α ∈{sc, sq, hc} where zα is the coordination number of the lattice and dα are the fit parameters with dsc = (0.228 ± 0.0007)J (simple cubic lattice zsc = 6), dsq = (0.208 ± 0.0016)J (square lattice zsq = 4) and dhc = (0.191±0.0019)J (honeycomb lattice zhc = 3). The data for the square and simple cubic lattices are taken from Ref. 15.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24695_figures/2604.24695_fig10.jpg" alt="Figure 10">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 10.</b> Fig. 10. The dynamics of the sublattice magnetization under staggered switching field in the anisotropy range χ ∈[0.8, 1). Panel a) shows the dynamics when the field is active at t &gt; 0 whereas panel b) shows the dynamics for the case when the field is applied only till the magnetization reaches its first minimum. The strength of the field is chosen always 5% higher than the threshold value of the field at the given anisotropy.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** Investigating the control and switching of the Neel vector in a quantum antiferromagnetic honeycomb lattice, specifically determining the threshold magnetic fields required for magnetization reorientation.

**Main result.** The study establishes universal scaling laws for threshold fields in different lattices and demonstrates that staggered fields enable 'exchange-enhanced switching' at much lower magnitudes than uniform fields.

**Method.** The authors use time-dependent Schwinger boson mean-field theory (SB-MFT) and Continuous Similarity Transformation (CST) to model the non-equilibrium switching process and validate equilibrium properties.

**Summary.** This paper explores how to efficiently flip the magnetic orientation in a quantum antiferromagnetic honeycomb lattice. By using Schwinger boson mean-field theory, the authors show that applying a staggered magnetic field can trigger 'exchange-enhanced switching' at much lower energy costs than a uniform field. They also identify universal scaling laws that relate the required switching field to the lattice's coordination number. These findings are significant for the development of ultrafast, energy-efficient spintronic and magnonic devices.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** A spin-1/2 anisotropic XXZ Heisenberg model on a quantum antiferromagnetic honeycomb lattice, including both uniform and staggered external magnetic fields.

**Key observables.** Sublattice magnetization (Neel vector), spin gap, boson occupation numbers, and oscillation frequencies of the Neel vector.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Anisotropy parameter (chi), coordination number (z), exchange coupling (J), and external magnetic field strengths (hu and hs).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The study focuses on the ground state at zero temperature and assumes the validity of the Schwinger boson mean-field approximation for low-symmetry lattices.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the switching process, the honeycomb lattice structure, spin gap and magnetization vs. anisotropy, temporal evolution of magnetization and boson occupation, and scaling laws for threshold fields across different lattice types.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by validating the SB-MFT framework against CST and DMRG for equilibrium properties, then moves to modeling the non-equilibrium switching dynamics under uniform and staggered fields, and concludes with a comparative analysis of lattice coordination numbers and universal scaling laws.

**Why it may be interesting.** The paper provides deep insights into many-body dynamics, specifically how collective modes (magnons) drive the reversal of an order parameter and how dephasing leads to steady-state convergence without explicit relaxation.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

The switching of antiferromagnetic order and its efficient control promise to enable ultrafast manipulation of data and large storage capacity. Recently, the time-dependent Schwinger boson mean-field theory has been successfully developed to study the Néel vector switching in hypercubic antiferromagnetic lattices. In the present article, we aim at demonstrating that the approach is a well-justified framework to capture the essentials of the switching process, even in low-symmetry quantum antiferromagnets. To this end, we show the possibility of the sublattice magnetization reorientation in the quantum antiferromagnetic honeycomb lattice. First, equilibrium properties of the honeycomb lattice are analyzed using the Schwinger boson mean-field theory and compared to the continuous similarity transformation method to justify the applicability of the approach. Then, the Schwinger boson mean-field theory is employed for switching process. We provide a comprehensive answer to the question what the threshold switching fields are when the coordination number of the lattice is varied. Indeed, the results of the study reveal a correspondence between lattice structures and the threshold fields by comparing them for the square and the simple cubic lattices and the honeycomb lattice. The findings of the present article extend the foundation for future theoretical and computational advancements in the field of antiferromagnetic switching. These advancements are of particular relevance for the development of ultrafast spintronic or magnonic devices.

</details>

Results under uniform dephasing rates are shown in gray, and under the optimized dephasing rates in color. Arrows indicate mean values. The optimized-noise coherence lengths show average improvements over the uniform-noise coherence lengths of (a) 3.5%, (b) 14.1%, and (c) 21.5%. The optimized-noise population fluxes show improvements over the uniform-noise population fluxes of (a) 21.9%, (b) 21.8%, and (c) 19.6%.</figcaption>
</figure>

**Main problem.** Investigating whether spatially structured (site-dependent) environmental noise can be engineered to enhance quantum transport and overcome localization effects like Wannier-Stark and Anderson localization.

**Main result.** Site-optimized dephasing consistently outperforms uniform dephasing by increasing transport efficiency and promoting spatial delocalization, with specific optimal profiles depending on the tunneling range and energy landscape.

**Method.** Numerical optimization of site-dependent dephasing rates using the Adamax algorithm within a Lindblad quantum master equation framework, supplemented by analytical solutions for three-site models.

**Summary.** This paper explores how engineering site-dependent environmental noise can enhance quantum transport in 1D systems prone to localization. By optimizing dephasing profiles, the authors show that one can overcome energy detuning and destructive interference in both ramped and disordered potentials. The study identifies specific design rules, such as alternating dephasing for short-range tunneling and increasing dephasing for long-range tunneling. These findings offer a strategy for controlling population flux and spatial coherence in open quantum systems.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** One-dimensional lattices with power-law tunneling (parameterized by exponent alpha) subject to either ramped (Wannier-Stark) or energy-disordered (Anderson) potentials.

**Key observables.** Steady-state population flux, spatial delocalization of the steady state, coherence length, and coherence maps (off-diagonal density matrix elements).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Tunneling power-law exponent (alpha = 1, 3, 5), dephasing rates (site-dependent vs. uniform), energy landscape gradient/disorder strength, and system size (N).

**Assumptions / limitations.** Single-particle regime, independent local environments for each site, and small tunneling/leak rates relative to energy differences and dephasing.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate energy structures, localization development, population flux as a function of dephasing, optimized dephasing profiles, and steady-state coherence maps for different tunneling ranges and potentials.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of noise-assisted transport, presents the theoretical model and optimization framework, details results for ramp potentials (short vs. long-range tunneling), extends findings to disordered systems, and concludes with a discussion of design principles and experimental implications.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides new design principles for controlling transport and coherence in open quantum systems by treating the environment as a tunable, spatially structured resource rather than a uniform nuisance.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Environmental noise can enhance transport, an effect known as environmental noise-assisted quantum transport. Most theoretical studies focus on optimizing system parameters under spatially uniform system-environment coupling. Here, instead, we optimize the environmental noise itself by allowing for site-dependent dephasing. We investigate steady-state transport in one-dimensional lattices with either ramped or disordered energy landscapes, considering both short- and long-range coherent tunneling. In the absence of environmental effects, in the thermodynamic limit these systems can exhibit localization, and thus suppressed transport, arising from destructive interference. Using a Lindblad master equation framework, we implement local dephasing optimized to maximize steady-state population flux. We find that for ramp potentials, short-range tunneling favors selective dephasing on alternating sites, whereas long-range tunneling benefits from a dephasing profile that increases with distance from the injection site. In energetically disordered systems, strongly detuned sites require enhanced local dephasing under short-range tunneling to facilitate transport. In all cases, we find that site-optimized dephasing allows higher transport efficiency than uniform dephasing, and it is accompanied by increased spatial delocalization of the steady state. Our results provide microscopic insight into the interplay between coherent dynamics and environmental noise. Dephasing broadens energy levels locally, helping to overcome detuning and destructive interference. More generally, we establish spatially-structured environmental noise as a strategy for controlling both quantum transport and state coherence in open systems.

</details>

<a id="paper-2604.23768"></a>
### [Minimal spin-rotor model for Barnett and Einstein--de Haas physics](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23768v1)

**Authors:** Saikat Banerjee  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.23768v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `entanglement & information structure` **4/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **3/5** · `spintronics-quantum-optics interface` **2/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23768_figures/2604.23768_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> FIG. 1. (a) Exact spectrum ε± m of the minimal spin-rotor model as a function of the rotor angular-momentum quantum number m, showing the Barnett-split branches around the underlying rotor dispersion. (b) Time dependence of the reduced spin purity Pspin(t) for m = 2, 4, 8, corresponding to η = gm/∆= 0.5, 1, 2 for the parameters used here. These three cases illustrate the weak-coupling, maximally entangling, and large-η regimes, respectively. The purity reduction is maximal near the balanced point η = 1, while the cases η and 1/η have the same minimum purity but different oscillation frequencies. The reduction of Pspin(t) below unity signals the onset of coherent spin-rotor entanglement. (c)...</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** Investigating whether the classical effective-field description of the Barnett effect remains valid when the mechanical rotation is quantized.

**Main result.** The study shows that quantizing the rotor causes the Barnett field to become operator-valued, leading to the generation of coherent spin-rotor entanglement and a breakdown of the classical Zeeman-like analogy.

**Method.** The author uses an exact analytical solution of a spin-rotor Hamiltonian, calculating eigenenergies, time-evolution, and entanglement metrics like spin purity and entanglement entropy.

**Summary.** This paper explores the quantum limits of the Barnett and Einstein-de Haas effects. By using a minimal model of a spin-1/2 particle coupled to a quantum rotor, the author demonstrates that quantizing the rotation transforms the Barnett field from a static magnetic field into an operator-valued field. This transition leads to the creation of entanglement between the spin and the rotor. The results show that the classical effective-field picture fails qualitatively in the quantum regime, replaced by a reciprocal relationship between spin dynamics and rotor coherence.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** A minimal, exactly solvable model consisting of a spin-1/2 particle coupled to a planar quantum rotor with a Hamiltonian containing spin-rotation coupling.

**Key observables.** Reduced spin purity (P_spin), entanglement entropy (S_ent), rotor coherence (|K(t)|), and spin-state-dependent phase evolution.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The coupling parameter eta = gm/Delta, which defines weak-coupling (eta < 1), balanced (eta = 1), and large-coupling (eta > 1) regimes.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The model is a minimal setting; the author notes that adding more degrees of freedom (like angular dependence) is a potential extension.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1(a) shows the energy spectrum and Barnett-split branches; Fig 1(b) shows the time dependence of spin purity for different coupling strengths; Fig 1(c) shows the periodic buildup and decay of entanglement entropy.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the physical problem of the Barnett effect, presents the minimal spin-rotor model, provides an exact analytical solution, analyzes the quantum-mechanical breakdown of the effective-field picture through entanglement and purity, and concludes with the identification of a reciprocal quantum manifestation of the effect.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a fundamental look at how the quantization of a mechanical degree of freedom fundamentally changes the nature of an effective field, moving from a static parameter to an operator that generates entanglement.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

The Barnett effect is usually understood through an effective magnetic field generated by mechanical rotation, while its reciprocal Einstein--de Haas effect describes the transfer of spin angular momentum to mechanical motion. We show that this effective-field picture changes qualitatively once the mechanical degree of freedom itself is quantized. To demonstrate this, we introduce an exactly solvable minimal spin-rotor model in which a spin-$1/2$ is coupled to a quantum rotor. In a fixed angular-momentum sector, the model reproduces the conventional Barnett splitting and remains formally equivalent to a Zeeman problem. For a superposition of rotor sectors, however, the Barnett field becomes operator-valued and the resulting dynamics generates coherent spin-rotor entanglement. This is directly visible in the reduced spin purity, rotor coherence, and entanglement entropy. Our results identify a minimal quantum setting in which the Barnett effective-field picture departs from its classical form and acquires a reciprocal manifestation through spin-dependent rotor coherence.

</details>

</figure>

**Main problem.** The paper proposes and investigates a cavity/circuit QED analogue of the Stern-Gerlach experiment to explore how quantum contextuality and measurement backaction affect the stability of dressed states.

**Main result.** The study demonstrates that different measurement unravellings (homodyne vs. heterodyne) lead to non-equivalent physical outcomes, such as biased vs. symmetric dressed-state populations, and identifies the emergence of spontaneous dressed-state polarization and coherent-state superpositions.

**Method.** The author uses quantum trajectory theory, specifically Monte Carlo wave-function simulations and the Stochastic Schrödinger Equation, to unravel the Lindblad master equation.

**Summary.** This paper proposes a quantum optical version of the Stern-Gerlach experiment using a cavity QED platform. It shows that the way a system is monitored (e.g., via homodyne or heterodyne detection) changes the physical outcomes, demonstrating quantum contextuality. The research highlights how measurement backaction can drive the system into spontaneous polarization or create transient Schrödinger cat-like states. Additionally, it explores how photoelectron statistics can reveal squeezed fluctuations in the bad-cavity limit.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** A Jaynes-Cummings system consisting of a two-state atom coupled to a cavity mode, driven by a resonant field, and subject to cavity photon loss and spontaneous emission.

**Key observables.** Quadrature phase operators, measurement signals (homodyne/heterodyne), Wigner functions, Bloch-sphere trajectories, and photoelectron waiting-time distributions.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Driving strength (epsilon), coupling strength (g), cavity decay (kappa), atomic decay (gamma), and the local oscillator phase (theta).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The study utilizes the rotating wave approximation (RWA), the secular approximation, and considers the 'zero system size' limit (gamma/2kappa = 0) in certain regimes.

**Figures summary.** Figure 3 depicts Bloch-sphere trajectories and conditioned Wigner functions showing the evolution of the system.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces a cavity QED analogue of the Stern-Gerlach experiment, defines the Hamiltonian and master equation, explores different measurement unravellings (homodyne and heterodyne), analyzes the effects of local oscillator phase on state stability, and concludes with the use of photoelectron statistics as a diagnostic tool.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides deep insights into how the choice of measurement scheme (unravelling) fundamentally alters the observed steady-state dynamics and symmetry breaking in open quantum systems, which is central to quantum optics and the study of quantum contextuality.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We propose a contextual cavity/circuit QED analogue and extension of the Stern-Gerlach experiment, where the pseudo-spin of a two-state `atomic' transition plays the role of the ``spin'', while the resonant field driving the transition stands for the ``magnetic field''. A phase-sensitive continuous detection of the cavity field coupled to the induced `atomic' dipole affects the stability of the two distinct outcomes. The dressed states comprising the latter give their place to a self-consistent spontaneous dressed-state polarization as the driving strength is lowered. The associated evolution proves anew highly contextual, underpinned by a persistent production of coherent-state superpositions for a particular setting of the monitoring device. Finally, when bistability is absent, we employ the photoelectron `atomic' emission statistics as a diagnostic tool of the cavity field fluctuations.

</details>

**Authors:** Hiroo Azuma  
**Type:** both · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.23086v1>
