<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> Figure 5: A schematic illustration of the implementation for the approximate generation of |ϕm⟩s. The procedure is as follows. A source emits a two-mode squeezed vacuum state in modes s and A. Vacuum states |0⟩1|0⟩2 · · · |0⟩s−1 are prepared in modes 1, 2, . . . , s −1, and the beam splitters ˆB1,s, ˆB2,s, ..., ˆBs−1,s are applied to modes 1, 2, ..., s. Then, the displacement operators ˆDj(αj) (j = 1, 2, ..., s) are applied to these modes. Single-photon detection is individually performed on modes 1, 2, ..., s. Finally, a phase plate is placed on mode A to obtain |ϕm⟩s approximately, with ϕ0 = 0.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23086_figures/2604.23086_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> Figure 6: Plots of P, the probability that all four imperfect detectors click simultaneously, as a function of r = arctanh q. Both the horizontal and vertical axes are displayed by the logarithmic scale. The solid red, dashed blue, and dotted purple curves represent η = 1, 0.8, and 0.6, respectively. We observe from these curves that P depends only weakly on η. All these curves can be well fitted by log10 P = c + 8 log10 r, where c is a constant.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23086_figures/2604.23086_fig5.jpg" alt="Figure 5">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> Figure 7: Plots of F, the fidelity between the post-selected output state and the equal- amplitude superposition, as a function of r = arctanh q. The solid red, dashed blue, and dotted purple curves correspond to η = 1, 0.8, and 0.6, respectively. From these curves, we note that F depends strongly on η.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23086_figures/2604.23086_fig6.jpg" alt="Figure 6">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 6.</b> Figure 8: Plots of V, the negativity volume of the approximate state emitted as |ϕ0⟩4 in mode A of the quantum-optical circuit shown in Fig. 5. For fixed squeezing parameter r, we plot V as a function of η, the efficiency of the single-photon detectors. The solid red, dashed blue, and dotted purple curves represent r = 0.1, 0.2, and 0.3, respectively.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23086_figures/2604.23086_fig7.jpg" alt="Figure 7">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 7.</b> Figure 9: A schematic illustration of the phase-estimation experiment. The value of ϕk can be estimated using the reference beam |ϕj⟩s,1.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates the non-Gaussianity of Pegg-Barnett phase-operator eigenstates and proposes an experimental quantum-optical circuit for their generation and for phase estimation.

**Main result.** The authors confirm that these eigenstates exhibit negative Wigner functions, with negativity volume increasing with Hilbert space dimension, and identify single-photon detection as the source of their non-Gaussianity.

**Method.** The study uses Wigner function calculations and negativity volume metrics to quantify non-Gaussianity, alongside numerical simulations of a quantum-optical circuit including imperfect detectors.

**Summary.** This paper explores the properties and experimental generation of Pegg-Barnett phase-operator eigenstates. It demonstrates that these states are non-Gaussian, characterized by negative regions in their Wigner functions that grow with the Hilbert space dimension. The authors propose a quantum-optical circuit using single-photon detection to create these states and evaluate how detector inefficiency affects state fidelity. Finally, the paper presents a scheme for using these states in a phase-estimation experiment.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The system consists of Pegg-Barnett phase-operator eigenstates in a finite-dimensional Hilbert space, generated via a quantum-optical circuit involving two-mode squeezed vacuum, beam splitters, displacement operators, and single-photon detectors.

**Key observables.** Wigner function negativity volume, fidelity, detection probability, and photon-number detection probabilities at beam splitter outputs.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Hilbert space dimension (s), detector efficiency (eta), squeezing parameter (r), and phase (phi).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The use of infinite-dimensional negativity volume measures for finite-dimensional states is justified by the limit s to infinity; the study also assumes a small displacement limit for certain calculations.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows Wigner functions for increasing s; Figure 2 shows a rotating Wigner pattern; Figure 3 and 4 show the monotonic increase of negativity volume and Wigner function radius with s; Figure 9 provides a schematic of the phase-estimation setup.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by analyzing the Wigner functions and non-Gaussianity of the eigenstates, moves to the design and simulation of a generation circuit under detector inefficiency, and concludes with a practical application in a phase-estimation experiment.

**Why it may be interesting.** This paper is highly relevant to quantum optics and quantum information as it explores the generation of non-Gaussian states, which are essential resources for continuous-variable quantum computing and high-precision phase estimation.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

In this paper, we study the non-Gaussianity of the eigenstates of the Pegg-Barnett phase observable. By computing the Wigner functions of the eigenstates, we confirm that they take negative values in specific regions of the phase space. The Pegg-Barnett phase-operator eigenstates lie on a finite-dimensional Hilbert space. Thus, we examine how their negativity volumes depend on the dimension of the Hilbert space. Moreover, we present a quantum-optical circuit that generates these eigenstates and identify single-photon detection as the origin of their non-Gaussianity. To investigate a more realistic experimental implementation, we introduce imperfect single-photon detectors with non-unit efficiency into the circuit and evaluate the dependence of the detection probability, the output-ideal fidelity, and the negativity volume of the approximate eigenstate output from the circuit on the detector efficiency. Finally, as a practical application, we consider a phase-estimation experiment of an arbitrary unknown state by injecting both the unknown state and a known Pegg-Barnett eigenstate into a 50-50 beam splitter and individually counting the numbers of photons emitted from its two output ports.

</details>

**Authors:** Hanlin Tang, Kidae Shin, Ashwin K. Boddeti, Sebastian P. Horvath, Adam Turflinger, Joseph Alexander, Jeffrey A. Dhas, Zihua Zhu, Shuhang Pan, Jeff D. Thompson, Yingge Du, Frederick J. Walker, Charles H. Ahn

**Main problem.** The spin coherence of Er3+ ions in CaWO4 is limited by the nuclear spin bath of the 14.3% natural abundance 183W isotope, hindering the development of scalable quantum interconnects.

**Main result.** The researchers successfully grew isotopically enriched Ca186WO4 thin films via MBE, reducing 183W abundance from 14.3% to 1.2% and demonstrating single-ion resonance features in integrated nanophotonic devices.

**Method.** Molecular Beam Epitaxy (MBE) was used for growth, with isotopic analysis performed via ToF-SIMS and optical characterization via photoluminescence and spectroscopy in integrated photonic crystal cavities.

**Summary.** This paper reports the successful synthesis of isotopically enriched Ca186WO4 thin films designed to improve the spin coherence of Er3+ ions. By reducing the concentration of the decohering 183W isotope from 14.3% to 1.2%, the authors provide a scalable platform for quantum interconnects. They demonstrate high crystalline quality and the ability to resolve single-ion resonances when integrated with nanophotonic cavities. While spectral diffusion was observed in non-stoichiometric films, the results establish a path toward high-performance, nuclear-spin-limited quantum interfaces.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The experimental platform consists of Er3+ ions implanted into epitaxial, isotopically engineered Ca186WO4 thin films integrated with silicon-on-insulator (SOI) 1D photonic crystal cavities.

**Key observables.** Inhomogeneous linewidth, 183W isotopic abundance, surface roughness, photoluminescence emission, and optical spectral diffusion.

**Important parameters / regimes.** 183W concentration (1.2%), 1.53 um telecom C-band transition, 214 MHz linewidth, and 20 mK operating temperature.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The study assumes that reducing the 183W nuclear spin concentration will directly lead to increased spin coherence times (T2) by reducing the nuclear spin bath.

**Figures summary.** The figures demonstrate the successful growth of high-quality films, the reduction of 183W isotopes, the identification of spectral diffusion in off-stoichiometric films, and the resolution of individual Er3+ ion resonances in cavities.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of spin decoherence, describes the MBE growth and isotopic enrichment process, details the structural and isotopic characterization, discusses the optimization of film stoichiometry via annealing, and concludes with the demonstration of single-ion spectroscopy and identified limitations.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work is highly relevant for quantum optics and open quantum systems as it demonstrates a material engineering approach to controlling the environment (the nuclear spin bath) to extend the coherence of a quantum emitter.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Rare earth ion (REI)-doped oxide thin films are attractive for the application of quantum interconnects due to their stable optical levels and scalability$^{1-3}$. Among them, Er$^{3+}$ doped CaWO$_{4}$ is promising because it possesses narrow optical linewidth transitions and a long spin coherence time$^{4-6}$. The electron spin coherence is limited at high temperatures by paramagnetic impurities and by the presence of the 14.3% $^{183}$W nuclear spin. To further increase the spin coherence time at millikelvin temperatures, where the paramagnetic impurities are frozen out, our approach is to synthesize chemically and isotopically purified thin films as a host material. We first grow non-isotopically enriched Er$^{3+}$ doped CaWO$_{4}$ thin films, which exhibit a 214(13) MHz photoluminescence (PL) inhomogeneous linewidth, indicating the thin film has high crystalline quality. We then grow isotopically enriched CaWO$_{4}$ thin films using an isotopically purified $^{186}$WO$_{3}$ source. Time of flight secondary ion mass spectrometry (ToF-SIMS) was used to measure the relative concentration of W isotopes. $^{183}$W, the only W isotope that has a net nuclear spin and is the major cause of spin decoherence, was at a relative abundance of 1.2%, a factor of 10 lower than natural abundance. We also observed PL emission from single ions after integrating nano-photonic devices with the thin film. These results establish isotopically engineered CaWO$_{4}$ thin films as a promising platform for future studies of nuclear-spin-limited coherence and for scalable rare-earth-ion-based quantum nanophotonic devices.

</details>

### [Quantum average correlations and complementarity relations via metric-adjusted skew information](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23504v1)

**Authors:** Xiaoyu Ma, Qing-Hua Zhang, Cong Xu  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.23504v1>

**Main problem.** The paper seeks to establish a unified framework for quantifying quantum average correlations and complementarity relations using metric-adjusted skew information.

**Main result.** The authors prove that quantum average correlation is an intrinsic quantity independent of the averaging scheme (MUBs, Haar measure, etc.) and derive new wave-particle-entropy complementarity relations.

**Method.** The study utilizes the mathematical framework of metric-adjusted skew information based on the Morozova-Chentsov function and employs various averaging procedures including twirling channels and Mutually Unbiased Bases.

**Summary.** This paper introduces a unified framework to study quantum correlations and complementarity using metric-adjusted skew information. It demonstrates that quantum average correlation is an intrinsic property that remains invariant regardless of whether one averages over different bases or uses twirling channels. Furthermore, the authors establish new complementarity relations that link wave-like features, particle-like features, and quantum entropy. These results provide a consistent way to quantify the interplay between different quantum properties in bipartite systems.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The study focuses on bipartite quantum systems (rho_AB) acting on a composite Hilbert space (H_A tensor H_B) and single-party systems.

**Key observables.** Metric-adjusted skew information, average coherence, quantum average correlation, wave feature (W_c), particle feature (P_c), and quantum f-entropy.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Hilbert space dimensions (d_A, d_B), purity of the environment, and the Morozova-Chentsov function (f).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The equivalence of averaging schemes assumes the existence of a complete family of MUBs (requiring d_A to be a prime power) and assumes the metric is regular (f(0) > 0).

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the framework of metric-adjusted skew information, demonstrates the equivalence of various averaging schemes for correlation, establishes new complementarity relations for single and bipartite systems, and provides formal mathematical proofs in the appendices.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides a rigorous, unified mathematical foundation for understanding the fundamental trade-offs between coherence, uncertainty, and correlation, which is central to the study of open quantum systems and quantum information processing.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We investigate quantum average correlations and complementarity relations based on metric-adjusted skew information. Several natural averaging procedures are considered, including complete families of mutually unbiased bases, all orthonormal bases, operator orthonormal bases, and twirling channels induced by the unitary group. All these approaches lead to the same closed expression, which identifies the resulting average correlation as an intrinsic quantity independent of the averaging scheme. By defining measures of wave and particle features via metric-adjusted skew information, we establish complementarity relations among wave and particle features, quantum entropy, and average correlation. These results provide a unified framework for investigating quantum average correlations and complementarity relations in terms of metric-adjusted skew information.

</details>

**Authors:** Agung Budiyono, Sebastian Deffner

**Main problem.** The paper investigates the fundamental limits on the speed at which measurement probabilities can evolve, specifically determining the minimum time, energy, and quantum resources required to transform one set of probabilities into another.

**Main result.** The authors derive a quantum speed limit (QSL) where the speed of measurement probabilities is upper-bounded by the product of the energetic cost and a quantumness resource (noncommutativity). They also show this limit can witness bipartite correlations and quantify the time needed to generate local athermality.

**Method.** The study utilizes mathematical tools such as Jensen's inequality, Hölder inequality, and Schatten p-norms, alongside the Kirkwood-Dirac quasiprobability framework to bound the rate of change of surprisal.

**Summary.** This paper establishes a new type of quantum speed limit specifically for the evolution of measurement probabilities. It shows that the speed at which these probabilities change is fundamentally constrained by the amount of quantumness (noncommutativity) and energy available in the system. The authors demonstrate that this limit can be used to detect quantum correlations and to calculate the minimum time required to drive a system away from thermal equilibrium. This provides a deeper understanding of the physical resources required for quantum information tasks.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The work considers a quantum system with a finite-dimensional Hilbert space undergoing continuous-time unitary evolution generated by a Hamiltonian H(t), including bipartite systems (system and ancilla) and single-qubit models.

**Key observables.** Measurement probabilities (POVM outcomes), surprisal, quantumness resource (U_M), local athermality, and Kirkwood-Dirac quasiprobability.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Energetic cost (Hamiltonian norm), quantumness resource (noncommutativity), and the time-average of measurement uncertainty.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The primary derivation assumes unitary dynamics and a bounded Hamiltonian to avoid infinite speeds.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of probability evolution speed, defines the mathematical framework for the speed of measurement probabilities, derives the fundamental quantum speed limit, demonstrates applications to bipartite correlation witnessing and athermality generation, and provides supplementary proofs for qubit complementarity.

**Why it may be interesting.** This is highly relevant for researchers in quantum information and open quantum systems as it provides a new fundamental bound on information processing rates based on quantumness and uncertainty rather than just state-space distance.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Any protocol to process quantum information has to conclude with a measurement, aimed at producing a specific set of probabilities of measurement outcomes. In this work, we investigate the time, energy and importantly the genuine quantum resources necessary for transforming a set of measurement probabilities generated by a positive-operator-valued measure (POVM), to a target set of measurement probabilities. To this end, we first show that the speed of measurement probabilities, defined as the average rate of the surprisal of measurement outcomes, is constrained by the genuine quantum fluctuations contained in the measurement probabilities. Interestingly, this quantum speed limit can act as a witness for bipartite quantum correlations by selecting an optimal local projective measurement. Furthermore, we obtain a minimum time to transform an initial measurement probabilities to a target measurement probabilities, and apply this result to analyzing the cost of generating a local athermality in terms of genuine quantum uncertainty.

</details>

**Main problem.** The study investigates whether the dynamic characteristics of entanglement (growth and spread) actively drive quantum state evolution in Variational Quantum Algorithms (VQAs) or act as a passive byproduct.

**Main result.** In problem-agnostic Hardware-Efficient Ansätze (HEA), entanglement dynamics are decoupled from state evolution, which is dominated by geometric phase. In contrast, in problem-inspired Hamiltonian Variational Ansätze (HVA), entanglement acts as a dynamical resource where increased entanglement consumption correlates with faster state evolution toward the target.

**Method.** The authors use a geometric framework analyzing state trajectories in projective Hilbert space via Fubini-Study distance and Matrix Product State (MPS) simulations to compute entanglement and geodesic distances.

**Summary.** This paper explores the functional role of entanglement in Variational Quantum Algorithms from a geometric perspective. It distinguishes between problem-agnostic architectures (HEA), where entanglement is decoupled from state evolution, and problem-inspired architectures (HVA), where entanglement acts as a dynamical resource that drives the state toward the target. By analyzing the geometric and dynamical phases of the state trajectory, the authors show that structured ansätze can harness entanglement to accelerate convergence. This suggests that future quantum algorithm design should focus on guiding entanglement consumption rather than just generating it.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The study focuses on Variational Quantum Algorithms (VQAs) applied to the one-dimensional Transverse-Field Ising Model (TFIM) at the critical point (J=1, h=1) using two circuit architectures: Hardware-Efficient Ansatz (HEA) and Hamiltonian Variational Ansatz (HVA).

**Key observables.** Bipartite Von Neumann entanglement entropy, geodesic distance from initial state and to target space, geometric phase fraction, and dynamical phase contribution.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Circuit depth (layers), number of qubits (N=6 to 10), and comparison between randomized and optimized parameter regimes.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes that the effectiveness of an ansatz can be characterized by the relationship between entanglement dynamics and the geometric properties of the Hilbert space trajectory.

**Figures summary.** Figures show the HVA circuit structure, statistical correlations between entanglement variations and geodesic distance/geometric phase fraction for both randomized and optimized circuits, and averaged entanglement/distance/phase trends across layers.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of entanglement's role in VQAs, defines a geometric framework for analyzing state evolution, compares HEA and HVA architectures using MPS simulations, presents statistical correlations and results for both randomized and optimized circuits, and concludes with implications for future ansatz design.

**Why it may be interesting.** This is interesting for many-body dynamics researchers as it provides a geometric perspective on how entanglement-generating operations can be structured to drive a system toward a specific target state, bridging the gap between circuit architecture and many-body evolution.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Calibrating the role of entanglement in quantum algorithms is a crucial task in the development of quantum computing. Most existing studies have primarily focused on how the static properties of entanglement-such as its magnitude and phase-affect key performance metrics. In this work, we instead explore the relationship between the dynamical behaviors of entanglement and the execution of variational quantum algorithms from a geometric perspective. We find that, in contrast to conventional Hamiltonian dynamics where the evolution process is dominated by the dynamical phase, quantum state evolution in quantum algorithms is primarily governed by the geometric phase with the trajectory determined by the parameter-dependent Hilbert space geometry. In the problem-agnostic Hardware-Efficient Ansatz (HEA), entanglement dynamics and state evolution are decoupled. Conversely, in the problem-inspired Hamiltonian Variational Ansatz (HVA), the dynamical phase contribution is enhanced, allowing entanglement to function as a dynamical resource: more entanglement consumption correlates directly with faster quantum state evolution.

</details>

**Main problem.** Mitigating decoherence in donor spin systems for scalable silicon-based quantum information processing by identifying and probing magnetic clock transitions.

**Main result.** The researchers successfully observed magnetic field clock transitions in near-surface 75As ensembles using low-field EDMR and demonstrated that linewidth broadening near these transitions can be modeled by the interplay of magnetic and hyperfine disorder.

**Method.** Continuous-wave electrically detected magnetic resonance (EDMR) using RF frequency modulation and lock-in detection on a silicon-based device.

**Summary.** This paper demonstrates the use of low-field electrically detected magnetic resonance (EDMR) to identify magnetic clock transitions in arsenic-doped silicon. By operating at points where the transition frequency is insensitive to magnetic field fluctuations, the researchers show a path to reducing spin decoherence. They provide a detailed model of how linewidths behave near these transitions due to hyperfine and magnetic disorder. This work establishes EDMR as a powerful tool for characterizing near-surface donor environments in silicon-based quantum technologies.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** An ensemble of near-surface 75As (I = 3/2) donor spins in isotopically enriched 28Si, modeled by a Hamiltonian including electron Zeeman, nuclear Zeeman, and isotropic hyperfine interaction terms.

**Key observables.** Changes in source-drain current (conductivity), resonance positions, and peak-to-peak linewidth (delta Bpp).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Low magnetic field regime (< 10 mT), hyperfine coupling constant A = 198.4 MHz, electron g-factor 1.99837, and nuclear g-factor 0.959.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The linewidth broadening is primarily driven by static magnetic field inhomogeneities and hyperfine variations (strain/Stark shifts).

**Figures summary.** Fig 1 shows the EDMR device schematic; Fig 2 displays the energy level diagram and transition frequencies; Fig 3 shows spectral convolution at low fields; Fig 4 illustrates the relationship between linewidth and magnetic field.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of decoherence, describes the experimental EDMR device and 75As system, presents the identification of clock transitions, analyzes linewidth broadening through Hamiltonian modeling, and concludes with the utility of EDMR as a probe for quantum device engineering.

**Why it may be interesting.** The study explores the suppression of decoherence via clock transitions, which is directly relevant to the design of protected qubits and the study of open quantum systems interacting with magnetic noise.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Magnetic clock transitions (CTs), defined by vanishing first-order sensitivity of the transition frequency to magnetic field fluctuations, provide a powerful route to suppress decoherence in donor spin systems. Here, we present the observation of magnetic field CTs from an ensemble of near-surface $^{75}$As ($I = 3/2$) spins in silicon using low-field ($< 10$~mT) continuous-wave electrically detected magnetic resonance (EDMR). As the CT condition is approached, pronounced linewidth broadening is observed, consistent with a donor Hamiltonian informed linewidth model. These results establish low-field EDMR as a sensitive probe of CTs in near-surface donor systems relevant to silicon-based quantum devices.

</details>

### [Few-Shot Cross-Device Transfer for Quantum Noise Modeling on Real Hardware](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.24397v1)

**Authors:** Sahil Al Farib, Sheikh Redwanul Islam, Azizur Rahman Anik  
**Type:** both · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.24397v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24397_figures/2604.24397_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> Fig. 1. Dataset composition. (a) Sample counts by backend: 85 per device. (b) Circuit type distribution: 40 random, 15 Bell, 15 GHZ, 15 QFT. (c) Qubit count distribution across 2–5 qubits. The balanced structure across circuit types ensures the dataset covers a representative range of noise regimes.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24397_figures/2604.24397_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Fig. 2. Device calibration drift between ibm_fez (Backend A) and ibm_marrakesh (Backend B). Left: coherence times T1 and T2 in microseconds. Right: readout error and CX gate error rates. Backend B has longer coherence times but higher gate and readout error rates—a qualitatively distinct noise profile that motivates the need for explicit adaptation.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24397_figures/2604.24397_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> Fig. 3. Training convergence curve (KL divergence, log scale) on Backend A. Both training and validation loss decrease monotonically before early stopping. The gap between training and validation curves is small, indicating limited overfitting despite the small dataset size (68 training samples). Best val KL = 0.4605 at epoch 27; early stopping at epoch 52.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24397_figures/2604.24397_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> Fig. 4. Results summary table. Rows: in-domain, zero-shot, and K=5/10/20 few-shot conditions. Columns: KL divergence (mean ± std), TV distance, and KL improvement percentage. Note that TV increases slightly (+0.0221), indicating that improvements in low-probability states (KL-sensitive) may come at the cost of small deviations in dominant states.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24397_figures/2604.24397_fig5.jpg" alt="Figure 5">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> Fig. 5. KL divergence as a function of few-shot sample count K. Dashed red line: zero-shot baseline (1.6706). Dotted green line: in-domain reference (0.3014). Blue curve with shaded ±1 std confidence band: few-shot fine-tuning over 5 seeds. Percentage annotations indicate per-K improvement over zero- shot. The monotonic improvement with K confirms that the pre-trained source model provides a useful initialization for adaptation.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24397_figures/2604.24397_fig6.jpg" alt="Figure 6">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 6.</b> Fig. 6. Total Variation distance as a function of K. Unlike KL divergence, TV distance does not decrease monotonically: it remains approximately flat for K ≤10 and increases slightly at K = 20 (0.5282 →0.5503). This divergence from the KL trend is discussed in Section V-A.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24397_figures/2604.24397_fig7.jpg" alt="Figure 7">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 7.</b> Fig. 7. Probability distribution comparison for a representative circuit on Backend B (circuit type and qubit count shown in panel title). Grouped bar chart: red = noisy input, blue = ideal target, green = model prediction over computational basis states. Side panel: KL and TV metrics for each sample, for all three pairs. The model effectively redistributes mass to the correct distribution, bringing down the noisy-to-ideal KL.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24397_figures/2604.24397_fig8.jpg" alt="Figure 8">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 8.</b> Fig. 8. Feature ablation study. Bar heights show KL divergence when each calibration feature is zeroed out at inference time (model weights unchanged). Red bar: removing this feature increases KL (feature is informative under zero-shot transfer). Green bar: removing this feature decreases KL, indicating distribution mismatch rather than benefit—the model’s learned association is miscalibrated for the target device’s regime. Both readout error and CX gate error exhibit cross-device mismatch (negative ∆), with CX gate error having the dominant effect.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** Quantum noise in the NISQ era is hardware-specific and time-varying, making it difficult to develop error mitigation strategies that generalize across different quantum devices.

**Main result.** The study demonstrates that a noise model trained on one device can be effectively adapted to a different device using few-shot learning (K=20 samples), achieving a 28.6% improvement in KL divergence over zero-shot transfer.

**Method.** The researchers used a Residual Neural Network (ResNet) for supervised distribution regression, employing a layer-selective fine-tuning strategy and a replay buffer to adapt the model from a source device to a target device.

**Summary.** This paper addresses the challenge of cross-device quantum error mitigation in the NISQ era. By using transfer learning, the authors show that a neural network trained on one IBM quantum processor can be quickly adapted to a new processor using only a handful of circuit samples. This approach significantly reduces the error gap caused by hardware-specific noise like CX gate errors. The method provides a scalable, low-overhead alternative to more computationally expensive error mitigation techniques.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The experimental platform consists of two IBM superconducting quantum processors, ibm_fez (source) and ibm_marrakesh (target), using circuits with 2-5 qubits and depths of 2-8.

**Key observables.** KL divergence, Total Variation (TV) distance, and hardware calibration features including T1 (relaxation), T2 (dephasing), readout error, and CX gate error.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Number of fine-tuning samples (K = 5, 10, 20), circuit depth (2-8), and qubit count (2-5).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The model assumes that quantum noise can be sufficiently modeled using circuit structure and mean-aggregated hardware calibration features, and it does not account for per-qubit spatial heterogeneity.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows dataset composition; Figure 2 compares calibration drift between backends; Figure 6 shows TV distance vs. K; Figure 7 visualizes probability distribution redistribution; Figure 8 shows the results of the feature ablation study.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the challenge of device-specific noise, describes the dataset and hardware, details the ResNet architecture and transfer learning protocol, presents results for zero-shot and few-shot performance, performs an ablation study to identify error drivers, and discusses limitations.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

In the noisy intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) regime, quantum devices contain hardware-specific noise sources which restrict device-invariant error mitigation strategies. We explore transfer learning approaches to apply noise models learned on one quantum device to a different device with the help of a small amount of data. We create a real-hardware dataset from two IBM quantum devices, ibm_fez (source) and ibm_marrakesh (target), comprising 170 noisy and ideal circuit output distributions, with device calibration features added. We train a residual neural network on the source device to map noisy to ideal outcomes. The zero-shot transfer test shows a KL divergence of 1.6706 (up from 0.3014), establishing device specificity. With K = 20 fine-tuning samples, KL drops to 1.1924 (28.6% improvement over zero-shot), recovering 34.9% of the gap between zero-shot and in-domain KL. Ablation studies reveal that the major cause of mismatches across devices is CX gate error, followed by readout error. The results show quantum noise can be learned and fine-tuned with minimal samples, and provide a plausible approach to cross-device quantum error mitigation.

</details>

<a id="paper-2604.23797"></a>
### [From Random Fringes to Deterministic Response: Statistical Foundations of Time-Reversed Young Interferometry](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23797v1)

**Authors:** Jianming Wen  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.23797v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `interference shaping light` **4/5** · `quantum measurements` **2/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23797_figures/2604.23797_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> FIG. 1. Statistical distinction between conventional Young interference and TRY interference. In the standard geome- try, the detector coordinate x is the random variable and the fringe is reconstructed from a detection histogram. In TRY, the detector is fixed and the source coordinate y is a con- trolled label. The measured fringe is a conditional response function R(y; θ) or operational hybrid correlator G(2) op (y).</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** The paper aims to distinguish the statistical nature of conventional Young interference from a proposed Time-Reversed Young (TRY) geometry, specifically addressing how randomness is handled in each.

**Main result.** The authors demonstrate that in TRY, the interference fringe is a deterministic response function indexed by a programmed source coordinate, shifting the role of randomness from pattern formation to the precision of response estimation.

**Method.** The study utilizes estimation theory, specifically Fisher information and the Cramér-Rao bound, to compare the precision of parameter estimation in both conventional and TRY geometries.

**Summary.** The paper proposes a new way to view Young interference called Time-Reversed Young (TRY) interferometry. Unlike standard interference, which builds a pattern from random detection events, TRY uses a controlled source to measure a deterministic response. This shift allows for more precise parameter estimation by optimizing how measurement time is allocated across different source coordinates. The framework enables advanced sensing techniques like lock-in readout and superresolution by treating interference as a conditional response rather than a spatial histogram.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The system is a generalized Young interferometer setup, applicable to both classical and quantum (single-photon) regimes, involving a programmable source and a fixed detector under paraxial, linear, and reciprocal propagation.

**Key observables.** The conditional response function R(y; theta) in TRY and the spatial intensity or detection probability mu_Y(x; theta) in conventional Young interference.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The parameter of interest theta (e.g., phase, slit separation), the source coordinate y, the detector coordinate x, and the allocation of measurement resources like dwell time or samples.

**Assumptions / limitations.** Assumes paraxial, linear, and reciprocal propagation; stationary illumination during sampling; independent noise between source-coordinate bins; and unbiased estimators.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 compares the conventional Young configuration (random detector outcomes) with the TRY configuration (controlled source coordinates). Figure 2 illustrates the TRY response-measurement problem and how parameter sensitivity is governed by the response slope and noise.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the distinction between conventional and TRY geometries, establishes a mathematical framework using estimation theory, compares the statistical character and noise scaling of both regimes, and concludes with the practical advantages for sensing and metrology.

**Why it may be interesting.** This is highly relevant to quantum optics and quantum information as it provides a new framework for high-precision metrology and sensing by engineering the source-plane basis to optimize Fisher information.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Young interference is usually read as the gradual statistical accumulation of random detection events. Here we show that a time-reversed Young (TRY) geometry has a different statistical character: the fringe is not a marginal distribution of detector positions, but a conditional response indexed by a programmed source coordinate. With a fixed detector and a scanned source basis, the observable is an operational hybrid correlator between detector signal and source label. The resulting interference is deterministic at the response-function level, while noise enters only through estimation precision. We formulate this distinction using Fisher information, estimator variance, and noise scaling, clarifying why TRY naturally supports calibration, lock-in readout, null-fringe sensing, and source-plane superresolution.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

<a id="paper-2604.23258"></a>
### [Introducing the Correlation Concentration Ratio (CCR): Quantitative Framework for Comparing Quantum Cluster States](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23258v1)

**Authors:** Amin Ahadi, Saman Sarshar  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.23258v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `entanglement & information structure` **4/5** · `quantum measurements` **2/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23258_figures/2604.23258_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> Figure 1: schematic cluster for linear, square, and T-shaped cluster states</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23258_figures/2604.23258_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Figure 2: Covariance matrix for the four-mode linear cluster with squeezing parameter 3-16dB</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23258_figures/2604.23258_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> Figure 3: Covariance matrix for the four-mode square cluster with squeezing parameter 3-16dB</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23258_figures/2604.23258_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> Figure 4: Covariance matrix for the four-mode T-shaped cluster with parameter 3-16dB</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23258_figures/2604.23258_fig5.jpg" alt="Figure 5">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> Figure 5: CCR as a function of squeezing level (2–16 dB) for linear, square, and T-shaped cluster topologies.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** The lack of a quantitative framework to compare how different cluster state topologies (linear, square, and T-shaped) distribute entanglement and affect information propagation in measurement-based quantum computation.

**Main result.** The authors introduce the Correlation Concentration Ratio (CCR), a new metric that quantifies the concentration of correlations on graph edges, and demonstrate that square topologies are most scalable due to their balanced correlation distribution.

**Method.** Numerical simulations using the symplectic representation and covariance matrix formalism to model the generation of Gaussian cluster states via controlled-phase gates.

**Summary.** This paper introduces the Correlation Concentration Ratio (CCR) to quantitatively compare different quantum cluster state topologies. By simulating continuous-variable Gaussian cluster states, the authors show how graph geometry influences the distribution of quadrature correlations. They demonstrate that while squeezing increases correlation intensity, the CCR effectively captures topology-dependent features, such as the 'quantum hub' effect in T-shaped states. Ultimately, the work identifies square topologies as superior for scalable, fault-tolerant measurement-based quantum computation.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** Four-mode continuous-variable (CV) Gaussian cluster states with linear, square, and T-shaped (or star-shaped) topologies, constructed from momentum-squeezed vacuum states.

**Key observables.** Covariance matrix elements (diagonal quadrature variances and off-diagonal inter-mode correlations), nullifier relations, and the Correlation Concentration Ratio (CCR).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Squeezing parameter (ranging from 3 to 16 dB) and the graph adjacency matrix (topology).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The study is limited to Gaussian states and focuses on the canonical quadrature basis without considering non-Gaussian elements.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the topology schematics; Figures 2-4 display covariance matrix plots for linear, square, and T-shaped structures across different squeezing levels; Figure 5 illustrates the variation of CCR as a function of squeezing for different topologies.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of topological comparison, describes the symplectic modeling of CV cluster states, presents the new CCR metric, provides numerical results for 4-mode systems, and extends the analysis to scaling behavior in larger N-mode systems.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a new structural metric (CCR) for analyzing entanglement distribution in continuous-variable systems, which is highly relevant for designing robust architectures for CV-MBQC and understanding error propagation in quantum networks.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

In this paper, numerical simulations of four-mode continuous-variable cluster states with different topologies in the framework of measurement-based quantum computation are presented. By utilizing the symplectic representation and covariance matrix, the process of generating cluster states with linear, square, and T-shaped topologies has been systematically modeled. The simulation results show that the cluster graph structure is directly reflected in the pattern of quadrature correlations; in other words, the theoretical nullifier relations of the cluster states are reproduced in the final covariance matrices. Increasing the squeezing parameter leads to the strengthening of the target correlations and the suppression of unwanted components arising from anti-squeezing; such that the off-diagonal elements of the covariance matrix in the linear and square topologies increase to significant values, and in the T-shaped topology a stronger central correlation (similar to GHZ-like behavior in the continuous-variable domain) is observed. In order to quantitatively analyze these structural differences, a metric titled CCR (Correlation Concentration Ratio) is introduced that quantifies the concentration of effective correlations on the graph edges relative to the total correlations of the system. This index enables direct comparison of different topologies from the perspective of structural entanglement distribution and provides a framework for evaluating the efficiency of cluster graphs in MBQC architectures. The results show that CCR can be used as a practical tool for designing and selecting optimal topologies in larger clusters and more complex structures.

</details>

### [On Realization of Back-Action-Evading Measurements and Quantum Non-Demolition Variables via Linear Systems Engineering](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.24243v1)

**Authors:** Zhiyuan Dong, Weichao Liang, Guofeng Zhang  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.24243v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `quantum measurements` **4/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **2/5**

**Main problem.** Establishing a systematic framework to realize Back-Action-Evading (BAE) measurements and Quantum Non-Demolition (QND) variables in linear quantum systems.

**Main result.** The authors derive algebraic conditions for BAE and QND realization, proving that a purely imaginary Hamiltonian with specific coupling properties enables BAE, and that coherent feedback or direct coupling can engineer these properties in non-compliant systems.

**Method.** Utilizing linear systems engineering tools, including Kalman canonical forms, transfer function analysis, and coherent feedback control design.

**Summary.** This paper develops a unified mathematical framework for achieving Back-Action-Evading measurements and QND variables in linear quantum systems. By using linear systems engineering, the authors identify the specific algebraic constraints on Hamiltonians and coupling operators needed to prevent noise from affecting measurement records. They demonstrate that even systems that do not naturally satisfy these conditions can be re-engineered using coherent feedback or direct coupling. The results are particularly applicable to quantum optomechanics and precision sensing.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The framework applies to linear quantum systems in continuous-mode measurement scenarios, described by Quantum Stochastic Differential Equations (QSDEs) and the Quantum Stochastic Master Equation (QSME).

**Key observables.** Conjugate quadratures (position and momentum), output quadratures, and the coupling operator.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Hamiltonian parameters (Omega), scattering matrix (S), coupling strength (lambda), and the distinction between bilateral and unilateral BAE regimes.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The framework is restricted to linear quantum systems and assumes continuous-mode measurement.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows a coherent feedback network using a beamsplitter; Figure 2 illustrates a quantum optomechanical system platform.

**Paper structure.** The paper moves from defining BAE and QND conditions to establishing structural requirements, investigating SISO and MIMO regimes, and finally presenting synthesis methods via feedback and direct coupling.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a rigorous control-theoretic approach to suppressing measurement back-action, which is essential for high-precision sensing and maintaining quantum coherence in open quantum systems.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We establish a framework for realizing back-action-evading (BAE) measurements and quantum non-demolition (QND) variables in linear quantum systems. The key condition, a purely imaginary Hamiltonian with a real or imaginary coupling operator, enables BAE measurements of conjugate observables. Symmetric coupling further yields QND variables. For non-compliant systems, coherent feedback is designed to engineer BAE measurements. Crucially, the QND interaction condition simultaneously ensures BAE measurements and promotes the coupling operator to a QND observable.

</details>

### [Partial solvability induced by dark states in a box trap with decentered two-body interaction](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23507v1)

**Authors:** Hossein Abedi, Nathan L. Harshman, Peter Schmelcher  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum gases · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.23507v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `scars & prethermalization` **4/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **2/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23507_figures/2604.23507_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> FIG. 1. Configuration space for two particles confined in a box trap of length L. The interaction potential has support on the two lines x2 −x1 = ±c (dashed lines), which divides the space into three regions.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23507_figures/2604.23507_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> FIG. 2. The ground-state wave function ψ(x1, x2) in the bosonic even-parity sector (σ, π) = (+1, +1), for interaction strengths g = 0, 1, 20, 100 (rows, top to bottom) and displacement parameters c = 0, 0.1, 0.5, 0.9 (columns, left to right). The dashed lines indicate the support of the interaction potential. At c = 0, the model reduces to the standard contact interaction.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23507_figures/2604.23507_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> FIG. 3. The six lowest-lying eigenfunctions Ψnm(x1, x2) on the right isosceles triangular domain of leg length a. Quantum numbers (n, m) are given in the figure.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23507_figures/2604.23507_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> FIG. 4. Energy levels at g →∞. (a) Bosonic even-parity and fermionic odd-parity sectors. (b) Bosonic odd-parity and fermionic even-parity sectors. εout nb,f denotes the doubly degenerate fermionic and bosonic energy levels of the region outside. εin nb and εin nf denote the non-degenerate levels of the region inside for bosonic and fermionic states, respectively. The exact crossings between the lowest-energy levels εout 0b,f and εin 0b,f define four regimes: (E) exclusion, with only εout nb,f ; (T) truncation, with only εin nb,f ; and two crossover regions (C) where they coexist; red: εout nb with εin nb; green: εout nb,f with both εin nf , εin nb. The triple degeneracy indicated by the...</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23507_figures/2604.23507_fig5.jpg" alt="Figure 5">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> FIG. 5. Wavefunctions of the lowest-lying g = 0 eigenstates with diagonal nodal lines. The corresponding symmetry sector is shown in the figure. (a) and (d) carry diagonal nodal lines only at c = 0, and are therefore dark at c = 0. (b) and (e) States that become dark at c = 1/2. (c) State that becomes dark at c = 1/3. (f) State that becomes dark at c = 2/3.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23507_figures/2604.23507_fig6.jpg" alt="Figure 6">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 6.</b> FIG. 6. Energy vs. g in the bosonic even parity sector at c = 1/2. The 4th excited state is dark. The 2nd and 3rd excited states approach the energy of the dark state asymptotically, giving rise to a triple degeneracy in the g →∞limit.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23507_figures/2604.23507_fig7.jpg" alt="Figure 7">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 7.</b> FIG. 7. Dark state quantum numbers (n, m) as a function of displacement c = p/q. For each point shown, an exact analytical solution exists. Integer scaling generates an infinite tower at fixed c; only (2, 1) →(4, 2) at c = 1/2 is shown.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates the emergence of partial solvability and 'dark states' in a non-integrable quantum system where a two-body interaction is displaced from the center.

**Main result.** The authors demonstrate that specific rational values of the displacement parameter c induce exactly solvable subspaces (dark states) within an otherwise non-integrable spectrum, where wavefunctions are insensitive to interaction strength.

**Method.** The study employs exact diagonalization (ED) using the Lanczos algorithm, compares results with the Bethe ansatz for the c=0 limit, and uses quantum billiards theory to analyze the strong interaction limit.

**Summary.** This paper explores a two-particle system in a 1D box where the interaction is shifted by a distance c. While the system is generally non-integrable, the authors find that for certain rational values of c, 'dark states' emerge that are completely unaffected by the interaction strength. These states create exactly solvable sectors within the spectrum. The work bridges the gap between integrable contact interactions and complex, non-integrable dynamics.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** A system of two non-relativistic particles (bosons or fermions) confined to a one-dimensional infinite square well trap with a decentered, zero-range contact interaction defined by strength g and displacement c.

**Key observables.** Eigenenergies, wavefunctions, parity, and the presence of spectral degeneracies.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Interaction strength (g), displacement parameter (c), particle statistics (bosonic vs. fermionic), and spatial parity.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The particles are non-relativistic and the system is restricted to a one-dimensional geometry.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1 shows the configuration space regions; Fig 3 visualizes the lowest energy states; Fig 5 illustrates wavefunctions with nodal lines; Fig 6 shows energy vs. interaction strength for a dark state.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the decentered interaction model, analyzes the symmetry properties, explores the integrable limits (c=0 and g=infinity), identifies the conditions for dark states via nodal alignment, and validates numerical results against analytical benchmarks.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a novel mechanism for partial integrability in non-integrable systems and connects the concept of dark states to quantum many-body scars, which is highly relevant to many-body dynamics and thermalization.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We consider a generalization of the two-body contact interaction for nonrelativistic particles confined to a one-dimensional box, in which the interaction is decentered, i.e., the particles interact only when they are separated by a distance c. In contrast to the harmonically trapped system, this model is nonintegrable. Despite this, we demonstrate that the system exhibits partial solvability due to the presence of dark states, i.e., bosonic or fermionic states unaffected by the interaction. These states form exactly solvable subspaces embedded within an interacting spectrum. We characterize the stationary properties of the system, identify the conditions for the appearance of dark states, and show how they structure the spectrum and delineate interacting and noninteracting sectors.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

<a id="paper-2604.23569"></a>
### [Quantum echo-enabled high harmonic generation using ultrafast electrons](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23569v1)

**Authors:** Yangfan He, Chenhao Pan, Bin Zhang, Yiming Pan  
**Type:** both · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.23569v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `interference shaping light` **4/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **2/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23569_figures/2604.23569_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> Figure 1: The setup and quantum process of Echo-Enabled High-Harmonic Generation (QEEHG). (a)The schematic setup of quantum echo-enabled high harmonic generation (EEHG) using free electrons. The setup is based on the ultrafast transmission electron microscopy that resembles a EEHG free electron laser seeding technique, which involves the usages of two laser modulations (modulator #1 and #2) and twice long-time free propagations (chirp #1 and chirp #2) to create a high-harmonic electron density modulation, in order to produce the high harmonics at the desired order</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23569_figures/2604.23569_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Figure 2: Quantum control and path selectivity in QEEHG. (a) Harmonic spectrum showing multiple echo orders. Blue bars: theoretical prediction from Eq. (3). Orange dots: numerical simulation results. (b) Wigner distribution corresponding to (a),</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23569_figures/2604.23569_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> Figure 3: Tailored harmonic spectroscopy through collaborative phase optimization. (a) Identifying optimal global phase conditions by scanning the second drift length ( 𝑑₂ ). Black and blue circles mark regions satisfying the</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** The need for compact, coherent, and tunable sources of EUV and X-ray radiation, specifically addressing the lack of selectivity and efficiency in existing PINEM-based high-harmonic generation.

**Main result.** The development of a Quantum Echo-Enabled High-Harmonic Generation (QEEHG) scheme that uses quantum interference to selectively enhance specific harmonic orders while suppressing others.

**Method.** A theoretical framework using a multi-path quantum interferometer approach and numerical simulations via a quantum wavepacket evolution algorithm (FFPG) to model electron-laser interactions.

**Summary.** This paper proposes a new method called Quantum Echo-Enabled High-Harmonic Generation (QEEHG) to create tunable, coherent X-ray and EUV radiation. By using a sequence of laser modulations and dispersive drift sections, the researchers show how to manipulate the quantum phase of electron wavepackets. This allows for the selective enhancement of specific high-order harmonics through constructive interference while suppressing unwanted radiation. The technique provides a promising path toward compact, high-brightness radiation sources for electron microscopy and diffraction.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** An ultrafast electron beamline interacting with two optical modulation stages and two dispersive drift (chirp) sections, modeled using a time-dependent Schrödinger equation with momentum-dependent chirping and position-dependent modulation operators.

**Key observables.** The q-th harmonic bunching factor b(q), the Wigner distribution of the electron wavepacket, and the harmonic spectrum amplitude.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Modulation strengths (g1, g2), laser frequencies (omega1, omega2), chirping coefficients (xi), drift lengths (d0, df), and the large recoil condition.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The interaction time is much shorter than the free-drift times (impulsive limit), the laser field is co-propagating, and the large recoil condition (momentum kick much larger than momentum spread) is assumed for analytical derivation.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the QEEHG setup and quantum interference network; Figure 2 displays the harmonic spectrum, Wigner distribution, and path selection maps; Figure 3 illustrates the optimization of global phase conditions and tailored spectra (periodic and plateau regimes).

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the scientific problem of radiation source development, presents the proposed QEEHG scheme and its physical components, derives the mathematical operator-based framework for electron wavepacket evolution, provides numerical validation and optimization protocols, and concludes with the demonstration of spectrally tailored harmonic outputs.

**Why it may be interesting.** The paper presents a sophisticated application of quantum interference and wavepacket shaping, using a 'quantum echo' to control the interference of multiple quantum pathways, which is highly relevant to the control of coherent states in quantum optics.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Controlling and generating ultrafast free-electron wavepackets via laser is pivotal for photon-induced near-field electron microscopes (PINEM) and also for developing compact, coherent free-electron radiation sources. Here, we present a quantum echo-enabled high-harmonic generation (QEEHG) scheme that manipulates the quantum phase of electron wavepackets to produce tunable, coherent high-harmonic radiation at ultrashort wavelengths. This framework, inspired by the EEHG concept for free-electron lasers by Stupikov et al. (2009), leverages multiphoton PINEM scattering followed by dispersive chirp sections to induce quantum interference among photon sidebands. Such interference selectively enhances a targeted harmonic order - for instance, the 60th harmonic at 13.3nm from an 800nm seeding - while suppressing unwanted radiations. The optimization of harmonic orders and its non-classical spectral characteristics are analyzed. This quantum echo technique establishes a promising paradigm for compact coherent sources and provides new perspectives for quantum wavefunction shaping in ultrafast electron microscopy and diffraction.

</details>

### [DiffQEC: A versatile diffusion model for quantum error correction](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.24640v1)

**Authors:** Tianyi Xu, Qinglong Liu, Maolin Wang, Fei Zhang, Zhe Zhao, Yang Wang, Ye Wei  
**Type:** both · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.24640v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `QC/QI experiment` **4/5** · `quantum measurements` **1/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24640_figures/2604.24640_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> Fig. 1 Overview of DiffQEC. (a) Illustration of the analogy between the forward denoising diffusion process and the progressive accumulation of errors in the surface code. (b) The decoding pipeline of DiffQEC. Multi-round syndrome histories are encoded and used to condition the denoising network together with the diffusion timestep t and current noisy state xt, producing the estimated logical correction ˆx0. (c) Application scenarios of DiffQEC, including logical-qubit memory decoding, logical circuit decoding, and exploratory quantum error analysis.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24640_figures/2604.24640_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Fig. 2 Architecture of DiffQEC. (a) Syndrome processor. A 3D syndrome volume is encoded through per-round spatial convolution and pooling, followed by temporal aggregation across rounds with a GRU to produce a syndrome feature. In parallel, the diffusion timestep t and the current noisy state xt are encoded separately. These representations are combined with the syndrome feature to form the encoded feature for denoising. (b) Syndrome feature modulation. The encoded feature is further conditioned by the syndrome feature through a syndrome controller, which generates scale and shift signals together with a gate residual pathway before producing bitwise logits in the denoising network.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24640_figures/2604.24640_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">

(a) Relative improvements of over MWPM on Google’s Sycamore memory dataset. (b) and (c) show multi-round simulated surface-code decoding results at distances 3 and 5. (d) Large-distance decoding performance at distance 17 as a function of physical error rate. (e) Decoding time of DiffQEC and MWPM across increasing code distance. (f) Circuit-level decoding accuracy compared with MCCD .</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24640_figures/2604.24640_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> Fig. 4 Quantum error analysis, inference speed, and confidence-based post-selection with DiffQEC. (a) Measurement history is processed by the trained decoder to derive an attribution-based score over candidate physical error locations. (b) Incorporating this inferred error information into MWPM yields an improvement in decoding accuracy. (c) Representative visualizations of the inferred physical- error maps, including cases where the dominant predicted support agrees with the true error pattern and a representative failure case. Here, PE and TE denote predicted error and true error, respec- tively. (d) Multi-round decoding performance at distance 5 with post-selection (PS), where PS denotes...</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** The challenge of decoding quantum error correction (QEC) by inferring physical error configurations from syndrome patterns, specifically addressing the loss of uncertainty information in existing point-estimation decoders.

**Main result.** DiffQEC reduces logical error rates by up to 10.2% relative to MWPM and 5% relative to tensor-network decoding on Google's Sycamore processor, while providing useful posterior distributions for post-selection and error analysis.

**Method.** A generative decoder using discrete denoising diffusion probabilistic models that treats decoding as an iterative process of refining error hypotheses conditioned on syndrome history.

**Summary.** This paper introduces DiffQEC, a generative decoder for quantum error correction based on discrete denoising diffusion models. Unlike traditional decoders that provide a single error hypothesis, DiffQEC learns the full posterior distribution of errors. When tested on Google's Sycamore hardware, it significantly outperforms standard decoders like MWPM and provides actionable uncertainty estimates for error post-selection. The model scales well to large code distances and maintains efficiency suitable for real-time applications.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The study uses Google's Sycamore superconducting quantum processor data (rotated surface codes) and simulated depolarizing noise for scalability tests up to distance d=17.

**Key observables.** Logical error rate (LER), posterior distribution of errors, decoding latency, and saliency maps for error attribution.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Code distances (d=3, 5, up to 17), circuit depth (1 to 5), and error models (hardware-specific and depolarizing noise).

**Assumptions / limitations.** Assumes that simulated noise at large distances can sufficiently represent real hardware-specific error mechanisms for scalability testing.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1 shows the diffusion-error analogy and pipeline; Fig 2 details the 3D syndrome processor and modulation architecture; Fig 3 presents performance benchmarks (LER reduction, latency, and scalability); Fig 4 demonstrates error analysis via saliency maps and post-selection utility.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the decoding problem and the diffusion analogy, details the DiffQEC architecture (syndrome processor and modulation), presents performance benchmarks on experimental and simulated data, and concludes with error analysis and utility for post-selection.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Quantum computers could solve problems beyond the reach of classical devices, but this potential depends on quantum error correction (QEC) to protect fragile quantum states from noise. A central challenge in QEC is decoding: inferring likely physical errors from syndrome patterns generated by repeated stabilizer measurements. Existing decoders, including graph-based and neural approaches, typically return a single correction hypothesis and therefore discard the richer posterior structure of the error distribution conditioned on the observed syndrome. Here we recast QEC decoding as posterior inference using discrete denoising diffusion, exploiting the analogy between stochastic error accumulation and the forward diffusion process. We introduce DiffQEC, a generative decoder that combines a syndrome processor for multi-round spatial-temporal syndrome histories with syndrome feature modulation to condition denoising on the observed syndrome throughout inference. On experimental data from Google's superconducting quantum processor, DiffQEC reduces logical error rates by up to 10.2% relative to minimum-weight perfect matching and by about 5% relative to tensor-network decoding. These improvements persist for larger code distances up to 17 under depolarizing noise and for logical circuits of increasing depth. Beyond accuracy, the learned posterior provides confidence estimates for post-selection and reveals physically meaningful error structure, establishing posterior generative decoding as a practical framework for QEC.

</details>

### [On the complexity of quantum numerical integration: an angle-structure characterization](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.24289v1)

**Authors:** Francisco Chinesta, Antonio Falco, Daniela Falco-Pomares  
**Type:** both · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.24289v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** 🔥 `QC/QI experiment` **4/5** · `quantum measurements` **1/5**

The quantum oracle count M = O(ε−1) (dashed blue, horizontal) is independent of the Sobolev regularity s. The classical evaluation count N = Ω(ε−1/s) (solid red) grows without bound as s →0. The shaded region shows the separation gap; the ratio M/N ≤ Cε1/s−1 →0 as ε →0 for every fixed s &lt; 1/2. The vertical dotted line marks the boundary s = 1/2 beyond which the construction of Lemma 6.2 does not apply.</figcaption>

<img src="2604.24289_figures/2604.24289_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> Figure 3: Amplitude-encoding circuits Gg for n = 2 qubits, k = 0. (a) g1|X2 ∈G(1) 2 (d = 1, 3 gates, (57)): one unconditional rotation RY (ˆΘ∅) on the ancilla q2, one Cq0-RY (ˆΘ{0}), and one Cq1-RY (ˆΘ{1}). Circuit depth ≤4 layers. (b) g2|X2 ∈G(2) 2 \ G(1) 2 (d = 2, 4 gates, (58)): the d = 1 part (left brace) is followed by the degree-2 correction C{0,1}RY (ˆΘ{0,1}) (orange brace), decomposed into 2 CNOTs and 2 singly-controlled rotations [2]. Hadamard gates H on q0, q1 prepare the uniform superposition for the amplitude oracle Ag = Gg(H2 ⊗I2).</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** Determining whether the theoretical quadratic speedup of Quantum Amplitude Estimation (QAE) for numerical integration is preserved when the computational cost of constructing the amplitude oracle (state preparation) is included.

**Main result.** The authors introduce an angle-structure hierarchy of function classes where the gate count scales with the multilinear degree d, proving that for d=1, quantum integration can asymptotically outperform classical Monte Carlo and achieve a quantum-classical separation for certain Sobolev-regular functions.

**Method.** The study uses a multilinear expansion of the angle map via the Walsh-Hadamard Transform to decompose the encoding operator into multi-controlled RY gates, combined with Maximum Likelihood Amplitude Estimation (MLAE) for error analysis.

**Summary.** This paper addresses a critical bottleneck in quantum numerical integration: the cost of preparing the quantum state representing the integrand. By classifying functions based on the multilinear degree of their 'angle map,' the authors show that certain classes of functions allow for highly efficient quantum circuits. They demonstrate a clear quantum advantage over classical methods for specific function regularities and validate these findings using both NMR and superconducting quantum hardware.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The framework uses Quantum Amplitude Estimation applied to numerical integration on the interval [0, 1], with experimental validation performed on the SpinQ Triangulum (3-qubit NMR processor) and IBM Kingston (127-qubit superconducting processor).

**Key observables.** The encoded amplitude (Riemann sum), ancilla qubit measurement probabilities (pk), and the target integration accuracy (epsilon).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Multilinear degree (d), Sobolev regularity (s), target accuracy (epsilon), number of qubits (n), and quadrature order (p).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes the cost of building the oracle is a structural component of the total complexity, rather than treating it as a black box; it also assumes the multilinear degree is the primary driver of circuit depth.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows a log-log plot of the depth-versus-accuracy trade-off highlighting the quantum advantage region; Figure 2 illustrates the quantum-classical separation gap; Figure 3 depicts the amplitude-encoding circuits for n=2; and Figure 4/5 show hardware-specific performance and probability comparisons.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of oracle complexity, defines the angle-structure hierarchy and its multilinear expansion, establishes the encoding-circuit theorem and depth-accuracy trade-offs, proves quantum-classical separation for specific function classes, and concludes with experimental validation on NMR and superconducting hardware.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We study numerical integration on $[0,1]$ by quantum amplitude estimation (QAE), focusing on the cost of constructing the amplitude oracle. Although QAE improves the statistical component of the integration error, this advantage is relevant only when the integrand has low encoding complexity.   We introduce a hierarchy of grid function classes $\mathcal{G}_n^{(d)}$, defined by requiring the angle map $Θ_g:\{0,1\}^n\to[0,π]$ to be multilinear of degree at most $d$. Membership is classically checkable in $O(n2^n)$ time by the Walsh--Hadamard transform. For $g\in\mathcal{G}_n^{(d)}$, the encoding operator factorises into $\sum_{k=0}^d\binom{n}{k}$ multi-controlled $R_Y$ gates, interpolating between an affine $O(n)$ regime and the generic exponential regime.   Combining this structure with classical discretisation estimates for $g\in C^α[0,1]$, we obtain a depth-versus-accuracy trade-off: gate count $O((\log(1/\varepsilon))^d\varepsilon^{-1})$ suffices to achieve $\varepsilon$-accuracy with constant probability. For $d=1$ this becomes $O(\varepsilon^{-1}\log(1/\varepsilon))$, improving over classical Monte Carlo for every $α\ge1$. We also prove an unconditional separation: $\mathcal{G}_n^{(1)}$ contains functions of Sobolev regularity $s<1/2$ for which the quantum oracle cost is $O(1/\varepsilon)$, whereas classical deterministic or randomised quadrature requires $Ω(\varepsilon^{-1/s})$ evaluations.   These results identify explicit integrand classes for which the full cost of QAE-based integration, including state preparation, is asymptotically better than classical methods. Experiments on SpinQ Triangulum and IBM Kingston illustrate the hierarchy at $n=2$: circuits inside $\mathcal{G}_n^{(d)}$ run successfully, while those exceeding the Triangulum coherence budget fail as predicted.

</details>

**Authors:** Zeki Zeybek, Rick Mukherjee, Peter Schmelcher  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** numerical methods · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.24467v1>
