<a id="paper-2604.23708"></a>
### [Gradual eigenvector ergodization in coupled Ginibre matrices](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23708v1)

**Authors:** Margherita Disertori, Yan V. Fyorodov  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** disordered systems and neural networks · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.23708v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `driven-dissipative phase transition` **3/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **2/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **2/5** · `non-equilibrium universality` **2/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23708_figures/2604.23708_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> FIGURE 1. The density P∞(p) plotted for the values of coupling parameter, from left to right, |c|2 = 0.125 (blue), |c|2 = 0.25 (yellow), |c|2 = 0.5 (green), |c|2 = 1 (red), |c|2 = 2 (navy blue), |c|2 = 4 (brown) and |c|2 = 8 (dark blue).</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates the gradual ergodization of eigenvectors and the splitting of eigenvalue density support in a system of two coupled non-Hermitian random matrices.

**Main result.** The authors derive an explicit formula for the joint probability density of eigenvalues and eigenvectors at the spectral origin and identify a critical coupling strength where the spectral support splits into two disjoint domains.

**Method.** The study employs a non-perturbative approach using Grassmann (anticommuting) variables, the Hubbard-Stratonovich transformation, and the Laplace method for asymptotic analysis in the large N limit.

**Summary.** This paper studies how the interaction between two separate non-Hermitian quantum systems leads to the spreading of eigenvectors across the combined system. By using a model of two coupled Ginibre matrices, the authors provide an explicit mathematical description of the transition from a state where subsystems retain their identity to a fully ergodic state. They also show that at high coupling strengths, the eigenvalue distribution can split into two separate regions in the complex plane. This provides fundamental insights into the loss of subsystem identity in dissipative quantum chaos.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** A 2N x 2N non-Hermitian random matrix model consisting of two independent N x N complex Ginibre matrices coupled via a deterministic matrix c*1_N, representing interacting dissipative quantum chaotic systems.

**Key observables.** Squared norms of eigenvector components (p1, p2), joint probability density of eigenvalues and eigenvectors, and mean eigenvalue density.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Coupling strength |c|, matrix size N, and the scaling limit c = sqrt(N)*c_tilde.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The random matrix elements are assumed to be independent and normally distributed; the analysis focuses on the spectral origin (z=0) for mathematical tractability.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the model and the problem of eigenvector ergodization, presents a theorem for the joint probability density, provides a corollary for eigenvector norms, and concludes with a detailed mathematical appendix proving the asymptotic behavior of the integrals using the Laplace method.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work is highly relevant for researchers studying open quantum systems and many-body dynamics, as it provides a quantitative framework for understanding how interaction strength drives the transition from localized to ergodic states in dissipative chaotic systems like coupled SYK models.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Non-Hermitian random matrices provide a useful framework for understanding universal characteristics of dissipative quantum chaotic systems with loss or gain. We consider a model of two such system represented by two independent $N\times N$ complex Ginibre matrices interacting via a deterministic matrix $c{\bf 1}_N$, where $c$ is the complex coupling parameter whose magnitude $|c|$ controls the interaction strength. We characterize quantitatively how the eigenvectors of the whole system, initially localized in one of the individual subsystems for $|c|=0$, eventually spread over the full system with growing interaction strength. The resulting asymptotic formula describing such spread in the limit $N\to \infty$ is very explicit and provides a full picture of the gradual ergodization of eigenvectors as a function of the coupling parameter $|c|$ in the whole transition regime. As a by-product of our method we also compute the mean eigenvalue density for our model at the origin of the spectral bulk $z=0$ in the fully ergodic regime, when the coupling is scaled with the matrix size as $c=\sqrt{N}\tilde{c}$. We find that as $N\to \infty$ the limiting density at the origin vanishes beyond the critical value $|\tilde{c}|=1$, signalling of a split of the density support in the complex plane into two disjoint domains.

</details>

**Main problem.** Optimizing the two-photon excitation probability of a three-level ladder-type atom using indistinguishable photons and comparing this with experimentally accessible Gaussian and coherent states.

**Main result.** The authors identify an optimal two-photon state, which is the time-reversed counterpart of spontaneous cascade decay, capable of achieving perfect excitation in the limit of an infinitely long pulse. They also show that quantum interference in Gaussian pulses can shift spectral maxima away from atomic resonances.

**Method.** The study employs the Wigner-Weisskopf approximation, input-output formalism, and a quantum-trajectory/stochastic approach to solve the hierarchy of coupled differential equations for the atomic density matrix.

**Summary.** This paper investigates how to maximize the excitation of a three-level atom using pairs of indistinguishable photons. The authors find that the most efficient excitation occurs when the input light is the time-reversed version of the atom's own spontaneous decay process. By comparing this ideal state to practical Gaussian and coherent pulses, they demonstrate how quantum interference and photon correlations can be tuned to optimize light-matter interactions in quantum-optical interfaces.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** A three-level atom in a ladder configuration (|g>, |e>, |f>) interacting with a unidirectional, continuous-mode two-photon field. The dynamics are governed by a master equation within the Wigner-Weisskopf approximation.

**Key observables.** Population of the upper atomic state P_f(t), marginal probability densities in time and frequency domains, and average residence time in the final state.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Atomic decay rates (Gamma_e, Gamma_f), atomic transition frequency difference (delta_a), photon pulse widths, time delay (mu), and frequency offsets.

**Assumptions / limitations.** Rotating-wave approximation, Wigner-Weisskopf approximation, unidirectional field, and an initial ground state population.

**Figures summary.** Figures show the excitation probability and marginal distributions as functions of time and decay rate ratios; plots of optimized parameters (widths, delays, detunings) for various light states; and a comparison of excitation efficiency for coherent vs. two-photon states.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins with the analytical derivation of the optimal two-photon state, followed by an investigation of the spectral and temporal properties of this state. It then compares the ideal case to realistic Gaussian and coherent pulse models, analyzing the impact of decay rate ratios and detuning, and concludes with an appendix detailing the dynamical equations and optimized parameter landscapes.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work is highly relevant for quantum optics and open quantum systems as it provides fundamental guidance for designing efficient light-matter interfaces and explores how photon indistinguishability and quantum interference can be leveraged to control nonlinear atomic excitation.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We investigate the excitation of a three-level ladder-type atom by a unidirectional field with a pair of indistinguishable photons. Starting from an analytical expression for the two-photon absorption probability, we determine the two-photon state that maximizes the population of the upper atomic state at a chosen time and show that, in the limit of an infinitely long pulse, perfect excitation is possible. The optimal state is identified as the time-reversed counterpart of the two-photon state emitted in spontaneous cascade decay. We then compare this ideal excitation strategy with experimentally accessible families of states, including symmetrized Gaussian product states, temporally correlated Gaussian states, and coherent pulses. We analyze how the optimal excitation conditions depend on the ratio of atomic decay rates and on the separation of the atomic transition frequencies. For indistinguishable photons described by Gaussian pulses, quantum interference may shift the maxima of the marginal spectral distribution away from the atomic resonances and qualitatively modify the optimal excitation strategy. Our results clarify the role of indistinguishability and correlations in two-photon absorption and provide guidance for designing realistic excitation schemes in quantum-optical light-matter interfaces .

</details>

</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** Investigating how to mitigate Entanglement Sudden Death (ESD) and preserve the operational utility of multipartite entangled states under amplitude-damping noise.

**Main result.** Applying local NOT gates can transform entanglement sudden death into asymptotic decay for GMC, though the optimal number of gates depends on the specific noise regime and the metric being preserved (GMC vs. teleportation fidelity).

**Method.** Analytical derivation of entanglement dynamics and teleportation fidelity using GHZ-symmetric parametrization, twirling operations, and the Horodecki criterion for Bell nonlocality.

**Summary.** This paper studies how local NOT gates can be used to prevent the abrupt disappearance of entanglement (Entanglement Sudden Death) in 2, 3, and 4-qubit systems under amplitude damping. The authors show that while a single NOT gate can often turn sudden death into a slower asymptotic decay for entanglement, the best strategy for preserving teleportation fidelity may differ. The study highlights a decoupling between the amount of entanglement preserved and the actual utility of the state for quantum communication tasks. The results offer practical guidance for using simple $\pi$-pulses to protect quantum information in noisy hardware.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** Multipartite GHZ-type and Bell-type states consisting of 2, 3, and 4 qubits subject to a two-stage Amplitude Damping Channel (ADC).

**Key observables.** Genuine Multipartite Concurrence (GMC), teleportation fidelity (F), Bell-CHSH parameter (S), and Localizable Entanglement (LE).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Number of qubits (n=2,3,4), number of NOT gates applied (m), damping parameters (p, p'), and initial state population imbalance (alpha).

**Assumptions / limitations.** Local NOT operations are treated as instantaneous relative to the noise timescale; the ADC is modeled as two sequential, independent processes.

**Figures summary.** Schematics of the noise/control framework and teleportation protocols; plots of GMC and fidelity dynamics showing ESD vs. ADE; geometric projections of 4-qubit state trajectories in the GHZ-symmetric parameter space; and a summary map of optimal NOT-gate strategies.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of ESD, defines the noise model and control mechanism, provides analytical derivations for entanglement and fidelity, explores the hierarchy of correlations, classifies states using GHZ-symmetric parameters, and concludes with an optimization strategy for gate application.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides simple, experimentally accessible unitary control strategies (local bit-flips) to extend the lifetime of quantum resources in noisy environments, which is highly relevant for superconducting and trapped-ion platforms.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We study the entanglement dynamics of $n=2,3,4$-qubit Bell- and GHZ-type states under an amplitude-damping channel (ADC). We quantify multipartite entanglement using the genuine multipartite concurrence (GMC) and evaluate its utility through the optimal teleportation fidelity. For $2$-qubit states, we analyze the standard (Bennett) teleportation protocol. For $3$- and $4$-qubit states, we study controlled quantum teleportation (CQT) with one and two \emph{controllers}, respectively. Entanglement sudden death (ESD) denotes the abrupt, finite-time disappearance of entanglement caused by decoherence in contrast to asymptotic decay. To counteract ESD, we apply local NOT ($\hatσ_x$) operations on $m$ of the $n$ qubits ($m \leq n$) and derive analytic formulae, revealing that a single-NOT operation often suffices to alter ESD into asymptotic decay when handling GMC. In contrast, teleportation fidelity can decay more rapidly for single-NOT flipped states, whereas flipping all qubits is more useful for preserving teleportation fidelity in certain regimes, highlighting that the amount of entanglement alone does not guarantee teleportation utility. Remarkably, in the case of GHZ-type states, ADC-evolved mixed biseparable states can be exploited successfully in the CQT protocol. Further, using the GHZ-symmetric parametrization, we map the 2- and 3-qubit ADC-evolved mixed states onto a $(x,y)$ plane, revealing their SLOCC (Stochastic Local Operations and Classical Communication) entanglement classes. We also explicitly check the Bell-CHSH nonlocality hierarchy in the 2-qubit teleportation alongside localizable-entanglement diagnostics for 3-qubit CQT. Our results clarify the distinct roles of global versus localizable bipartite correlations and suggest simple, experimentally accessible unitary controls for preserving useful quantum resources in noisy channels.

</details>

### [Single-copy stabilizer learning: average case and worst case](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.24099v1)

**Authors:** Gyungmin Cho, Dohun Kim  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.24099v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `entanglement & information structure` **3/5** · `quantum measurements` **3/5** · `measurement-induced transitions` **2/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>

(b) Random Clifford measurement uses a single copy of ρ, applies a random Clifford circuit C ∼Cl(n), and then measures in the computational basis. In general, implementing C requires cir- cuit depth O(n). (c) Random shallow Clifford measurement, as considered in this work, also uses a single copy of ρ but replaces Cl(n) by the block-Clifford ensemble Ck = Cl(k)⊗n/k</figcaption>
</figure>

In particular, conditioning on S ∩A = M reduces the analysis of S ∩B to that of SM ∩BM in the quotient symplectic space.</figcaption>

(d) Color-code logical states, plotted as a function of the number of physical qubits n for different code distances. Each point shows the mean and standard deviation over 1000...</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates the complexity of learning the stabilizer group (Weyl support) of an n-qubit quantum state using single-copy measurements, specifically comparing average-case and worst-case scenarios.

**Main result.** In the average case, logarithmic-depth local Clifford circuits are sufficient to efficiently learn almost all stabilizer groups with codimension t = O(log n), but in the worst case, any single-copy measurement scheme requires an exponential number of samples in t.

**Method.** The authors use a block-Clifford ensemble (shallow-depth circuits) and a computational difference sampling technique to extract Pauli symmetries, supported by symplectic geometry and probabilistic analysis.

**Summary.** This paper addresses the challenge of identifying the hidden Pauli symmetries of a quantum state. It proves that for most states, we can use very shallow, local circuits to learn these symmetries efficiently. However, it also reveals a fundamental bottleneck: for certain 'hard' states like the GHZ state, single-copy measurements are exponentially less efficient than multi-copy methods. This highlights a potential quantum advantage in using entangled measurements for symmetry detection.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The study focuses on n-qubit quantum states, specifically t-doped stabilizer states and highly structured states like GHZ, Toric, and Color codes.

**Key observables.** The Weyl support (Pauli symmetries), Pauli collision coefficient, and the trace of the squared density matrix relative to the learned subspace.

**Important parameters / regimes.** n (number of qubits), t (codimension of the stabilizer group), k (circuit depth/block size), and epsilon/delta (error and failure parameters).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes the ability to perform randomized measurements using block-Clifford ensembles and that the block size k divides n.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 compares three measurement protocols (Bell, Random Clifford, and Shallow Clifford); Figure 2 shows recovery success vs. number of samples and noise; Figure 3 illustrates the reduction of intersection problems to quotient spaces; Figure 4 and 5 show success probabilities under noise and performance across different code types.

**Paper structure.** The paper is structured into two parts: Part I establishes an upper bound for the average case using probabilistic arguments and subspace counting; Part II establishes a lower bound for the worst case using reduction to property testing and analyzing the GHZ state.

**Why it may be interesting.** This is highly relevant for many-body dynamics and topological order, as it provides fundamental limits on how efficiently one can identify hidden symmetries and topological features in quantum systems using local, shallow-depth measurements.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We study single-copy stabilizer learning, the problem of identifying a stabilizer group of dimension $n-t$ from an $n$-qubit quantum state $ρ$. We obtain two complementary results. First, in the average case, logarithmic-depth local Clifford circuits suffice to efficiently learn almost all stabilizer groups with $t=O(\log n)$, instead of the linear-depth measurements required in previous approaches. We support this result with numerical simulations for systems of up to 100 qubits. Second, we show that, in the worst case, any adaptive single-copy measurement scheme requires a number of samples that scales exponentially in $t$. Together with existing results on two-copy learning, our findings suggest that, for large $t$, identifying Pauli symmetries of a quantum system exhibits a quantum advantage in the learning setting.

</details>

### [Beyond average: heterogeneous first-passage dynamics in many-particle systems with resetting](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.24406v1)

**Authors:** Juhee Lee, Seong-Gyu Yang, Ludvig Lizana  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.24406v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `non-equilibrium dynamics` **3/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **2/5** · `non-equilibrium universality` **2/5**

Symbols denote simulations and dashed lines indicate theoretical approximations, Eq. (4). Each color corresponds to a different resetting rate r. We note that the tail of the distributions extends with increasing r. Both (ensemble) averages ⟨τf⟩and ⟨τf⟩diverge for large r [(b) and (e)], but behave in the opposite way with respect to N: ⟨τf⟩decreases while ⟨τm⟩increases [(c) and (f)].</figcaption>

The distributions’ flanks rise with r, indicating that trajectories become more dissimilar (N = 100). (c,d) The distribution of τ i f,m between 0.45 &lt; wf,m ij &lt; 0.55 (orange shaded area) from panels (a) and (b), respectively (r = 10). The blue shaded areas are from Fig. 2(a) and (d).</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** Investigating how collective stochastic resetting affects first-passage dynamics and heterogeneity in many-particle interacting systems.

**Main result.** The study finds that group resetting produces broad hitting-time distributions with heavy tails and extended plateaus, leading to a lack of a single characteristic time scale and high trajectory heterogeneity.

**Method.** The authors use Langevin equation simulations (Euler-Maruyama method) combined with an analytical theory based on asymptotic behaviors and extreme value theory.

**Summary.** This paper examines how a 'group resetting' mechanism, where all particles in a system reset to the position of the most extreme particle, affects first-passage times. The researchers show that this protocol creates highly heterogeneous dynamics with arrival time distributions that span several orders of magnitude. They demonstrate that as the resetting rate increases, the mean arrival time can actually diverge. Ultimately, the study highlights that in such many-particle systems, average arrival times are insufficient to characterize the system due to the lack of a single characteristic time scale.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** A system of N overdamped Brownian particles in a one-dimensional harmonic potential with an absorbing boundary at the origin, subject to a group resetting protocol where all surviving particles relocate to the position of the rightmost particle.

**Key observables.** First Group Hitting Time (fGHT), Median Group Hitting Time (mGHT), and a Uniformity Index (w_ij) to quantify trajectory dissimilarity.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Resetting rate (r), group size (N), and the diffusion coefficient (D).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analytical derivation assumes the underlying single-particle dynamics follow an Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process (harmonic potential).

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows a schematic of the resetting dynamics and boundary conditions; Figure 2 illustrates hitting-time distributions and the development of plateaus; Figure 3 shows position distributions of the group center of mass.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of collective resetting, defines the physical model and resetting protocol, presents numerical simulations and analytical approximations for hitting-time distributions, analyzes trajectory heterogeneity using a uniformity index, and concludes with the implications for defining characteristic time scales.

**Why it may be interesting.** This is relevant for researchers in open quantum systems and many-body dynamics as it explores how non-equilibrium resetting protocols can fundamentally alter the collective transport and arrival statistics of a many-body ensemble.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We study how stochastic resetting affects first-passage processes in systems of many interacting particles. While resetting is well understood for single-particle dynamics, its consequences for collective behavior remain less clear. We consider a protocol in which all surviving particles are reset to the position of the most extreme one, motivated by problems in artificial selection and avoidance. Using stochastic simulations of particles diffusing in a confining potential with an absorbing boundary, we examine two notions of arrival: when the first particle reaches the boundary and the point at which half of the particles do. We find that resetting produces broad distributions of arrival times with heavy tails and extended plateaus that span several orders of magnitude. As the resetting rate increases, the mean arrival time grows and diverges beyond a threshold. Trajectory-level analysis also reveals strong heterogeneity, with very short and very long absorption times. These results show that collective resetting lacks a single characteristic time scale and that the definition of arrival is crucial for understanding and controlling such systems.

</details>

<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 10.</b> FIG. 10. Radial distribution deformation under radial cou- pling. (a) Heat map of the derivative of the average radius ⟨r⟩k (in units of ⟨r⟩0) with respect to coupling strength k ver- sus k and the inverse eﬀective temperature β. (b) Heat map of the derivative of the radial variance Vark(r) (in units of Var0(r)) with respect to k versus k and β. (c) The covariance of the squares of the mean and relative radius coordinates ¯r and δ (Eq. (23)), respectively, versus k and β.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** The study investigates how different types of mutual coupling (radial, phase, and Cartesian) between stochastic limit-cycle oscillators affect the steady-state entropy production rate (EPR) and energy dissipation.

**Main result.** The authors find that Cartesian coupling always reduces the EPR relative to the uncoupled system, while radial and phase couplings can either increase or decrease dissipation depending on the effective temperature and system size.

**Method.** The researchers use analytical derivations via the Fokker-Planck equation and numerical simulations of Langevin equations using the Euler-Maruyama method.

**Summary.** This paper explores the thermodynamics of coupled stochastic oscillators, specifically focusing on the energy cost of maintaining oscillations. By analyzing different coupling mechanisms, the authors demonstrate that while some couplings can increase dissipation, Cartesian coupling consistently reduces the entropy production rate. The study reveals a complex competition between the suppression of fluctuations and shifts in the radial distribution. These results provide fundamental insights into the energetic efficiency of biological and physical oscillators in noisy environments.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** An idealized model of stochastic circular limit-cycle oscillators described by overdamped Langevin equations in polar and Cartesian coordinates, including Stuart-Landau dynamics.

**Key observables.** Steady-state entropy production rate (EPR/sigma), radial probability distribution, tangential velocity fluctuations, and radial variance.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Coupling strength (k), inverse effective temperature (beta), intrinsic frequency (omega), system size (N), and frequency detuning (delta omega).

**Assumptions / limitations.** Noise is interpreted in the Stratonovich sense, and the system is assumed to be in a non-equilibrium steady state.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows a single stochastic oscillator, its trajectory, and radial probability distributions. Figure 2 illustrates EPR vs. coupling strength for different temperatures. Figure 3 shows EPR for phase coupling with varying system sizes. Figure 11 displays gradients of the average radius and radial variance.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by defining the single-oscillator model and its energetics, then progresses to analyze radial coupling, followed by phase/Kuramoto-like coupling, and finally examines full Cartesian coupling and network topology effects.

**Why it may be interesting.** The findings regarding how coupling-induced synchronization can reduce energy dissipation are highly relevant to understanding the energetic costs of maintaining coherence in open quantum systems and many-body driven-dissipative dynamics.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Non-linear oscillators serve important functions in many biological systems, including within the inner ear and neuronal networks. The sustainment of oscillations in noisy environments requires continuous energy dissipation, quantified by the steady-state entropy production rate (EPR). We study an idealized, analytically tractable model of a stochastic circular limit cycle and examine how mutual coupling in pairs and populations alters dissipation. For a single oscillator, the EPR depends on three key factors: intrinsic frequency, tangential velocity fluctuations, and mean tangential velocity. The dynamics are characterized by a dimensionless effective temperature given by the ratio of intrinsic relaxation and diffusion timescales. For radial (amplitude), phase (Kuramoto-like), and Cartesian couplings, we derive analytical expressions for the EPR and confirm them numerically. Varying the effective temperature and system size strongly influences how the EPR depends on coupling strength and, in some cases, results in qualitatively distinct behaviors. Moreover, the coupling types affect the tangential velocity distributions differently. Notably, in all cases studied, Cartesian coupling reduces the EPR relative to the uncoupled system, irrespective of effective temperature and system size. The analysis of idealized non-linear oscillators reveals that different classes of coupling interactions and competing timescales present in the oscillators have distinct effects on energy dissipation.

</details>

<a id="paper-2604.24133"></a>
### [Quantum algorithm for solving high-dimensional linear stochastic differential equations via amplitude encoding of the noise term](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.24133v1)

**Authors:** Koichi Miyamoto  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.24133v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `methods for driven-dissipative` **3/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **2/5** · `quantum measurements` **2/5**

</figure>

**Main problem.** Developing quantum algorithms to solve high-dimensional linear stochastic differential equations (SDEs) with an exponential speed-up in the dimension N, specifically addressing the challenge of amplitude encoding the noise term.

**Main result.** The paper proposes two methods (Dyson series-based and Euler-Maruyama-based) that achieve polylog(N) query complexity, providing an exponential speed-up in the dimension N compared to classical methods.

**Method.** The algorithms utilize amplitude encoding of the noise term via a quantum pseudorandom number generator (PRNG), quantum linear systems solvers (QLSS), and block-encoding techniques like the Linear Combination of Unitaries (LCU).

**Summary.** This paper presents new quantum algorithms for solving high-dimensional linear stochastic differential equations. By using amplitude encoding for the noise term and a quantum PRNG, the authors achieve an exponential speed-up in the system dimension N. They propose two distinct approaches: one based on the Dyson series and another based on the Euler-Maruyama discretization. The work also provides methods for estimating expectations of functions of the solution at various time points.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The model consists of high-dimensional linear SDEs of the form dX_t = A(t)X_t dt + B(t)dW_t, where X_t is an N-dimensional random process driven by m-dimensional Brownian motion.

**Key observables.** Expectations of functions of X_t at multiple time steps (history states) and expectations of quantities defined at the terminal time.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Dimension N, dimension of Brownian motion m, time interval [0, T], error tolerance epsilon, and stability/dissipativity parameter eta.

**Assumptions / limitations.** Assumes access to oracles for arithmetic operations and data loading, a bounded norm for the drift matrix A(t), and a bounded noise coefficient B(t).

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 provides a schematic outline of the Dyson and EM-based methods; Figure 2 illustrates the quantum circuit for block-encoding a product of matrices; Figure 3 shows the circuit implementation of the operator.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the SDE problem, presents two algorithmic approaches (Dyson-based and EM-based), details the construction of quantum oracles and block-encodings, provides rigorous error analysis and complexity bounds, and concludes with discussions on limitations and future extensions.

**Why it may be interesting.** This is highly relevant for researchers in open quantum systems and many-body dynamics, as SDEs are fundamental for modeling dissipative dynamics, and efficient quantum algorithms for high-dimensional stochastic processes are crucial for simulating complex quantum trajectories.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

This work studies quantum algorithms to solve high-dimensional stochastic differential equations (SDEs) $\mathrm{d} \mathbf{X}_t = A(t) \mathbf{X}_t \mathrm{d} t + B(t) \mathrm{d} \mathbf{W}_t$. Aiming for a speed-up in the dimension $N$ of $\mathbf{X}_t$, we generate quantum states that encode $\mathbf{X}_t$ in the amplitudes, while most of the existing quantum methods for SDEs employ binary encoding. A key challenge is the amplitude encoding of the noise term, and we address this by utilizing the quantum circuit implementation of a pseudorandom number generator (PRNG). We propose two methods: the Dyson series-based method and the Euler-Maruyama (EM)-based method. In the former, we express the noise term via the Dyson series approximation of the time evolution operator, while in the latter, it is approximated using the EM time discretization. Both methods use the quantum linear systems solver to generate the amplitude-encoding state of $\mathbf{X}_t$, making only ${\rm polylog}(N)$ queries to the PRNG circuit and the block-encodings of $A$ and $B$. Additionally, going beyond state preparation, we present methods to estimate expectations of functions of $\mathbf{X}_t$ using the state.

</details>

### [Quantum average correlation based on average coherence](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23501v1)

**Authors:** Xiaoyu Ma, Qing-Hua Zhang, Cong Xu  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.23501v1>

**Main problem.** The paper aims to develop a basis-independent (intrinsic) quantum correlation measure for bipartite systems to address the basis-dependent nature of existing measures.

**Main result.** The authors prove the equivalence of average correlation measures defined via MUBs, unitary group integration, and orthonormal bases, and derive a complementarity relation linking wave-particle duality to system-environment correlations.

**Method.** The study utilizes the Wigner-Yanase skew information framework, applying Haar measure integration over the unitary group and summation over Mutually Unbiased Bases (MUBs).

**Summary.** This paper introduces a new way to measure quantum correlations that does not depend on the choice of basis. By using Wigner-Yanases skew information, the authors show that averaging over different sets of bases (like MUBs or all possible unitaries) yields the same correlation value. They also discover a new rule for quantum systems: the loss of local wave-particle duality is exactly balanced by the correlation between the system and its environment. This provides a deeper understanding of how entanglement and correlations affect local quantum properties.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The study focuses on bipartite quantum systems (rho_AB) and bipartite pure states (rho_AE) within a composite Hilbert space, specifically considering multi-path interferometry contexts.

**Key observables.** Wigner-Yanase skew information, average coherence, quantum average correlation, wave feature (W), and particle feature (P).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Hilbert space dimensions (dA, dB), Schmidt coefficients (lambda_mu), and the existence of complete sets of MUBs for prime power dimensions.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The existence of a complete set of Mutually Unbiased Bases (MUBs) is assumed for dimensions that are prime powers.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of basis-dependence, defines average coherence and correlation using skew information, proves the mathematical equivalence of different averaging methods, establishes the properties of the new correlation measure, and concludes with a complementarity relation for wave-particle duality.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a fundamental link between local wave-particle duality and global system-environment correlations, offering a new way to quantify how information is lost to the environment in open quantum systems.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

This paper studies the quantification and structural properties of quantum average correlation based on average coherence. Motivated by two mathematically equivalent approaches to define average coherence: one by averaging over complete sets of mutually unbiased bases, and the other by integrating over all orthogonal bases under the Haar measure, we define an average correlation for bipartite systems as the difference between global and local skew information. This correlation measure is shown to satisfy essential properties including non negativity, contractivity under local quantum channels, and local unitary invariance. We further prove the equivalence between the average correlation defined via mutually unbiased bases and that defined via unitary groups. Finally, we derive a complementarity relation that connects wave-particle duality with the average correlation between a system and its environment.

</details>

**Main problem.** Investigating the discrepancies between quantum and classical descriptions of spin dynamics in dipole-coupled systems, specifically regarding the onset of chaos and long-term fidelity.

**Main result.** While quantum and classical simulations show qualitative agreement at short timescales, they diverge significantly at long timescales due to the nonlinearity of classical dynamics, which leads to chaos and a continuous frequency spectrum.

**Method.** The study compares exact diagonalization of the Schrödinger equation for quantum spins with numerical integration of nonlinear gyration equations for classical spins, using Lyapunov exponents to quantify chaos.

**Summary.** This paper compares quantum and classical simulations of dipole-coupled spin systems. It demonstrates that while classical models can approximate quantum dynamics for short periods, they fail at longer timescales because classical nonlinear dynamics lead to chaos and continuous spectra. The authors use Lyapunov exponents to show that classical systems become chaotic for more than three spins, whereas quantum systems remain regular. This research highlights the fundamental limitations of using classical spin-dynamics simulations to represent quantum many-body systems.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** A system of N interacting spins (spin-1/2 for quantum, magnetic vectors for classical) in a regular lattice, governed by a Hamiltonian containing a Zeeman term and a dipole-dipole interaction term.

**Key observables.** Free Induction Decay (FID) of transverse magnetization, Fourier transform of the FID (frequency spectrum), and the maximum Lyapunov exponent.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Dimensionless dipole interaction parameter (dp), number of spins (N), and the characteristic dipole time (t*).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The system is assumed to be a rigid lattice, spin-lattice relaxation is neglected, and the classical model relies on a mean-field approximation.

**Figures summary.** Figures show the time evolution and Fourier transforms of FID for various N, comparisons with Abragam's trial function, the divergence of classical trajectories signaling chaos, and the scaling of the Lyapunov exponent.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of quantum-classical spin discrepancies, details the quantum and classical models, presents results on FID and spectral features, analyzes the onset of chaos via Lyapunov exponents, and provides an analytical study of the two-spin case in an appendix.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides critical insights into the validity of using classical approximations for many-body spin dynamics and highlights how nonlinearity-induced chaos in classical models can obscure quantum spectral features.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We investigate the spin dynamics of a dipole-coupled system by comparing a direct solution of the Schrodinger equation for quantum spins with simulations of classical spins. Although classical spins have long been used in microscopic spin dynamics simulations, we demonstrate that their results differ significantly from those of quantum spins. Using Free Induction Decay as a benchmark, we find that while the overall patterns are qualitatively similar, significant discrepancies emerge at both short and long timescales. We trace these differences to fundamental distinctions in the two descriptions.

</details>

### [Heralded Entanglement Transfer from Entangled Atomic Pair to Free Electrons](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.22974v1)

**Authors:** Du Ran, Reuven Ianconescu, Shuai Liu, Ya-dong Li, Ji-Yuan Bai, Ze-Long He, Zhi-Cheng Shi, Yan Xia, Avraham Gover  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.22974v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `entanglement & information structure` **3/5** · `quantum measurements` **3/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>

<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> FIG. 1. Entanglement-transfer dynamics between the TLSs and the free-electrons. (a) Unconditional entanglement mea- sures. Left axis: TLS concurrence C12(t). Right axis: electron logarithmic negativity EAB N (t), shown for the RWA numerics, the full bilinear model (FULL) numerics, and the resonant closed-form RWA benchmark. (b) Heralded extraction into the two-dimensional target manifolds H(±) 2 . Left axis: un- conditional target-manifold weight w(±) 2 (t). Right axis: log- arithmic negativity EAB,(2,±) N (t) of the normalized projected state ˆρ(2,±) AB (t). Parameters: ∆= 0 and pulse area g = π/4.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.22974_figures/2604.22974_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> FIG. 2. Closed-form relation between the postselected elec- tron log-negativity and the initial TLS concurrence. (a) shows EAB,(2,−) N (T) versus C12(0), where the analytic prediction in Eq. (24) (solid) is compared with numerical RWA evolution (dashed). In the symmetric resonant case the (2, +) and (2, −) branches coincide. (b) displays the same quantity as a func- tion of the TLS amplitude α for the initial resource.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** Developing a protocol to transfer entanglement from an existing entangled atomic two-level-system (TLS) resource to a pair of free electrons in quantum electron optics.

**Main result.** The protocol achieves heralded entanglement transfer where the final electron entanglement is determined solely by the initial TLS concurrence, following the relation E_AB = log2(1 + C_12).

**Method.** The authors use analytical derivations in the rotating-wave approximation (RWA) and compare them with numerical integration of the full bilinear Hamiltonian to quantify corrections.

**Summary.** This paper proposes a method to transfer quantum entanglement from an atomic pair to free electrons. By measuring the atoms after the interaction (heralding), one can certify the creation of an entangled electron state. The study shows that the amount of entanglement transferred depends directly on the initial atomic entanglement. The authors also quantify how much the results deviate from ideal models due to detuning and higher-order physical effects.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The system consists of two spatially separated free electrons interacting with two spatially separated atomic two-level systems (TLS) via Coulombic dipole coupling, modeled using an energy-sideband ladder.

**Key observables.** TLS concurrence (C_12), electron logarithmic negativity (E_AB^N), heralding probability, and target-manifold weight.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Detuning (delta), pulse area (g), interaction time (T), and the initial TLS entanglement (alpha, beta).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The primary analytical framework relies on the Rotating-Wave Approximation (RWA) and a discrete-sideband plane-wave approximation.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1(a) shows the time-resolved dynamics of the decreasing TLS concurrence.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the entanglement transfer problem, defines the Hamiltonian and interaction model, derives analytical solutions under RWA, performs numerical benchmarking against the full model, and discusses systematic corrections and future extensions.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a theoretical blueprint for generating entanglement in free-electron systems, a key platform for quantum electron optics, and explores the fundamental limits of entanglement transfer via heralded measurements.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We propose a protocol that transfers entanglement from an entangled atomic two-level-system (TLS) resource to a pair of free electrons in an energy-sideband ladder via local electron-TLS interactions. In a controlled rotating-wave regime, closed-form reduced states are derived. TLS heralding then prepares a maximally entangled electron state in a two-dimensional single-excitation manifold, with a simple dependence on the initial TLS resource entanglement. Numerical integration of the full bilinear Hamiltonian quantifies the impacts of detuning and pulse shaping and identifies the leading beyond-rotating-wave corrections. The results establish a heralded route to entangled free electrons and will facilitate further advances in quantum electron optics.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

<a id="paper-2604.24741"></a>
### [Universal tracer statistics in single-file transport](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.24741v1)

**Authors:** Soumyabrata Saha, Jitendra Kethepalli, Benjamin Guiselin, Jacopo De Nardis, Tridib Sadhu  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.24741v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `non-equilibrium dynamics` **3/5** · `non-equilibrium universality` **3/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24741_figures/2604.24741_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> Figure 1. Schematic trajectories of the (a) diffusive and (b) ballistic hard-rod gas. All rods have equal length a and unit mass. In (a), particles undergo Brownian motion between collisions, while in (b) they move along straight lines governed by their velocities, which are exchanged upon collision. The trajectories of two tracers are highlighted, with their positions at time t denoted by zt and yt.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24741_figures/2604.24741_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Figure 2. Results for both gases in the quenched ensemble with D0 = 0.5, a = 1, and ¯ρ = 0.25. Markers (D: diffusive, B: ballistic) are numerical results; solid lines are analytical predictions. Plot (a) shows the tracer LDF (2c) and the current LDF,</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** Investigating whether an emergent universality exists in the large-scale, long-time statistics of tracer particles in single-file transport, specifically comparing stochastic (diffusive) and unitary (ballistic) dynamics.

**Main result.** The authors uncover that the one-time joint distribution of multiple tracers exhibits identical non-Gaussian fluctuations for both diffusive and ballistic gases, up to a simple dynamical scaling, though differences appear in multi-time statistics.

**Method.** The study employs exact microscopic solutions, hydrodynamic approaches (MFT and BMFT), and rare-event numerical simulations using importance sampling and multi-histogram reweighting.

**Summary.** This paper demonstrates a surprising universality in single-file transport where fundamentally different microscopic dynamics—stochastic diffusion and ballistic motion—produce identical one-time statistics for tracer particles. By using both exact analytical methods and advanced rare-event simulations, the authors show that the large-deviation functions for particle positions and currents are the same up to scaling. This universality holds for both annealed and quenched initial conditions, though the distinction between the two dynamics is preserved in multi-time correlations and aging effects.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** A one-dimensional hard-rod gas with finite particle length 'a' subject to either diffusive (Brownian) or ballistic (velocity-exchange) dynamics in both annealed and quenched ensembles.

**Key observables.** One-time tracer position, joint distribution of tracer pairs, two-time statistics of a single tracer, and time-integrated current.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Scaling exponent H (1/4 for diffusive, 1/2 for ballistic), rod length 'a', density 'rho', and diffusion coefficient 'D0'.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes the large-time and large-system limits and relies on a coordinate transformation mapping hard rods to non-interacting point particles.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows schematic trajectories of diffusive vs. ballistic motion; Figure 2 presents numerical and analytical results for the Large Deviation Function (LDF) of tracers and currents, as well as equal-time and two-time covariances.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of universality in single-file transport, details the two dynamical models, presents analytical derivations using microscopic and hydrodynamic methods, provides numerical validation via rare-event simulations, and concludes with discussion on the breakdown of universality in multi-time statistics.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We uncover an emergent universality in the large-scale, long-time statistics of a one-dimensional hard-rod gas evolving under two fundamentally different classes of microscopic dynamics: stochastic (diffusive) and unitary (ballistic). Remarkably, despite the difference of the two systems, the one-time joint distribution of the positions of multiple tracers exhibits identical non-Gaussian fluctuations, up to a simple dynamical scaling. This universality holds in both annealed and quenched ensembles, demonstrating a persistent memory of the initial state. Differences between the dynamics manifest at large scales only in multi-time statistics. Our conclusions are based on explicit large-deviation results for the one-time statistics of tracer pairs and the two-time statistics of a single tracer. Similar physics extends to current fluctuations, demonstrated explicitly in the quenched ensemble. We obtain these results from exact microscopic solutions for both dynamics and, independently, from fluctuating hydrodynamics in the ballistic case in the annealed ensemble. Our rare-event simulations further corroborate these findings and provide a novel demonstration of sampling atypical fluctuations in both types of hard-rod gas.

</details>

<a id="paper-2604.22996"></a>
### [Accelerating quantum Gibbs sampling without quantum walks](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.22996v1)

**Authors:** Jiaqi Leng, Jiaqing Jiang, Lin Lin  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.22996v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `methods for driven-dissipative` **3/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **2/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>

Panel (c) shows the full circuit assembling these ingredients with the quadrature-weight preparation oracle Uprep and a branch ancilla to realize the linear combination corresponding to I ⊗B♯ j,tk −(B♭ j,tk)⊺⊗I.</figcaption>

We plot the overlap Tr[ρ(t)∣σ1/2⟩⟩⟨⟨σ1/2∣] as a function of the evolution time t, for several inverse tem- peratures β. As β increases, the global mixing time grows substantially, but the overlap with the target purified Gibbs state already exceeds 0.2 at t = 1 for all values of β shown. Therefore, the auxiliary dynamics can generate states with non-negligible target overlap on a timescale much shorter than the global mixing time.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.22996_figures/2604.22996_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">

</figure>

<img src="2604.22996_figures/2604.22996_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> Figure 4. Spectral gaps of three classes of Lindbladian operators: the auxiliary dynamics (without unitary dressing), the auxiliary dynamics with unitary dressing, and the original DLL Gibbs sampler.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** The challenge of efficiently preparing purified Gibbs states on a quantum computer, specifically overcoming the quadratic dependence on the spectral gap found in standard Szegedy-type quantum walk approaches.

**Main result.** The authors present a walk-free quantum algorithm that achieves a quadratic improvement in spectral gap dependence, reducing it from O(1/Delta) to O(1/sqrt(Delta)), and introduce a warm-start strategy using auxiliary dissipative dynamics.

**Method.** The algorithm uses a singular-value filtering approach via Quantum Singular Value Transformation (QSVT) applied to an explicit factorization of the parent Hamiltonian into non-commutative first-order operators.

**Summary.** This paper introduces a new quantum algorithm for preparing thermal (Gibbs) states that is faster than previous quantum walk-based methods. By using a mathematical factorization of the system's Hamiltonian, the authors reduce the computational complexity related to the spectral gap from 1/Delta to 1/sqrt(Delta). They also provide a method to generate good starting states for the algorithm using auxiliary dissipative dynamics. This advancement makes the simulation of many-body systems at finite temperatures more efficient on future quantum hardware.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** Quantum many-body systems at finite temperature governed by Lindbladian dynamics that satisfy the Kubo-Martin-Schwinger (KMS) detailed balance condition, including Davies and CKG samplers.

**Key observables.** Thermal expectation values of observables, spectral gap (Delta), and overlap between the prepared state and the target purified Gibbs state.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Inverse temperature (beta), spectral gap (Delta), precision (epsilon), and the number of jump operators (J).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The Lindbladian must satisfy the KMS detailed balance condition and require coherent access to the first-order factors and a warm-start initial state.

**Figures summary.** Numerical simulations of the Transverse-Field Ising Model (TFIM) showing the overlap of auxiliary dynamics with the target state as a function of time and temperature, and a comparison of spectral gaps for different Lindbladian classes.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of Gibbs sampling speedups, presents a mathematical factorization of the parent Hamiltonian, develops the QSVT-based algorithm, proposes an auxiliary dynamics for warm-starting, and validates the approach with numerical simulations on the TFIM.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work is highly relevant for researchers in open quantum systems and many-body dynamics as it provides a more efficient way to simulate thermal equilibrium states and addresses the practical difficulty of state preparation in the low-temperature regime.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Szegedy's quantum walk gives a generic quadratic speedup for reversible classical Markov chains, but extending this mechanism to quantum Gibbs sampling has remained challenging beyond special cases. We present a walk-free quantum algorithm for preparing purified Gibbs states with a quadratic improvement in spectral-gap dependence for a broad class of quantum Gibbs samplers that satisfy exact Kubo-Martin-Schwinger detailed balance. Our main structural result is an explicit factorization of the corresponding parent Hamiltonian into noncommutative first-order operators. This turns purified Gibbs-state preparation into a singular-value filtering problem and enables a quantum singular value transformation algorithm with quadratically improved gap dependence under standard coherent-access assumptions. The framework applies to several efficiently implementable Gibbs samplers beyond the Davies setting. We also introduce an auxiliary dissipative dynamics based on the same factorization, which can be used to generate warm starts in the doubled Hilbert space in metastable regimes.

</details>

### [Contextuality from the Projector Overlap Matrix](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23898v1)

**Authors:** Ali Can Günhan, Semahi Serhat Aksoy, Zafer Gedik  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.23898v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `entanglement & information structure` **3/5** · `quantum measurements` **2/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>

<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23898_figures/2604.23898_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> FIG. 1. Three of the four state-dependent witnesses of Table I evaluated on the KCBS mixing family ˆρ(p) = p |0z⟩⟨0z|+(1−p)ˆI/3, with threshold p⋆= (3 √</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23898_figures/2604.23898_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> FIG. 2. Operational quantity D(GKCBS, ˆρ) of Paper I [16] on states outside the time-reversal-invariant family ˆρ(p) of Fig. 1, where D ≡0. Throughout, “TR” abbreviates time reversal; a state is TR-even if it is invariant under the antiunitary time- reversal operation, and TR-broken otherwise. (a) Pure-state sweep |ψ(s)⟩= cos s |0z⟩+ sin s |+1z⟩. The endpoints are qualitatively distinct: s = 0 is the TR-even state |0z⟩(open circle, D = 0) and s = π/2 is the TR-broken state |+1z⟩(filled circle, D ≈6.4984). Interior values 0 &lt; s &lt; π/2 correspond to superpositions of the two endpoints and are neither purely TR-even nor purely TR-broken; D rises across the sweep, with a mild non-monotonicity...</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** The paper seeks to unify various indicators of Kochen-Specker contextuality into a single framework based on a projector overlap matrix and investigates the relationship between state-independent geometric properties and state-dependent contextuality witnesses.

**Main result.** The authors establish that the quantity S2 (derived from the overlap matrix) is a necessary configuration-level condition for observable contextuality and can remain positive even when state-dependent witnesses are silent. They also identify a structural mechanism (shared eigenstates) that explains why certain entropic uncertainty relations fail to detect contextuality in the KCBS scenario.

**Method.** The study uses a geometric framework involving a projector overlap matrix T, analyzing scalar contractions such as mutual information energy E and the Maassen-Uffink extremal overlap cMU. Analytical derivations and numerical evaluations are applied to KCBS and CHSH scenarios.

**Summary.** This paper introduces a new, state-independent indicator of quantum contextuality based on the geometric overlap of projectors. By analyzing the 'Projector Overlap Matrix,' the authors show that certain geometric properties can signal the presence of contextuality even when traditional, state-dependent witnesses fail to detect it. The work provides a unified view of several known contextuality measures and explains the failure of certain entropic uncertainty relations in specific spin-1 configurations.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The research focuses on finite-dimensional Hilbert spaces, specifically the spin-1 KCBS pentagon (5-cycle) and the two-qubit CHSH 4-cycle configuration.

**Key observables.** KCBS correlator (chi), contextual fraction (CF), Shannon-entropic n-cycle inequality, operational commutator witness (D), and the projector overlap entropy (S2).

**Important parameters / regimes.** The mixing parameter p (controlling state purity), the Maassen-Uffink parameter cMU, and the angular configurations of observables in the CHSH and KCBS scenarios.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The framework assumes the existence of compatible observable pairs within measurement contexts and focuses on specific n-cycle geometries.

**Figures summary.** The figures illustrate the relationship between different contextuality witnesses, the trade-off between Bell-inequality violation and entropic-inequality violation in the CHSH scenario, and the scaling of entropy for large n-cycles.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces a unified framework using the overlap matrix, defines key scalar contractions, proves properties like monotonicity and additive exactness, and then applies the framework to the KCBS and CHSH scenarios to compare state-independent and state-dependent indicators.

**Why it may be interesting.** This paper provides a deep geometric understanding of contextuality, which is fundamental to quantum information processing and the study of non-classical correlations in many-body systems.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We place several known indicators of Kochen--Specker contextuality -- the KCBS correlator $χ$, the contextual fraction $\CF$, the Shannon-entropic $n$-cycle inequality of Chaves and Fritz, and the operational commutator witness $D$ of Paper~I -- into a single projector-geometric framework organized around the overlap matrix $\Tcal_{ij} = d^{-1}\tr[(\hat P_i \hat Q_j)^2]$, where $\hat P_i$ and $\hat Q_j$ are the joint-eigenspace projectors of the two compatible observable pairs within a measurement context. The state-independent scalar content of $\Tcal$ is carried by two independent contractions: the mutual information energy $E = \sum_{ij}\Tcal_{ij}$ of Paper~I (equivalently, its logarithmic form $S_2 = -\log_2 E$), and the Maassen--Uffink extremal overlap $c_\MU = \max_{i,j}|\langle a_i,b_i | c_j,b_j\rangle|$. We prove that $S_2$ is non-increasing under coarse-graining, that $S_2(\Gcal) = 0$ is a necessary configuration-level condition for observable contextuality, and that the additive composition $S_2(\Gcal) = \sum_αS_2(\Gcal_α)$ is exact for the KCBS pentagon. We further show that in the spin-$1$ realization of the KCBS pentagon, a shared $m_s=0$ eigenstate in each context forces $c_\MU = 1$, rendering every Maassen--Uffink-type bound trivial -- a structural mechanism that makes explicit why outcome-entropic uncertainty relations based on $c_\MU$ are silent on KCBS contextuality, while $S_2 \approx 2.7266$~bits throughout. Applied to KCBS and CHSH, the framework identifies regimes in which every state-dependent witness considered here is silent yet $S_2(\Gcal) > 0$ by an amount set by the projector geometry alone.

</details>

<a id="paper-2604.24423"></a>
### [Dualistic operational characterization of device-dependent correlation sets via convex analysis in the $(2,m,2)$ Bell scenario](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.24423v1)

**Authors:** Ryosuke Nogami, Jaeha Lee  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.24423v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `entanglement & information structure` **3/5** · `quantum measurements` **2/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24423_figures/2604.24423_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> FIG. 1. Duality structure of the support and gauge functions. The upper level represents the</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** The paper aims to provide a dualistic operational characterization of device-dependent correlation sets (separable, quantum, and maximal) within the (2,m,2) Bell scenario.

**Main result.** The authors derive explicit formulae for the support and gauge functions of these correlation sets, establishing that detection sensitivity for entanglement and beyond-quantum states is fundamentally limited by the number of linearly independent measurement directions.

**Method.** The study employs convex analysis, utilizing support functions for entanglement witnessing and gauge functions for quantifying noise robustness, alongside matrix algebra and Singular Value Decomposition (SVD).

**Summary.** This paper provides a complete geometric and operational characterization of correlation sets in the (2,m,2) Bell scenario. By using convex analysis, the authors derive explicit formulas for support and gauge functions that allow for optimal entanglement witnessing and noise robustness quantification. They show that the ability to detect entanglement or beyond-quantum correlations depends directly on the number of linearly independent measurement directions available to the parties. Specifically, they demonstrate that for certain measurement settings, the detection threshold can reach the optimal PPT limit.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The system consists of two-qubit systems (C2 tensor C2) in a (2,m,2) Bell scenario, where two parties perform m dichotomic measurements with outcomes in {+1, -1}.

**Key observables.** Correlation matrix (C), support functions (phi), and gauge functions (gamma).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Number of measurement settings (m), rank of measurement matrices (r), and the critical noise threshold (p_crit) for Werner states.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis is restricted to device-dependent (fixed local measurements) rather than device-independent scenarios and is focused on two-qubit systems.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the (2,m,2) scenario and three state space models, develops the mathematical framework of support and gauge functions via convex analysis, derives explicit analytic formulae for these functions, analyzes detection thresholds for entanglement and beyond-quantum states, and concludes with discussions on dimensionality and higher-dimensional generalizations.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides fundamental bounds on the robustness of non-classical correlations against noise, which is critical for designing reliable quantum communication protocols and understanding the limits of entanglement detection in noisy environments.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We analyze device-dependent correlation sets generated by fixed local dichotomic measurements for two-qubit systems in the $(2,m,2)$ Bell scenario. We consider three fundamental state spaces for the composite system: the separable state space, the standard quantum state space, and the maximal tensor-product state space, which contains beyond-quantum states compatible with local quantum measurements. We formulate the corresponding correlation sets for general fixed dichotomic measurements and, in the traceless case, derive particularly simple explicit formulae for their support and gauge functions. These functions furnish dual operational characterizations of the three correlation sets: the support functions give optimal witnesses for entanglement and beyond-quantum states, whereas the gauge functions quantify the robustness of these detections against depolarizing noise. We further derive convex-hull representations that elucidate the extremal structures of the correlation sets and the physical states realizing them, showing in particular that extremal quantum correlations are realized by maximally entangled states. The fundamental limits of these dual operational tasks are governed solely by the smaller of the numbers of linearly independent measurement directions available to Alice and Bob. When both parties have three linearly independent measurement directions, our entanglement criterion detects Werner states up to the optimal PPT threshold $p_{\mathrm{crit}}=2/3$. For beyond-quantum-state detection, a nontrivial separation from the quantum set occurs only under the same measurement condition; in that case, the same optimal noise threshold is attained for an extremal state in the maximal tensor-product state space.

</details>

**Authors:** Nicholas Franklin, Jacob Richards, Harry Lane  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.24687v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `scars & prethermalization` **3/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **2/5**

</figure>

The I domain can be transformed to the II domain by the action of ˆR. (b) Action of a spin flip on a polyhex tiling. B1 and B2 correspond to two degenerate states created by acting on the bee polyhex. E1 and E2 are degenerate edge excitations. (c) One possible tiling of a 6 × 6 supercell by three 12-hexes.</figcaption>

<figure class="paper-figure-card">

Intensities are seen at Γ due to the finite magnetization in the A phase. (b) Autocor- relation A(t) as a function of field at kBT = 0.75|J1|. Dashed lines correspond to the field values in (a). Overlaid is the magnetization curve for this temperature.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** The study investigates the ground state properties and dynamics of a new 'jammed polyhex spin liquid' phase in the honeycomb Ising antiferromagnet under an external magnetic field.

**Main result.** The authors identify a phase with extensive ground state degeneracy that maps to a polyhex tiling problem, characterized by 'jamming' where the lack of local zero modes constrains dynamics. They also show that quantum fluctuations partially lift this degeneracy, selecting a specific subspace of non-periodic tilings.

**Method.** The study employs combinatorial tiling analysis using Knuth's Algorithm X, Monte Carlo simulations with parallel tempering, and degenerate perturbation theory to derive an effective Hamiltonian.

**Summary.** This paper identifies a novel 'jammed polyhex spin liquid' phase in the honeycomb Ising antiferromagnet under a magnetic field. By mapping the spin configurations to a polyhex tiling problem, the authors demonstrate that the system possesses extensive ground state degeneracy but lacks the local zero modes typical of conventional spin liquids, leading to 'jammed' dynamics. They show that the magnetic field can control spin diffusion and that quantum fluctuations select a specific subset of non-periodic tilings. The findings are particularly relevant to the study of transition metal trichalcogenides like FePS3.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** A honeycomb Ising antiferromagnet with competing interactions (J1, J2, J3) subjected to an external magnetic field, specifically focusing on the regime where J1 is ferromagnetic and J2, J3 are antiferromagnetic.

**Key observables.** Magnetization (M/Ms), specific heat (C), magnetic susceptibility (chi), spin autocorrelation function (A(t)), and static structure factor (S(Q)).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Magnetic field strength (h), interaction strengths (J1, J2, J3), and temperature (kT).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes the validity of the mapping to polyhex tilings at the M/Ms = 1/2 plateau and uses second- and third-order perturbation theory to account for quantum fluctuations.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1 shows the phase diagram of the honeycomb Ising model; Fig 2 displays an example of bee polyhex tiling and the scaling of tiling degeneracy; Fig 4 illustrates the action of spin flips on polyhex tilings and the resulting excitations.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by defining the Hamiltonian and the tiling mapping, proceeds to analyze the classical ground state degeneracy and its scaling, explores the dynamics and jamming via Monte Carlo, introduces quantum fluctuations via perturbation theory, and concludes with experimental relevance to FePS3.

**Why it may be interesting.** The discovery of a 'jammed' spin liquid phase provides a unique example of a highly degenerate system where dynamics are constrained by topological/combinatorial structures rather than local zero modes, which is relevant to many-body dynamics and constrained dynamics.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We analyze the ground state properties of the honeycomb Ising antiferromagnet in an external magnetic field. We demonstrate the existence of extensive ground state degeneracy at finite field that maps to a polyhex tiling problem. This state is shown to be a jammed spin liquid, with no local zero modes connecting ground states. Through Monte Carlo simulations, we explore the properties of these states and show that the spin diffusion can be controlled by the magnetic field strength. By considering quantum fluctuations, we demonstrate that transverse coupling partially lifts the ground state degeneracy, selecting an extensive subspace of non-periodic tilings of 12-hexes. We suggest that the jammed polyhex spin liquid phase exists in an experimentally realizable region of parameter space and may be present in the FePX$_3$ compounds.

</details>

### [Fraunhofer Patterns in Atomic Josephson Junctions](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.22923v1)

**Authors:** Kevin T. Geier, Giampiero Marchegiani, Vijay Pal Singh, Juan Polo, Luigi Amico

In the main lobe, the extrapolated current–phase relation sys- tematically overestimates the critical current realizable in practice with our protocol due to dynamical instabilities. The solid lines show the pattern Ic(Φ) = Ic(0) sinc[k(Φ)Leff/2], where the wave numbers k(Φ)...</figcaption>
</figure>
