**Main problem.** Investigating the emergence of Fraunhofer-like interference patterns in the critical current of atomic Josephson junctions under synthetic magnetic fields and understanding the role of Josephson vortices.

**Main result.** The study demonstrates that synthetic magnetic fields induce Fraunhofer-like modulations of the critical current in neutral superfluids, with deviations from ideal superconducting behavior due to the lack of Meissner screening and the presence of large-scale superflows.

**Method.** Numerical simulations based on the time-dependent Gross-Pitaevskii equation (GPE) using sine pseudospectral discretization and Magnus integration.

**Summary.** This paper explores how synthetic magnetic fields can induce Fraunhofer-like interference patterns in the critical current of atomic Josephson junctions. Using Gross-Pitaevskii simulations, the authors show that while the pattern is analogous to superconducting junctions, the neutral nature of the BEC leads to distinct features like shifted interference minima. The study also elucidates how the entry of Josephson vortices modulates the current density. These findings are significant for developing matter-wave circuits and atomtronic quantum technologies.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** A quasi-2D Bose-Einstein Condensate (BEC) in a rectangular box trap, featuring an atomic Josephson junction created by a repulsive Gaussian potential barrier and subjected to a synthetic vector potential.

**Key observables.** Critical current (Ic), chemical potential difference (delta-mu), current density distribution (J(y)), synthetic magnetic flux (Phi), and vortex count.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Synthetic magnetic flux (Phi/Phi0), barrier velocity (v), healing length (xi), chemical potential (mu), and the DC/AC regimes.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The system is in the deep tunneling regime where the current can be approximated by the barrier velocity; the synthetic gauge field does not experience reciprocal effects (no screening) from the neutral matter.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the experimental setup; Figure 2 illustrates the temporal evolution of density and vortex entry during flux sweeps; Figure 3 shows signatures of Josephson vortices; Figure 4 displays the current-phase relation; Figure S8 compares wave numbers extracted from current density and phase difference.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the setup of atomic Josephson junctions in synthetic fields, presents numerical results for critical current modulation (Fraunhofer patterns), analyzes the microscopic role of Josephson vortices, compares extraction methods for critical current, and discusses the impact of superflows on phase gradients.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a bridge between superconductivity and atomtronics, showing how neutral superfluids can mimic complex electromagnetic phenomena like Fraunhofer patterns while introducing unique physics due to the absence of the Meissner effect.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Driven atomic Josephson junctions allow one to monitor phase-coherent dynamics with unprecedented control and flexibility of the system's physical conditions. While cold-atom manifestations of the Josephson effect have been extensively studied in a wide variety of settings, atomic Josephson junctions in synthetic electromagnetic fields remain largely unexplored. Here, we show that synthetic magnetic fields can induce Fraunhofer-like modulations of the critical current in atomic Josephson junctions. Although this effect presents analogies to the Fraunhofer patterns found in superconducting devices, distinctive features emerge due to the neutral nature of the superfluid. We investigate the underlying spatial interference mechanisms and elucidate the role of Josephson vortices in the formation of spatially modulated current distributions based on numerical simulations. Our results open up new avenues for matter-wave circuits to deepen our understanding of spatial coherence in Josephson junctions, which are fundamental to the development of novel quantum technologies.

</details>

**Authors:** Zander Scholl, Justin Woods, Charudatta Phatak, Hanu Arava  
**Type:** both · **Category:** disordered systems and neural networks · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.23431v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `spintronics-quantum-optics interface` **3/5** · `QC/QI experiment` **1/5** · `quantum measurements` **1/5**

(C, D) Line profiles comparing the measured stray field (Raw BNV) with the final reconstructed field from the model, showing excellent agreement for both magnetic configurations. (E, F) MSE error as a function of standoff distance for Landau and Dipole domains, indicating the...</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** Reconstructing in-plane magnetization from scanning NV magnetometry data is an ill-posed inverse problem because infinitely many magnetization configurations can produce the same stray field distribution.

**Main result.** A physics-informed Deep Image Prior (DIP) framework using a convolutional autoencoder can qualitatively and quantitatively reconstruct complex magnetic textures, with optimal spatial mask alignment improving SNR by up to 3 dB.

**Method.** The researchers use a convolutional autoencoder-based Deep Image Prior (DIP) framework with a physics-informed MSE loss function in Fourier space, incorporating spatial masking and magnetostatic forward modeling.

**Summary.** This paper presents a new way to reconstruct magnetic patterns in thin films using a neural network approach called Deep Image Prior. By using a physics-informed model, the researchers can overcome the mathematical ambiguity of magnetic field measurements. The method works without needing pre-trained datasets and can even use the reconstruction accuracy to help identify the correct orientation of magnetic domains. This provides a more efficient tool for analyzing nanoscale magnetic textures in spintronic materials.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The study uses Permalloy (Ni80Fe20) nanostructures with lithographically patterned ellipse shapes, specifically investigating Landau and dipole domain configurations, measured via scanning Nitrogen-Vacancy (NV) magnetometry.

**Key observables.** Stray field maps (B_NV), magnetization maps (Mx, My), signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), and Mean Square Error (MSE).

**Important parameters / regimes.** NV-to-sample standoff distance (z), spatial mask orientation, and spatial filter cutoff frequencies.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The method relies on accurate spatial masking and may be limited when dealing with topologically non-trivial textures like skyrmions where signatures are highly ambiguous.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1 shows the NV setup and the ill-posed nature of the problem; Fig 2 displays SEM, MFM, and NV field maps; Fig 3 details the CAE architecture and reconstruction epochs; Fig 4 shows MSE and SNR performance relative to mask orientation and standoff distance.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the inverse problem, describes the experimental NV platform and Permalloy samples, details the DIP neural network architecture and physics-informed loss, presents results for Landau and dipole domains, and discusses the impact of standoff distance and masking.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Reconstructing magnetization in nanoscale magnetic thin films is essential for developing next-generation memory, sensors, and various spintronic technologies. However, this remains challenging due to the ill-posed nature of the stray field inverse problem, i.e., there are infinitely many magnetization solutions to a given stray field distribution. Here, we demonstrate that a physics-informed deep image prior (DIP) framework, using a simple convolutional autoencoder conditionally achieves a reasonable qualitative and quantitative reconstruction of complex in-plane magnetization patterns from scanning NV magnetometry. We find that the orientation of user-defined masks implemented to restrict the reconstruction solution space dramatically affects convergence. The optimal alignment of the mask improves the reconstruction signal-to-noise ratio by up to $\SI{3}{\decibel}$, thereby also serving as a diagnostic tool. The DIP approach requires no pre-trained datasets and is considered computationally less intensive as compared to supervised learning approaches. We analyze both Landau and dipole domain structures in lithographically patterned Permalloy nanostructures by incorporating experimentally-guided spatial constraints. Complementary magnetic force microscopy measurements were carried out to support the Scanning NV measurements.

</details>

**Main problem.** Understanding the dynamics of singlet-triplet oscillations in Si/SiGe double quantum dots, specifically how spin-valley coupling and valley-splitting fluctuations affect qubit initialization and dephasing.

**Main result.** The study provides a theoretical framework for singlet return probability that accounts for multiple valley occupation patterns and demonstrates that valley-splitting fluctuations from electric field noise are a dominant dephasing mechanism near spin-valley resonances.

**Method.** The authors use a multi-dimensional Hamiltonian approach (up to 17-dimensional) and Landau-Zener theory to model charge separation, comparing theoretical predictions of frequency renormalization and g-factor symmetry against experimental Fourier-transformed singlet return probability signals.

**Summary.** This paper investigates how spin-valley coupling and valley-dependent g-factors influence the dynamics of singlet-triplet oscillations in silicon double quantum dots. By modeling the charge separation process from (4,0) to (3,1) states, the authors show that spin-valley resonances significantly renormalize oscillation frequencies. They also identify that fluctuations in valley splitting, driven by electric field noise, are a primary source of dephasing. The theoretical model is validated by comparing it to experimental measurements in Si/SiGe heterostructures, providing insight into the valley-dependent nature of g-factors.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The system consists of two electrons in Si/SiGe or SiMOS double quantum dots (DQDs) transitioning between (4,0) and (3,1) charge states, modeled by a Hamiltonian including Zeeman splitting, valley splitting, and spin-valley coupling.

**Key observables.** Singlet return probability (Ps), singlet-triplet oscillation frequencies, and g-factor differences (delta g).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Valley splitting (Ev), Zeeman splitting (Ez), spin-valley coupling (v), tunnel coupling (tc), and charge noise-induced fluctuations (sigma_epsilon).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The model assumes the lowest energy orbital states and the two lowest energy valley eigenstates per dot, and treats valley coupling components as uncorrelated Gaussian random variables.

**Figures summary.** Figures show Fourier transforms of singlet return probability for various datasets, illustrating multiple frequency components and the presence of different valley occupation patterns.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of spin-valley dynamics, presents a theoretical model for charge separation and singlet-triplet mixing, applies this model to analyze spin-valley resonances, compares results with experimental data from Si/SiGe heterostructures, and discusses dephasing mechanisms.

**Why it may be interesting.** The paper explores the interplay between spin and valley degrees of freedom and the impact of environmental noise on quantum coherence, which is highly relevant to the study of open quantum systems and the development of robust spin qubits.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Charge separation from the $(4,0)$ to the $(3,1)$ state in a Si/SiGe double quantum dot is commonly used for initialization of spin qubits and Pauli-spin-blockade readout. It was used in recent experiments involving creation of the $(3,1)$ singlet, and subsequent shuttling of one of the electrons. We present a theoretical description of the process of charge separation and singlet-triplet mixing, arriving at expressions for the singlet return probability that take into account experimentally observed finite probabilities of the creation of singlets with various patterns of valley occupations. In our analysis we focus on magnetic fields for which the electron spin Zeeman splitting is close to the valley splitting in one of the dots, when the spin-valley coupling causes a strong renormalization of the frequency of oscillations of singlet return probability. The latter effect has been recently used to perform valley splitting mapping by shuttling of one quantum dot to various locations with respect to the other. We give a detailed description of singlet-triplet dynamics near these spin-valley resonances and compare the results of calculations with measurements on double quantum dots in two distinct Si/SiGe heterostructures. Comparison of theory with experiments in which the presence of a few valley occupation patterns is visible, gives insight into the valley dependence of $g$-factors in these structures, providing support for a recently proposed theoretical model of this dependence. We also discuss how dephasing of singlet return probability oscillations near the spin-valley resonances is affected by valley splitting fluctuations caused by electric field noise.

</details>

### [Witnessing entanglement between photon and matter due to graviton exchange](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.24496v1)

**Authors:** Arijit Dutta, Marko Toroš, Sougato Bose, Anupam Mazumdar

**Main problem.** Developing an experimental scheme and an entanglement witness to detect quantum correlations between a photon and a massive particle induced by graviton exchange.

**Main result.** The authors propose a PPT-based witness using Stokes parameters that can effectively detect entanglement in the regime 0.71 <= |gamma| < 1, with a maximum negativity of -0.052.

**Method.** The study employs the Peres-Horodecki (PPT) criterion to derive an observable witness, mapping effective Pauli operators to physical Stokes operators and using a local oscillator for phase-sensitive measurement.

**Summary.** This paper proposes a method to witness entanglement between a photon and a massive particle mediated by the exchange of virtual gravitons. By using a high-intensity laser and a massive particle in spatial superposition, the authors derive an entanglement witness based on Stokes parameters. The study quantifies the effectiveness of this witness and determines the limits of its robustness against isotropic noise. This provides a potential experimental pathway to test the quantum nature of gravity in a laboratory setting.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** A bipartite system consisting of a massive particle (spin qubit) in a spatial superposition and an optical field in a strong coherent state, interacting via a perturbative framework of massless spin-2 graviton exchange.

**Key observables.** Stokes parameters (S0, S1, S2, S3), photon-number difference, and the overlap parameter |gamma|.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Mass m (0.1 to 10 kg), spatial separation (up to 100 um), photon intensity (|alpha|^2 = 10^13), and interaction time (1 s).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The interaction is modeled via a perturbative quantum field theoretic framework; the system is subject to isotropic noise; the particle is treated as an effective qubit in a two-dimensional subspace.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the interaction schematic; Table I displays the overlap |gamma| as a function of mass and separation; Figure 2 plots overlap vs. spatial separation; Figure 3 shows the witness value vs. |gamma|; Figure 4 illustrates the relationship between |gamma| and the critical noise threshold.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of detecting gravity-induced entanglement, describes the physical model and interaction Hamiltonian, derives the entanglement witness using the PPT criterion, proposes an experimental measurement strategy using Stokes parameters and a local oscillator, and concludes with a noise robustness analysis.

**Why it may be interesting.** It bridges quantum optics and quantum gravity by proposing a concrete, measurable signature (entanglement witness) for the spin-2 nature of gravitons using macroscopic optical observables.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

The paper presents a scheme to detect entanglement arising from the quantum nature of gravity between a spin qubit and photons, using Stokes parameters. One of the crucial tests of the general theory of relativity is the bending of light due to the curvature. Recently, a quantum counterpart of this experiment to test the quantum nature of the gravitational interaction has been proposed, in which the spin-2, massless graviton yields entanglement between matter and a photon sector. Hence, it provides one of the most crucial experimental signatures for testing the quantum nature of gravity in a lab, since only spin-2-induced entanglement can yield the correct deflection of light due to matter. Here, we propose a positive partial-transpose (PPT) witness criterion for witnessing such an entanglement. We scan the entangled states in this context by studying the overlap of the final state, which is proportional to the entanglement phase. We exploit the Stokes observables to measure the photon state and the spins in the matter sector, thereby constructing a witness for the quantum nature of gravity in this setup. To quantify this entanglement, we will couple the photon to a local oscillator, whose phase need to be controlled to probe the orthogonal components of the macroscopic interference in the laser beam. We have shown that for a non-maximally entangled state mediated by the quantum nature of gravity, the witness attains a maximal negativity of $-0.052$. Our findings indicate that this witness effectively detects entanglement within the range $0.71 \leq |γ| < 1$, where $γ$ is the overlap between the two coherent states of the photon, providing a clear signature of quantum correlations.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

<a id="paper-2604.24511"></a>
### [Deterministic Nucleation and Dynamics of Infilled Multiply-Charged Vortices in an Immiscible $^{87}\mathrm{Rb}$-$^{41}\mathrm{K}$ Mixture](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.24511v1)

**Authors:** R. Doran, K. E. Wilson

**Main problem.** Developing a method for the deterministic generation and studying the stability and dynamics of multiply-charged vortices in an immiscible binary Bose-Einstein condensate.

**Main result.** The authors demonstrate that a spiral stirring technique can reproducibly tune vortex charge and that the presence of an infilling component can stabilize high-charge vortices, though distinct dynamical instabilities (core elongation and node development) occur at high winding numbers.

**Method.** Numerical evolution of coupled 2D Gross-Pitaevskii equations using the xmds2 code with an adaptive Runge-Kutta method, supplemented by a massive point-vortex model for predicting precession.

**Summary.** This paper presents a method to controllably create multiply-charged vortices in a mixture of 87Rb and 41K Bose-Einstein condensates. By using a spiral laser stirring technique, the researchers show they can deterministically set the vortex charge. They find that the second component fills the vortex cores, which helps stabilize high-charge vortices against splitting. The study also characterizes the resulting dynamics, including precession and shape oscillations, and identifies specific ways these vortices eventually become unstable.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** An immiscible mixture of 87Rb and 41K atoms in a quasi-2D, hard-walled circular trap, modeled by coupled Gross-Pitaevskii equations including intra- and inter-species interaction strengths.

**Key observables.** Vortex winding number (charge q), density profiles of both components, phase winding, precession frequency, and the aspect ratio (shape oscillations) of the infilling component.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Mass ratio (approx 2.12), interaction strength ratios (alpha and beta), vortex charge (q), stirring parameters (beam width, number of spirals, and stirring time), and atom number ratio.

**Assumptions / limitations.** Zero-temperature limit and a quasi-2D approximation via strong harmonic trapping in the z-direction.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the evolution of density and phase during the spiral stirring process; Figure 2 relates phase winding to stirring parameters; Figure 4 shows vortex precession and its linear dependence on charge; Figure 5 shows the temporal evolution and frequency of infilling component shape oscillations; Figure 6 illustrates the mechanism of vortex dislocation for high charges.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of vortex generation, describes the numerical setup and stirring technique, presents results on deterministic charge tuning, analyzes the stability and dynamics (precession and breathing modes) of the vortices, and concludes with an investigation of specific instability mechanisms.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work is highly relevant for many-body dynamics and AMO physics as it explores how a second species can be used to control and stabilize topological defects, providing a platform for studying complex superfluid dynamics and symmetry-breaking instabilities.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We propose a method for controllably generating multiply-charged vortices in immiscible Bose-Einstein condensates. We achieve this by applying a laser stirring technique to a $^{87}\mathrm{Rb}$-$^{41}\mathrm{K}$ mixture, where the vortices generated are infilled by the secondary component. We numerically demonstrate that the charge of the vortex can be tuned reproducibly by varying the stirring parameters, allowing the deterministic generation of stable infilled vortices with high topological charge. We then consider the dynamics of these multiply-charged vortices in a circular trap; in contrast to single-component condensates, we observe long-lived precession of the multiply-charged vortices with a charge dependent frequency and collective breathing modes of the infilling component. For sufficiently large winding numbers, we observe distinct dynamical instabilities leading to vortex dislocation.

</details>

**Main problem.** Investigating the competition between attractive spin-spin interactions and spin-dependent external forces in a 1D Fermi-Hubbard model, specifically how a linear potential gradient induces the successive breaking of fermion pairs.

**Main result.** The study identifies three regimes: robust pairing, a phase-separated regime with a staircase-like decrease in doublon number due to one-by-one pair breaking, and complete spin separation. The authors also provide analytical estimates for the critical threshold gradients.

**Method.** The researchers use Density-Matrix Renormalization Group (DMRG) simulations, supplemented by a phenomenological model and Local-Density Approximation (LDA) analysis to derive closed-form analytical estimates.

**Summary.** This paper explores how a spin-dependent linear potential affects the stability of fermion pairs in a 1D attractive Hubbard model. It reveals that increasing the potential gradient leads to a discrete, 'staircase' breaking of pairs, transitioning from a paired state to a fully spin-separated state. The authors provide analytical tools to predict these transitions and show that the effect is robust even under harmonic confinement. These findings offer a way to control the number of bound pairs in experimental cold-atom setups.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** A one-dimensional Fermi-Hubbard model with attractive on-site interactions (U < 0) and open boundary conditions, subject to a spin-dependent linear potential gradient (beta) that pushes different spin species toward opposite ends of the lattice.

**Key observables.** Doublon number (number of doubly occupied sites), spin-resolved density distributions, local relative spin polarization, and critical threshold gradients (beta_c1 and beta_c2).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Potential gradient strength (beta), interaction strength (U), lattice size (L), filling fraction/density (n), and harmonic confinement strength (w).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The system is focused on balanced spin populations (N_up = N_down); the staircase structure is intrinsically tied to finite system size and open boundaries; the LDA is unable to capture the discrete stepwise descent in the number of pairs.

**Figures summary.** Figures show the dependence of the doublon number on beta for various U values, the evolution of critical thresholds (beta_c1, beta_c2) as functions of U and density, and a comparison between DMRG and LDA predictions for density profiles and phase boundaries.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by defining the many-body Hamiltonian and symmetry properties, moves to single-particle spectrum analysis and exact diagonalization, presents large-scale DMRG results for density and pair-breaking, and concludes with analytical modeling and LDA-based threshold estimations.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work is highly relevant for many-body dynamics and AMO physics as it demonstrates a method for precise, integer-level control of bound fermion pairs using spin-dependent potentials, a technique directly testable in cold-atom experiments.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We investigate the competition between attractive spin-spin interactions and spin-separating external forces in the ground state of a one-dimensional Fermi-Hubbard model. We consider a lattice with open boundary conditions, subject to a linear external potential whose gradient is opposite for the two spin components, so that each spin species sees a potential minimum at a different end of the lattice. Using density-matrix renormalization group (DMRG) simulations, we map the ground-state density distributions and the number of doubly occupied sites as a function of the potential gradient $β$ and interaction strength. We identify three distinct regimes separated by critical threshold gradients: (i) a small-$β$ regime where fermion pairing remains robust against the external potential; (ii) an intermediate-$β$ phase-separated regime characterized by a staircase-like decrease in the doublon number, corresponding to the successive, one-by-one breaking of bound pairs; and (iii) a large-$β$ regime where the two spin components are completely spatially separated. We complement the numerical results with a phenomenological model and a local-density approximation analysis, from which we derive closed-form analytical estimates for these critical threshold values. We also verify that the staircase structure persists under additional harmonic confinement. Our results are directly testable in cold-atom experiments, and demonstrate that a spin-dependent linear potential enables precise, integer-level control of the number of bound fermion pairs.

</details>

### [Noise spectroscopy of insulating and itinerant altermagnets](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23297v1)

**Authors:** Lucas V. Pupim, Mathias S. Scheurer  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.23297v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `spintronics-quantum-optics interface` **3/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">

<img src="2604.23297_figures/2604.23297_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> FIG. 1: (a) Schematic checkerboard lattice and its couplings in the insulating (Heisenberg model) and metallic (tight- binding) regime. In the insulating regime, magnonic exci- tations generate a fluctuating magnetic field B(t), while in the conducting regime, current fluctuations cause these mag- netic oscillations. The magnetic noise, in turn, is detected as shown in (b). The fluctuating magnetic field couples to the qubit and introduces noise into the coherence factor φ(t). This noise, in turn, is detected as a decoherence rate 1/T2. Alternatively, one can start the qubit in the state |1⟩and see the effect of magnetic noise in the relaxation rate 1/T1.</figcaption>
</figure>

</figure>

The qubit axis lies in the sample’s plane (θ = π/2) and we vary the azimuthal angle (ϕ). In (a) we use d = 2, while in (b) we use d = 10. For larger distances (d ≫a), we note that the noise tends to becoming isotropic, while for shorter distances (d ∼a), N∥(ω) is suppressed when the qubit is aligned with the magnetic order (ϕ = 0◦, 180◦). In (c) we show that the relative anisotropy δN∥decays as d increases, eventually showing the continuum limit, where the noise is isotropic. For all plots, we use T = 1, ω = 0.25 and h = 0.5.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23297_figures/2604.23297_fig5.jpg" alt="Figure 5">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> FIG. 5: (a) Berry curvature for the AM. The solid and dashed lines show the corresponding Fermi surfaces. (b) Imaginary part of the Hall conductance as a function of axial strain (see text). Here, we used the Poisson ration ν = 0.25. (c) Spatial profile of the (normalized) effective potential due to a domain wall. (d) Imaginary part of the Hall conductance at different positions (angles) of the domain wall for a qubit at kF d = 100. We used a kF W = 10 and R/W = 25. In all plots, we considered µ = 0.3, δt2 = 0.3, αR = 0.02 and ωq = 10−4. Note that the relevant contribution to the decoherence difference δΓ comes from momenta q with |q| = 1/2d.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23297_figures/2604.23297_fig6.jpg" alt="Figure 6">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 6.</b> FIG. 6: δΓ for an AM (η = 1/ √</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** Developing unambiguous experimental methods to distinguish altermagnetic (AM) order from antiferromagnetic (AFM) order and identifying tools to access the orbital character of the magnetic order parameter.

**Main result.** Noise spectroscopy, particularly through charge fluctuations in itinerant altermagnets, provides unique symmetry-sensitive signatures (such as finite relaxation rate differences under strain) that distinguish AM from AFM. Additionally, the angular dependence of noise around domain walls can reveal the orbital character (d-wave vs. g-wave) of the order parameter.

**Method.** The study employs Linear Spin Wave Theory (LSWT) for the insulating regime and a tight-binding model for the itinerant regime, utilizing the Holstein-Primakoff transformation, Bogoliability transformations (Colpa method), and the Kubo formula to calculate magnetic noise tensors and qubit decoherence rates.

**Summary.** This paper proposes using noise magnetometry to experimentally identify altermagnetism. By analyzing the magnetic noise spectra of both insulating and metallic altermagnets, the authors show that charge fluctuations in itinerant systems provide the most striking signatures to distinguish altermagnets from antiferromagnets. They demonstrate that symmetry-breaking perturbations, such as strain or domain walls, induce unique, measurable changes in qubit relaxation and dephasing rates. This work provides a roadmap for using quantum sensors like NV centers to characterize the orbital symmetry of new magnetic materials.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The paper investigates both insulating altermagnets (modeled via a Heisenberg Hamiltonian on a checkerboard lattice) and itinerant altermagnets (modeled via an electronic tight-binding model with spin-orbit coupling). The experimental probe is modeled as a qubit (e.g., an NV center) sensitive to magnetic field fluctuations.

**Key observables.** Qubit relaxation rate (1/T1), dephasing rate (1/T2), magnetic noise tensor components (N_ab), relaxation rate difference (delta_Gamma), and Hall conductivity (sigma_H).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Zeeman field (h), temperature (T), distance between probe and sample (d), lattice spacing (a), and strain parameters (epsilon).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The study assumes the continuum limit (d >> a) for certain spatial analyses, uses the 1/S expansion in the spin-wave theory, and neglects the momentum dependence of screening and non-coplanarity in domain wall textures.

**Figures summary.** Figure 2 compares magnon modes in AFM and AM; Figure 3 shows parallel noise components and their dependence on distance d; Figure 6 illustrates the difference in relaxation rates.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by defining the problem of AM/AFM distinction, then details the insulating regime using spin-wave theory, moves to the itinerant regime focusing on charge fluctuations and symmetry breaking (strain/domain walls), and concludes with technical discussions on lattice effects and the dipole tensor.

**Why it may be interesting.** The paper explores how local magnetic symmetries and topological features (like Berry curvature) manifest in the noise spectrum of a quantum probe, which is highly relevant to the study of open quantum systems and the characterization of novel many-body phases.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

One of the central goals in the emergent field of altermagnetism is the unambiguous experimental identification and characterization of altermagnetic order across a variety of compounds. This motivates exploring tools that can clearly distinguish altermagnets from antiferromagnets, based on symmetry signatures, and offer access to the dominant orbital character (e.g., $d$-wave vs. $g$-wave) of the magnetic order parameter. In this work, we theoretically explore the potential of noise magnetometry for this task, studying contributions from both magnons and itinerant electrons in different regimes and scenarios. While altermagnetism and antiferromagnetism also lead to different noise spectra for magnons, we find the most striking and symmetry-sensitive signatures in the charge fluctuations of itinerant altermagnets. Both for the homogeneous bulk case and in the presence of strain and/or around domain walls, we identify noise contributions that are only permitted by symmetry in the altermagnet and, thus, provide a unique signature of altermagnetism. Furthermore, the angular dependence of noise around domain walls also offers access to the orbital character of the altermagnet. On a more technical note, we discuss the role and relevance of lattice effects related to the dipole tensor. We hope that our work will help pave the way towards the clear experimental identification of altermagnetism across a wide range of candidate materials.

</details>

**Main problem.** Determining whether all NPT Bell-diagonal qutrit states are 1-distillable and constructing a noise-robust 1-copy distillation protocol for them.

**Main result.** The authors prove that for Bell-diagonal qutrit states with Weyl structure, violating the PPT criterion is both necessary and sufficient for 1-distillability, and they provide a noise-resilient distillation scheme.

**Method.** The study uses a constructive proof involving the partial transpose and the construction of a Schmidt rank 2 witness vector based on the eigenvectors of the partially transposed density matrix.

**Summary.** This paper solves the distillability problem for a specific class of Bell-diagonal qutrit states, proving that any state violating the PPT criterion can be distilled using a single copy. The authors develop a new distillation protocol that uses local filters to transform qutrit states into qubit states. This protocol is specifically designed to be resilient to white noise by utilizing the properties of the partially transposed density matrix's eigenvectors. These findings make noisy, high-dimensional entangled states more useful for practical quantum technologies.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** Bipartite qutrit-qutrit systems (d=3) with Weyl structure, specifically Bell-diagonal states subject to white-noise-affected models.

**Key observables.** The expectation value of the partial transpose and the smallest eigenvalue of the partially transposed matrix.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The noise parameter p, the Schmidt rank of the witness vector, and the success probability q of the filtering process.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The results are specifically applicable to Bell-diagonal qutrit states with Weyl structure and do not extend to general dimensions d > 3.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the distillability problem, defines the Weyl-structure qutrit model, provides a constructive proof for the sufficiency of PPT violation, details the construction of the noise-robust witness and protocol, and discusses limitations regarding higher dimensions.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides a fundamental solution to a specific case of the distillability problem and offers a practical method for enhancing the robustness of entanglement against white noise, which is critical for quantum communication and networking.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Entanglement distillation is the process of converting noisy entangled states into maximally entangled pure states via local operations and classical communication. A long-standing, unresolved question is which entangled states are amenable to distillation, known as the distillability problem. We solve this for Bell-diagonal qutrits with Weyl structure, and present a noise-robust scheme for entanglement distillation. In particular, we find that violating the positive partial transposition (PPT) criterion is necessary and sufficient for the 1-distillability of these states. For this, we construct a Schmidt rank 2 eigenvector of the partially transposed density matrix associated with its unique, three-fold degenerate negative eigenvalue. This feature makes the derived entanglement distillation protocol resilient to white-noise effects on the quantum states. Our results thus make noisy entangled qutrit pairs more accessible for future quantum technologies.

</details>

### [Quantum Sufficiency for Self-Adjoint Statistical Models via Likelihood-Type Operators on Real $*$-Subalgebras and Real Jordan Algebras](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23292v1)

**Authors:** Koichi Yamagata  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.23292v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `quantum measurements` **3/5**

**Main problem.** The existing theory of quantum sufficiency is limited to complex *-subalgebras and requires faithful reference states, creating a gap when dealing with local quantum statistical structures like SLDs.

**Main result.** The paper develops a unified theory of quantum sufficiency using real *-subalgebras and real Jordan algebras, allowing for degenerate reference states and the inclusion of self-adjoint operators like SLDs.

**Method.** The author employs algebraic decomposition of real Jordan algebras, operator theory involving rho-modular invariance, and the study of real-linear positive maps and conditional expectations.

**Summary.** This paper generalizes the theory of quantum sufficiency from the standard complex *-algebraic setting to the more flexible framework of real *-subalgebras and real Jordan algebras. By doing so, it allows for the treatment of statistical models that include non-state self-adjoint operators, such as symmetric logarithmic derivatives, and accommodates reference states that are not strictly positive. The author establishes new characterizations for minimal sufficient algebras and provides a generalized Koashi-Imoto decomposition. Ultimately, the work suggests that real Jordan structures are the natural language for the statistical aspects of quantum theory.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The framework considers general self-adjoint statistical models on finite-dimensional Hilbert spaces, including both absolutely continuous models and local models (e.g., derivatives of states).

**Key observables.** Square-root likelihood ratios and symmetric logarithmic derivatives (SLDs).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Finite-dimensional Hilbert space; the distinction between strictly positive and degenerate reference states.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The framework is presented for finite-dimensional Hilbert spaces and assumes the existence of a supporting operator.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces a new framework for sufficiency, establishes a hierarchy of sufficiency (complex, real, and Jordan), characterizes minimal sufficient algebras via likelihood-type objects and modular invariance, provides Koashi-Imlamo type decompositions, and derives bounds for POVM support size.

**Why it may be interesting.** This is highly relevant for quantum estimation and quantum Fisher information, as it provides a more robust mathematical foundation for handling the local statistical structures (SLDs) used in quantum metrology and asymptotic quantum statistics.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We develop a theory of quantum sufficiency on real *-subalgebras and real Jordan algebras. In contrast to the conventional formulation, which is based on families of states, complex completely positive coarse-grainings, and Radon-Nikodym cocycles associated with faithful reference states, our framework allows models consisting of general self-adjoint operators, including derivatives of states. Within this framework, square-root likelihood ratios and symmetric logarithmic derivatives arise naturally as fundamental self-adjoint likelihood-type objects. This makes it possible to treat ordinary quantum statistical models and local quantum statistical structures within a unified setting.   We introduce sufficient real positive maps and show that sufficient complex *-subalgebras, sufficient real *-subalgebras, and sufficient real Jordan algebras correspond respectively to complex completely positive maps, real completely positive maps, and real positive maps. We characterize minimal sufficient real *-subalgebras by the likelihood-ratio set together with rho-modular invariance, and show that the real Jordan algebra generated by the likelihood-ratio set and the projected reference state is the minimal sufficient real Jordan algebra. We also obtain Koashi-Imoto type decompositions for sufficient real *-subalgebras and sufficient real Jordan algebras. Our formulation admits degenerate reference states and separates the likelihood-ratio aspect of sufficiency from its genuinely quantum modular aspect. These results suggest that real Jordan structure provides a natural framework for the statistical aspect of quantum theory beyond the conventional complex *-algebraic setting.

</details>

### [Principles of relativistic quantum statistical thermodynamics: a class of exactly solvable models](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23409v1)

**Authors:** A. Yu. Zakharov  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.23409v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `Keldysh / 2PI / non-Gaussian methods` **2/5** · `correlated / nonlocal dissipation` **2/5** · `driven-dissipative phase transition` **2/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **2/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23409_figures/2604.23409_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">

</figure>

<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Figure 2: Graph of the function f(τs) in the low-temperature region (τs ≪1) in the case of a weak atom-field coupling constant (αs = 0.005 ≪1).</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23409_figures/2604.23409_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> Figure 3: Qualitative form of functions f2 (αs, τ) for some particular values of αs</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** The paper addresses the lack of a microscopic mechanism for irreversibility in classical mechanics and the difficulty of finding exactly solvable 3D models for the relativistic quantum statistical thermodynamics of interacting atoms.

**Main result.** The author establishes a relativistic framework where interatomic interactions are represented by an auxiliary field, demonstrating that quantization resolves the classical ultraviolet catastrophe and that a phase transition occurs at a critical temperature.

**Method.** The study uses a relativistic field theory approach, employing Fourier transformations, Legendre transformations, and the quadratic approximation of the Hamiltonian to reduce the complex partition function to a solvable form.

**Summary.** This paper develops a theory for the relativistic quantum statistical thermodynamics of interacting atoms. By representing interatomic potentials as an auxiliary scalar field, the author transforms a complex many-body problem into an exactly solvable model of an ideal gas of quasiparticles. The work demonstrates that quantizing this auxiliary field prevents the classical ultraviolet catastrophe and proves the existence of a phase transition at a specific critical temperature.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** A relativistic system consisting of two subsystems: a collection of interacting atoms and an auxiliary scalar covariant field (a superposition of Klein-Gordon fields) that mediates the interatomic potential.

**Key observables.** Total energy, average energy of the field modes, heat capacity, and the dispersion law of the effective field.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Critical temperature (T_crit), particle density (n), field mass parameters (mu_s), coupling constants (gamma_s, kappa_s), and the high-temperature/low-temperature regimes.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The model assumes atoms do not undergo destruction (valid for v << c), the interatomic potential is representable via simple poles in its Fourier transform, and the field is treated as a superposition of Klein-Gordon fields.

**Figures summary.** The figures show the domain of definition and behavior of a function f(T/T_s) for different coupling parameters and its behavior in the low-temperature region.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by defining the foundational problem of irreversibility, introduces the relativistic atom-field model and its Hamiltonian, applies mathematical transformations to solve the partition function, analyzes the thermodynamic properties (energy, heat capacity), and concludes with the identification of a phase transition.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a rare example of an exactly solvable 3D relativistic many-body model and offers a physical mechanism for irreversibility through field-induced 'lag' in interactions, which is relevant to the study of open quantum systems and many-body dynamics.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

A system of interacting atoms is represented as an union of two subsystems, one of which is the system of atoms, and the other is an auxiliary scalar covariant field, which is equivalent to a given static interatomic potential of general form only in the non-relativistic approximation. It is shown that the auxiliary field is a superposition of Klein-Gordon fields, the parameters of which are related to singular points of the Fourier transform of the corresponding interatomic potential. The general form of the relativistic Hamiltonian system of interacting atoms is established. It is shown that the exact calculation of the relativistic partition function of a system of interacting atoms, taking into account the field degrees of freedom, reduces to renormalizing the parameters of the auxiliary field. It is established that the field degrees of freedom lead to a divergence in the total energy of a classical relativistic system - an analogue of the ultraviolet catastrophe. Quantization of the auxiliary field eliminates this divergence. The existence of a phase transition within the framework of relativistic quantum statistical thermodynamics has been proven.

</details>

### [Catalytic Enhancement of Coherence Fraction in Noisy Quantum Channels and Characterization of Strictly Incoherent Operations](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.24291v1)

**Authors:** Priyabrata Char, Dipayan Chakraborty, Indrani Chattopadhyay, Debasis Sarkar  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.24291v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `correlated / nonlocal dissipation` **2/5** · `entanglement & information structure` **2/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **2/5** · `quantum measurements` **1/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>

The incoherent state σ = diag(0.3, 0.7) is non-degenerate. The SIO E is not of Schur-multiplier form. The nonzero deviations confirm viola- tion of the multiplicative relation.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** Investigating whether quantum catalysis can be used to pre-process input states to enhance the coherence fraction of an output state after passing through a noisy quantum channel, and characterizing the structural properties of Strictly Incoherent Operations (SIO).

**Main result.** The authors prove that correlated catalysis can prevent the degradation of the coherence fraction in noisy channels and establish a necessary and sufficient condition for a CPTP map to be a Schur-multiplier type SIO.

**Method.** The study uses the framework of Quantum Resource Theory (QRT) of coherence, employing analytical proofs, algebraic derivations (such as polynomial interpolation), and numerical simulations using the Ginibre ensemble.

**Summary.** This paper explores how quantum catalysis can be used to protect or even enhance the coherence fraction of a quantum state when it passes through a noisy channel. By pre-processing the input state using an auxiliary catalyst, the authors show that the output coherence can be higher than if the original state were used. Additionally, the work provides a new structural characterization of a specific class of incoherent operations. These findings offer practical insights for optimizing quantum protocols in realistic, noisy environments.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The work focuses on single-party d-dimensional quantum systems subject to noisy CPTP channels, specifically including the quantum addition channel and the qutrit phase-damping channel.

**Key observables.** Coherence fraction (CF), l1 norm of coherence, robustness of coherence, and success probability in phase discrimination tasks.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Noise parameter (p) for dephasing channels, the parameter (alpha) for the quantum addition channel, and the regularized coherence fraction.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes the use of the coherence fraction as the primary figure of merit and focuses on the regime of noisy channels that reduce coherence.

**Figures summary.** Figures 1-3 compare the coherence fraction of standard versus processed states under different noise levels; Figure 4 is a scatter plot showing the Frobenius norm deviation to demonstrate that the multiplicative property fails for SIOs that are not Schur multipliers.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of coherence degradation, defines the catalytic scheme and operational classes (MIO, IO, SIO), provides analytical theorems for coherence enhancement, applies the results to phase discrimination, characterizes the SIO class via a multiplicative condition, and concludes with numerical validation.

**Why it may be interesting.** This paper is highly relevant to open quantum systems and quantum optics as it provides a theoretical method (catalysis) to mitigate the effects of environmental noise on a key quantum resource (coherence).

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

In realistic quantum information processing tasks, quantum states are inevitably affected by environmental noise, leading to decoherence and degradation of useful quantum resources. The coherence fraction, which serves as an important figure of merit for several quantum protocols, may decrease significantly after the action of a noisy channel. Such degradation can result in unsatisfactory performance in real-world applications. In this work, we investigate whether catalysis can be used to pre-process the input state to enhance the coherence fraction of an output state from a quantum channel. Specifically, we study whether using a processed state $ρ_s'$ as the input to a quantum channel $Λ$, instead of the original state $ρ_s$, can yield an output state $Λ(ρ_s')$ whose coherence fraction exceeds that of $Λ(ρ_s)$. We analyze the conditions under which such an improvement is possible. We also provide a practical application of our setup for the phase discrimination task. Furthermore, we establish a necessary and sufficient condition for an incoherent state preserving CPTP(Completely Positive Trace Preserving) map $\mathcal{E}$ to be a particular type of Strictly Incoherent Operation (SIO). This characterization provides a new structural understanding of SIO and clarifies its role in coherence manipulation. Our results offer practical insights into coherence preservation and enhancement in noisy quantum processes and may be useful for optimizing quantum information protocols under realistic conditions. We also provide numerical examples to support our claims.

</details>

<a id="paper-2604.23857"></a>
### [Dissipative Vortex Binaries in Compact Fluid Domains with Geometric Corrections](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23857v1)

**Authors:** Aswathy K. R., Rickmoy Samanta  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum gases · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.23857v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `correlated / nonlocal dissipation` **2/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **2/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **2/5** · `analog quantum simulation` **1/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23857_figures/2604.23857_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> FIG. 3: Numerical verification of the dissipative dipole dynamics (Γ1 = 1, Γ2 = −1) on a flat torus. (a) Separation R(t) showing agreement between numerics (solid), planar theory (dashed), and torus-corrected solution (dotted). (b) Orientation θ(t), where the planar theory predicts no evolution, while the torus correction captures the observed drift. (c,d) Radial and angular errors. The torus-corrected solution reduces the error by several orders of magnitude compared to the planar approximation. Sharp minima correspond to zero-crossings of the error and do not indicate improved accuracy.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23857_figures/2604.23857_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> FIG. 4: Numerical verification of the dissipative same-sign vortex pair (Γ1 = Γ2 = 1). (a) Separation R(t) showing outward growth and improved agreement of the torus-corrected solution with numerics. (b) Combined radial and angular error comparison. The torus-corrected theory significantly reduces both errors relative to the planar logarithmic-spiral solution. The oscillatory structure reflects the phase-dependent corrections along the trajectory, while the overall reduction confirms the importance of toroidal geometry. 37</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** The study investigates how the combination of dissipation (mutual friction) and toroidal geometry (periodic boundary conditions) modifies the classical planar dynamics of vortex binaries.

**Main result.** The researchers derive torus-corrected analytic solutions that capture geometric effects like angular drift in dipoles and a specific nonlinear chirp (frequency blow-up) in unequal-sign pairs, significantly reducing errors compared to planar theory.

**Method.** The authors use a perturbative expansion of the interaction kernel via the Schottky-Klein prime function and q-special functions, comparing analytical results against full numerical simulations on a flat torus.

**Summary.** This paper explores how the periodic geometry of a torus and the presence of dissipation change the way vortex pairs move and interact. While planar models predict simple behaviors like fixed-orientation dipoles, the authors show that a torus induces an angular drift and a unique nonlinear frequency chirp in unequal-sign pairs. By using advanced mathematical tools like the Schottky-Klein prime function, they provide new analytical solutions that are much more accurate than standard planar approximations. These findings are particularly important for understanding vortex dynamics in finite-sized quantum fluids like Bose-Einstein condensates.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The system consists of two-dimensional incompressible, dissipative point-vortices in a doubly periodic fluid domain (flat torus), modeled by a mixed symplectic-gradient flow inspired by mutual friction in superfluids and BECs.

**Key observables.** Radial separation (r or R), angular orientation (theta), orbital rotation frequency (omega), and energy decay rate.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Circulation strengths (Gamma), dissipation coefficient (gamma), torus modulus/aspect ratio (rho), and the sum/difference of circulations (Sigma and Delta).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The fluid is incompressible, vortices are treated as point-like, and the analytical solutions are derived in the 'local regime' where vortex separation is small relative to the torus periods.

**Figures summary.** Figure 3 shows numerical verification for dipole dynamics, including plots of separation, orientation, and error reduction; Figure 4 shows numerical verification for same-sign pairs, demonstrating improved radial and angular error tracking.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the dissipative vortex model in a periodic domain, develops the mathematical framework using q-special functions, derives analytical solutions for different vortex configurations in the local regime, and concludes with numerical validation of the geometric corrections.

**Why it may be interesting.** This is highly relevant for researchers studying the dynamics of quantized vortices in BECs and superfluids, as it provides precise corrections to vortex motion when the system size is comparable to the vortex separation.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We study a dissipative extension of vortex-binary motion in a doubly periodic fluid domain. The underlying conservative system admits an exact integrable reduction to a single complex relative coordinate. Dissipation is introduced via a minimal rotated-velocity (mutual-friction) term, as motivated by finite-temperature superfluid dynamics, converting the Hamiltonian evolution into a mixed symplectic--gradient flow with monotonic energy decay for quantized vortices. In the local regime, the dissipative binary remains analytically solvable and admits closed-form solutions, with systematic corrections arising from the toroidal geometry. Equal same-sign vortices execute outward spiraling motion, while equal opposite-sign pairs (dipoles) undergo finite-time collapse in the planar limit. On the torus, however, the dipole orientation is no longer invariant: the geometry induces a slow angular drift, even in regimes where planar dynamics would preserve alignment. For unequal opposite-sign pairs, dissipation induces coupled contraction and rotation, leading to a finite-time nonlinear chirp characterized by $\dotω\proptoω^2$, in contrast with electromagnetic and gravitational inspirals where $\dotω\propto ω^{3}$ and $\dotω\propto ω^{11/3}$. These results highlight the interplay between Hamiltonian structure, dissipation, and geometry in periodic fluid systems.

</details>

<a id="paper-2604.23316"></a>
### [Complementarity between bosonic and fermionic many-body interferences with partially distinguishable particles](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23316v1)

**Authors:** Marco Robbio, Michael G. Jabbour, Nicolas J. Cerf  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.23316v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `analog quantum simulation` **2/5** · `entanglement & information structure` **2/5** · `interference shaping light` **2/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23316_figures/2604.23316_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> FIG. 1. Schematics of the case of a 50:50 beam-splitter with single particle inputs (red for bosons and blue for fermions) given an overlap between the degrees of freedom |⟨φ1|φ2⟩| = x. In this case, Theorem 1 can be written explicitly given the low number of possible combination. In particular, we would have: 1−x2</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23316_figures/2604.23316_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> FIG. 2. We consider a simple protocol where a set of particles (bosons, fermions, classical) interfere through a linear interferometer. The goal is to measure a quantity encoded by means of the phase shifter with phases ϕi. Any measurement can be performed on them.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates the fundamental relationship and complementarity between bosonic and fermionic many-body interference when particles are partially distinguishable.

**Main result.** The authors establish an exact algebraic identity relating bosonic and fermionic transition probabilities and prove a sum rule where the sum of their covariance matrices equals twice that of classical particles.

**Method.** The study employs multilinear algebra, generating functions, and the Khatri-Rao product, utilizing MacMahon's master theorem and Weingarten calculus for Haar random averages.

**Summary.** This paper establishes a formal mathematical complementarity between bosonic and fermionic interference in linear interferometers. It proves that even when particles are partially distinguishable, a fundamental sum rule holds between their correlation matrices and classical particles. The work reveals a metrological trade-off: increasing particle indistinguishability enhances bosonic phase sensitivity but can degrade fermionic sensitivity. Additionally, the findings provide new mathematical identities relating the permanent and determinant of tensors.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The system consists of bosons and fermions passing through an m-mode linear interferometer characterized by a unitary matrix U, where partial distinguishability is modeled via a Gram matrix S.

**Key observables.** Transition probabilities, particle number distributions, covariance matrices, and Quantum Fisher Information (QFI).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Degree of indistinguishability (Gram matrix S), unitary transformation U, and particle number (even vs. odd).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The interferometer is assumed to be linear (unitary), and internal degrees of freedom are modeled as pure states; the analysis focuses on thermal and Fock input states.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows a 50:50 beam-splitter illustrating bosonic bunching and fermionic antibunching; Figure 2 depicts a general protocol for phase estimation in a linear interferometer.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of bosonic-fermionic complementarity, develops a mathematical framework using generating functions and 3-tensors, proves fundamental theorems regarding transition probabilities and covariance matrices, and concludes with metrological implications and computational complexity considerations.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a deep mathematical link between permanents and determinants in the context of quantum statistics, offering new insights into the limits of quantum metrology and the simulation of Boson/Fermion sampling.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

It is well known that bosons and fermions exhibit opposite behaviors when experiencing interference, in the sense that bosons have a tendency to bunch whereas fermions have a tendency to antibunch. Recently, this complementarity was mathematically characterized in [arXiv:2312.17709] by means of an identity relating the transition probabilities of both types of particles in a linear interferometer. Here, we show that such a complementarity still holds even when particles become partially distinguishable, for example, when they have slightly different polarizations or time delays. Namely, we establish a relation that combines bosonic and fermionic multiparticle interferences in an arbitrary linear interferometer, in the presence of partial distinguishability. Incidentally, this also provides a new mathematical identity relating the permanent and determinant of tensors of order 3. Importantly, this complementarity has direct operational consequences in quantum metrology. Indeed, we show that the correlation matrices for bosonic and fermionic particle number distributions at the output of the interferometer obey a simple sum rule: their sum equals twice the correlation matrix for classical particles. This, in turn, constraints the achievable quantum Fisher information in phase-estimation protocols, highlighting a trade-off whereby greater indistinguishability enhances bosonic sensitivity whereas reduced indistinguishability can benefit fermionic schemes.

</details>

### [Diagnostic Disagreement as an Information-Projection Divergence: An Information-Theoretic Reading of the Quiet-Sun Temperature Ratio](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23752v1)

**Authors:** V. Edmonds

**Main problem.** Explaining the observed discrepancy between EUV and radio-derived electron temperatures in the quiet-Sun corona.

**Main result.** The temperature ratio is reinterpreted as a measure of relative entropy (Kullback-Leibler divergence) between two different Maxwellian projections of a non-equilibrium kappa-distribution.

**Method.** Information-theoretic analysis using I-projections, Bregman divergences, and the Itakura-Saito distance to link diagnostic kernels to entropy.

**Summary.** This paper provides an information-theoretic reading of the temperature discrepancy observed in the solar corona. It argues that the difference between EUV and radio temperatures is not an error, but a measurable consequence of projecting a non-equilibrium kappa-distribution onto different Maxwellian families. By establishing a mathematical link between the temperature ratio and the Itakura-Saito distance, the work shows that the observed 'gap' represents the relative entropy between different diagnostic projections. This framework provides a way to quantify the non-equilibrium nature of the plasma through standard observational diagnostics.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The quiet-Sun corona, modeled as a non-equilibrium plasma with an isotropic electron distribution following a kappa-distribution (D&D convention).

**Key observables.** Temperature ratio (R = Teuv/Tb), EUV line intensity ratios (Fe IX-XIII), and radio brightness temperature (150-450 MHz).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Kappa parameter (kappa = 2.5), effective temperature (Teff), and the Rayleigh-Jeans limit.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The electron distribution is isotropic; the radio diagnostic operates in the Rayleigh-Jeans limit; both diagnostics sample the same plasma volume.

**Figures summary.** Not specified.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the diagnostic discrepancy, establishes a theoretical framework using information projection, derives analytical identities linking the temperature gap to the Itakura-Saito distance, and performs numerical evaluations against solar observations.

**Why it may be interesting.** The use of information-theoretic projections (I-projections) and Bregman divergences to interpret discrepancies between different measurement kernels is highly relevant to the study of non-equilibrium steady states and open quantum systems.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

The quiet-Sun coronal electron-temperature ratio $R \equiv T_\mathrm{EUV}/T_B \approx 2.4$, stable across an eight-year solar cycle, is read here as a measurement of relative entropy between two diagnostic projections of the coronal electron distribution onto the one-parameter Maxwellian family. The EUV ionization temperature is a moment-matching projection against a Bethe-type ionization kernel; the radio brightness temperature is the Rayleigh-Jeans source function of thermal bremsstrahlung. For a kappa distribution in the mean-energy convention, Fleishman & Kuznetsov (2014) give the radio-side projection in closed form as $T_B = T_\mathrm{core}$; the EUV side returns $T_\mathrm{eff}$ up to a shape-dependent correction within the Dudík et al. (2014) intensity-ratio envelope. At $κ= 2.5$ the Kullback-Leibler divergences between the true distribution and its two Maxwellian projections evaluate to $0.32$ and $1.20$ nats, and their difference satisfies $ΔD_\mathrm{KL} = (3/2)[R_0 - \ln R_0 - 1] = (3/2) d_\mathrm{IS}(T_\mathrm{eff}, T_\mathrm{core})$, where $R_0 \equiv κ/(κ- 3/2)$ is the ideal closed-form ratio and $d_\mathrm{IS}$ is the Itakura-Saito distance. The identity is offered as an analytical reference for observational systems in which two diagnostics project different moments of a common non-equilibrium distribution; the eight-year stability of $R$ expresses a stability of that projection structure.

</details>

### [Nesting Controls Phase Transitions in Higher-Order Contagion](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23337v1)

**Authors:** Hugo P. Maia, Guilherme Ferraz de Arruda, Silvio C. Ferreira, Yamir Moreno  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.23337v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `driven-dissipative phase transition` **2/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **2/5** · `non-equilibrium universality` **2/5**

The coefficient represents the ratio between the number of 1st-order hyperedges and the maximum number of hyperedges that could be formed within the hyperedge of order m = 3. (b) Illustration of the rewiring mechanism used to control embedding. Two hyperedges of order m = 3 exchange one node (arrow), reducing the number of embedded pairwise interactions and yielding C(1) h = 2/3. Simulations are performed on networks with maximum order mmax = 5 (see also SM).</figcaption>

<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> FIG. 3: Phase diagrams of the higher-order SIS dynamics in the (β(1), b) parameter space for different levels of embedding (controlled by the rewiring parameter f and corresponding ⟨C⟩). Regions denote: I, absorbing phase (ρ = 0 globally stable); II, bistable regime; III, active phase (ρ &gt; 0 globally stable). (a) Networks with only triadic interactions. (b) Networks with maximum order mmax = 5. Both systems have comparable average pairwise degree ⟨k(1)⟩≈8. Simulations are performed on synthetic networks with N = 104 nodes (see also the SM).</figcaption>
