<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23337_figures/2604.23337_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> FIG. 4: Effect of order-dependent embedding on higher-order SIS dynamics. (a) Embedding patterns across interaction orders, shown for (i) negative, (ii) neutral, and (iii) positive correlations between embedding and order. Negative correlations correspond to stronger embedding at lower orders, while positive correlations correspond to stronger embedding at higher orders. (b) Stationary prevalence ρ as a function of the first-order spreading rate β(1) for the corresponding patterns in (a), showing that embedding concentrated at lower orders facilitates activation while having a limited impact on hysteresis. The higher-order infection rate is b = 0.34.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23337_figures/2604.23337_fig5.jpg" alt="Figure 5">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> FIG. 5: Empirical evidence of order-dependent embedding and its dynamical impact. (a) Embedding patterns across interaction orders for representative datasets [35]. Top rows and right columns show the marginal averages over inner- and outer-order embedding, C(m) ↓ and C(M) ↑ , respectively (see also the SM). The overall embedding ⟨C⟩is indicated in each panel. (b) Scaled</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23337_figures/2604.23337_fig6.jpg" alt="Figure 6">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 6.</b> FIG. 6: Example of the three steps employed to generate networks through the bipartite configuration model (see SM text). In this example, a bipartite graph is built with five nodes in partition A (circles) and four nodes in partition B (squares) following from predefined degree distributions P A k and P B k . Nodes from each partition can connect only once to each other, and nodes from partition B with the same degree cannot possess the same set of neighbors. The resulting graph is reinterpreted as a higher-order network with five nodes and four hyperedges.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23337_figures/2604.23337_fig7.jpg" alt="Figure 7">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 7.</b> FIG. 7: Overall average nesting coefficient ⟨C⟩as a function of rewires per hypergraphs f. Starting from a SC with ⟨C⟩= 1, the nesting coefficient drops exponentially as a function of the amount of rewires per hyperedge.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23337_figures/2604.23337_fig8.jpg" alt="Figure 8">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 8.</b> FIG. 8: Nesting coefficient measured from empirical datasets. Extra rows and columns on top and left represents the average inner and outer-order nesting coefficient respectively. Nesting coefficients for orders greater than 6 were omitted for better visibility.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** The lack of a general structural principle to govern how the organization of higher-order interactions (nesting) influences contagion dynamics and phase transitions on hypergraphs.

**Main result.** Increasing the nesting coefficient (the embedding of lower-order interactions within higher-order ones) lowers the activation threshold and promotes continuous phase transitions, while low nesting favors explosive, discontinuous behavior and hysteresis.

**Method.** The authors introduce a nesting coefficient and use a bipartite configuration model with a node-exchange rewiring mechanism to create a continuum between simplicial complexes and random hypergraphs. They perform simulations on synthetic and empirical networks using a higher-order SIS model.

**Summary.** This paper identifies 'nesting'—the degree to which lower-order interactions are embedded within higher-order ones—as a fundamental structural determinant of phase transitions in hypergraphs. The authors show that high nesting stabilizes continuous transitions and lowers infection thresholds, whereas low nesting leads to explosive, discontinuous spreading and hysteresis. By testing this on both synthetic and real-world networks, they establish a predictive link between network architecture and collective dynamics. This provides a new framework for understanding how the organization of group-based interactions governs large-scale transitions.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** A higher-order Susceptible-Infected-Susipctible (hyper-SIS) model on hypergraphs and simplicial complexes, where infection occurs through $m$-order hyperedges and recovery follows standard SIS dynamics.

**Key observables.** Stationary epidemic prevalence (rho), activation threshold, transition type (continuous vs. discontinuous), hysteresis (bistability), and the nesting coefficient.

**Important parameters / regimes.** First-order spreading rate (beta 1), higher-order spreading rate (b), rewiring fraction (f), and the nesting coefficient (C).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The bipartite configuration model assumes nodes can connect at most once, which may lead to frustrated configurations where connections cannot be completed.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 illustrates the nesting coefficient and rewiring mechanism; Figure 2 shows prevalence plots demonstrating the transition from smooth to hysteretic curves; Figure 3 presents phase diagrams in the beta(1)-b plane; Figure 4 shows the effect of order correlations on dynamics; Figure 5 displays embedding patterns in empirical datasets; Figure 7 shows the exponential decay of nesting with rewiring; Figure 8 compares nesting across various real-world networks.

**Paper structure.** Introduction of the nesting problem, definition of the nesting coefficient and network generation methods, analysis of the role of nesting in phase transitions, investigation of correlations between embedding and interaction order, empirical validation using real-world datasets, and discussion of structural implications.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

The organization of higher-order interactions plays a central role in shaping collective dynamics, yet a general structural principle governing contagion on hypergraphs remains lacking. Here we introduce a nesting coefficient that quantifies how lower-order interactions are embedded within higher-order ones, defining a continuum between simplicial complexes and random hypergraphs. Using a higher-order susceptible-infected-susceptible model, we show that increasing nesting lowers the activation threshold and suppresses discontinuous transitions, while weak embedding favors explosive behavior. We further demonstrate that correlations between nesting and interaction order modulate the onset of activity while only weakly affecting transition discontinuity. Analysis of synthetic and empirical networks reveals that nesting strongly predicts hysteresis, establishing it as a key structural determinant of phase transitions in higher-order systems.

</details>

### [Quantum Dynamics and Collapse-and-Revival Phenomena in the Dunkl Anharmonic Oscillator](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.22945v1)

**Authors:** D. Ojeda-Guillén, R. D. Mota, M. Salazar-Ramírez  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.22945v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `entanglement & information structure` **2/5** · `interference shaping light` **2/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **2/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>

<img src="2604.22945_figures/2604.22945_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> Figure 1: Evolution of the quadrature ⟨Xµ(t)⟩over a full cycle. The Dunkl parameter µ produces a half-cycle revival at t ≈π, while a complete resynchronization of all wave packets occurs at t ≈2π. For this numerical simulation, the anharmonicity parameter is set to λ = 1, the free oscillator frequency to ω = 20, and the initial coherent state amplitude is α = 2.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.22945_figures/2604.22945_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Figure 2: Survival probability F(t) for different values of the Dunkl parameter: µ = 0 (stan- dard), µ = 0.5 and µ = 1.0. The vertical alignment at t ≈6.28 confirms the universal revival period 2π/λ (evaluated here with the free oscillator frequency ω = 20, the anharmonicity parameter λ = 1, and the initial amplitude α = 2). For µ = 0.5, a perfect additional revival occurs at t = π/λ.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.22945_figures/2604.22945_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> Figure 3: Temporal evolution of the quadrature variance (∆Xµ(t))2 for µ = 0 (top), µ = 0.5 (middle), and µ = 1.0 (bottom), setting the free oscillator frequency to ω = 20, the Kerr parameter to λ = 1, and the coherent amplitude to α = 2. The red dashed horizontal line marks the Standard Quantum Limit (SQL) at 0.5. Values below this line indicate the generation of quadrature squeezed light.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** The study investigates how Dunkl deformation affects the quantum dynamics, specifically the energy spectrum and the collapse-and-revival phenomena, of an anharmonic (Kerr) oscillator.

**Main result.** The Dunkl parameter mu modulates fractional revivals and can generate interference-induced squeezed states and perfect state reconstructions at half-periods for specific deformation values.

**Method.** An algebraic approach using the su(1,1) Lie algebra and Dunkl-deformed Heisenberg-Weyl algebra is employed to solve the Hamiltonian and compute temporal evolution.

**Summary.** This paper explores a generalized version of the Kerr medium using Dunkl-deformed operators. By applying an algebraic su(1,1) approach, the authors derive an exact energy spectrum that depends on the parity of the states. They show that the Dunkl parameter acts as a tuning knob for quantum dynamics, specifically modulating fractional revivals and enabling the generation of interference-induced squeezed states. The results demonstrate that the standard Kerr dynamics are recovered when the deformation parameter goes to zero.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** A Dunkl-deformed Tana's anharmonic oscillator (Kerr medium) characterized by a Hamiltonian that incorporates a parity-dependent centrifugal barrier term via Dunkl operators.

**Key observables.** Energy spectrum, field quadrature, quadrature variance (squeezing), survival probability (fidelity), and the Mandel Q parameter.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Dunkl parameter (mu), Kerr nonlinearity (lambda), and the parity-dependent Bargmann indices (k+ and k-).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The initial state is a superposition of even and odd Dunkl coherent states, and the coherent parameter alpha is assumed to be real for certain analytical derivations.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the temporal evolution of the quadrature; Figure 2 displays the survival probability for different mu values highlighting additional revivals; Figure 3 illustrates the temporal evolution of quadrature variance and squeezing dips.

**Paper structure.** The paper follows a logical progression from defining the Dunkl-deformed Hamiltonian and its algebraic su(1,1) structure, to deriving the exact energy spectrum, and finally analyzing the temporal dynamics of observables like quadrature and survival probability.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work is relevant for quantum optics and quantum information because it demonstrates how a deformation parameter can be used to tune revival phases and generate new types of squeezed states and macroscopic quantum superpositions (cat states).

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We study the Dunkl anharmonic oscillator (Kerr medium) Hamiltonian from an algebraic approach of the $SU(1,1)$ group. In order to obtain the exact energy spectrum of this problem, we write its Hamiltonian in terms of the Dunkl creation and annihilation operators, which close the $su(1,1)$ Lie algebra. This allows us to exactly solve this Hamiltonian and obtain its parity-dependent energy spectrum. Then, we investigate the quantum dynamics of the system, particularly the collapse and revival phenomena, by using an initial state given by a superposition of even and odd Dunkl coherent states. We compute the field quadrature and the survival probability, showing that the Dunkl parameter $μ$ modulates the fractional revivals and produces perfect state reconstructions at half-periods for specific deformation values. We analyze the quadrature variance to show that the Dunkl deformation generates interference-induced squeezed states around $t \approx π$. The standard Kerr medium dynamics are exactly recovered in the limit $μ\rightarrow 0$.

</details>

<a id="paper-2604.24161"></a>
### [Quantum Prediction of Transport Dynamics in Discretized State Spaces](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.24161v1)

**Authors:** Felix Govaers  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.24161v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `methods for driven-dissipative` **2/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **2/5** · `quantum measurements` **2/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24161_figures/2604.24161_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> Fig. 1. Prediction circuit for nx = 4 qubits for the position and nv = 4 qubits for the velocity register, respectively.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24161_figures/2604.24161_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Fig. 2. Marginal densities px(x) and pv(v) at the initialization (top), after the exact classical solution using the matrix exponential (middle) and the resulting marginal densities from the quantum circuit (bottom) for Scenario 1 (left) and 2 (right).</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** Developing an efficient gate-based quantum algorithm for the prediction step of Bayesian state estimation in high-dimensional, discretized position-velocity state spaces.

**Main result.** The authors propose a quantum algorithm that achieves exponential memory compression and polynomial complexity scaling, accurately reproducing classical drift dynamics and approximating diffusion via a unitary surrogate.

**Method.** The algorithm uses amplitude encoding of probability densities and a spectral method in the Fourier domain, employing Quantum Fourier Transforms and a Wick rotation to transform non-unitary diffusion into a unitary dispersive phase evolution.

**Summary.** This paper presents a quantum algorithm designed to predict the evolution of probability densities in a discretized position-velocity space. By encoding densities into quantum amplitudes, the method avoids the exponential memory scaling of classical grid-based methods. While the deterministic drift is implemented exactly, the authors use a Wick rotation to approximate the non-unitary diffusion process as a unitary phase evolution. Numerical tests show that the algorithm accurately captures the mean and transport dynamics, making it a promising tool for high-dimensional Bayesian filtering.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** A Continuous White Noise Acceleration (CWNA) model, also known as the Nearly Constant Velocity (NCV) model, governed by the Fokker-Planck equation in a discretized position-velocity state space.

**Key observables.** Posterior probability density, mean, and covariance matrix.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Drift component (deterministic), diffusion coefficient (stochastic), and grid discretization parameters (Nx, Nv).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The diffusion term is approximated via a Wick rotation (unitary surrogate), and the state space is assumed to be a periodic, uniform grid.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the quantum circuit architecture for a specific qubit configuration; Figure 2 compares marginal densities of the initial, exact classical, and quantum-predicted states across different transport scenarios.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of high-dimensional Bayesian estimation, defines the Fokker-Planck model, details the quantum encoding and spectral implementation of drift and diffusion, provides an error analysis of the unitary surrogate, and concludes with numerical validation.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work is highly relevant for researchers in open quantum systems and many-body dynamics as it provides a framework for using quantum computers to simulate complex, high-dimensional stochastic transport and filtering problems that are classically intractable.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We propose a gate-based quantum algorithm for the prediction step of Bayesian state estimation based on the Fokker-Planck equation on a discretized position-velocity state space. The probability density is encoded in the amplitudes of a quantum state, enabling a compact representation of high-dimensional distributions. Exploiting the circulant structure of finite-difference operators, the evolution is realized in the spectral domain using quantum Fourier transforms and phase rotations.   A key result is that the drift component can be implemented exactly in amplitude space, leading to an accurate reproduction of the classical transport dynamics. In contrast, the diffusion term does not admit a linear representation in amplitude space due to the nonlinear relation between probability density and wave function. To enable a quantum implementation, we introduce a unitary surrogate based on a Wick rotation, transforming diffusion into a dispersive phase evolution. This yields a fully unitary propagation that can be implemented efficiently on a gate-based quantum computer. The proposed method is evaluated numerically for different scenarios and shows strong agreement with the exact solution of the Fokker-Planck equation. The approach demonstrates the potential of quantum computing for Bayesian state estimation, as the representable state space grows exponentially with the number of qubits. This allows the efficient representation and propagation of probability densities that would otherwise require complex tensor decompositions on classical hardware, making the method a promising candidate for high-dimensional filtering problems.

</details>

<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> FIG. 2. A. 2 × 3 tensor network with all indices xi (black squares) labelled. B-D. </figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** Efficiently contracting large-scale tensor networks containing loops, particularly when dealing with frustration, complex entries, or the need to preserve physical symmetries.

**Main result.** Generalized Belief Propagation (GBP) significantly improves accuracy and stability over standard Belief Propagation (BP) in frustrated and highly correlated regimes, and can correctly preserve symmetries like O(2) that BP breaks.

**Method.** The authors implement and analyze Generalized Belief Propagation (GBP) using a hierarchy of overlapping regions (clusters) and Kikuchi free energy minimization, comparing it against BP, cluster expansions, and CTMRG.

**Summary.** This paper presents an implementation of Generalized Belief Propagation (GBP) for the approximate contraction of tensor networks. By using overlapping regions rather than single tensors, GBP captures higher-order correlations and maintains physical symmetries that standard Belief Propagation fails to preserve. The authors demonstrate its efficacy on frustrated Ising models, 3D ice models, and the AKLT state. The study also identifies a convergence threshold related to the presence of negative or complex tensor entries.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The method is applied to various 2D and 3D tensor networks, including the fully frustrated (Villain) Ising model, 3D ice-type models (hexagonal and diamond), and the deformed AKLT quantum state on a honeycomb lattice.

**Key observables.** Partition functions, free energy density, energy density, entropy density, ground state degeneracy, and local spin correlators.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Inverse temperature (beta), deformation parameter (a) for AKLT, fraction of negative/complex entries in tensors (alpha), and bond dimension (chi).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The algorithm assumes no index appears more than twice in the network; convergence may fail when the fraction of negative or complex entries exceeds a certain threshold.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the hierarchy of GBP regions (plaquettes/voxels), the tensor network representation of the Villain model, and comparative plots showing error in free energy and symmetry breaking across different models.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the GBP framework, derives the message-passing update equations via Kikuchi free energy minimization, applies the method to various classical and quantum models, and provides analytical solutions for specific cases in the appendix.

**Why it may be interesting.** This paper provides a more robust numerical tool for approximating the properties of many-body systems where standard message-passing fails due to frustration or symmetry breaking, which is crucial for studying phase transitions in complex quantum states.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Recent years have seen a growing interest in the use of belief propagation - an algorithm originally introduced for performing statistical inference on graphical models - for approximate, but highly efficient, tensor network contraction. Here, we detail how to apply generalized belief propagation (GBP) - where messages are passed within a hierarchy of overlapping regions of the tensor network - to approximately contract tensor networks and obtain accurate results. The original belief propagation algorithm is a corner case of this approach, corresponding to a particularly simple choice of regions of the tensor network. We implement the GBP algorithm for a number of different region choices on a range of two- and three-dimensional, infinite and finite tensor networks, solving the corresponding fixed point equations both numerically and, in certain tractable cases, analytically. Our examples include calculating the partition function of the fully frustrated Ising model, computing the ground state degeneracy of three-dimensional ice models, measuring observables on the deformed AKLT quantum state and evaluating the norm of randomly generated tensor network states.

</details>

<a id="paper-2604.23031"></a>
### [How fast can a quantum gate be? Exact speed limits from geometry](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23031v1)

**Authors:** Hunter Nelson, Edwin Barnes  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.23031v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `entanglement & information structure` **2/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **2/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23031_figures/2604.23031_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> FIG. 1. Paths on the manifold SU(n) emanating from the identity and terminating at three different gates U1, U2, and U3. A space curve in SCQC represents these paths in a Eu- clidean space Rn2−1, whose dimension equals the number of group generators.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23031_figures/2604.23031_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> FIG. 2. Time-optimal space curves generated by Pauli cer- tifiers and corresponding to several commonly used gates in quantum information science. Extra curve dimensions rep- resent longer QSL times for gates. For instance the curve realizing CNOT is a circular helix which necessarily dips into a third dimension. This leads to a longer QSL over the locally equivalent UZX gate, which admits a planar realization.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23031_figures/2604.23031_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> FIG. 3. Circular arc space curves generated by non-Pauli cer- tifiers constructed from the eigenbasis of the optimal Hamil- tonian. Here, for UZX, CNOT, and U4d a larger QSL geomet- rically corresponds to a circular arc with a larger radius for the same depicted tangent-endpoint angle of π/4.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** Determining the fundamental, tight quantum speed limits (QSLs) for implementing specific quantum gates under a bounded Hamiltonian spectral width.

**Main result.** The authors derive an exact, saturable QSL for any unitary evolution where the minimal gate time is determined by the 'slowest' operator in a certifying set. They show that time-optimal gates correspond to geometric helices in Euclidean space and that QSLs can vary significantly even for gates with identical entangling power.

**Method.** The Space Curve Quantum Control (SCQC) formalism is used to map unitary evolution in SU(n) to space curves in Euclidean space, recasting the QSL problem as finding minimal-length curves subject to a curvature bound.

**Summary.** This paper establishes exact quantum speed limits for implementing quantum gates by treating unitary evolution as a geometric curve in operator space. By bounding the spectral width of the Hamiltonian, the authors show that the minimum time required for a gate is determined by the most difficult-to-evolve observable in a certifying set. They demonstrate that optimal gates follow helical trajectories and that the complexity of these trajectories varies by gate type. This provides a new way to classify and optimize quantum gate implementations.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The system consists of unitary evolution U(t) in the SU(n) group, subject to a constraint on the spectral width of the Hamiltonian: w(H) <= Omega_max.

**Key observables.** A set of certifying operators (e.g., Pauli strings) used to uniquely specify the target gate and identify the bottleneck dynamics.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The maximum spectral width (Omega_max), the gate time (T), and the curvature (kappa) of the evolution curve.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The system is assumed to be fully controllable, and the primary constraint is the spectral width of the Hamiltonian.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 illustrates paths on the SU(n) manifold and their representation as space curves in Euclidean space; Figure 2 shows a helix trajectory for a CNOT certifier; Figure 3 depicts circular arcs of varying radii for different gates.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of gate speed limits, develops the SCQC geometric formalism, derives the tight QSL using the bottleneck principle, applies the results to specific two- and three-qubit gates, and classifies gates based on their geometric complexity.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides a fundamental geometric framework for understanding the speed of quantum operations, which is directly relevant to optimizing gate sequences and understanding the dynamical constraints in quantum computing and many-body control.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

The speed of quantum evolution is limited under finite energy resources. While most quantum speed limits (QSLs) are formulated in terms of quantum states, they can be extended to the evolution operator itself, and thus impose fundamental limits on how quickly logical gate operations can be implemented on a quantum computer. Here, we derive a general, tight QSL that holds for any unitary evolution under the constraint that the spectral width of the Hamiltonian is bounded. We apply this result to obtain QSLs for several standard quantum gates, including Hadamard, CNOT, and Toffoli gates, finding that the QSL can vary significantly across different gates, including ones with the same entangling power. These findings can be understood geometrically using the Space Curve Quantum Control formalism, which maps unitary evolution to space curves in Euclidean space. In this formalism, the problem of finding QSLs is recast as the problem of finding minimal-length curves obeying a curvature bound. We find that time-optimal gates map to helices of varying dimensions, and that QSLs can be understood from the perspective of a bottleneck principle in which the operator that evolves the slowest governs the minimal gate time.

</details>

**Main problem.** Optimizing the ordering of Pauli strings in Trotterization to minimize the 'true' state-dependent Trotter error in quantum simulations.

**Main result.** Commutation-based grouping strategies, which use graph coloring on a commutation graph, consistently outperform random and baseline ordering methods, with the optimal permutation depending on the Trotter order and lattice geometry.

**Method.** The authors use graph coloring techniques (e.g., XYZ-coloring, greedy, and Gurobi-based optimization) to partition Hamiltonian terms into commuting groups and evaluate fidelity using sparse Krylov matrix exponentiation.

**Summary.** This paper investigates how the sequence of Hamiltonian terms in Trotterization affects simulation fidelity. By using graph coloring to group commuting Pauli strings, the authors develop 'commutation-based' ordering strategies. They demonstrate that these strategies significantly reduce the true Trotter error compared to random or simple heuristic orderings. The study shows that the best ordering strategy is highly dependent on the specific lattice geometry and the order of the Trotter decomposition used.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** Heisenberg-style Hamiltonians including 1D XXZ spin chains, 2D rectangular lattices, and 2D triangular lattices (including nearest and next-nearest neighbor interactions).

**Key observables.** State-dependent fidelity (F) between the Trotterized and exact time-evolved states.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Trotter order (1st and 2nd order Suzuki), number of Trotter steps (s = 3 to 20), system size (L up to 20 qubits), and Hamiltonian parameters like anisotropy (delta) and transverse field (g).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The Hamiltonians are assumed to be represented as a linear combination of Pauli strings, specifically 'non-mixed' types where each string contains at most one Pauli type.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows fidelity distributions for 1D XXZ chains; Figure 2 shows fidelity distributions for 2D lattices; Figure 4 compares mean fidelity across different lattice geometries and Trotter orders; Figure 5 shows fidelity decay as a function of system size; Figure 6 illustrates the interaction graph for a 2D triangular lattice.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of Trotter error bounds, formalizes the commutation graph and coloring-based grouping strategies, presents theoretical results on the chromatic number of these graphs, and concludes with numerical simulations comparing various ordering strategies across different 1D and 2D models.

**Why it may be interesting.** This is highly relevant for many-body dynamics as it provides a practical, structure-aware method to improve the accuracy of digital quantum simulations of spin systems without increasing the number of Trotter steps.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Trotterization is a technique that allows one to approximate a time evolution of a Hamiltonian by repeatedly evolving the individual terms of the Hamiltonian one-at-a-time for small time durations. Bounds on the error of this approximation exist; however, they are typically loose and moreover, it is known that the true error can be greatly influenced by the order in which the terms of the Hamiltonian are evolved. In this work, we consider various ordering strategies that exploit the commutation structure of the Hamiltonian, in addition to a few other baseline ordering strategies. These commutation-based strategies involve dividing the terms of the Hamiltonian into groups where all the terms within each group commute with one another. These groupings can be obtained by using graph coloring techniques on what we call the "commutation graph" of the Hamiltonian. We prove various results regarding the structure and properties of such commutation graphs for certain classes of Hamiltonians. We also empirically calculate the (true) Trotter error using these ordering strategies on various 1D and 2D Heisenberg-style systems.

</details>

<a id="paper-2604.24554"></a>
### [Balancing Quantum Memories in Asymmetric Repeaters for High-Fidelity Entanglement Distribution](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.24554v1)

**Authors:** Karim S. Elsayed, Amr Rizk  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.24554v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `entanglement & information structure` **2/5**

Quantum nodes may be end nodes or quantum repeaters. (b) Schematic illustration of the considered repeater ar- chitecture: The repeater contains N memories dynamically allocated between the left and right sides to form entangle- ments with neighboring nodes. The number of memories Nl allocated to the left is depicted by the left memory bank, while Nr memories are allocated to the right. The memories in the left and right banks simultaneously attempt to generate entanglements with left and right adjacent nodes, respectively, which is depicted by the dashed red curves....</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24554_figures/2604.24554_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Fig. 2: Schematic illustration of the standard repeater archi- tecture. The repeater contains N memories, each of which sequentially forms an entanglement with the two adjacent nodes. The red and black dashed curves depict the sequential entanglement generation with the left and right nodes, respec- tively.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24554_figures/2604.24554_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> Fig. 6: (a) Schematic illustration: A repeater chain consisting of two repeaters, R1 and R2, connecting two end nodes, Q1 and Q2. Each node, i.e., repeater or end node, contains a total number of N memories. Each repeater has the architecture considered in this work from Fig. 1b and optimally assigns memories to its sides using Lem. 1. (b) Numerical evaluation: Comparison of the expected fidelity and expected rate of end- to-end entanglements between (i) optimal memory allocation, (ii) fixed equal allocation and (iii) standard repeaters. The initial fidelity F0 = 1, coherence time tc = 0.001 sec and distances d1 = d3 = 20 km and d2 = 30 km.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** How to mitigate the mismatch problem in asymmetric quantum repeaters, where unequal link distances cause an accumulation of unmatched entanglements, leading to decoherence and reduced fidelity.

**Main result.** The authors derive a dynamic optimal memory allocation strategy that significantly improves entanglement fidelity while maintaining a comparable generation rate to standard repeaters.

**Method.** The paper uses a mathematical framework involving binomial distributions, the Central Limit Theorem, and a zero-mean condition for the mismatch to derive optimal allocation rules and statistical lower bounds.

**Summary.** This paper addresses the performance bottleneck in asymmetric quantum repeaters caused by the mismatch of entanglement generation rates on either side of a node. By dynamically allocating quantum memories between the left and right banks, the authors show it is possible to minimize the time unmatched entanglements spend in memory. This optimization significantly boosts entanglement fidelity without sacrificing the generation rate. The study also introduces a 'hard-cutoff' strategy to prevent performance degradation when the number of memories is low.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** An asymmetric quantum repeater architecture with $N$ total memories divided into left and right banks, using photon-mediated probabilistic entanglement generation over optical fibers with decay rate $\sigma$.

**Key observables.** Entanglement generation rate ($R$), entanglement fidelity ($E[F_{swap}]$), and the number of unmatched entanglements ($\alpha$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Total number of memories ($N$), link distances ($d_l, d_r$), success probabilities ($p_l, p_r$), fiber decay rate ($\sigma$), and coherence time ($t_c$).

**Assumptions / limitations.** Entanglement generation follows a Bernoulli process; the number of memories is large enough to use Gaussian approximations; the photon trip time dominates the round duration.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the system-level view and repeater architecture; Figure 3 compares the entanglement rate across different allocation regimes; Figure 4 compares fidelity across regimes; Figure 5 evaluates the hard-cutoff strategy; Figure 6 demonstrates performance in a multi-node repeater chain.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the mismatch problem, defines the physical model of asymmetric repeaters, derives the optimal dynamic allocation and statistical bounds, proposes a hard-cutoff regime for handling allocation violations, and concludes with numerical simulations and extensions to repeater chains.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and quantum networks as it provides a fundamental optimization strategy for managing decoherence and resource allocation in realistic, non-symmetric quantum communication infrastructures.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

At the core of the quantum Internet lie quantum repeaters that enable remote end-to-end entanglement generation. Fundamentally, the entanglement generation rate and fidelity of quantum repeaters constitute the bottleneck for end-to-end performance. To achieve high rates, quantum repeaters employ quantum memory multiplexing. In a high-rate standard repeater, each memory sequentially generates an entanglement with its neighboring nodes and then applies entanglement swapping. This, however, results in low fidelity due to decoherence of the first-formed entanglement in the sequential generation process. By allocating different numbers of memories to simultaneously form entanglements with the left and right adjacent nodes, quantum repeaters reduce high waiting times and achieve high fidelity. In such a repeater, a mismatch problem arises due to the difference between the probabilistic number of generated entanglements on both sides. Consequently, some entanglements remain stored until opposite entanglements are available. The mismatch problem reduces the repeater rate and particularly the entanglement fidelity. In this paper, we consider the mismatch problem in an asymmetric repeater with different distances to its adjacent nodes. To mitigate the mismatch problem, we derive a dynamic optimal memory allocation. Under the optimal allocation, we derive statistical lower bounds on the achievable rate and fidelity. We demonstrate that the optimal allocation significantly improves the fidelity while maintaining a comparable rate to the standard repeater. In contrast, our results show that fixed memory allocation may be detrimental to the fidelity.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

<a id="paper-2604.24059"></a>
### [Beyond Monolithic Scaling: Modularity and Heterogeneity as an Architectural Imperative for Utility-Scale Quantum Computing](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.24059v1)

**Authors:** Bo Fan, Renzhou Fang, Yuntao Zhang, Xiaolong Yuan, Dafa Zhao  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.24059v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `entanglement & information structure` **2/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24059_figures/2604.24059_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> FIG. 1. The Control Light Cone and geometric penalty in monolithic quantum architectures. A 3D spacetime representation where the X-Y plane denotes the physical qubit footprint and the Z-axis denotes time. The blue horizontal plane represents the coherence lifetime limit (τq). (a) At intermediate scales (N &lt; Nc), the control signal cone (yellow) remains within the τq budget, enabling global coordination (τc ≪τq). (b) At utility scales (N &gt; Nc), the spatial footprint expands such that the control cone breaches the τq horizon. The region exceeding the causal horizon (red shaded) incurs a superlinear geometric penalty (ϵ &gt; 0), physically mandating modular decomposition to truncate this penalty.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24059_figures/2604.24059_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> FIG. 2. Scaling-induced architectural phase transition and modular crossover. Asymptotic structural cost C(N) versus physical qubit count N, plotted on a log-log scale, where the physical footprint relates to logical capacity as N ≈κNL, with κ ∼103 representing a typical surface-code overhead factor. The homogeneous architecture (red solid line) incurs a su- perlinear coordination penalty, Chom(N) = Θ(N 1+ϵ) with ϵ &gt; 0, arising from finite signal propagation and control bandwidth constraints; the modular architecture (blue dashed line) achieves Cmod(N) = Θ(N γ), where γ reflects network routing eﬀi- ciency. Their intersection defines a crossover scale Nc(A, B, ηtrans) see Eq. (18)), beyond...</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24059_figures/2604.24059_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> FIG. 3. Conceptual shift from monolithic integration to a distributed LOCC supply chain. Left: Monolithic global unitaries suffer from severe spatial crosstalk and global synchronization errors (τc &gt; τq) as physical boundaries expand. Right: The distributed paradigm enforces strict modular isolation. Nonlocal operations are synthesized via Local Operations and Classical Communication (LOCC), consuming bipartite entanglement (managed as physical state ρij at the hardware layer, and abstracted as metadata tuples eij for classical scheduling) pre-distributed asynchronously by a dedicated quantum network, thus accommodating hardware heterogeneity while circumventing the geometric penalty.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24059_figures/2604.24059_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> FIG. 4. Time-aware Reserve–Commit Protocol timing diagram. The protocol execution flow is shown across three architectural layers (Classical Control Plane, Entanglement Management Layer, and Quantum Hardware Layer), constrained by the physical coherence deadline (tgen + τ (p) q , red line). In Scenario A (insuﬀicient temporal margin), the control plane performs a preemptive abort when tcurrent + τ ∗ exec &gt; tgen + τ (p) q , explicitly outputting a semantic erasure marker (orange) to prevent scheduling-induced decoherence. In Scenario B (suﬀicient margin), the protocol commits to the execution of the LOCC sequence (Local Entangle →Measure →Coord →Feedforward) under the condition tcurrent + τ...</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24059_figures/2604.24059_fig5.jpg" alt="Figure 5">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> FIG. 5. The architectural semantic error degradation pipeline. The time-aware scheduling framework operates as a selective semantic classifier: heralded protocol failures (timeout aborts, heralded photon loss) are converted into location-known erasure metadata, while unheralded local decoherence bypasses the classifier as standard depolarizing noise. This structured routing supplies downstream erasure-aware QEC decoders with explicit error metadata, elevating the effective fault-tolerant threshold and reducing syndrome decoding complexity.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** The fundamental bottleneck in scaling quantum computing is a temporal mismatch where classical coordination latency grows with system size and eventually exceeds the bounded quantum coherence time.

**Main result.** The paper proves that modular, heterogeneous architectures are a physical necessity beyond a crossover scale of 10^5-10^6 qubits, as they replace superlinear geometric scaling penalties with manageable topological routing costs.

**Method.** The authors use asymptotic scaling analysis and a formalization of a structural phase transition (1 + epsilon > gamma) to compare monolithic versus modular architectures, alongside a proposed 'Reserve-Commit' protocol for time-aware scheduling.

**Summary.** This paper argues that monolithic quantum computing is physically impossible at large scales because classical control signals cannot keep up with quantum coherence. It proposes that we must move toward modular, heterogeneous architectures where different quantum platforms are linked via a network. By using a 'Reserve-Commit' protocol, the system can manage entanglement as a resource and convert timing failures into known erasure errors. The authors predict that this architectural shift becomes mandatory once a system reaches 100,000 to 1,000,000 physical qubits.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** A large-scale, macroscopic quantum-classical hybrid system modeled via a Hamiltonian H = H_sys + H_ctrl + H_env, comparing monolithic arrays to modular architectures using Local Operations and Classical Communication (LOCC).

**Key observables.** Classical coordination latency (tau_c), quantum coherence time (tau_q), transduction efficiency (eta_trans), and the geometric penalty (epsilon).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Crossover scale (Nc) of 10^5-10^6 physical qubits, transduction target (eta_trans) ~ 0.1, and the coordination wall at approximately 8.3 x 10^4 qubits.

**Assumptions / limitations.** Assumes no disruptive new technology emerges to resolve the isolation-interactability-topology trilemma and assumes the existence of a fixed hardware cost ratio (B/A).

**Figures summary.** Figure 2 shows a log-log plot of asymptotic structural cost; Figure 3 illustrates the conceptual shift from monolithic global unitaries to distributed LOCC; Figure 4 shows a timing diagram for the Reserve-Commit protocol; Figure 5 depicts an error degradation pipeline.

**Paper structure.** The paper identifies the scaling bottleneck, formalizes the geometric penalty and structural phase transition, proposes a modular/heterogeneous architecture and the Reserve-Commit protocol, and concludes with a quantitative prediction of the crossover scale and architectural convergence.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a rigorous physical argument for why the future of quantum computing must be distributed and heterogeneous, linking classical signal propagation limits to the fundamental limits of quantum error correction and architecture design.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Scalable quantum computing is fundamentally bottlenecked not by qubit count or fabrication yield, but by a rigid temporal mismatch: macroscopic classical coordination latency ($τ_c$) inevitably grows with system diameter, while microscopic quantum coherence ($τ_q$) remains strictly bounded. Beyond a critical scale, this mismatch breaches the classical control light cone, triggering a superlinear geometric penalty ($ε> 0$) that renders monolithic synchronization physically impossible. We formalize the resulting structural phase transition through a governing scaling law, $1+ε> γ$, which mandates modular decomposition and a shift from global unitaries to Local Operations and Classical Communication (LOCC). To manage the resulting resource contention under strict coherence budgets, we introduce a layered semantic architecture and a time-aware Reserve--Commit protocol. By embedding predictive temporal pre-validation, the protocol acts as an architectural semantic classifier: it preemptively aborts transactions that exceed the causal horizon and explicitly converts scheduling-induced failures into location-known erasure metadata, directly relaxing hardware fidelity thresholds for downstream QEC decoders. Under near-term transduction targets ($η_{\mathrm{trans}} \sim 0.1$), we project a crossover scale at $N_c \sim 10^5$--$10^6$ physical qubits. This threshold marks a profound architectural convergence: the footprint required for modularity aligns precisely with early fault-tolerant utility, establishing time-aware distributed orchestration, rather than monolithic expansion or centralized classical control, as the physical imperative for utility-scale quantum computing.

</details>

For the optical branch, the exper- imental ratio is small, γ/ω ≤0.22 across all studied q-points, indicating that the optical magnons are underdamped, and thus coherent [22–24], which is striking given the significant influence of atomic disorder on the dispersion at Γ. In the acoustic branch, the experimental ratio γ/ω is larger, but remains within the underdamped limit (γ/ω ∼0.6 &lt; 1). To determine the origin of the anisotropy of γ, we compared a nearly undamped (UD) scenario (αCu/F e i,i = 0.01) with a local-damping (LD) case. A peak at Γ appears even in the UD...</figcaption>

</div>

**Main problem.** Investigating whether atomic disorder in high-entropy materials necessarily degrades the coherence of collective magnetic excitations, specifically in the YBaCuFeO5 system.

**Main result.** The study reveals a paradox where disorder in an entropy-driven mixed phase actually favors the formation of coherent, underdamped acoustic and optical spin-wave branches with a large optical gap.

**Method.** The researchers combined Resonant Inelastic X-ray Scattering (RIXS) at the Cu and Fe L3 edges with theoretical modeling using Linear Spin Wave Theory (LSWT), Atomistic Spin Dynamics (ASD), and DFT+U.

**Summary.** This paper explores the magnetic dynamics of the high-entropy material YBaCuFeO5. Contrary to the expectation that disorder destroys coherence, the authors demonstrate that an entropy-stabilized mixed phase of Fe and Cu atoms supports well-defined, underdamped spin waves. By using RIXS and spin dynamics simulations, they show that this disordered state possesses a large optical gap and robust collective modes. This finding suggests new strategies for engineering disordered materials to host stable, high-energy magnetic excitations.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The physical system is YBaCuFeO5 (YBCFO), an antiferromagnetic layered compound featuring a maximal entropy mixed phase where Fe and Cu atoms are disordered within the ab-planes.

**Key observables.** Dynamically spin structure factors S(q, omega), magnon dispersion omega(q), optical spin gap, and linewidths (damping).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Exchange couplings (J_ab, J_perp), spin quantum numbers, configurational entropy, and the transition temperatures T_CM and T_S.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The use of the Heisenberg model and the approximation of the spiral phase using a weak ferromagnetic next-nearest-neighbor interaction.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the crystal structure and RIXS intensity; Figure 2 compares LSWT results for different atomic orderings; Figure 3 shows spin-wave evolution as a function of disorder; Figure 4 compares experimental vs. simulated peak energies and intensities.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of disorder-induced damping, describes the YBCFO system, presents RIXSS experimental data, compares various atomic ordering models (monoatomic, checkerboard, mixed), and concludes with the identification of the entropy-stabilized mixed phase as the source of coherent modes.

**Why it may be interesting.** It challenges the conventional intuition that disorder leads to localization and damping, providing a mechanism where entropy-driven disorder can actually stabilize coherent many-body excitations.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

In solids, disorder is conventionally regarded as detrimental to coherence. It typically localizes and dampens collective excitations, as exemplified by Anderson localization or the broadening of magnetic modes in systems lacking long-range order. While high-entropy materials are specifically designed to harness disorder and stabilize homogeneous mixed-phase structures that can display unique properties, this same disorder is nonetheless expected to preclude the formation of coherent magnetic excitations. To test the limits of this picture, we selected the antiferromagnetic system YBaCuFeO5, as it features two distinct transition metal atoms with significantly different magnetic moments, rendering its spin dynamics exceptionally sensitive to local atomic ordering. Combining resonant inelastic x-ray scattering and linear spin wave theory, we reveal a surprising paradox: YBaCuFeO5 exhibits an unexpected, entropy-driven mixed phase, in which disorder, rather than reducing the lifetime of the collective excitations, favors coherence. In this mixed phase, the spin waves remain dispersive, markedly distinct from those expected for an ordered ground state, and exhibit well-defined acoustic and optical branches separated by a large optical gap. These results demonstrate that in entropy-stabilized magnets, disorder can favor coherent collective modes previously thought to be exclusive to low-entropy systems.

</details>

**Main problem.** The exponential computational cost of classically simulating and analyzing constrained quantum optimization algorithms like QAOA.

**Main result.** The authors prove that constrained quantum optimization via Quantum Zeno Dynamics allows for an exact reduced model, enabling exponential state-space reduction in specific problems like 3-SAT.

**Method.** A model reduction approach that uses the projection property of quantum measurements to simulate dynamics in a lower-dimensional Zeno subspace.

**Summary.** This paper presents a method to significantly speed up the classical simulation of constrained quantum optimization algorithms. By leveraging the Quantum Zeno effect, the authors show that one can restrict the simulation to a smaller, 'safe' subspace of the Hilbert space without losing essential information. They demonstrate that for certain problems like 3-SAT, this leads to an exponential reduction in the required computational space. This approach provides a powerful tool for analyzing large-scale quantum algorithms that involve feasibility constraints.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The system involves n-qubit states evolving under the QAOA framework, using a problem Hamiltonian (e.g., for 3-SAT or agent coordination) and a transverse field Hamiltonian.

**Key observables.** Complex amplitudes, probabilities of measurement outcomes, and Hamming distance between bitstrings.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Number of qubits (n), dimension of the Zeno subspace (d), number of QAOA layers (nu), and discretization steps (tau).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The method assumes the existence of a defined 'safe' or feasible subspace and that information regarding states outside this subspace is irrelevant to the optimization goal.

**Figures summary.** The notes mention tables showing the exponential growth of the original space versus the reduced space for 3-SAT and agent coordination benchmarks.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of simulation cost, establishes the mathematical framework of Quantum Zeno Dynamics, proposes the model reduction technique, provides formal proofs for equivalence and complexity, and validates the approach with 3-SAT and agent coordination benchmarks.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Quantum optimization algorithms promise advantages for difficult problems but are costly to simulate and analyze on classical machines. Recently, constrained quantum optimization has been investigated through the lens of Quantum Zeno dynamics, an approach which constrains the search to a subspace by means of quantum measurements. Exploiting that quantum measurements are projections, we propose a model reduction approach and show that simulations can be conducted in a lower-dimensional space. As possible applications, we demonstrate exponential state-space reduction of constrained quantum optimization in case of random 3-SAT and agent coordination problems over graphs.

</details>

**Main problem.** Developing a mathematically consistent framework to calculate electronic final states (both bound and continuum) following the sudden change in nuclear charge during beta decay.

**Main result.** The paper establishes a robust many-electron framework using a lambda-homotopy to relate non-orthogonal initial and final states, validated by an analytical one-electron benchmark for tritium decay.

**Method.** A sudden-approximation framework using a lambda-parametrized Hamiltonian family, a transport scheme based on SVD and overlap metrics, and multireference wave-function methods (CAS/RAS).

**Summary.** This paper presents a new theoretical framework for modeling how electrons respond to the sudden change in nuclear charge during beta decay. By introducing a continuous interpolation parameter (lambda), the author creates a 'bridge' between the initial and final electronic states, solving the numerical instability caused by non-orthogonal basis sets. The method is successfully applied to a one-electron tritium decay model and generalized to complex many-electron systems. This approach provides a stable way to calculate both bound-state populations and ionization probabilities.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The system is nuclear beta decay (e.g., tritium decay) where a change in nuclear charge (Z) occurs. The model uses a lambda-homotopy Hamiltonian H(lambda) to continuously connect the initial and final electronic Hamiltonians.

**Key observables.** Transition amplitudes (c_n), transition probabilities for bound and continuum channels, and a completeness diagnostic (delta_comp).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Nuclear charge (Z), interpolation parameter (lambda), reduced mass (mu_N), and the sudden approximation limit.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The sudden approximation (nuclear decay is much faster than electronic relaxation) and a non-relativistic treatment of the electronic motion.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows a schematic of the sudden approximation process; Figure 2 illustrates how increasing nuclear charge contracts electronic density and induces level splitting/mixing.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins with the physical premise of the sudden approximation, develops a one-electron analytical benchmark using a lambda-path, generalizes the formalism to many-electron systems using determinant overlaps, and concludes with a structured 6-step computational methodology.

**Why it may be interesting.** The use of a lambda-homotopy to regularize the transition between non-orthogonal Hilbert spaces and the development of a transport scheme for tracking eigensubspaces is highly relevant to many-body dynamics and the study of non-adiabatic transitions.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Electronic final states generated by sudden changes of the Hamiltonian are studied here, with emphasis on nuclear charge variation in $β$ decay. A $λ$-parametrized family $\hat H(λ)$ that continuously connects the initial and final Hamiltonians, so that the electronic response can be represented as a continuous deformation in Hilbert space, is introduced. Within the sudden approximation, transition amplitudes are written as overlaps between eigenstates of distinct Hamiltonians. To relate non-orthogonal one-electron basis sets in a stable way, the paper uses a practical transport scheme based on overlap metrics and truncated singular value decomposition (SVD). This mapping is interpreted as a discrete counterpart of continuous transport along the $λ$ path. The formalism is first developed for the one-electron case, where analytic structure and selection rules are made explicit, and then generalized to many-electron systems via nonorthogonal determinant overlap expressions. The resulting formulation gives transition probabilities in bound and continuum channels in a way that is both numerically stable and easy to interpret.

</details>

**Authors:** Omer Rathore, Alastair Basden, Nicholas Chancellor, Halim Kusumaatmaja  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.24694v1>

<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> Figure 1: Summary circuit diagram for the HHL algorithm, adapted from [17] (CC BY). The five main components are (a) State preparation, (b) Quantum phase estimation, (c) Controlled rotation, (d) Inverse quantum phase estimation and (e) Ancilla qubit measurement. Quantum phase estimation consists of Hadamard gates (H), controlled unitaries (U) and the inverse quantum Fourier transform (IQFT). Meanwhile, the input consists of three registers, referred to here as the ancilla, clock and input registers.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24694_figures/2604.24694_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Figure 2: Illustrative application examples of the Quantum Integration Algorithm to fluid and transport problems. Top: compressible flow through a convergent-divergent nozzle with a normal shock, adapted from [99]. Bottom: tephra transport in the umbrella region of a submarine volcanic eruption, adapted from [100]. In each case, the left panel shows the physical setup and the right panel shows a representative numerical result. Figures licensed under CC BY.</figcaption>
</figure>

The figure reports the loss and mean squared error (MSE) as functions of training epoch. The results are problem-dependent: while Elvet ultimately achieves higher accuracy in two of the three cases, Qade performs especially well for the wave equation example, where it attains a lower MSE and a comparable final loss. The circuit carries out a full time step by combining state encoding (a), the collision step via controlled operators C1 and C2 (b), propagation through the shift operators R and L (c), and final evaluation of the macroscopic variable (d).</figcaption>

Rows 1 and 2 show a quantum-inspired matrix-product-state formulation applied to flow around a cylinder at increasing Reynolds number from left to right, with velocity streamlines in the top row and vorticity in the bottom row. As the Reynolds number increases, the onset of the classical Kármán vortex street is observed in the wake of the cylinder. Adapted from [168]. Row 3 shows a quantum support vector machine for flow classification around an airfoil, including the decision boundaries for the classical (g) and quantum (h) cases, together with a performance comparison (i), where...</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** The central challenge is determining how to effectively encode fluid information onto quantum hardware to achieve practical quantum advantage in Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD).

**Main result.** The authors demonstrate that encoding choice is a primary design variable that fundamentally shapes algorithmic feasibility, showing that no single encoding is universally optimal and that trade-offs exist between compactness and interaction simplicity.

**Method.** An architecture-agnostic review and assessment of various encoding paradigms (amplitude, basis, unary, one-hot, and block encoding) and their relation to quantum algorithms like HHL and QSVT.

**Summary.** This review paper evaluates how different data encoding strategies impact the feasibility of using quantum computers for fluid dynamics simulations. It highlights the fundamental trade-offs between compact encodings, which offer theoretical speedups but suffer from readout bottlenecks, and less compact encodings that are more compatible with near-term hardware. The authors argue that encoding should be treated as a primary, iterative design variable in the development of quantum CFD algorithms. Ultimately, the paper provides a roadmap for navigating the complexities of mapping nonlinear, dissipative fluid physics onto linear, unitary quantum frameworks.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The paper focuses on the Navier-Stokes equations for fluid dynamics, including turbulent flows and large-scale meteorological simulations, analyzed through the lens of quantum computing models (gate-model and analog).

**Key observables.** Velocity fields, pressure, density, energy, temperature, vorticity, and streamlines.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Reynolds number (Re), precision (epsilon), condition number (kappa), sparsity (s), and gate counts (specifically CNOT gates).

**Assumptions / limitations.** Assumes the potential for quantum advantage in CFD; notes limitations such as the exponential cost of Quantum State Tomography and the difficulty of implementing nonlinearities due to the no-cloning theorem.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows an HHL algorithm circuit diagram; Figure 6 illustrates application-level successes including velocity streamlines/vorticity from matrix-product-states, flow classification via QSVM, and hybrid PINN results for 3D mixers.

**Paper structure.** The paper provides an architecture-agnostic assessment of encoding strategies, examines the trade-offs between different encoding paradigms, relates these to specific quantum algorithms, and discusses the implications for practical CFD implementation and hardware constraints.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Quantum computing has emerged as a powerful potential accelerator for computational fluid dynamics (CFD), but whether this promise can be realized in practice depends on how fluid information is encoded on quantum hardware. This review provides an architecture-agnostic assessment of encoding strategies for quantum-enhanced fluid simulation, focusing on the trade-offs they impose on state preparation, measurement, boundary treatment, nonlinear dynamics, and temporal evolution. We examine the principal encoding paradigms used in the literature and relate them to representative quantum algorithms for fluid simulation. Through these examples, we show that encoding choices fundamentally shape both the algorithm itself and also the practical feasibility of quantum CFD. For example, highly compact encodings can offer attractive asymptotic advantages but might introduce severe bottlenecks in readout, state preparation, and nonlinear processing, whereas less compact representations may simplify interactions and improve compatibility with analog and near-term hardware. No single encoding is universally optimal, rather the most suitable choice depends strongly on the structure of the fluid problem, the computational objective and the constraints of the target quantum platform. We therefore argue that encoding should be treated as a primary design variable in quantum CFD and revisited iteratively throughout the design pipeline, as different algorithmic components interact and influence one another.

</details>

**Main problem.** The study investigates the fragmentation transition of 1D and 3D quantum droplets in Bose-Einstein condensate mixtures at finite temperatures, driven by the competition between energy and entropy.

**Main result.** The authors identify a fragmentation temperature (Tf) where droplets split into multiple smaller droplets or a gas; in 3D, this occurs at low atom numbers and high interspecies interaction, while in 1D, droplets expel atoms to form a gas of free atoms and pairs as temperature rises.

**Method.** The study uses the extended Gross-Pitaevskii equation (eGPE) with Lee-Huang-Yang corrections, a thermodynamic framework involving partition functions, and numerical imaginary time propagation.

**Summary.** This paper explores how quantum droplets in BEC mixtures fragment into multiple pieces or a gas as temperature increases. It provides a theoretical description for both 1D and 3D systems, showing that fragmentation is driven by entropy. In 3D, fragmentation is linked to low atom numbers and strong interspecies interactions, while in 1D, rising temperatures cause droplets to expel atoms into a gas of free atoms and pairs. The results offer critical insights into the stability and lifetime of these self-bound states in ultracold gases.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** A mixture of two Bose-Einstein condensates (BECs) in 1D and 3D configurations, modeled with a Hamiltonian including kinetic energy, external potentials, and s-wave contact interactions (intraspecies and interspecies).

**Key observables.** Fragmentation temperature (Tf), average fragmentation rate (number of fragments), specific heat (Cv), and droplet density profiles.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Interspecies interaction strength (g12), intraspecies interaction strength (gjj), atom number (N), temperature (T), system size (L or V), and the interaction deviation (delta g).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The system is in the ultracold regime; droplets are treated as non-interacting; the 3D model requires regularization/renormalization; the 1D model assumes the droplet expels atoms into a gas or small clusters.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows ground state density profiles and energy vs. atom number; Figure 6 displays the average fragmentation rate and specific heat vs. temperature for 1D systems; Figure 7 shows the fragmentation temperature as a function of interaction strength and density.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of droplet fragmentation, establishes the 1D and 3D models using eGPE and LHY corrections, develops a thermodynamic framework for fragmentation, presents results for 3D stability and fragmentation, details 1D fragmentation and temperature effects, and concludes with scaling analysis.

**Why it may be interesting.** This paper is highly relevant for many-body dynamics and AMO physics as it explores the thermodynamic stability and phase transitions of self-bound quantum objects, providing new insights into how thermal fluctuations can destroy quantum droplets.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

In a mixture of two Bose-Einstein condensates, the interactions can be tuned such that self-bound objects called quantum droplets appear. Whereas the ground states of such quantum droplets at finite temperature have been studied for three- and one-dimensional configurations, the possible fragmentation of these droplets has so far not been considered in these studies. In this paper, we show that droplets can lower their free energy by splitting or fragmenting in a combination of multiple smaller droplets and/or a gas. Three-dimensional droplets will split when the interspecies interaction strength is considerably stronger than the intraspecies interaction strength, and the number of atoms is of the same order as the minimum number of atoms necessary to form a droplet. One-dimensional droplets will fragment as long as the intraspecies and interspecies interactions strength do not vary too much in strength and the density is not to big compared with the scattering length. If the temperature rises, 1D droplets will split by expelling atoms, forming a gas of predominantly free atoms and pairs of atoms. These pairs remain present in the system up to considerably high temperatures compared to the transition temperature. Our results provide important insights on the stability of these droplets.

</details>

**Main problem.** Quantifying how gate-dependent offset charge shifts and capacitance renormalization impact the energy spectrum and anharmonicity of gatemon qubits in the weak tunneling regime.

**Main result.** The study predicts that junction asymmetry induces new gate-voltage-dependent charge offsets and that capacitance renormalization significantly influences qubit anharmonicity, providing a protocol for experimental detection.

**Method.** The authors use a many-body derived effective Hamiltonian and a WKB approach (including an extended WKB method) to solve the Schrödinger equation and analyze Andreev bound state dynamics.

**Summary.** This paper investigates the impact of gate-dependent effects on the performance of gatemon qubits. By using a many-body treatment of S-QD-S junctions, the authors show that tunneling asymmetry leads to new, detectable charge offsets. They also demonstrate that capacitance renormalization plays a crucial role in determining qubit anharmonicity. These findings provide a theoretical framework for understanding and controlling the energy spectrum of gatemon-based quantum processors.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** A gatemon qubit consisting of a superconductor-quantum dot-superconductor (S-QD-S) junction in the weak tunneling regime, modeled via an effective Hamiltonian incorporating Andreev bound states and continuum energy shifts.

**Key observables.** Qubit transition energy oscillations (E01), charge dispersion, qubit anharmonicity, and effective charge offset shifts.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Gate voltage (epsilon_g), tunneling rates (Gamma), junction asymmetry (delta_Gamma), charging energy (EC), and superconducting gap (Delta).

**Assumptions / limitations.** Weak tunneling regime, slow phase approximation, and the assumption that the even and odd parity sectors are well-separated.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows junction transparency vs. gate voltage; Figure 3 compares WKB theory to exact numerics for charge dispersion; Figure 5 compares relative contributions to anharmonicity; Figure 6 shows numerical anharmonicity vs. gate voltage.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the physical problem of gatemon dynamics, presents a many-body derived effective Hamiltonian, applies WKB and numerical methods to study charge dispersion and offsets, analyzes anharmonicity drivers, and concludes with experimental implications.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work is relevant for researchers in open quantum systems as it explores how the coupling to a continuum and quantum phase fluctuations lead to renormalization of fundamental qubit parameters like capacitance and charge offsets.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Gatemon qubits are based on a superconductor-quantum dot-superconductor (S-QD-S) junction which enables in situ electrostatic tuning via a gate electrode. For a single-channel QD this structure gives rise to two subgap Andreev bound states (ABSs), and generally leads to a richer quantum phase dynamics as compared to conventional transmons. In a recent work [Phys. Rev. B 111, 214503 (2025)] we derived the quantum phase dynamics from a many-body treatment which leads to an effective gate voltage-dependent Hamiltonian that self-consistently incorporates the phase quantization. It predicts (i) a renormalization of the junction's effective capacitance and (ii) the presence of gate voltage and occupation-dependent charge offsets in junctions with tunneling asymmetry. Here, we quantify the observable impact of these effects on the qubit's energy spectrum and anharmonicity, by studying the interplay of the two Andreev branches as a function of dot-gate voltages and junction transparencies. We show the relation of these predictions to simplified gatemon models and propose a protocol to experimentally detect the predicted charge offsets.

</details>
