<img src="2604.23743_figures/2604.23743_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> Figure 1: Quantum Reservoir Computing predictions on the Lorenz system. (a) 3D view of the Lorenz attractor showing the reference trajectory and QRC predictions. (b) Time series comparison of the x and y components (offset for clarity), demonstrating accurate tracking of chaotic dynamics.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23743_figures/2604.23743_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Figure 2: QRC circuit diagram: 5 qubits, 2 fixed reservoir layers with ring-topology entangle- ment. Input encoding maps the Lorenz state (x, y, z) to RY rotations on qubits q0–q4. Each reservoir layer applies random (frozen) single-qubit RXRY RZ rotations followed by a CNOT ring (including a wrap-around gate from q4 to q0) with post-CNOT RZ coupling. All reservoir parameters are fixed after random initialisation; only the classical linear readout is trained. The 32-dimensional measurement probability vector from each time step is concatenated over a win- dow w = 5 to produce a 160-dimensional feature for Ridge regression.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23743_figures/2604.23743_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> Figure 3: Summary comparison of QPINN and QRC (5 matched seeds). (a) Accuracy: QRC achieves 81% lower train MSE (17.1 ± 3.7 vs. 91.3 ± 21.9). (b) Training speed: QRC is approx- imately 52,000× faster due to closed-form Ridge regression.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23743_figures/2604.23743_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> Figure 4: QPINN training dynamics over 200 iterations for a representative seed (seed 0), showing convergence to train MSE ≈37.8; the mean across 5 seeds is 91.3 ± 21.9 (Table 1). (b) Gradient magnitude remains non-vanishing (∼10), indicating capacity limitations rather than barren plateaus.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23743_figures/2604.23743_fig5.jpg" alt="Figure 5">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> Figure 5: Ablation study showing the effect of temporal window size on QRC performance. Larger windows provide more temporal context, improving accuracy by 54% from w = 1 to w = 5.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** Comparing the efficiency and accuracy of two quantum machine learning architectures, QPINN and QRC, for predicting chaotic time-series dynamics on NISQ devices.

**Main result.** QRC achieves significantly lower error and trains approximately 52,000 times faster than QPINN, with its success attributed to the fixed-reservoir architecture rather than barren plateaus.

**Method.** The study uses classical simulations via Qiskit Aer to benchmark a variational approach (QPINN) against a non-variational reservoir approach (QRC) using temporal windowing.

**Summary.** This paper compares two quantum machine learning approaches for predicting chaotic dynamics: the variational QPINN and the non-variational QRC. It finds that QRC is vastly more efficient, training 52,000 times faster while providing much higher predictive accuracy. The study reveals that QPINN's failures are due to circuit capacity and competing loss terms rather than the vanishing gradient problem known as barren plateaus. The results suggest that fixed-reservoir architectures are highly promising for near-term quantum applications involving complex dynamical systems.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The study focuses on chaotic dynamical systems, specifically the Lorenz, Rössler, and Lorenz-96 systems, using a 4-5 qubit setup with a transverse-field Ising Hamiltonian for the QRC reservoir.

**Key observables.** Mean Squared Error (MSE) for training and testing, and gradient norms for diagnosing barren plateaus.

**Important parameters / regimes.** 4-5 qubits, 2-3 circuit layers, window size w=5, and training time per seed (0.2s vs 2.4h).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The study is limited to classical simulations (no hardware noise) and assumes the performance gap is driven by the training architecture rather than quantum-specific features.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows Lorenz attractor reconstructions and time-series comparisons; Figure 2 details the QRC circuit diagram; Figure 3 compares QRC and QPINN performance; Figure 4 illustrates QPINN training dynamics and loss oscillations.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of NISQ training overhead, describes the QPINN and QRC architectures, presents a comparative performance analysis on Lorenz-type systems, performs a failure analysis regarding gradients and capacity, and concludes with generalizations to other chaotic systems.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Deploying quantum machine learning on NISQ devices requires architectures where training overhead does not negate computational advantages. We systematically compare two quantum approaches for chaotic time-series prediction on the Lorenz system: a variational Quantum Physics-Informed Neural Network (QPINN) and a Quantum Reservoir Computing (QRC) framework utilizing a fixed transverse-field Ising Hamiltonian. Under matched resources ($4$--$5$ qubits, $2$--$3$ layers), QRC achieves an $81\%$ lower mean-squared error (test MSE $3.2 \pm 0.6$ vs. $47.9 \pm 36.6$ for QPINN) while training $\sim 52,000\times$ faster ($0.2$\,s vs. $\sim 2.4$\,h per seed). Drawing on the classical delay-embedding principle, we formalize a temporal windowing technique within the QRC pipeline that improves attractor reconstruction by providing bounded, structured input history. Analysis reveals that QPINN instability stems from capacity limitations and competing loss terms rather than barren plateaus; gradient norms remained large ($10^3$--$10^4$), ruling out exponential suppression at this scale. These failure modes are absent by construction in the non-variational QRC approach. We validate robustness across three canonical systems (Lorenz, Rössler, and Lorenz-96), where QRC consistently achieves low test MSE ($3.1 \pm 0.6$, $1.8 \pm 0.1$, and $12.4 \pm 0.6$, respectively) with sub-second training. Our findings suggest the fixed-reservoir architecture is a primary driver of QRC's advantage at these scales, warranting further investigation at larger qubit counts and on hardware where quantum-specific advantages are expected to emerge.

</details>

**Main problem.** Determining the asymptotic behavior (recurrence vs. transience) of 'true' self-avoiding random walks (TSAW) on general infinite, locally finite trees.

**Main result.** The paper establishes a sharp phase transition: the walk is recurrent if the branching-ruin number of the tree is less than 1/2 and transient if it is greater than 1/2, resolving a conjecture by Kosygina.

**Method.** The author uses Rubin's construction, maps the problem to a quasi-independent percolation model, and employs Markov chain analysis, martingale theory, and Tauberian theorems.

**Summary.** This paper solves an open problem regarding the behavior of 'true' self-avoiding walks on complex tree structures. It proves that these walks undergo a phase transition between being recurrent (visiting sites infinitely often) and transient (visiting sites finitely often) based on a specific geometric property of the tree called the branching-ruin number. The study connects random walk dynamics to percolation theory and the fractal dimension of the tree's boundary. This provides a rigorous mathematical framework for understanding self-repelling processes in non-Euclidean geometries.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** A 'true' self-avoiding random walk on an infinite, locally finite tree where edge weights decay exponentially with the number of traversals, $w(n) = \exp(-eta n)$, representing edge-repulsion.

**Key observables.** Recurrence and transience, branching-ruin number, ruin probability, and the Hausdorff dimension of the tree boundary.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The branching-ruin number (critical parameter), the exponential decay parameter $eta$, and the tree growth properties.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The tree is assumed to be infinite and locally finite; the results focus on trees with polynomial growth.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by defining the TSAW model and the branching-ruin number, then reduces the walk's behavior to a ruin percolation problem. It develops a Markov chain construction to study 1D paths, applies quasi-independent percolation theory to general trees, and concludes with a proof of the phase transition using flow-based criteria.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We study the asymptotic behavior of ``true" self-avoiding random walks on general infinite locally finite trees. In this model, the walk starts at the root and, at each step, from its current vertex chooses a neighboring edge to traverse with probability proportional to the current weight of that edge, where the weight of each edge after being traversed $n$ times is given by $w(n)=\exp(-βn)$. We show that the process exhibits a sharp phase transition between recurrence and transience. The critical value is determined by the branching-ruin number of the tree, which coincides with the Hausdorff dimension of the boundary of the tree under a suitable metric. We prove that the walk is almost surely transient when the branching-ruin number is greater than $1/2$, and recurrent when it is less than $1/2$. This resolves an open question posed by Kosygina.

</details>

**Authors:** Ali Al-Bayaty  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.23242v1>

**Main problem.** The lack of a standardized, technology-dependent framework for constructing ternary quantum gates (qutrits) that are analogous to contemporary binary gate designs.

**Main result.** The paper establishes a framework where complex ternary permutative and shift gates can be generated from a set of native Chrestenson (CH) and Z3 gates, and identifies a more cost-effective method for resetting the CH gate.

**Method.** A conceptual approach using gate decomposition and algebraic construction to derive one-qutrit and two-qutrit gates from pre-implemented native gates.

**Summary.** This paper proposes a conceptual framework for designing ternary quantum gates that are directly analogous to existing binary gate technologies. By using a 'technology-dependent' approach, the author demonstrates how complex qutrit gates, such as permutative and shift gates, can be constructed from simpler native gates like the Chrestenson and Z3 gates. The framework is applicable to superconducting and photonic systems and focuses on reducing the quantum gate cost. This work provides a foundation for more efficient ternary quantum circuit design.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** A theoretical ternary quantum computing system using qutrits with basis states |0>, |1>, and |2>, designed for implementation in superconducting or photonic quantum platforms.

**Key observables.** Quantum cost (total number of utilized one-qutrit and two-qutrit gates).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Rotational angles (alpha_3 and beta_3) for ternary SWAP gates; complex roots of unity (omega).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The framework assumes the existence of pre-implemented, technology-dependent native ternary gates, specifically Z3 and various Ternary Superposition Gates (TSGs).

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows a 3D Bloch sphere analogy for binary gates; Figures 2-4 illustrate two-qutrit circuits for CCH, C12, and C+1 gates; Figure 5 provides a summary of the proposed gate framework; Figure 6 shows a conceptual ternary SWAP gate design.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of ternary gate standardization, presents three design postulations for constructing the Chrestenson gate, derives permutative and shift gates, extends the construction to controlled two-qutrit gates, and concludes with optimization and future work.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a blueprint for scaling quantum architectures from qubits to qutrits, which could significantly increase computational space and efficiency in hardware-specific implementations.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

This paper introduces a conceptual framework of technology-dependent ternary quantum gates that could be implemented and fabricated into various superconducting and photonic quantum systems for operating 3-valued quantum bits (qutrits). The "technology-dependent" term means that such ternary quantum gates are on-purpose designed analogy to the contemporary binary quantum gates. Conceptually, the final built technology-dependent one-qutrit and two-qutrit ternary quantum gates are Chrestenson, Z3, 01, 02, 12, +1, +2, and their corresponding controlled gates, respectively.

</details>

<img src="2604.23535_figures/2604.23535_fig6.jpg" alt="Figure 6">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 6.</b> Fig. 9: a) Three grayscale test images. b) The resulting edge maps our algorithm outputs.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** The development of an efficient, scalable quantum algorithm for image edge detection that avoids the need for classical post-processing and reduces the high qubit/gate complexity of previous NEQR-based methods.

**Main result.** The paper presents a fully quantum algorithm that achieves exponential compression of the output edge map and polynomial-time improvements in both time and space complexity compared to existing quantum edge detection models.

**Method.** The algorithm uses NEQR encoding for grayscale images, implements gradient computation via cyclic shifts and quantum arithmetic, and employs a novel Quantum Partitioning Algorithm with a Fast Threshold Phase Oracle (FTPO) for efficient thresholding.

**Summary.** This paper introduces a new quantum algorithm for edge detection that operates entirely within a quantum circuit without requiring classical post-processing. By using the NEQR representation and a novel phase oracle for thresholding, the authors reduce the number of required ancilla qubits and improve runtime complexity. The approach includes a unique direction-aware shifting mechanism to improve edge localization accuracy. Ultimately, it demonstrates a practical quantum advantage for image processing tasks by achieving exponential compression of the output.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The algorithm operates within the quantum circuit model using the Novel Enhanced Quantum Representation (NEQR) to encode grayscale pixel intensities deterministically in the computational basis.

**Key observables.** The magnitude of the intensity gradient (delta I) and the sign bit of the gradient.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Image resolution (2^n x 2^n), grayscale levels (2^q), and the intensity threshold (T).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The efficiency of the thresholding module assumes that thresholds have a constant number of leading zeros, and the NEQR encoding step is assumed to have a manageable overhead.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1-2 show quantum arithmetic submodules (QRCA, MAJ, UMA); Fig 3-5 detail the algorithm pipeline, pixel neighborhood generation, and subtraction modules; Fig 6-8 illustrate direction-aware shifting and the FTPO implementation; Fig 9 shows experimental edge map results.

**Paper structure.** The paper identifies limitations in classical and prior quantum edge detection, introduces the NEQR-based pipeline (encoding, gradient, shifting, thresholding), provides detailed circuit constructions for arithmetic and partitioning, performs complexity analysis, and concludes with experimental validation.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

This work introduces a novel quantum algorithm for gradient-based edge detection that operates entirely within the quantum circuit model. Grayscale images are encoded using the Novel Enhanced Quantum Representation (NEQR), allowing exact arithmetic on pixel intensities. Directional gradients are computed by generating superpositions of neighboring pixels via cyclic shift operations and performing subtraction with an exact quantum arithmetic circuit. To refine accuracy, we introduce a direction-aware shifting mechanism that aligns edges with the darker side of intensity transitions. Our novel Quantum Partitioning Algorithm enables efficient in-place thresholding of edge candidates. This work exhibits polynomial-time improvements and optimizes the ancilla count compared to previous NEQR-based quantum edge detection algorithms. These results demonstrate a resource-efficient and fully quantum approach to edge detection, highlighting a practical quantum advantage in image processing.

</details>

**Authors:** Yudong Li, Wenkui Shi, Chunfa Wang, Zhihao Qian, Zhiqiang Feng, Moubin Liu  
**Type:** both · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.24159v1>

</figure>

**Main problem.** Addressing the computational bottleneck in Smoothed Particle Hydrodynamics (SPH) by bridging the gap between the linear nature of qubits and the nonlinear dynamics of fluid mechanics.

**Main result.** The proposed hybrid crossed-QMLP architecture achieves fitting accuracy comparable to classical SPH, significantly outperforming pure quantum circuits in generalization and convergence.

**Method.** A sequential hybrid quantum-classical framework using variational quantum circuits (VQC) with Pauli-Z expectation values and classical neural network layers for bidirectional data compression.

**Summary.** This paper introduces a novel 'quantum-intelligent SPH' paradigm that integrates quantum kernel networks with classical Smoothed Particle Hydrodynamics. By using a hybrid 'crossed' architecture where quantum circuits are sandwiched between classical neural layers, the authors overcome the generalization failures of pure quantum circuits. The method demonstrates that quantum-classical hybrid models can match the accuracy of traditional classical SPH for complex fluid simulations. This work establishes a foundation for mapping unstructured Lagrangian particle topologies into quantum circuits.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** A hierarchy of Lagrangian quantum network models (QMLP and QCNN) applied to Smoothed Particle Hydrodynamics (SPH), a meshfree particle-based method for fluid dynamics.

**Key observables.** Pauli-Z expectation values, density, pressure, velocity vectors, and relative norm errors.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Trainable variational parameters (theta, phi, lambda), learning rates, and spatial parameters like Euclidean distance and particle volume.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The study operates within the constraints of the NISQ era, assuming that classical layers can effectively mitigate barren plateaus and quantum noise.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate Bloch sphere representations, QNN/QMLP/QCNN architectures, the hybrid quantum-classical pathway, SPH discretization, and numerical comparisons of error, convergence, and fluid field reconstructions.

**Paper structure.** The paper progresses from defining the limitations of classical SPH and the potential of quantum computing to detailing specific hybrid architectures (QMLP, QCNN, and crossed-QMLP), followed by numerical validation through vortex reconstruction and scalar field advection tests, and concludes with a discussion of NISQ-era limitations.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Currently, quantum computing and artificial intelligence are driving revolutionary advancements in computational science. This study pioneers the integration of quantum kernel networks on smoothed particle hydrodynamics (SPH). SPH has matured into a highly versatile meshfree/particle method, exceptionally suited for tracking spatiotemporal trajectories and dynamic modeling phenomena. We developed a hierarchy of Lagrangian quantum network models built upon an improved quantum multilayer perceptron (QMLP). Specifically, a sequential hybrid quantum-classical framework is constructed, utilizing Pauli-Z expectation values over traditional probability outputs to ensure robust gradient-based optimization and mitigate barren plateaus. It combines smoothing kernels with quantum learning, establishing a novel quantum intelligent SPH paradigm. The framework is validated through some continuous benchmarks on eurypalynous quantum neural networks, static multi-level nebula vortex interference reconstructions and transient scalar field advectional tests. Numerical results demonstrate that while pure elementary quantum circuits struggle with parameter-specific generalization in unstructured domains, the proposed hybrid crossed-QMLP seamlessly matches the fitting accuracy of classical SPH in quantum optimized space. Although this approach currently faces limitations in computational efficiency and hardware implementation, it nonetheless paves the way for a novel investigation into quantum SPH, by mapping unstructured Lagrangian particle topologies into integrated quantum circuits.

</details>

### [A Spectral Gap Informed Parameter Schedule for QAOA](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.24580v1)

**Authors:** Kieran McDowall, Konstantinos Georgopoulos, Petros Wallden  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.24580v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>

</figure>

<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 6.</b> Fig. 6. Solving the MIS problem with degree 3 graphs where the QAOA depth p required to reach an optimal solution threshold P th s at different problem sizes is plotted.</figcaption>
</figure>

The shaded region correspond to the standard deviation error of the noisy points. In Figure 11 we plot the noiseless LR–QAOA and SGIR–QAOA showing behaviour at larger p .</figcaption>
</figure>

The gaps for n = 6 −12 are calculated which inform the SGIR– QAOA schedule for n &gt; 12. At each n the experiment is repeated 10 times with a different randomly generated instance. A log scale is used on the y-axis. The error in the fit for the exponential scaling coefficient is included in the legend.</figcaption>
</figure>

The gaps for n = 6 −12 are calculated which inform the SGIR–QAOA schedule for n &gt; 12. At each n the experiment is repeated 10 times with a different randomly generated instance. A log scale is used on the y-axis. The error in the fit for the exponential scaling coefficient is included in the legend.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24580_figures/2604.24580_fig10.jpg" alt="Figure 10">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 10.</b> Fig. 9. The percentage improvement of SGIR-QAOA against LR- QAOA, p = 10, with the minimum spectral gap of the problem (Pearson correlation coefficient ρ = −0.68).</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** Finding optimal variational parameters for the Quantum Approximate Optimization Algorithm (QAOA) is computationally difficult, and existing 'de-variationalized' schedules like the Linear Ramp (LR-QAOA) fail to account for the closing of the spectral gap.

**Main result.** The proposed Spectral Gap Informed Ramp (SGIR-QAOA) outperforms LR-QAOA on Grover's problem and the Maximum Independent Set (MIS) problem, requiring shallower depths to reach target success probabilities and demonstrating better exponential scaling.

**Method.** The authors develop a parameter schedule that uses smooth ramps informed by the spectral gap of an adiabatic Hamiltonian, performing slower evolution where the gap is small, and use extrapolation techniques to scale the method to larger problem sizes.

**Summary.** This paper introduces a new parameter scheduling strategy for the QAOA algorithm called SGIR-QAOA. By using information about the spectral gap of an adiabatic Hamiltonian, the algorithm slows down its evolution during periods where the gap is small, effectively mitigating diabatic transitions. The authors demonstrate that this approach provides superior performance and better scaling for both Grover's search and the Maximum Independent Set problem compared to standard linear ramp methods. Furthermore, the method proves to be scalable through gap extrapolation and remains effective even in the presence of depolarizing noise.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The study utilizes the QAOA framework with alternating mixer and cost Hamiltonians, specifically testing on Grover's search problem, Erdős–Rényi random graphs, and 3-regular sparse graphs for the Maximum Independent Set problem.

**Key observables.** Optimal solution probability (Ps), required QAOA depth (p), and the minimum spectral gap (gmin).

**Important parameters / regimes.** QAOA depth (p), problem size (n), spectral gap (g), and depolarizing noise strength.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The method assumes that the spectral gap information from smaller system sizes can be accurately extrapolated to larger system sizes where exact calculation is impossible.

**Figures summary.** Figures show the eigenvalue spectrum of Grover's problem, comparisons of success probability (Ps) across different schedules, plots of required depth (p) vs. problem size (n), and the correlation between performance improvement and the minimum spectral gap.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of parameter optimization in QAOA, presents the SGIR-QAOA method, validates it using Grover's problem, extends the analysis to combinatorial optimization (MIS) on various graph types, demonstrates scalability via extrapolation, and tests robustness against depolarizing noise.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

A challenge with the Quantum Approximate Optimisation Algorithm (QAOA), and variational algorithms in general, is finding good variational parameters, a task which in itself can be NP-hard. Recent work has sought to de-variationalise QAOA by picking well-informed guesses for the variational parameters. The Linear Ramp QAOA (LR-QAOA) achieves this by using parameter schedules inspired by the quantum adiabatic algorithm. We go a step further and use spectral gap information from an adiabatic Hamiltonian, with the QAOA mixer Hamiltonian as our initial Hamiltonian, to make smooth ramps which we call Spectral Gap Informed Ramps (SGIR-QAOA). SGIR-QAOA schedules perform slow evolution where the spectral gap of the adiabatic Hamiltonian is small. We show that SGIR-QAOA has performance improvements over LR-QAOA on Grover's problem at constant depth and that SGIR-QAOA requires shorter depths to achieve the same optimal solution probability. We then show that these performance benefits extend to a problem with potential practical applications -- the Maximum Independent Set (MIS) problem. Finally, we demonstrate the scalability of the SGIR-QAOA method using extrapolated spectral gap information for scales that the spectral gap cannot be exactly evaluated, and show that the advantage appears to persist under mild depolarising noise.

</details>

**Main problem.** Investigating the applicability of a Quantum Amplitude Redistribution Algorithm (QARA) to the data filtering problem, specifically for removing outliers from digitized signals and images.

**Main result.** The algorithm achieves a computational complexity of O(n) relative to bit-width, providing a significant speedup over classical O(M log M) median filters for large arrays, though it performs slightly lower in filtering quality.

**Method.** The authors develop a quantum circuit model using controlled rotations and a recursive unitary matrix construction to redistribute probability amplitudes, then compare its performance against classical median filters on 1D signals and 2D images.

**Summary.** This paper proposes a quantum algorithm designed to replace classical median filters for tasks like signal and image denoising. By using quantum amplitude redistribution, the algorithm can identify and filter outliers with a complexity that depends on the bit-width rather than the array size. While the filtering quality is slightly lower than classical methods, the potential for significant computational speedup on large datasets is demonstrated. The study includes testing on 1D signals and 2D images across various resolutions and formats.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** A quantum circuit model using n-qubit registers, where Register D contains array elements and Register C contains indices, utilizing a parameterized unitary transformation R_n(phi).

**Key observables.** The measured index j from the counter register, which yields the filtered value A_j, and the probability distribution P(C_k) of measuring specific indices.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Array size M, number of qubits n, reference value r, rotation angle phi, and window width for image processing.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The algorithm assumes input data can be represented as non-negative n-bit integers and requires elements in the processed array to be distinct for optimal performance.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the quantum loading process, the schematic of probability redistribution, the recursive gate decomposition, and comparative results of filtering noisy signals and various image formats (PNG, TIFF, JPG).

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by defining the algorithm's input/output, provides a mathematical proof of the transformation's unitarity, details an optimized gate decomposition, and concludes with a comparative performance analysis against classical filters.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

This paper presents an analysis of the applicability of a quantum amplitude redistribution algorithm to the data filtering problem and the results of modeling the algorithm's operation in comparison with a median filter.

</details>

<a id="paper-2604.23777"></a>
### [Architecture-aware Unitary Synthesis](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23777v1)

**Authors:** Frans Perkkola, Arianne Meijer-van de Griend, Jukka K. Nurminen  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.23777v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23777_figures/2604.23777_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> Fig. 1. A three-qubit unitary example of the CNOT merging optimization procedure. a) Decompose only the A and C multiplexers. b) Decompose the uniformly controlled Rz gates. c) Migrate the Hadamard gates through the CNOT targets. d) Merge VC, WA and the CZ gates into the central gate B.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23777_figures/2604.23777_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Fig. 3. Four-qubit uniformly controlled Rz gates. a) The UC gate constructed using a binary reflected Gray code as in [21]. b) An equivalent construction for the UC gate using a different Gray code.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23777_figures/2604.23777_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> Fig. 5. Efficient construction of a three-qubit UC gate using the long range CNOT ladders. a) The logical decomposition of the UC gate, as in Fig. 1. b) The decomposition of the UC gate on physical qubits v0, v1 and v2 on a path graph using the binary reflected Gray code. c) A more efficient decomposition, using the heuristic of removing the nearest neighbor CNOT gate after a long range CNOT gate. d) The final decomposition after merging the rightmost CNOT gates into the neighboring unitary gate, as in Fig. 1.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23777_figures/2604.23777_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> Fig. 4. An example of the swapping procedure for a 6-qubit UC gate on the IQM Garnet quantum hardware. a) The physical qubit 10 is selected as the target. Physical qubits 9, 5, 4, 2 and 1 are the control qubits of the UC gate. b) Physical qubits 10 and 5 are swapped. This gives a more efficient construction for the UC gate, as the target qubit 10 is now overall closer to the rest of the physical qubits.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** The inefficient transpilation of generic n-qubit unitary transformations into hardware-specific gate sequences, which leads to high CNOT gate counts and long compilation runtimes.

**Main result.** The proposed architecture-aware method achieves up to a 36% reduction in CNOT counts and up to a 553x speedup in transpilation time compared to leading compilers like TKet, Qiskit, and PennyLane.

**Method.** An integrated approach that embeds transpilation into the recursive block-ZXZ decomposition using greedy qubit mapping, adaptive Gray code selection, and a CNOT reduction heuristic for long-range ladders.

**Summary.** This paper presents a new way to compile quantum circuits by making the synthesis process aware of the underlying hardware architecture from the start. Instead of treating hardware constraints as a final step, the method integrates qubit mapping and gate optimization directly into the mathematical decomposition of the unitary. This results in significantly fewer CNOT gates and much faster compilation speeds. It is particularly effective for scaling up the synthesis of larger unitaries on real-world superconducting hardware.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The method is benchmarked on superconducting quantum architectures, specifically the 20-qubit IQM Garnet (square lattice) and the 156-qubit IBM Marrakesh (heavy-hex) topologies.

**Key observables.** CNOT gate count, transpilation runtime, and CNOT gate ratio relative to the theoretical lower bound.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Qubit counts ranging from 3 to 11; time limit of 30 minutes for scalability testing.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The method focuses on exact unitary synthesis rather than numerical approximations and assumes hardware connectivity is restricted to adjacent physical qubits.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows CNOT merging in a 3-qubit unitary; Figure 2 visualizes a long-range CNOT ladder; Figure 3 compares standard vs. optimized Gray codes; Figure 4 illustrates qubit swapping; Figure 5 shows the logical-to-physical decomposition of a UC gate.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of post-processing inefficiency, details the architecture-aware synthesis algorithm (including mapping, Gray code, and swapping techniques), presents benchmarking results against industry-standard transpilers, and discusses scalability and limitations.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We present a novel architecture-aware transpilation method for exact general unitary gate synthesis on superconducting quantum hardware. Our approach is tightly integrated with the optimized block-ZXZ decomposition, exploiting its recursive structure to make hardware-aware decisions at each level of the recursion rather than treating transpilation as an independent post-processing step. The method introduces three key techniques: a greedy qubit mapping strategy that minimizes pairwise distances between physical qubits, an adaptive Gray code selection combined with qubit swapping that optimizes the construction of uniformly controlled Rz gates for the target topology, and a heuristic for reducing CNOT gates by exploiting the structure of long-range CNOT ladders. We benchmark our method against TKet, Qiskit, and Pennylane on the 20-qubit IQM Garnet (square lattice) and the 156-qubit IBM Marrakesh (heavy-hex) architectures with qubit counts ranging from 3 to 11. Our method achieves CNOT count reductions of up to 36 percent on the IQM Garnet and up to 34 percent on the IBM Marrakesh compared to the best competing transpiler, while simultaneously achieving transpilation speedups of up to 553x. Furthermore, our method is the only one capable of transpiling circuits beyond 10 qubits within a 30-minute time limit across both architectures.

</details>

### [CAbLECAR: efficiently scheduling QLDPC codes on a tileable spin qubit chip with shuttling](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.24739v1)

**Authors:** Jason D. Chadwick, Frederic T. Chong  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.24739v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>

<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> Fig. 1. Overview of the CAbLECAR framework. (Left) QLDPC codes demand complex, non-local parity checks, represented by arbitrary connec- tions between data qubits (black) and ancilla qubits (colored). (Middle) To map these abstract requirements onto physical hardware, CAbLECAR applies noise-aware circuit tailoring to mitigate shuttling-induced dephasing and utilizes a novel optimization algorithm Q-SIPP to compute collision-free routing paths. (Right) The output is a hardware-compatible, time-optimized shuttling schedule where mobile ancilla qubits physically route along a tileable grid architecture to interact with stationary data qubits. Applied to efficient QLDPC codes, these optimized...</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24739_figures/2604.24739_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Fig. 2. Depiction of the structure of a quantum error correction code. A code block consists of a group of data qubits (black) and the logical code state is stabilized by repeated measurement of X and Z stabilizers. Typically, each stabilizer is measured using an ancilla qubit, which is initialized, entangled with the relevant data qubits, and then measured. Example X and Z checks are shown in pink and blue. The measurement results must then be decoded to determine whether any correction is needed to maintain an error-free code state. In this figure, X and Z checks both have weight 4, meaning that each ancilla qubit interacts with four data qubits in a syndrome extraction (SE) cycle.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24739_figures/2604.24739_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> Fig. 3. The tiled unit cell architecture considered in this work. On the left is a closeup of one unit cell, which has (a) one interaction zone where gates are performed, (b) one readout port, where a qubit can be measured, and (c) shuttling channels, where qubits can travel within and between unit cells. (d) shows a simplified version of the unit cell, and (e) shows how the unit cells can be tiled into a grid-like arrangement.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24739_figures/2604.24739_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> Fig. 4. Tailoring syndrome extraction circuits to mitigate shuttling noise. (a) Ancilla qubits are shuttled between CNOTs with data qubits. Shown here is a Z ancilla qubit, which is sensitive to shuttling errors. (b) Shuttling errors are heavily biased towards Z errors (dephasing). When such an error occurs, it propagates through the following CNOTs and manifests as one or more Z errors on the data qubits. (c) The tailored version of the circuit inserts H gates before and after each shuttling period. (d) This switches the Z error to an X error during each of the remaining CNOTs, preventing error propagation. The consequence is an X error on the ancilla qubit at the end of the circuit, which...</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24739_figures/2604.24739_fig5.jpg" alt="Figure 5">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> Fig. 6. Snapshot of optimized ancilla movement schedule for the [[72, 12, 6]] bicycle code. Data qubits (black) are in fixed positions, one per unit cell. Ancilla qubits (blue and pink) are shuttled between data qubits, performing CNOTs along the way. Colored arrows show ancilla movement over a 1µs time period.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24739_figures/2604.24739_fig6.jpg" alt="Figure 6">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 6.</b> Fig. 5. Performance of the surface code shuttling schedule on the unit cell architecture [40] under varying shuttling noise, with and without CAbLECAR circuit tailoring. Tailoring allows the surface code to tolerate 5-10× higher shuttle error rates on the ancilla qubits. This increased noise resilience paves the way for considering more complex codes with longer shuttling paths.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24739_figures/2604.24739_fig7.jpg" alt="Figure 7">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 7.</b> Fig. 7. A visualization of the Q-SIPP scheduling process resolving a spatial collision. Ancilla B must route to di but its shortest path through IL is temporarily blocked by Ancilla A. Instead of forcing B to take a collision-free detour, Q-SIPP projects the timeline of IL into continuous safe intervals. By calculating the heuristic cost h(s) of the detour versus the known penalty twait to utilize the next available safe interval (i1), the algorithm correctly determines that waiting at IL yields the time-optimal schedule.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24739_figures/2604.24739_fig8.jpg" alt="Figure 8">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 8.</b> Fig. 8. Comparing average shuttle distance for CAbLECAR schedules compared to ideal minimum shuttle counts (assuming ancilla qubits can move freely past each other without collisions). We also compare to a “lockstep” schedule (all ancilla qubits moving together in a pattern) for codes where such a schedule exists. We find that CAbLECAR outperforms all lockstep schedules and is close to the collision-free ideal, with a minor 12.5% overhead (geomean). CAbLECAR reduces shuttle cost for Tile and BB codes by 19% and 86%, respectively, compared to hand-optimized lockstep schedules.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24739_figures/2604.24739_fig9.jpg" alt="Figure 9">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 9.</b> Fig. 9. Comparing shuttling overhead to efficiency factor kd2/n. Higher- efficiency codes from the Radial and Tile families improve encoding efficiency by more than their increase in shuttle count.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24739_figures/2604.24739_fig10.jpg" alt="Figure 10">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 10.</b> Figure 8 shows the average number of unit-edge shuttles per ancilla qubit across the studied code instances. We compare the CAbLECAR optimized schedules against two baselines. First, the “ideal collision-free” schedule assumes that shuttled qubits are allowed to occupy the same shuttling channel and even pass by one another without collisions. Compared to this baseline, the CAbLECAR schedules (which prevent simulta- neous occupation of any shuttling channel) incur a geomean 12.5% shuttling overhead, demonstrating surprisingly near-</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** The difficulty of implementing high-rate QLDPC codes on semiconductor spin qubit architectures due to the mismatch between non-local code connectivity and the physical constraints of qubit shuttling, specifically regarding collision avoidance and shuttling-induced dephasing.

**Main result.** The development of the CAbLECAR framework and Q-SIPP algorithm enables efficient, collision-free ancilla routing, demonstrating that specific QLDPC codes can outperform surface codes by orders of magnitude in logical error rate when shuttling errors are sufficiently low.

**Method.** The authors use a noise-aware circuit tailoring technique to mitigate dephasing and a quantum-adapted Safe Interval Path Planning (Q-SIPP) algorithm for automated, time-optimized ancilla scheduling, validated via Stim circuit-level simulations.

**Summary.** This paper addresses the challenge of implementing high-rate QLDPC codes on spin qubit platforms that rely on physical qubit shuttling. By introducing a new scheduling algorithm (Q-SIPP) and a noise-aware circuit tailoring technique, the authors show how to mitigate dephasing and avoid collisions during ancilla movement. Their results demonstrate that under appropriate noise regimes, these advanced codes can provide massive improvements in both encoding efficiency and logical error rates compared to the standard surface code.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** A tileable, modular semiconductor spin qubit architecture featuring a 2D grid of stationary data qubits and mobile ancilla qubits that are physically transported (shuttled) through interaction and readout zones.

**Key observables.** Logical error rate (pL), encoding efficiency (kd^2/n), shuttling overhead (ancilla movement counts), and physical error rates (p_sh, p_CX, p_H).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Shuttling error rates (ranging from 10^-1 to 10^-4), code parameters [[n, k, d]], and gate fidelities (approx 99.9%).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The evaluation assumes a simplified grid layout for data qubits and relies on the assumption that extremely low shuttling error rates (10^-4) are physically achievable.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the CAbLECAR framework overview; Figure 2 illustrates QEC syndrome extraction; Figure 3 depicts the tiled unit cell architecture; Figure 4 compares baseline vs. tailored circuits; Figure 5 shows logical error rate vs. shuttle error; Figure 6 displays an optimized movement schedule; Figure 7 visualizes collision resolution; Figure 8 compares shuttling overhead; Figure 9 shows encoding efficiency vs. overhead; Figure 12 compares various code families across error regimes.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the scalability bottleneck of surface codes, presents the CAbLECAR framework and Q-SIPP algorithm, details the noise-aware circuit tailoring technique, evaluates various QLDPC code families through circuit-level simulations, and concludes by identifying the performance crossover regime relative to the surface code.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Semiconductor spin qubits are a promising platform for large-scale quantum computing, but have yet to take full advantage of the broad class of quantum low-density parity check (QLDPC) codes, which promise high encoding rates and efficient logic but require nonlocal connectivity between physical qubits. In this work, we investigate the implementation of QLDPC codes on a tileable, shuttling-based spin qubit architecture. By tailoring syndrome extraction circuits to the shuttling noise model, we significantly improve on previous surface code proposals and extend the feasible shuttling range of the architecture by 5-10x, enabling the implementation of more complex codes with long-range interactions. Taking inspiration from the field of robotics, we develop a coordinated shuttle scheduling algorithm that supports arbitrary codes and use it to benchmark the logical performance of a variety of promising code families. We find that the optimized schedules are up to 86% faster than hand-optimized schedules for certain code families. Through detailed circuit-level simulations, we identify specific QLDPC codes that improve upon prior surface code implementations by orders of magnitude, increasing encoding efficiency and reducing logical error rates. This work demonstrates the potential of shuttling-based spin qubit hardware platforms for scalable and efficient fault-tolerant quantum computation.

</details>

<a id="paper-2604.24675"></a>
### [Conformal Invariance of the large-$N$ limit of the $O(N)$ universality class](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.24675v1)

**Authors:** Santiago Cabrera, Gonzalo De Polsi, Adam Rançon, Nicolás Wschebor  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.24675v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24675_figures/2604.24675_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> FIG. 2. Diagrammatic representation of two contributions to ˆΓ(4) vertex. Plain lines represent propagators. Integration over the loop is assumed. Dashed lines with a dot represent ˆΓ(2) vertices. The loop parts are equal to I(3) or I(4). Signs and factors N are omitted (see Eq. (42)). For each diagram, there are two possible extra permutations of momenta.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24675_figures/2604.24675_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> FIG. 4. Independent diagrams contributing to the only channel in H(3), where the continuous internal lines correspond to propagators given in Eq. (64) and vertices are just 1.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24675_figures/2604.24675_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> FIG. 5. Independent diagrams contributing to H(4), where the continuous internal lines correspond to propagators given in Eq .(64) and vertices are just 1. Notice that in every column, diagrams are equivalent up to a cyclic permutation of the explicit external momenta (p1, p2, p3), while diagrams within a row are equivalent up to a cyclic rotation of all external momenta. We thus refer to each row of diagrams as a different “channel”.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates whether scale invariance automatically enhances to full conformal invariance at renormalization group fixed points in dimensions d > 2, specifically for the O(N) universality class.

**Main result.** The authors provide two rigorous proofs—one functional and one vertex-by-vertex—demonstrating that the large-N limit of the O(N) universality class is conformally invariant at the Wilson-Fisher fixed point.

**Method.** The study utilizes the Non-Perturbative Renormalization Group (NPRG) framework, employing Legendre transforms and analyzing modified Ward identities induced by an infrared regulator.

**Summary.** This paper provides a rigorous mathematical proof that the large-N limit of the O(N) universality class possesses full conformal symmetry. Using the non-perturbative renormalization group framework, the authors show that the scale-invariant Wilson-Fisher fixed point satisfies the necessary Ward identities for special conformal transformations. The work clarifies how the theory's structure reorganizes to realize this symmetry and provides general insights into the conditions for symmetry enhancement at criticality.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The model is the O(N)-symmetric scalar phi^4 theory, which includes universality classes such as the Ising, XY, and Heisenberg models, analyzed in the large-N limit.

**Key observables.** Correlation functions, critical exponents (such as nu), scaling dimensions (D_phi, D_sigma), and the energy-momentum tensor.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Dimension d (2 < d < 4), the large-N limit, the renormalization group scale k, and the vanishing anomalous dimension (eta = 0).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The proofs are specifically restricted to the large-N limit where the anomalous dimension eta is zero, and they assume the system is at the Wilson-Fisher fixed point.

**Figures summary.** The figures consist of diagrammatic representations of various vertex functions (H2, H3, gamma4) and their constituent loop channels and momentum-dependent contributions.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of conformal symmetry in d > 2, establishes the NPRG framework and modified Ward identities, presents a functional proof of conformal invariance, provides a vertex-by-vertex proof in Fourier space, and concludes with detailed mathematical appendices verifying the algebraic identities.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Conformal symmetry is expected to be realized in many equilibrium statistical mechanical systems at criticality. Although this is certainly true in two-dimensional systems, the three-dimensional case is subtler, and only a few proofs exist, only so in very specific cases. In this work, we give two proofs for the large $N$ limit of the $O(N)$ universality class within the non-perturbative renormalization group framework: one functional, and one vertex-by-vertex in Fourier space. While doing so, we unveil how the theory is structured in order for conformal symmetry to be realized. As a consequence, we shed light on what to expect, on rather general grounds, for a theory to be conformally invariant.

</details>

### [Do Quantum Transformers Help? A Systematic VQC Architecture Comparison on Tabular Benchmarks](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23931v1)

**Authors:** Chi-Sheng Chen, En-Jui Kuo  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.23931v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>

(b) ResNet-VQC with dashed skip connections; the dimension-mismatched branch uses a linear projection W · z2. (c) QT — hybrid transformer with classical multi-head attention and a quantum VQC FFN, LayerNorm retained. (d) FQT — fully quantum attention via transpose-and-entangle T-qubit VQCs, with no LayerNorm by default.</figcaption>
</figure>

Bottom: 5-qubit cross-token VQC (depth 2) used in FQT’s quantum attention — each qubit corresponds to one token, enabling all-to-all entanglement across tokens.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23931_figures/2604.23931_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> Fig. 3. Parameter efficiency across three regression datasets. Each point is a model; x-axis is parameter count (log scale), y-axis is 3-seed mean test R2. VQCs (blue circles) achieve competitive accuracy with far fewer parameters than tree-based methods (red diamonds), while MLPs (green squares) at equal capacity (MLP-720) underperform FC-VQC on Boston but catch up on larger datasets.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23931_figures/2604.23931_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> Fig. 4. Training and validation loss curves on Boston Housing (3-seed mean ± std shading). FC-VQC converges fastest; QT and FQT show higher variance across seeds.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23931_figures/2604.23931_fig5.jpg" alt="Figure 5">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> Fig. 5. Left: fidelity distributions for VQC at various depths vs. Haar-random reference (dashed). Right: KL divergence (log scale).</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">

<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 6.</b> Fig. 6. Training and validation loss on CA Housing (n=20,640). Unlike Boston, QT (orange) converges to a lower val loss than FC-VQC (blue), consistent with its higher R2 on larger datasets.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">

<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 7.</b> Fig. 7. Prediction vs. ground truth on Boston Housing test set (best-seed run). Points near the diagonal indicate accurate predictions. FC-VQC tracks ground truth closely despite using only 720 parameters.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** The study addresses the lack of systematic architectural comparisons for Variational Quantum Circuits (VQCs) on classical tabular data, specifically investigating the trade-off between accuracy and parameter efficiency in quantum transformer architectures.

**Main result.** FC-VQCs achieve 90-96% of the R2 of attention-based VQCs with 40-50% fewer parameters and outperform equal-capacity MLPs, while Fully Quantum Transformers (FQT) show superior robustness to depolarizing noise compared to hybrid Quantum Transformers (QT).

**Method.** The authors perform a systematic empirical comparison of four VQC families across five regression and classification benchmarks using PennyLane simulations, evaluating metrics like R2, accuracy, expressibility (KL divergence), and noise robustness.

**Summary.** This paper provides a systematic comparison of different variational quantum circuit architectures for processing tabular data. It finds that simpler fully-connected VQCs are highly parameter-efficient and often outperform more complex quantum transformer models on small datasets. Notably, it demonstrates that fully quantum architectures are more robust to depolarizing noise than hybrid architectures. These findings offer practical guidance for designing efficient quantum machine learning models for near-term quantum hardware.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The study uses various VQC architectures (FC-VQC, ResNet-VQC, QT, and FQT) based on a parameterized ansatz of single-qubit rotations and CNOT entangling layers, applied to classical tabular datasets.

**Key observables.** R2 (coefficient of determination), classification accuracy, macro-averaged F1 score, KL-divergence (for expressibility), and parameter efficiency.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Circuit depth (d), number of attention heads (H), number of tokens (T), and depolarizing noise rate (pd).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The study assumes the relevance of NISQ-era constraints and relies on classical CPU simulations of quantum circuits.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 provides an architectural overview; Figure 2 shows circuit diagrams; Figure 3 compares accuracy vs. parameter count; Figure 4 shows training/validation loss curves; Figure 5 shows fidelity distributions; Figure 6 shows convergence on larger datasets; Figure 7 shows prediction vs. ground truth.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the architectural problem, defines the four VQC families, details the experimental setup and benchmarks, presents comparative results on accuracy and efficiency, analyzes expressibility and noise robustness, and concludes with practical guidance for NISQ deployment.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Variational quantum circuits (VQCs) are a leading approach to quantum machine learning on near-term devices, yet it remains unclear which circuit architecture yields the best accuracy-parameter trade-off on classical tabular data. We present a systematic empirical comparison of four VQC families -- multi-layer fully-connected (FC-VQC), residual (ResNet-VQC), hybrid quantum-classical transformer (QT), and fully quantum transformer (FQT) -- across five regression and classification benchmarks. Our key findings are: \textbf{(i)}~FC-VQCs achieve 90-96\% of the $R^2$ of attention-based VQCs while using 40-50\% fewer parameters, and consistently outperform equal-capacity MLPs (mean $R^2{=}0.829$ vs.\ MLP$_{720}$'s $0.753$ on Boston Housing, 3-seed average); \textbf{(ii)}~FC-VQC's Type~4 inter-block connectivity provides partial cross-token mixing that approximates the role of attention -- explicit quantum self-attention yields only marginal gains on most datasets while significantly increasing parameter count; \textbf{(iii)}~expressibility saturates at circuit depth~${\approx}\,3$, explaining why shallow VQCs already cover the Hilbert space effectively; \textbf{(iv)}~LayerNorm on the fully quantum transformer improves classification accuracy, suggesting normalization is important when all operations are quantum; \textbf{(v)}~in our noise study on Boston Housing, FQT degrades gracefully under depolarizing noise while QT collapses. All results are validated across three random seeds. These findings provide practical architectural guidance for deploying VQCs on near-term quantum hardware.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

<a id="paper-2604.23490"></a>
### [Efficient Quantum Fully Homomorphic Encryption](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23490v1)

**Authors:** Fengxia Liu, Zixian Gong, Kun Tian, Yi Zhang, Zhiming Zheng, Maozhi Xu  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.23490v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23490_figures/2604.23490_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> Figure 1: QFHE Quantum Circuit: T-gate Evaluation.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23490_figures/2604.23490_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Figure 2: MBQC Dependency Flow - Adaptive Control via Flow Functions</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** Existing Quantum Fully Homomorphic Encryption (QFHE) schemes suffer from an exponential growth in quantum resource demands, specifically the number of EPR pairs required for quantum gadgets, relative to circuit depth.

**Main result.** The authors achieve an exponential improvement in efficiency, reducing the gadget size from O(lambda^2.58) to O(lambda log^2 lambda) EPR pairs, representing a concrete improvement factor of 2^15 to 2^18 for standard security parameters.

**Method.** The paper introduces a unified framework integrating Modular Arithmetic Programs (MAP), the garden-hose model, and Measurement-Based Quantum Computation (MBQC) to exploit the algebraic structure of LWE decryption.

**Summary.** This paper presents a highly efficient framework for Quantum Fully Homomorphic Encryption (QFHE) that significantly reduces the quantum resource overhead. By replacing generic Boolean circuit simulations with a specialized Modular Arithmetic Program (MAP) that tracks partial sums, the authors reduce the required number of EPR pairs from exponential to near-linear scaling. The resulting scheme supports fully classical clients and relies on standard classical LWE assumptions. This work moves QFHE from a theoretical concept toward a more practical implementation for secure quantum cloud computing.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The framework utilizes the Measurement-Based Quantum Computation (MBQC) model with a garden-hose protocol, employing the Quantum One-Time Pad (QOTP) and the Learning-with-Errors (LWE) cryptographic assumption.

**Key observables.** Gadget size (number of EPR pairs), branching program width and length, and security parameter lambda.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Security parameter lambda, LWE modulus q, and error standard deviation sigma (e.g., 3.2).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The scheme assumes a semi-honest server, the hardness of the classical LWE problem, and ideal, noiseless quantum operations (perfect EPR pairs and error-free measurements).

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows a quantum circuit diagram for T-gate evaluation; Figure 2 illustrates the MBQC dependency flow; Algorithm 1 and 2 compare different branching program approaches.

**Paper structure.** The paper identifies the efficiency bottleneck in QFHE, proposes a novel MAP tailored for LWE decryption, establishes a mapping via the garden-hose model to MBQC, provides complexity analysis and security proofs, and concludes with a comparison of concrete resource requirements.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Quantum fully homomorphic encryption (QFHE) promises secure delegated quantum computation but has been impeded by the prohibitive quantum resource demands of existing constructions. This paper introduces a unified framework that achieves an \textbf{exponential improvement} in efficiency by synergistically integrating three theoretical tools: \textbf{modular arithmetic programs (MAP)}, the \textbf{garden-hose model}, and \textbf{measurement-based quantum computation (MBQC)}.   Our central innovation is a novel MAP tailored to the algebraic structure of Learning-with-Errors (LWE) decryption. Unlike generic approaches that incur exponential overhead, our MAP computes the inner product $\langle \boldsymbol{sk}, \boldsymbol{c} \rangle \bmod q$ by tracking a partial sum modulo $q$, requiring only $O(\log q)$ bits of state width. This yields branching programs of width $O(\log λ)$ and length $O(λ\log λ)$, thereby reducing the size of the essential quantum gadget from $O(λ^{2.58})$ to $O(λ\log^2 λ)$ EPR pairs -- a concrete improvement factor of $2^{15}$ to $2^{18}$ for standard security parameters.   Critically, we demonstrate that LWE decryption is not a \textbf{symmetric function}, necessitating our specialized MAP design beyond prior symmetric-function optimizations. The framework provides a direct mapping from the MAP to an efficient gadget via the garden-hose model, with MBQC furnishing the deterministic control flow for homomorphic evaluation.   The resulting QFHE scheme supports \textbf{fully classical clients}, relies solely on the \textbf{classical LWE assumption} (avoiding circular security or quantum hardness assumptions), and maintains compactness. This work dramatically lowers the quantum resource barrier for practical QFHE, paving the way for realistic privacy-preserving quantum cloud computing.

</details>

<a id="paper-2604.24211"></a>
### [Electrical conductivity of crack-template-based transparent conducting films: mean-field approximation, effective medium theory, and simulation](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.24211v1)

**Authors:** Yuri Yu. Tarasevich, Andrei V. Esrkepov, Irina V. Vodolazskaya  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** disordered systems and neural networks · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.24211v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24211_figures/2604.24211_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> Figure 1. PDF of the length 𝑙and electrical conductivity 𝑔of edges in a network corresponding to the edges of a 2D PVD (5) for unit intensity of the Poisson process (one seed per unit area). The PDF of edge lengths was taken from [13], whereas the PDF of electrical conductivity was obtained using (5).</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24211_figures/2604.24211_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Figure 2. Node potentials as a function of the coordinate in networks corresponding to the edges of a 2D PVD with 1000 seeds. a) Original network, 𝜆= 1. b) Effective network, 𝑔m = 1. Insets: distributions of node potential fluctuations.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24211_figures/2604.24211_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> Figure 3. Comparison of direct calculation results of electrical conductivity of various networks with EMT predictions: for the original and effective networks obtained from a 2D PVD, and for a hexagonal network whose edge conductivities obey the distribution 𝑓𝐺(𝑔) shown in Fig. 1.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** Evaluating the accuracy of analytical models, specifically Mean Field Approximation (MFA) and Effective Medium Theory (EMT), in predicting the electrical conductivity of crack-template-based transparent conducting films.

**Main result.** The MFA overestimates conductivity by approximately 13% for the original network and 79% for the effective network, with EMT being more accurate for periodic hexagonal lattices than for random Poisson-Voronoi diagrams.

**Method.** The study uses analytical approaches (MFA and EMT) combined with direct numerical simulations of 2D Poisson-Voronoi diagrams and periodic hexagonal networks to compare predicted versus actual conductivity.

**Summary.** This paper investigates how accurately standard analytical approximations can predict the electrical conductivity of random networks used in transparent conducting films. By modeling these films as Poisson-Voronoi diagrams, the authors demonstrate that the Mean Field Approximation significantly overestimates conductivity, especially in simplified 'effective' networks. The study highlights that structural homogeneity, such as in a hexagonal lattice, is crucial for the accuracy of Effective Medium Theory. These findings serve as a warning for researchers using simplified models to design hierarchical or complex crack-based electronic components.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The system consists of crack-template-based transparent conducting films modeled as 3-regular planar graphs corresponding to the edges of a 2D Poisson-Voronoi diagram, where edge conductivity is related to edge length.

**Key observables.** Electrical conductivity (G), edge length (l), edge conductivity (g), and node potential fluctuations (delta u).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Seed concentration (ns), areal mass density (mu), and conductivity constant (lambda).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The network is assumed to be macroscopically homogeneous and isotropic, with edge orientations and lengths being independent variables; metal conductivity is assumed temperature-independent.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows probability density functions for edge length and conductivity; Figure 2 displays node potential distributions; Figure 3 compares conductivity results across different network types against theoretical predictions.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of modeling crack-based films, defines the mathematical model using Poisson-Voronoi diagrams, applies MFA and EMT analytical frameworks, performs numerical simulations for validation, and concludes with a discussion on the error sources and limitations of the approximations.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

In our work, crack-template-based transparent conducting films were modeled as networks corresponding to the edges of a two-dimensional Poisson--Voronoi diagram. Two types of networks were considered: the original one, in which the conductivity of each edge was inversely proportional to its length, and the effective one, where all edges had the same conductivity obtained from the effective medium theory. The mean field approximation was used for analytical evaluation of the electrical conductivity. Direct numerical calculations for the Poisson--Voronoi diagram showed that the mean field approximation overestimated the conductivity of the original network by approximately 13\%, and of the effective network by 79\%. In addition, a hexagonal network with an edge conductivity distribution corresponding to the Poisson--Voronoi diagram was studied: for it, the predictions of the effective medium theory turned out to be more accurate than for the Poisson--Voronoi diagram, which was explained by the greater structural homogeneity of the periodic hexagonal lattice. Our results showed that when modeling crack-template-based transparent conducting films, especially in the case of hierarchical cracks with variable width (where the resistance was not simply proportional to the length), the application of the mean field approximation could potentially lead to significant errors.

</details>

**Main problem.** The study aims to detect and quantify long-range electron-phonon coupling (EPC) across the van der Waals interface between transition metal dichalcogenides (TMDs) and hexagonal boron nitride (hBN).

**Main result.** The researchers demonstrate that quasiparticles in TMDs are dressed by a remote cloud of phonons from the adjacent hBN layer, characterized by a long-range, forward-scattering interaction that suppresses mass enhancement.

**Method.** The study uses micro-focus angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy (mu-ARPES) combined with a modified Fröhlich model and global optimization of a self-energy-based spectral function.

**Summary.** This paper identifies that electrons in TMD monolayers are significantly affected by phonons residing in the adjacent hBN layer. By using high-resolution ARPES, the authors observe 'replica bands' that serve as a fingerprint of this remote electron-phonon coupling. They show that this interaction is long-range and characterized by forward scattering, which explains why the expected increase in effective mass is not observed. This finding suggests that the properties of vdW heterostructures can be tuned by the vibrational modes of adjacent layers.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The physical system consists of monolayer TMDs (WS2 and WSe2) on hBN substrates, compared against a control system with a graphite buffer layer. The theoretical framework uses a 2D electron liquid coupled to a 3D polar substrate via a Fröhlich-type Hamiltonian.

**Key observables.** Replica bands (satellites), quasiparticle residue (Z), effective mass enhancement (m*/m), and energy distribution curves (EDCs).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Phonon energies (hBN LO/ZO and TMD LO/A1 modes), coupling strengths (alpha), separation distance (d), and number of hBN layers (Ns).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The model assumes dispersionless phonon modes and a 2D electron gas interacting with a polar 3D ionic substrate.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows AFM topography and ARPES spectral functions; Figure 2 displays spectral function analysis and EDCs; Figure 4 presents theoretical ARPES bandmaps and fits to experimental data.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the challenge of interlayer interactions, describes the experimental setup and sample preparation, presents the discovery of remote phonon satellites, develops a modified Fröhlich model to explain the data, and discusses the implications for mass enhancement and universality.

**Why it may be interesting.** The discovery of a long-range, forward-scattering interaction that decouples quasiparticle residue from effective mass provides a unique example of how momentum-dependent many-body interactions can fundamentally alter the renormalization of particles in 2D systems.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Many-body interactions can couple electronic states in one layer with collective excitations in the adjacent layer, providing a route to tailor properties of heterostructures. However, detecting and quantifying interlayer many-body interactions proved a major challenge. Here, we demonstrate that quasiparticles in monolayer transition metal dichalcogenides (TMDs) are dressed by a remote cloud of phonons in the adjacent hexagonal boron nitride slab. Using angle resolved photoemission, we identify replica bands in the TMDs which are a clear fingerprint of long-range electron-phonon interaction. We develop a modified Fröhlich model that shows semi-quantitative agreement with the experimental spectral functions. Our analysis shows that remote electron-phonon coupling is a generic property of interfaces with hBN. This has implications for electron mobilities in 2D materials, for superconductivity and possibly for moiré correlated phases.

</details>
