<a id="paper-2604.22995"></a>
### [Equations of Motion for an Economy: Capital Deepening, Technology, and Firm Survival](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.22995v1)

**Authors:** Robert T. Nachtrieb  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.22995v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.22995_figures/2604.22995_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> FIG. 1. Capital deepening drives output growth. BEA 2-digit NAICS, 1998–2023 (2020 $), 14 active sectors, L-weighted; Real Estate excluded (imputed rent artefact). (a) Real output per worker y = ηκ by sector (log scale); colours by growth rate: blue &lt; 1.25% yr−1, green 1.25–1.75%, crimson &gt; 1.75%; black = L-weighted aggregate (+1.9% yr−1). (b) Growth decomposition ˙y/y = ˙η/η + ˙κ/κ; open markers: non-active sectors. All sectors lie above ˙y/y = 0; capital deepening dominates; ˙η/η ≤0 in 13 of 17 sectors.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.22995_figures/2604.22995_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> FIG. 2. Falsifiable prediction: ϕ = 0.01 yr−1 at 2027, all 14 active sectors. Black dots: BEA observed y(t), 1998– 2023. Grey dashed: historical trend (+1.9% yr−1). Blue: business-as-usual (+1.5% yr−1). Red dash-dot: ϕ = 0.01 yr−1</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** The paper seeks to derive the equations of motion for economic growth and firm survival without assuming a production function, specifically distinguishing between capital deepening and productivity improvement.

**Main result.** The study finds that historical growth has been driven by a 'structural cheapening' channel (mu) rather than a 'productivity' channel (phi), which has been zero for 75 years, and predicts that a small positive phi would significantly accelerate growth.

**Method.** The author uses a dynamical systems approach with four coupled ordinary differential equations, calibrated against BEA and BDS economic datasets using the Nelder-Mead algorithm and Kalman filtering.

**Summary.** This paper develops a dynamical model of economic growth based on accounting identities rather than traditional production functions. It identifies two distinct channels for capital productivity: one that makes capital cheaper and one that makes it more capable. By analyzing decades of US economic data, the author demonstrates that the productivity channel has been stagnant (zero) for the last 75 years. The paper concludes with a specific, testable prediction that a small increase in the productivity channel could nearly double the aggregate growth rate.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** A competitive economy modeled at the sector level as a dynamical system of coupled relaxation equations governing capital per worker, capital productivity, and labor share, linked to micro-scale firm dynamics via a cash martingale and Zipf's law.

**Key observables.** Real output per worker (y), capital per worker (kappa), capital productivity (eta), and firm exit rates.

**Important parameters / regimes.** mu (structural cheapening rate), phi (productivity improvement rate), eta* (profit imperative/breakeven productivity), and alpha (Zipf exponent).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The model assumes a competitive economy, uses accounting identities instead of production functions, and treats firm cash reserves as a martingale under i.i.d. shocks.

**Figures summary.** Figure 2 provides a forecast of output per worker, comparing historical BEA trends and a business-as-usual projection against a testable prediction of increased growth if the productivity channel (phi) becomes positive.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins with the derivation of equations of motion from accounting identities, introduces the two-channel productivity model, presents calibration results against historical US sector data, connects macro-dynamics to micro-scale firm exit rates, and concludes with a falsifiable growth prediction.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We derive equations of motion for capital deepening in a competitive economy directly from accounting identities, without assuming a production function. A profit imperative $η^* \equiv (w/κ+ 1/τ)/(1-f_p)$ sets the minimum viable capital productivity, where $η= Y/K$ [yr$^{-1}$] is capital productivity, $κ= K/L$ is capital per worker, $w$ is the wage rate, $τ$ is the capital lifetime, and $f_p$ is the production tax share. Four coupled relaxation equations govern $κ$, $η$, the frontier productivity $η_{\rm new}$ of new investment, and the labor share $q \equiv w/y$, with the sandwich constraint $η^* \leq η_{\rm new} \leq η$ maintained as an exact invariant. The frontier equation separates two physically distinct channels: a structural cheapening channel ($μ$, always active, drives $η_{\rm new}$ downward) and a productivity channel ($φ$, historically zero). Calibration against BEA 2-digit NAICS sector data (1998--2023) confirms $φ= 0$ for all identifiable sectors over 25 years; the 75-year postwar record extends this finding across four capital lifetimes. A step $φ= 0.01$\,yr$^{-1}$ -- a 1\%/yr improvement in new-capital productivity, modest but historically unprecedented -- nearly doubles the aggregate growth rate within one capital lifetime, a falsifiable prediction with a precise observable signature: upward-curving $η(t)$ in BEA sector data. Firms near the zero-profit threshold have a cash martingale, predicting establishment exit rate $\sim t^{-1/2}$; convolved with the Zipf firm-size distribution~\cite{WP}, this yields firm exit rate $\sim t^{-1/2}\!\log t$ with apparent exponent $b = 0.295 \pm 0.03$, confirmed against BDS data with no free parameters.

</details>

### [Exact momentum-space analysis of small spin-1/2 $J_1$-$J_2$ rings](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23149v1)

**Authors:** Zimeng Li, Ning Wu  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.23149v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

The dashed curves link two Bloch states connected by the Z2 transformation. (b) The ten Bloch states defines a ten-dimensional single-particle problem on the r1-r2-r3 lattice.</figcaption>

Energy levels labeled by dif- ferent l’s are plotted in different colors, while those with k = 0 and k = −π are respectively indicated by solid and dashed curves. There are four ground-state level crossings at J1/|J2| = −2, −1.337, 2.924, and 4. A singlet-triplet crossing in the first excited state also occurs at J1/|J2| = −4.06.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23149_figures/2604.23149_fig5.jpg" alt="Figure 5">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> FIG. 5: The weight Pi = |⟨χi(k)|GS⟩|2 of the Bloch state |χi(k)⟩in the ground state |χi(k)⟩.</figcaption>

<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23149_figures/2604.23149_fig6.jpg" alt="Figure 6">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 6.</b> FIG. 6: Ground-state spin correlations Cr = ⟨GS|⃗S1⃗S1+r|GS⟩ (r = 1, 2, 3, and 4).</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** The paper seeks to establish an exact connection between real-space and momentum-space representations of the $J_1$-$J_2$ spin chain and to analyze the energy spectrum and ground-state properties of small spin-1/2 rings.

**Main result.** The authors successfully block-diagonalize the Hamiltonian for $N=6$ and $N=8$ rings into small matrices and demonstrate that momentum-space manifestations of the Majumdar-Ghosh and HKNN ground states are equivalent to their real-space dimer counterparts.

**Method.** The study utilizes an exact momentum-space analysis based on a set of few-magnon Bloch states to reduce the Hamiltonian into small block matrices, combined with analytical solutions and dimer expansion techniques.

**Summary.** This paper provides an exact analytical and numerical framework for studying small $J_1$-$J_2$ spin rings in momentum space. By using few-magnon Bloch states, the authors reduce the complex many-body problem into much smaller, manageable matrix blocks. They explicitly show how well-known real-space dimerized ground states, like the Majumdar-Ghosh state, appear in momentum space. The study also identifies zero-energy states and suggests that the HKNN ground state may behave as a bound state of magnons.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The system consists of $N=6$ and $N=8$ site spin-1/2 $J_1$-$J_2$ rings with nearest-neighbor and next-nearest-neighbor exchange interactions, including both isotropic and anisotropic (XXZ) cases.

**Key observables.** Eigenenergies, eigenstates, total angular momentum ($l$), momentum ($k$), magnetization ($M$), and spin correlation functions ($C_r$).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Exchange interaction ratio $J_1/J_2$, anisotropy parameters $\Delta_1$ and $\Delta_2$, and ring size $N$.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis is limited to small ring sizes ($N=6, 8$), and certain sectors of the Hamiltonian could not be solved in closed form using Mathematica.

**Figures summary.** Figure 6 plots ground-state spin correlation functions ($C_r$) for different distances $r$, illustrating how they evolve with the $J_1/J_2$ ratio.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the $J_1$-$J_2$ model, details the momentum-space block-diagonalization method using Bloch states, analyzes the $N=6$ anisotropic and $N=8$ isotropic spectra, discusses specific ground-state points (MG and HKNN), and concludes with a conjecture on the nature of the HKNN state.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

This paper considers an $N$-site spin-1/2 $J_1$-$J_2$ ring with $N=6$ and $8$. With the help of a set of exact few-magnon Bloch states, we obtain the block-diagonalized Hamiltonian consisting of block matrices of at most four dimensions. Partial of the eigenstates are analytically solved. For the six-site anisotropic ring, we reveal a subset of eigenstates that are simultaneous eigenstates of the Hamiltonian and the total angular momentum operator, even though the latter is not conserved. For both the six- and eight-site isotropic rings, we achieve momentum-space manifestations of several important states, including the famous Majumdar-Ghosh (MG) ground states and the Hamada-Kane-Nakagawa-Natsume (HKNN) ground state. The equivalence of these states with their real-space counterparts is explicitly shown for $N=6$. The structure of the HKNN ground state for small rings suggests that for any even number $N$ this state might behave like a ``bound state" with $N/2$ successive down spins binding together.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

<a id="paper-2604.24297"></a>
### [Exhaustive and feasible parametrisation with applications to the travelling salesperson problem](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.24297v1)

**Authors:** Marvin Schwiering, Timo Ziegler, Lennart Binkowski, Benjamin Sambale  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.24297v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24297_figures/2604.24297_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> Fig. 1. High-level overview of our abstract pipeline for constructing exhaustively parametrised, feasibility-respecting quantum circuits for combinatorial optimisation problems. The task of exactly and exclusively preparing all feasible solutions via paremtrised quantum circuits can be reduced to concepts of elementary group theory. The two key ingredients are a transitive group action on the problem’s feasible set, often available from decades of operational research studying symmetries in the feasible set, and a generating sequence of involutions. The output of the pipeline is an exhaustively parametrised, feasibility-respecting quantum circuit with a fixed number of parameters.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24297_figures/2604.24297_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Fig. 2. Abstract pipeline for constructing an exhaustively parametrised, feasibility-respecting quantum circuit from a transitive group action on the feasible set F ⊂𝕓m. Central to this construction is the existence of a generating sequence of involutions, whose elements can be represented as m-qubit operators. Exponentiating these operators, introducing real parameters in the exponent, and prepending the state preparation of some feasible computational basis state |x⟩, completes the construction.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24297_figures/2604.24297_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> Fig. 4. Qubit connectivity required for implementing the exhaustively parametrised quantum circuit based on the bubble sort sequence. The m data qubits, encoding the TSP solutions, can be aligned on a chain. The ancilla qubit, serving as control for the Fredkin gates, has to be connected to all m data qubits. Alternatively, one can introduce m −1 ancilla qubits, each connected to two contiguous data qubits.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24297_figures/2604.24297_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> Fig. 5. Approximation ratios obtained after each COBYLA iteration, using different parametrised quantum circuits on a 9-city TSP instance. The QAOA with Hadfield et al.’s mixers was not able to significantly improve initial state’s approximation ratio, neither if a single computational basis state, nor the uniform superposition of all feasible states, was used. In contrast, the exhaustively parametrised circuit based on bubble sort was able to push the initial ratio of ≈0.60 to 0.83, while the construction based on the binary insertion sequence even reached a final approximation ratio of 0.91. Not depicted here: the bubble sort-inspired approach did not terminate before 2300 steps, but was...</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** Addressing the reachability and feasibility issues in QAOA, where conventional circuits may not cover all optimal solutions or may include infeasible states for constrained combinatorial optimization problems.

**Main result.** The authors develop a pipeline to construct 'exhaustively parameterized, feasibility-respecting' quantum circuits that are guaranteed to reach every feasible solution with a fixed number of parameters, demonstrating superior performance on TSP instances.

**Method.** The method uses group theory, specifically identifying transitive group actions and generating sequences of involutions, to build parameterized unitaries that navigate the feasible subspace of a problem.

**Summary.** This paper introduces a new class of quantum circuits for combinatorial optimization that are guaranteed to reach all feasible solutions, including the optimum, without leaving the feasible subspace. By leveraging group theory and generating sequences of group elements, the authors provide a systematic way to build these circuits. They demonstrate that for the Traveling Salesperson Problem, a binary insertion-based construction significantly outperforms standard QAOA in numerical tests. This approach reduces the parameter space and improves the efficiency of the classical optimization process.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The study focuses on the Traveling Salesperson Problem (TSP) modeled as an optimization over the symmetric group $S_n$, using both one-hot and compact qubit encodings.

**Key observables.** Approximation ratio (normalized expectation value of the objective Hamiltonian).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Number of variational parameters (scaling as $O(n^2)$ for bubble sort and $O(n \log n)$ for binary insertion) and the number of layers $p$.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The numerical validation assumes noiseless qubits and focuses on small-scale instances (up to 9 cities); it does not guarantee that the required optimal parameters can be found efficiently.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the abstract pipeline; Figure 2 illustrates the circuit construction flow; Figure 3 shows the implementation of exponentiated involutions using an ancilla; Figure 4 shows qubit connectivity; Figure 5 compares approximation ratios vs. optimization iterations for different methods.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the concept of exhaustive reachability, provides a group-theoretic mathematical framework (the abstract pipeline), applies this to TSP via bubble sort and binary insertion constructions, and concludes with numerical benchmarks and limitations.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

This paper introduces the concept of exhaustively parametrised, feasibility-respecting quantum circuits for constrained combinatorial optimisation problems. Such circuits can reach, given the right parameter values, every feasible solution with certainty -- including the optimum -- with a fixed number of parameters, while avoiding infeasible solutions altogether. This is in sharp contrast to conventional quantum alternating operator ansatz schemes, which are merely guaranteed to reach the optimum asymptotically. We introduce an abstract pipeline for constructing exhaustively parametrised, feasibility-respecting circuits from a transitive group action on a problem's feasible set. Our constructions rely on the simple combination of the group action with group representation and the novel notion of generating sequences: group elements in fixed order, possibly with repetitions, that generate the entire group. That is, we trace expressivity of parametrised quantum circuits back to the most fundamental concepts of group theory. We apply this pipeline to two concrete examples for the travelling salesperson problem, thus showing that exhaustively parametrised, feasibility-respecting circuits are not an empty definition. Furthermore, we provide numerical proof-of-principles on instances with up to nine cities, comparing the suitability of our constructions for parameter optimisation purposes against established mixers.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

### [Explore Simpler Eigenmarking: Quantum Entailment Model Checking](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23531v1)

**Authors:** Tatpong Katanyukul  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.23531v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23531_figures/2604.23531_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> FIGURE 1. Quantum gate applications: (a) logical AND, (b) its simulated result, (c) logical OR, and (d) its simulated result.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23531_figures/2604.23531_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> FIGURE 2. Simpler Eigenmarking. Generic diagram (left) and Qiskit graphic draw of a three-qubit system (right): x2 is the most significant qubit (msq).</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23531_figures/2604.23531_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> FIGURE 3. Plots of experimental results on a two-qubit system simulation. A target state has prefix 01.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23531_figures/2604.23531_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> Figure 4 shows diagrams implemented entailment model checking for an example described in § Background. The simulation results are shown in Figure 5. Note that β3 and β4 introduces more logical symbols, hence more states.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23531_figures/2604.23531_fig5.jpg" alt="Figure 5">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> FIGURE 4. Diagrams: knowledge α : {A ⇒B,B ⇒C} (left), sentence β1 : A ⇒C (middle), and entailment checking (right).</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23531_figures/2604.23531_fig6.jpg" alt="Figure 6">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 6.</b> FIGURE 5. Entailment checking simulation. The decisive logic is shown as the first digit, e.g., β1 has no violation: all y’s is 1’s.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** The paper aims to optimize the 'Eigenmarking' search algorithm for quantum entailment model checking by reducing the hardware complexity of required quantum gates.

**Main result.** The proposed 'Simpler Eigenmarking' scheme achieves significantly higher winning margins and better distinguishability using only two-qubit controlled phase rotations (CCZ gates), regardless of input size.

**Method.** The study refines the 'subtle marking' algorithm by replacing multi-qubit-controlled phase rotations with simpler CCZ gates and evaluates the performance using Qiskit-based two-qubit system simulations.

**Summary.** This paper proposes a more efficient version of the Eigenmarking quantum search algorithm designed for checking logical entailment. By replacing complex multi-qubit controlled rotations with simpler two-qubit CCZ gates, the author reduces the hardware burden for quantum computers. Simulations show that this simpler scheme provides much better distinguishability and a higher winning margin than previous methods. This makes the algorithm more viable for implementation on near-term quantum hardware.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The research utilizes a quantum computing framework based on Grover's algorithm, specifically simulating a three-qubit system consisting of input qubits, a tag qubit, and an ancillary qubit.

**Key observables.** Winning Margin (W) and Distinguishability (D).

**Important parameters / regimes.** The relative local winning margin (W) and the worst-case distinguishability (D).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The results are based on two-qubit system simulations; scalability to many qubits and performance on real, noisy quantum hardware remain unproven.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows classical logic gate simulation; Figure 2 displays the circuit diagram for the Simpler Eigenmarking system; Figure 3 shows experimental counts for target states; Figure 4 and 5 illustrate the implementation and results of entailment checking.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of entailment model checking, reviews existing Eigenmarking schemes (Conventional and Subtle), proposes a refined CCZ-based scheme, presents simulation results comparing performance metrics, and discusses scalability and hardware implications.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Targeting entailment model checking, a recent study has pioneered an idea of Eigenmarking search, an improvement over Grover search using extra qubits. The extra qubits condition the quantum state evolution such that the answer states (if exist) are always in the minority. The minority criteria is essential to Grover probability-amplitude amplification and consequently the effectiveness of Grover search. In addition to enforce the minority criteria, Eigenmarking also employs complementary states (through well-orchestrated phase rotation) for easy identification of a no-answer case (related to a no-violation case in the context of model checking). Eigenmarking search has been shown effective in two-qubit simulations.   The three Eigenmarking schemes have been previously proposed. Two schemes require two extra qubits. One scheme (called ``subtle marking'') requires one extra qubit with a multiple-qubit-controlled phase rotation. Our study refines the mechanism using only one extra qubit with only two-qubit-controlled phase rotation, commonly known as \texttt{ccz}, regardless of how many qubits the input has. Using a multiple-qubit-controlled phase rotation (as in subtle marking) associates with highly entangled states. Highly entangled states in a real quantum hardware are difficult (or in some cases may even be unachievable) particularly in a scaled up scenario involving many qubits. Our proposed new Eigenmarking scheme has lightened the burden for the hardware requirement. The new Eigenmarking search has been experimented in two-qubit-system simulations and shown viable, achieving the minimal relative local winning margin of W=3.17 and the worst-case distinguishability of D=0.769 (cf. W=0.67; D=0.19 from conventional marking and W=0.28; D=0.55 from subtle marking).

</details>

**Authors:** Vladislav Kurichenko, Parul Rani, Björgvin Hjörvarsson  
**Type:** experiment · **Category:** disordered systems and neural networks · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.23222v1>

The origin of the anisotropy is believed to be the interplay between the external ﬁeld and the evolving moment, af- fecting the atomic conﬁgurations in the growing sample. Consequently, in the absence of an external...</figcaption>

**Main problem.** The study investigates how finite-size effects in amorphous Co70Zr30 thin layers influence magnetic properties, specifically the reduction in magnetic moment and ordering temperature.

**Main result.** The researchers found that decreasing layer thickness leads to a reduction in Tc and Ms following a 1/L scaling law, driven by interfacial regions with reduced magnetic interactions, and observed Griffiths phase behavior in the thinnest films.

**Method.** The study uses dc magnetron sputtering for film deposition, GIXRD and XRR for structural characterization, and SQUID magnetometry and MOKE for magnetic measurements.

**Summary.** This paper examines how the thickness of amorphous Co70Zr30 thin films affects their magnetic properties. As the films get thinner, the magnetic ordering temperature and saturation magnetization decrease due to the influence of interfacial regions. The study identifies a scaling relationship between these properties and the inverse of the thickness. Additionally, the researchers observe the presence of a Griffiths phase in very thin layers, where magnetic clusters persist above the critical temperature.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The system consists of amorphous Co70Zr30 single-layer thin films with thicknesses ranging from 25 to 400 Angstroms, modeled as an interior magnetic region flanked by two interfacial regions.

**Key observables.** Saturation magnetization (Ms), magnetic ordering temperature (Tc), coercivity, interatomic distances, and magnetic anisotropy.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Thickness range (25-400 A), interface thickness (approx 10 A), and the transition from ferromagnetic to Griffiths phase behavior.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The magnetic layer is modeled as having an interior region and two interfacial regions that are effectively 'magnetically dead'.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1 shows GIXRD patterns; Fig 2 shows XRR patterns; Fig 3 shows thickness-dependent MOKE hysteresis loops; Fig 4 shows temperature-dependent magnetization (SQUID) and Tc reduction.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of finite-size effects, describes the experimental deposition and structural characterization, presents magnetic measurement results, applies a scaling model to analyze thickness dependence, and discusses the emergence of the Griffiths phase.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Profound finite size effects are observed in both the moment and ordering temperature in thin Co$_{70}$Zr$_{30}$ layers. The results are consistent with the presence of interface regions with reduced magnetic interactions and moment. The extension of this region is determined to be around 1 nm thick at each interface. Above and near the apparent critical temperature, the magnetic properties can be understood in terms of Griffith phases.

</details>

### [GSC-QEMit: A Telemetry-Driven Hierarchical Forecast-and-Bandit Framework for Adaptive Quantum Error Mitigation](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.24551v1)

**Authors:** Steven Szachara, Sheeraja Rajakrishnan, Dylan Jay Van Allen, Jason Pollack, Travis Desell, Daniel Krutz  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.24551v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24551_figures/2604.24551_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> Fig. 1. GSC-QEMit implementation: An instrumented backend streams cycle-level telemetry to a classical context–forecast–bandit stack for adaptive mitigation selection.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24551_figures/2604.24551_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Figure 1 summarizes our end-to-end implementation. An instrumented backend provides streaming, per-cycle telemetry to a classical control stack consisting of a Spark–GHSOM context mapper, a Sparse Variational Gaussian Process (SVGP) forecaster, and a cost-aware Contextual Multi-Armed Bandit (CMAB) initialized via imitation learning. The controller is defined by a fixed telemetry interface and an abstract inter- vention library; the backend used here is a Qiskit Aer–based simulator for controlled evaluation, but it can be replaced by any simulator or hardware system that exposes compatible telemetry and supports a small set of intervention hooks.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24551_figures/2604.24551_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> Fig. 2. Real-time adaptive response (case study: QFT). A representative execution trace under drifting noise. Top strip (barcode): the selected intervention level over time (NONE →MODERATE →SEVERE). Barcode shading encodes action: white/green = NONE, gray/orange = MODER- ATE, black/purple = SEVERE. Bottom plot: logical fidelity FL (solid) alongside the drifting effective noise indicator (dotted). Key insight: during elevated-noise intervals, the policy increases intervention intensity (switching to SEVERE); during quiescent periods it returns to NONE/MODERATE, con- serving cost while maintaining high fidelity.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24551_figures/2604.24551_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> Figure 3 illustrates representative telemetry traces for the full benchmark suite under a controlled, time-varying noise process. In all the experiments, circuits are executed repeatedly over a fixed horizon Trun while the effective physical error rate peff(t) is deliberately modulated according to the drifting- noise model described in Section IV-B. No additional faults or adversarial perturbations are injected beyond this explicit drift process. The drift schedule is designed to probe two complementary regimes. In the first half of each run, the noise level is rapidly increased to a peak, producing a pronounced high- noise interval near the center of the trace. This stress-test phase...</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24551_figures/2604.24551_fig5.jpg" alt="Figure 5">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> Fig. 3. Cross-benchmark stabilization under drift. Time-series traces for the evaluation suite under a controlled, time-varying noise schedule with a deliberate mid-run noise peak. Dotted: imposed effective noise indicator peff(t). Dashed: unmitigated baseline logical fidelity. Solid: adaptive fidelity under GSC-QEMit. The reduced depth of the mid-run fidelity trough under the adaptive policy reflects active mitigation escalation during high-noise intervals, followed by de-escalation as noise relaxes.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** Managing quantum error mitigation (QEM) in the NISQ era by deciding when and how aggressively to apply mitigation techniques under time-varying, nonstationary noise while balancing fidelity gains against computational overhead.

**Main result.** The GSC-QEMit framework achieved a 9.0% average increase in logical fidelity relative to unmitigated execution and reduced unnecessary heavy interventions by approximately 35% compared to a static severe strategy.

**Method.** A three-module 'context-forecast-bandit' framework comprising a Growing Hierarchical Self-Organizing Map (GHSOM) for context discovery, a Sparse Variational Gaussian Process (SVGP) for fidelity forecasting, and a cost-aware contextual multi-armed bandit (CMAB) for decision making.

**Summary.** This paper presents GSC-QEMit, an adaptive framework designed to optimize quantum error mitigation in the presence of drifting noise. By using machine learning modules to cluster device telemetry, forecast future fidelity degradation, and select the most cost-effective mitigation level, the system avoids expensive interventions when noise is low. The approach demonstrates significant improvements in logical fidelity and resource efficiency across various quantum circuit workloads without requiring circuit-specific tuning.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** A simulated quantum backend using Qiskit Aer with a nonstationary noise model characterized by a bounded sinusoidal drift and stochastic fluctuations around a baseline physical error rate.

**Key observables.** Average logical fidelity, logical error rate (measured via Total Variation Distance), and intervention cost.

**Important parameters / regimes.** 13-dimensional telemetry feature vector, mitigation intensity levels (NONE, MODERATE, SEVERE), and the Lagrange multiplier (lambda) controlling cost-sensitivity.

**Assumptions / limitations.** Assumes the availability of streaming telemetry and a library of discrete mitigation primitives; assumes system health can be captured via telemetry and noise patterns can be forecasted.

**Figures summary.** Figure 2 shows a case study of the QFT circuit displaying a real-time barcode of selected intervention levels over time.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of adaptive QEM, describes the three-module GSC-QEMit architecture (GHSOM, SVGP, and CMAB), details the simulation environment and noise model, presents performance results across various circuit families, and discusses the framework's generalizability and cost-efficiency.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Quantum error mitigation (QEM) is essential for extracting reliable results from near-term quantum devices, yet practical deployments must balance mitigation strength against runtime overhead under time-varying noise. We introduce \emph{GSC-QEMit}, a telemetry-driven, \textbf{context--forecast--bandit} framework for \emph{adaptive} mitigation that switches between lightweight suppression and heavier intervention as drift evolves. GSC-QEMit composes three coupled modules: (G) a Growing Hierarchical Self-Organizing Map (GHSOM) that clusters streaming telemetry into operating contexts; (S) an uncertainty-aware subsampled Gaussian-process forecaster that predicts short-horizon fidelity degradation; and (C) a cost-aware contextual multi-armed bandit (CMAB) that selects mitigation actions via Thompson sampling with explicit intervention cost. We evaluate GSC-QEMit on benchmark circuit families (GHZ, Quantum Fourier Transform, and Grover search) under nonstationary noise regimes simulated in Qiskit Aer, using an instrumented testbed where action labels correspond to graded mitigation intensity. Across Clifford, non-Clifford, and structured workloads, GSC-QEMit improves average logical fidelity by \textbf{+9.0\%} relative to unmitigated execution while reducing unnecessary heavy interventions by reserving them for inferred noise spikes. The resulting policies exhibit a favorable fidelity--cost trade-off and transfer across the evaluated workloads without circuit-specific tuning.

</details>

**Authors:** Pawat Akara-pipattana, Sergei Nechaev  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.23152v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

Panel (d) shows that curves for the rewiring of RRG with d = 50 collapse in coordinates with the rescaled fugacity λ/ log N for various values of N.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">

<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> Figure 3. Left: the normalized number of triangles as a function of a number of Monte Carlo steps for rewiring of RRG of N = 1000 vertices and d = 50 at λ = 0.2, 0.5, 1, 2. The black vertical line emphasizes that the maximum number of triangles are not reached. Right: the same quantities now subtracted by one plotted in semi-logarithmic scale for better differentiation between the curves. Note higher value of lambda does not necessary lead to more triangles at the steady state.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23152_figures/2604.23152_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> Figure 5. Normalized number of triangles in TPP at N = 1000 and d = 30, 50, 100. The solid lines are taken from the leading order term of Eq.(11).</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23152_figures/2604.23152_fig5.jpg" alt="Figure 5">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> Figure 6. Inter-cluster degree distributions of rewired RRG from various degrees and sizes as indicated in the legend. The guide line highlights that all of the distribution follows power-law P(d) = d−γ with exponent γ ≈2.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23152_figures/2604.23152_fig6.jpg" alt="Figure 6">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 6.</b> Figure 7. Distribution of isosceles and equilateral triangle as defined in IV over the same samples of graphs in Fig. 6. For each sample, both numbers of triangle are normalized by its total number of triangles. The distribution of isosceles triangles is much broader than that of the equilateral triangles. There are more isosceles triangles than equilateral triangles in all individual samples.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** Investigating how the enrichment of local triangle motifs in rewired random regular graphs drives a phase transition and leads to the emergence of a scale-free degree distribution.

**Main result.** The authors identify a first-order phase transition between a triangle-poor and a triangle-rich phase, where the latter features an inter-cluster network with a universal power-law degree distribution exponent of gamma approximately 2.

**Method.** The study employs Metropolis Monte Carlo simulations, mean-field kinetic arguments, a chemical reaction model based on the law of mass action, and a hyperbolic embedding approach.

**Summary.** This paper explores how increasing the density of triangles in a network can spontaneously create a scale-free structure. By using a rewiring process controlled by a chemical potential (fugacity), the authors show that the network transitions from a homogeneous state to a clustered state where the connections between clusters follow a power-law distribution. They propose that this 'emergent preferential attachment' is driven by the way triangles aggregate. The study even suggests a deep connection between this topological phase and the Efimov effect in few-body physics.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The system consists of Rewired Random Regular Graphs (RRGs) with a fixed vertex degree d, studied in a canonical ensemble where the number of triangles is controlled by a fugacity parameter lambda.

**Key observables.** Vertex degree distribution P(d), triangle count, Ollivier-Ricci curvature (ORC), and the scaling exponent gamma.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Fugacity (lambda), vertex degree (d), and the critical transition points (lambda_minus and lambda_plus).

**Assumptions / limitations.** Assumes a rigid constraint on vertex degree, uniform distribution of cluster saturation times, and stochastic distribution of inter-cluster edges.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows different graph configurations (triangle-poor, triangle-rich, and maximal triangle) classified by ORC; Figure 6 shows the power-law degree distribution on a log-log scale; Figure 7 compares the frequency of isosceles vs. equilateral triangles.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the rewiring model and phase transition, describes the chemical reaction model for the triangle-poor phase, presents the mean-field derivation of the scale-free inter-cluster network, and concludes with a hyperbolic geometry interpretation and connection to Efimov-like states.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We study the statistics of rewired random regular graphs within the canonical ensemble, in which the average number of triangles is controlled by the fugacity $λ$. Using mean-field arguments, we estimate the location of the transition point $λ^{-}$. We focus primarily on the triangle-enriched phase above $λ^{-}$, where the graph becomes a loosely connected web of clusters. This inter-cluster network is scale-free and exhibits a power-law vertex degree distribution $P(d) \sim d^{-γ}$, with $γ\approx 2$ independent of degree and size of the graph. We attribute this behavior to an "emergent preferential attachment" for triangle motifs, derive the exponent $γ$ with a mean-field approach, and speculate on a possible connection between the typical topology of inter-cluster triangles and Efimov states in a conformally invariant potential.

</details>

**Authors:** V. N. A. Lula-Rocha, M. A. S. Trindade  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.23382v1>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates a non-unitary extension of Grover's search algorithm to achieve a single-step solution by modifying the hidden geometry of the Hilbert space.

**Main result.** The authors show that while a Kraus operator approach fails to provide a quantum advantage, using the Quantum Singular Value Transform (QSVT) with block encoding and Chebyshev polynomial approximation allows the algorithm to reach the Grover bound with only one additional qubit.

**Method.** The researchers use a geometric approach involving a generalized 2-rank tensor for the diffusion operator and analyze implementation via Kraus operators and QSVT-based block encoding.

**Summary.** This paper proposes a way to extend Grover's algorithm into a non-unitary regime by changing the metric tensor of the Hilbert space. While a simple non-unitary approach via Kraus operators results in no speedup over classical search, the authors demonstrate that using advanced techniques like the Quantum Singular Value Transform (QSVT) can achieve the optimal Grover complexity. This approach effectively performs the search in a single large rotation rather than many small ones, requiring only one extra ancilla qubit.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The model is a generalized quantum amplitude amplification framework acting on an unstructured search space of size N, utilizing a non-unitary diffusion operator D_g and a Grover oracle U_f.

**Key observables.** Success probability (P_succ) and the rotation angle (theta) in the Hilbert space.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Search space size N, number of solutions M, rotation angles theta and phi, and the normalization factor p.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The construction of the generalized operator requires prior knowledge of the number of solutions M; the implementation relies on the ability to perform block encoding.

**Paper structure.** The paper defines the geometric nature of the diffusion operator, reviews the standard Grover algorithm, develops the mathematical tools for the non-unitary extension, presents the new algorithm, and concludes with a complexity analysis.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We have developed a non-unitary extension of Grover's search algorithm by changing the hidden geometry of Hilbert space carried by diffusion operator. Our algorithm finds the solution for search problem by performing a unique bigger rotation rather than small rotations in order polynomial times in the size $N$ of search space. We analyze the complexity of implementing the non-unitary operation and we observed that the price paid by performing this rotation is due the normalization. In Kraus operator approach we need $O(N)$ repetition of the algorithm to have a chance of measuring a solution in a post-selection, this is no better than the classical solution. However, the quantum singular value transform in addition with block encoding and Chebyshev polynomial approximation, we got complexity $O(\sqrt{N})$ and reach the Grover's bound with an extra resource of one single qubit, compared with the standard Grover's algorithm.

</details>

<a id="paper-2604.24688"></a>
### [On the geometric algebras of the Ising model](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.24688v1)

**Authors:** N. Johnson, D. Marenduzzo, A. Morozov, E. Orlandini, G. M. Vasil  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.24688v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24688_figures/2604.24688_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> Figure 1. Sketch of a domain wall in the 1D Ising model, which can be associated with an e+−bivector acting on the edge which separates the two domains. This is because e+−=  0 1 1 0</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24688_figures/2604.24688_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Figure 2. Dispersion relation showing the angle αr = log(λr) (logarithm of the eigenvalues of M and of W) versus r/n (normalised wavenumber). (a) Low K (high temperature) phase at K = 0.4: there is a gap in the system. (b) Critical behaviour at K = Kc ≃0.4407 (defined by K∗ c = Kc): the gap closes, and a zero-energy mode (low-energy quasi-particle excitation) appears around r = n/2. (c) High K (low temperature) phase: the gap has reopened.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** The paper seeks to provide a unified, geometrically interpretable algebraic framework for the 1D and 2D Ising models using Clifford and Conformal Geometric Algebras (CGA).

**Main result.** The authors show that the transfer matrix, eigenvectors, and quasiparticle excitations can be naturally represented as elements of a conformal Clifford algebra, where the transfer matrix acts as a dilation and eigenvectors correspond to Majorana-like modes.

**Method.** The study employs Clifford algebra Cl(1,1) and Cl(n,n) to reformulate the transfer-matrix solution, utilizing Hestenes spinors and the properties of conformal bivectors to reinterpret the model's dynamics.

**Summary.** This paper provides a new geometric perspective on the exactly solvable 1D and 2D Ising models. By using Clifford and Conformal Geometric Algebras, the authors reinterpret the transfer matrix as a dilation and the model's excitations as Majorana-like fermions. While the underlying physical results are well-established, this framework offers a more compact and unified algebraic description of scale transformations, duality, and quasiparticle dynamics.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The paper analyzes the classical 1D and 2D Ising models on square lattices with periodic boundary conditions, focusing on the transfer-matrix formalism and the emergence of domain walls and quasiparticles.

**Key observables.** Partition function, transfer matrix eigenvalues, dispersion relation, and correlation length.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Coupling constant (K), dual coupling (K*), and the critical temperature/coupling (Kc).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The 2D model assumes periodic boundary conditions and the absence of an external magnetic field.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 illustrates a domain wall in the 1D Ising model as a spin-flip via a bivector; Figure 2 displays the dispersion relation across high-temperature, critical, and low-temperature regimes.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by introducing the Clifford algebra framework for the 1D model, extends the formalism to the 2D case using Cl(n,n), derives the dispersion relation and critical behavior, and concludes with a discussion on the pedagogical value of this geometric approach.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We revisit the classical transfer matrix solution of the one- and two-dimensional Ising model from the perspective of Clifford and conformal geometric algebras. Building on Kaufman's spinor formulation, we show that all elements entering the solution, including the transfer matrix, its eigenvectors, and the quasiparticle excitations, admit a natural and unified interpretation as elements of an appropriate conformal Clifford algebra. In particular, the transfer matrix can be viewed as a dilation generated by a conformal bivector, while its eigenvectors correspond to null combinations of Clifford generators, closely paralleling the emergence of Majorana fermionic degrees of freedom. In the two-dimensional case, the standard eigenvalue equation for the row-to-row transfer matrix is reinterpreted as a dispersion relation for quasiparticle excitations, exposing the connection between the Ising model and a theory of free Majorana fermions. While all the explicit exact results recovered are well known, this geometric reformulation provides a unified algebraic framework which is compact and physically interpretable. Specifically, this clarifies the role of scale transformations, fermionic modes, and duality in the Ising model. We believe this approach offers a useful pedagogical complement to more conventional fermionic, Grassmann, or field theoretic treatments.

</details>

**Main problem.** Determining the performance gap between theoretically optimal Prolate Spheroidal Wavefunction (PSW) modes and practical Gaussian beams in aperture-limited free-space optical communication links.

**Main result.** The paper proves that an aperture-truncated Gaussian beam with an optimized waist achieves the same transmissivity as the fundamental PSW mode, and derives a trace theorem showing total modal transmissivity depends only on the Fresnel number product.

**Method.** The authors use Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) of the Fresnel propagation kernel, numerical optimization of beam waist parameters, and analytical derivation of a trace theorem for the power-transfer operator.

**Summary.** This paper addresses the efficiency of light transmission in free-space optical communication. It demonstrates that while Prolate Spheroidal Wavefunctions are the mathematically optimal modes for aperture-limited channels, standard Gaussian beams with optimized widths can achieve identical transmission performance. Furthermore, the authors provide a fundamental theorem stating that the total capacity of the channel is determined solely by the Fresnel number product, regardless of the specific aperture shapes.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** A monochromatic, scalar, paraxial optical field propagating through a free-space link between two hard circular apertures (transmitter and receiver) over a distance L.

**Key observables.** Transmissivity (eta), input efficiency (eta_in), and total modal power transmissivity (sum of eigenvalues).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Fresnel number product (Df), beam waist (xi), and aperture radii ratios (rT:rR); propagation regimes include near-field, intermediate-field, and far-field.

**Assumptions / limitations.** Paraxial approximation, monochromatic field, vacuum propagation, and hard-edged circular apertures.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the propagation geometry; Figure 2 plots modal transmissivity vs Df; Figure 6 shows mode occupation statistics; Figure 7 compares input efficiency; Figures 8-10 illustrate the evolution of beam intensity profiles across different propagation regimes.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by defining the physical system and the PSW basis, moves to comparing Gaussian beams with PSW modes via numerical optimization, derives a trace theorem for total transmissivity, and concludes with an analysis of beam evolution across different Fresnel regimes.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Slepian developed the Prolate Spheroidal Wavefunction (PSW) spatial-mode basis, which forms the normal modes of the Fresnel-propagation kernel of a free-space optical communications channel bookended by hard-circular apertures. The zero-th order PSW mode has the highest power-transfer eigenvalue, exciting which on the transmitter side therefore maximizes the transmissivity for single-spatial-mode communications. We show that the transmissivity performance of this fundamental PSW mode can be obtained by an aperture-truncated Gaussian beam of an optimized beam waist, despite the two mode shapes deviating from one another in the near-field regime.

</details>

**Authors:** Hao Chen, Jumin Shi, Yinghao Li, Xiangyu Luo, Yiwen Chen, Chaohui Yin, Yingjie Shu, Jiuxiang Zhang, Taimin Miao, Bo Liang, Wenpei Zhu, Neng Cai, Xiaolin Ren, Chengtian Lin, Shenjin Zhang, Zhimin Wang, Fengfeng Zhang, Feng Yang, Qinjun Peng, Zuyan Xu, Guodong Liu, Hanqing Mao, Xintong Li, Tao Xiang, Lin Zhao, X. Formation of the Fermi pockets in terms of the mean-field t-U model and intrinsic doping evolution of the CuO2 plane in the antiferromagnetic Mott insulating state. a,b, Simulated band structures along high-symmetry directions (0,0)–(π,π)–(π,0)–(0,0) (a) and (π,0)–(0,π) (b) based on the mean-field t–U model using t = 0.32 eV, t′ = −0.063 eV, t′′ = 0.031 eV, and ∆MF = 0.68 eV. c-e, Simulated Fermi pockets derived from the band structures in a,b by setting the chemical potential to µ1 = −0.556 eV (c), µ2 = −0.609 eV (d), and µ3 = −0.643 eV (e), as indicated by dashed lines in a,b. These simulated pockets correspond to doping levels of 0.007 (c), 0.051 (d), and 0.088 (e), respectively....</figcaption>
</figure>

**Main problem.** Determining whether the transition from a Mott insulator to a metallic state in cuprates is intrinsic or disorder-driven, and understanding the origin of electron pairing in the lightly doped regime.

**Main result.** Discovery of persistent, gapless Fermi pockets in the innermost CuO2 planes at extremely low doping (p=0.007) and the observation of robust, large anisotropic superconducting gaps (up to 33 meV) in the second innermost planes.

**Method.** High-resolution, spatially-resolved laser angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy (laser-ARPES) using an ARToF electron energy analyzer to probe intrinsic electronic structures.

**Summary.** This paper investigates the electronic structure of the innermost layers of multilayer cuprate superconductors using high-resolution laser-ARPES. The researchers found that even at extremely low doping levels, the CuO2 planes exhibit well-defined metallic Fermi pockets, suggesting an abrupt transition from the Mott insulator. Furthermore, they observed large, anisotropic superconducting gaps in the second innermost planes, indicating that robust electron pairing can coexist with strong antiferromagnetic order. These findings provide a new framework for understanding the doping-driven phase diagram in high-temperature superconductors.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** Multilayer Bi-based cuprate superconductors (Bi2223 and higher-order n=5-8 structures) where inner CuO2 planes are shielded from disorder.

**Key observables.** Fermi surface topology (pockets, arcs), hole doping level (p), superconducting gap magnitude and anisotropy, and spectral weight distribution.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Hole doping levels (p) from 0.007 to 0.088; superconducting gaps up to 33 meV; hopping parameters (t, t', t'').

**Assumptions / limitations.** The inner CuO2 planes are sufficiently shielded from external impurities to provide a disorder-free environment.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1 shows crystal structures and Fermi surface mappings for different layers and dopings; subsequent figures show EDC peaks, spectral weight asymmetry, and gap evolution.

**Paper structure.** Introduction to the Mott insulator-to-metal transition; Experimental setup and sample characterization; Results showing Fermi pocket persistence and doping evolution; Discussion of pairing mechanisms and coexistence with AFM order; Conclusion.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

High temperature superconductivity in cuprate superconductors is generally considered to be generated from doping the Mott insulators. The fundamental nature of the doped parent compounds as well as the microscopic origin of electron pairing remain critical issues in understanding the emergence of superconductivity. Here, using high-resolution spatially-resolved laser angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy, we investigate the intrinsic electronic structures of the CuO$_2$ planes in multilayer cuprates Bi$_2$Sr$_2$Ca$_{n-1}$Cu$_n$O$_{2n+4+δ}$ (n=5$\sim$8). The inner CuO$_2$ planes are well shielded from the disorders and provide a rare and ideal platform to probe the intrinsic electronic phase diagram. We observe well-defined Fermi pockets with hole doping levels as low as 0.007, demonstrating an abrupt transition from the parent Mott insulator to a metallic state upon the introduction of an infinitesimal amount of doping. The innermost CuO$_2$ planes (IP$_0$) display gapless Fermi pockets, while the second innermost planes (IP$_1$) exhibit anisotropic superconducting gaps up to $\sim$33$\,$meV, indicative of robust electron pairing coexisting with strong antiferromagnetic order. Our findings provide a revised framework for understanding the doping-driven transitions and pairing mechanisms in cuprate superconductors.

</details>

### [Practical lower bounds for hybrid quantum interior point methods in linear programming](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.24362v1)

**Authors:** Lennart Binkowski  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.24362v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>

Each dashed arrow marks a benevolent assumption that reduces the bound, making it more favourable to the quantum method. If this lower bound already exceeds the classical runtime, no practical quantum advantage is possible. The dimension d and the sparsity s are calculated exactly, the condition number κ is lower-bounded. The QLSA is assumed to return the approximate solution with certainty, thus allowing to set the number of QAA iterations to nQAA = 1. Furthermore, the number of IR steps for improving the initial precision ε is benevolently set to 1. Lastly, the entire IPM is assumed to converge after one iteration, which...</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24362_figures/2604.24362_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Fig. 2. Effective difficulty γ = sκ of the Newton linear systems across all benchmark instances, for the MNES (left) and OSS (right) formulations. The three binary optimisation relaxations—Clique, Independent Set, and Vertex Cover—constitute predominantly easy Newton systems in both cases. In fact, the condition number κ was often found to be exactly 1, so that their difficulties are effectively given by their sparsity s. In contrast, the Max Flow set, consisting of relaxations of an integer optimisation problem, and the benchmark sets MIPib, Misc, Netlib, and StochLP constitute the most difficult Newton systems.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24362_figures/2604.24362_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> Fig. 3 shows the percentage of instances for which the quantum runtime lower bound lies below the classical HiGHS runtime, as a function of the assumed quantum cycle duration. The instances are broken down into the eight instances classes Clique, Independent Set, Max Flow, MIPlib, Misc, Netlib, StochLP, and Vertex Cover. Despite the looseness of the bounds due to the several benevolent assumptions described above, they result in slower quantum runtimes across all instances for any realistic (logical) quantum cycle duration around the current speed record of 800 ps for a physical en- tangling two-qubit gate. Most importantly, the exclusion result is robust with respect to the choice of...</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24362_figures/2604.24362_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> Fig. 3. Percentage of instances (y-axis), divided into eight problem classes, for which the lower bounds of the two QIPM variants’ runtimes lie below HiGHS’ solve runtime. The runtime lower bound is the product of the established lower bound on the cycle count times the assumed quantum cycle duration (x-axis). Despite the bounds’ looseness due to a chain of several benevolent assumptions, they result in slower quantum runtimes—for both QIPM variants and on all instances—in range of any realistic value for the quantum cycle duration around the current speed record for a physical entangling two-qubit gate at 800 ps [36].</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** Determining whether the asymptotic polynomial speed-ups promised by hybrid quantum interior point methods (QIPMs) translate into practical advantages for solving linear programming problems on realistic instances.

**Main result.** The study establishes that quantum runtime lower bounds exceed classical runtimes for all tested instances at realistic quantum cycle durations, effectively ruling out practical advantage for these hybrid QIPMs.

**Method.** A hybrid benchmarking approach that derives rigorous lower bounds on quantum runtime using highly benevolent assumptions and compares them against the classical HiGHS solver.

**Summary.** This paper investigates the practical viability of hybrid quantum algorithms for linear programming. By comparing the best-case quantum runtime lower bounds against state-of-the-art classical solvers, the author demonstrates that the overhead from state tomography and the complexity of the Newton systems prevent any practical advantage. The findings suggest that for realistic problem instances and hardware cycle times, classical solvers like HiGHS remain superior.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The mathematical model is Linear Programming (LP) solved via Primal-Dual Path-Following Interior Point Methods, utilizing a quantum pipeline with Chebyshev-based Quantum Linear System Algorithms (QLSAs) and state tomography.

**Key observables.** Quantum runtime lower bounds, effective difficulty (product of sparsity and condition number), and tomography overhead.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Sparsity (s), condition number (kappa), target precision (epsilon), and quantum cycle duration.

**Assumptions / limitations.** Highly benevolent assumptions including noise-free hardware, single-cycle oracle execution, convergence in a single iteration, and minimal iterative refinement steps.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 illustrates the quantum runtime lower bound pipeline; Figure 2 shows the distribution of effective difficulty across different problem classes; Figure 3 plots the percentage of instances where quantum lower bounds are below classical runtimes relative to quantum cycle duration.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the QIPM framework, defines the benchmarking methodology and benevolent assumptions, presents the numerical estimation of system parameters, provides the exclusion analysis results across various problem families, and identifies the tomography overhead as the primary bottleneck.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Quantum interior point methods (QIPMs) promise polynomial speed-ups over classical solvers for linear programming by outsourcing the solution of Newton linear systems to quantum linear solvers (QLSAs). However, asymptotic speed-ups do not necessarily translate to practical advantages on realistic problem instances. In this work, I evaluate whether practical advantage of a standard hybrid QIPM pipeline can already be excluded relative to the classical open-source solver HiGHS on a broad and diverse collection of LP instances spanning eight problem families, including public benchmark libraries, such as MIPlib, and relaxations of combinatorial optimisation problems. Following the hybrid benchmarking paradigm initiated by Cade et al., I derive rigorous lower bounds on the quantum runtime under a series of highly benevolent assumptions and compare them against classical runtimes. I equip the QIPMs with the best-performing functional QLSA, the Chebyshev-based method, as identified by Lefterovici et al., and evaluate two Newton system formulations proposed by Mohammadisiahroudi et al.: the modified normal equation system and the orthogonal subspace system. The exclusion analysis yields a consistent negative picture: across all instances and for any realistic quantum cycle duration, the quantum runtime lower bounds already exceed the classical runtimes, establishing that these hybrid QIPMs will offer no practical advantage over good classical solvers for realistic linear programming instances.

</details>

**Authors:** Yuval Idan, Eitan Zahavi, Elad Mentovich, Eliahu Cohen, Shmuel Zaks  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.23700v1>

**Main problem.** The paper addresses the computational complexity and optimization of quantum circuit cutting, which involves partitioning large quantum circuits into smaller fragments to be processed on multiple smaller QPUs.

**Main result.** The authors prove that finding the optimal circuit-cutting configuration (the Graph Duplication problem) is NP-complete in general and even for simplified 2-qubit gate circuits, but can be solved in polynomial time if the number of cuts is constant.

**Method.** The work uses a graph-theoretic framework mapping circuits to Directed A-cyclic Graphs (DAGs) and employs reductions from the 3-partition problem to establish complexity, alongside an SMT-based solver for practical optimization.

**Summary.** This paper provides a rigorous complexity-theoretic analysis of quantum circuit cutting, a vital technique for executing large circuits on fragmented NISQ hardware. By mapping the cutting process to a graph duplication problem, the authors demonstrate that finding optimal cuts is NP-complete. However, they also show that the problem is tractable when the number of cuts is small and provide an SMT-based solver to find these optimal configurations. This work helps define the computational limits of circuit decomposition strategies.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The study focuses on the NISQ era quantum circuit model, representing quantum operations as tensors and qubit trajectories as edges within a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) or Tensor Network.

**Important parameters / regimes.** beta (number of edge duplications/cuts), alpha (number of clusters), k (maximum number of input/output wires per cluster), and the qubit budget Q.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis focuses on 'timelike' cuts (wire cuts) and assumes 'legal' DAGs where the number of inputs equals the number of outputs.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows tensor network evolution; Figure 2 illustrates unitary decomposition; Figure 3 shows the graph duplication protocol; Figures 4-7 visualize complexity proofs and SMT solver performance; Figures 8-10 detail the mathematical construction for NP-completeness proofs.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the circuit cutting problem, establishes a graph-theoretic duality, provides a complexity characterization (identifying polynomial and NP-complete regimes), proposes an SMT-based optimization algorithm, and provides rigorous mathematical proofs in the appendix.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

The current noisy intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) era is characterized by substantial errors and noise, which limit the practical feasibility of deep, many-qubit circuits. To address these constraints, quantum circuit cutting has emerged as a promising tool. Recently, there has been significant research on methods for performing such cutting effectively. In this work, the duality between quantum circuits and classical graphs - specifically, directed acyclic graphs (dags) - is leveraged to analyze the complexity of finding an optimal circuit-cutting configuration that minimizes the number of cuts. After developing a rigorous graph-theoretic framework, the complexity of identifying cut locations that partition a given quantum circuit into smaller fragments is characterized. The corresponding graph-combinatorial task is then defined, and the resulting partition problem is shown to be NP-complete. Furthermore, even a simplified version of the problem, restricted to circuits composed only of one- and two-qubit gates, is shown to be NP-complete. Finally, based on these constraints, an algorithm grounded in satisfiability modulo theories (SMT) is proposed to find optimal cuts when the number of qubits per partition is bounded. This work therefore provides a complexity-theoretic characterization of cut placement and a practical solver for bounded-size decompositions.

</details>

<a id="paper-2604.23544"></a>
### [Regularization of Divergent Power Sums via Fractional Extension of Differential Generators](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23544v1)

**Authors:** Eric A. Galapon  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.23544v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23544_figures/2604.23544_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> Figure 1. The plot of Liα(exp(−z)). There is one main branch cut emanating from the zeros of z at z = 0. The other branch cuts emanate from the solutions to exp(−z) = 1 which are not zeros of z.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23544_figures/2604.23544_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Figure 2. The plot of Liα(exp(−z −z3)). There are three main branch cuts emanating from the zeros of (z +z3) at z = 0, ±i. The other branch cuts emanate from the solutions to exp(−z −z3) = 1 which are not zeros of (z + z3).</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** The paper addresses the lack of continuity in standard Riemann zeta regularization when moving from integer to non-integer exponents alpha in divergent power sums.

**Main result.** The author develops a new regularization prescription using fractional extensions of differential generators that recovers Riemann zeta regularization as a special case while ensuring continuity and providing additional terms determined by the generator.

**Method.** The method employs fractional calculus, contour integration using Hankel-type contours, and the construction of a Generalized Spectral Function (GSF) via a differential generator L.

**Summary.** This paper introduces a generalized mathematical framework for regularizing divergent power series. By using fractional extensions of differential operators, the author creates a regularization scheme that is continuous for both integer and non-integer exponents. This method generalizes the well-known Riemann zeta regularization and allows for new regularized values that depend on the specific choice of a differential generator. The work provides a consistent way to handle branch points and singularities in the context of spectral functions.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The mathematical framework is applied to the regularization of divergent sums of the form sum n^alpha, with physical motivation drawn from non-interacting fermions in a box and the calculation of restoring forces.

**Key observables.** Regularized power sums, regularized products, and the generalized zeta function.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The exponent alpha (complex order), the differential generator L, and the function h(t) defining the generator.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The Generalized Spectral Function must have a holomorphic complex extension with a pole at the origin, and the function Phi(z) is assumed to be an entire function.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows a complex plot of the polylogarithm Li_alpha(exp(-z)) highlighting branch cuts; Figure 2 shows a complex plot of the polylogarithm with a more complex generator, illustrating additional branch cuts.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by defining the regularization problem, introduces the differential generator and GSF framework, develops the fractional extension via contour integrals, proves the equivalence of differential and integral forms, and concludes with explicit examples and comparison to Riemann zeta regularization.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We reconsider the problem of regularizing the divergent series $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}n^α$ for $\operatorname{Re}α>-1$, and offer a regularization prescription that yields the Riemann zeta regularization as a special case. The development of the regularization is framed as a two-step problem. The first step is prescribing a regularization of the divergent sum $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}n^m$ for every non-negative integer $m$; and the second step is the extension of the sum for non-integer $α$. The extension is obtained under the consistency condition that the regularized sum for integer $m$ emerges continuously from the sum for non-integer $α$. The scheme is specified by a differential generator $L=L(\mathrm{d}/\mathrm{d}t)$ through which a generalized spectral function (GSF), $K_L(t)$, is constructed. Under the condition that the GSF has a holomorphic complex extension $K_L(z)$ with $z=0$ as a pole, the case for integer $m$ takes the regularized value $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} n^m = (2πi)^{-1}\oint_C L^m K_L(z) z^{-1}\mathrm{d}z$, where $C$ is a closed contour enclosing only the pole of $K_L(z)$ at the origin. On the other hand, under the consistency condition, the case for non-integer $α$ takes the value $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}n^α=(2πi)^{-1}\int_{\tilde{C}} L^α K_L(z) z^{-1}\mathrm{d}z$, where $L^α$ is the fractional extension of $L^m$ and $\tilde{C}$ is an appropriate deformation of the contour $C$. Here, we obtain the regularization corresponding to the generator $L=h(t) \mathrm{d}/\mathrm{d}t$, where $h(t)$ has the analytic extension $h(z)$ such that $1/h(z)$ is an entire function. We find that the regularized sum is equal to the Riemann zeta regularized value plus terms determined by the generator $L$.

</details>

**Authors:** Neelam Shukla, Oleksandr V. Marchukov, Bastien Humbert, Jan Arlt, Jeremy Armstrong, Artem G. Volosniev  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum gases · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.23219v1>

The lower panel is for 0.50 radians, and the oscillation pattern indicates the presence of other modes.
