<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> FIG. 4. Transmission and absorption for a non-relativistic Schr¨odinger particle traversing a static and noisy barrier as a function of k0L. With k0 = √2mU0. The top row corresponds to electrons with energy ϵ = 1.1 U0 that is larger than the barrier height: (a) transmission T and (b) absorption A. The bottom row corresponds to the tunneling regime ϵ = 0.9 U0: (c) transmission T and (d) absorption A. Curves are shown for noise strength V0/U0 = 0 (red), 0.01 (blue), and 0.02 (green).</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23279_figures/2604.23279_fig5.jpg" alt="Figure 5">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> FIG. 7. Transmission (a) and absorption (b) probabilities for electrons in graphene tunneling through a noisy barrier as a function of incoming energy for an incident angle ϕ = 45◦, barrier width L = 110 nm, and static barrier height is U0 = 200 meV . The three curves correspond to noise strength V0 = 0 (red), 20 (blue), and 40 meV(green).</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23279_figures/2604.23279_fig6.jpg" alt="Figure 6">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 6.</b> FIG. 6. Angular dependence of transmission and ab- sorption probabilities for tunneling through a noisy barrier in graphene. Panels (a,b) show the transmission T(ϕ) for U0 = 200 and 285 meV, and panels (c,d) show the correspond- ing absorption A(ϕ). The incoming energy is ϵ = 80 meV, the barrier width is L = 110 nm. Curves correspond to the noise strength V0 = 0, 20, and 40 meV.Parameters are chosen to facilitate comparison with standard Klein-tunneling setups in the literature, see Ref. [6].</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23279_figures/2604.23279_fig7.jpg" alt="Figure 7">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 7.</b> FIG. 8. Illustration of Quantum confinement in two cases:(a) Clean double barrier where Klein tunneling dominates, and the particle cannot efficiently be confined. (b) A noisy double barrier, based on our calculations, could suppress Klein tun- neling and confine the particle.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** Investigating how temporal noise (stochastic fluctuations in barrier height) can be used as a mechanism to suppress Klein tunneling in graphene.

**Main result.** Noise induces a complex longitudinal wavevector in graphene, leading to the exponential suppression of Klein tunneling even at normal incidence, effectively turning a noisy barrier into a tunable dissipative element.

**Method.** The authors map the stochastic dynamics of a time-dependent potential onto a time-independent Lindblad master equation for the density matrix, which is then solved using a spatial matching technique in a nine-region construction.

**Summary.** This paper explores how temporal fluctuations in a potential barrier can suppress the Klein tunneling effect in graphene. By using a Lindblad master equation approach, the authors show that noise introduces an imaginary component to the wavevector, causing exponential decay of transmission. This mechanism allows for the tuning of barrier opacity without changing the physical geometry. The findings suggest that controlled noise could improve electron confinement in graphene-based devices, such as quantum dots for spin qubits.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The study models electrons in graphene (massless Dirac fermions) and non-relativistic Schrödinger particles traversing a 1D rectangular potential barrier whose height fluctuates according to Gaussian white noise.

**Key observables.** Transmission probability (T), reflection probability (R), and absorption probability (A), representing the loss of coherent flux.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Barrier height (U0), noise strength (V0), barrier width (L), incident energy (epsilon), and incident angle (phi).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The noise is modeled specifically as Gaussian white noise (Markovian/memoryless), and the potential is assumed to be piecewise constant.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1 shows a schematic of the noisy barrier; Fig 2 shows a realization of Gaussian white noise; Fig 3 shows the nine-region construction in the (x, x') plane; Fig 4 shows T and A for Schrödinger particles; Fig 6 shows angular dependence of T and A; Fig 7 shows energy dependence; Fig 8 compares clean vs. noisy double barriers for confinement.

**Paper structure.** The paper establishes a benchmark with Schrödinger particles, develops the Lindblad formalism for noise, applies it to graphene to demonstrate Klein tunneling suppression, and discusses applications to quantum dots.

**Why it may be interesting.** The paper provides an analytical framework for treating stochastic driving as a control mechanism for transport, demonstrating how dissipation can be used to break symmetry-protected phenomena like Klein tunneling.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We study quantum tunneling through a potential barrier whose height fluctuates in time and is modeled by Gaussian white noise. We map the stochastic dynamics onto an equivalent time-independent Lindblad equation for the density matrix, allowing fully analytical solutions. For Schrödinger particles, noise introduces dissipation that suppresses Fabry-Pérot oscillations and yields an exponentially decaying transmission. Applying the same formalism to graphene, we demonstrate that noise induces a complex longitudinal wavevector within the barrier, leading to a strong suppression of transmission and Klein tunneling, even at normal incidence. Our approach promises improved control over Klein tunneling. These results demonstrate that noisy barriers can act as tunable dissipative elements, offering a pathway to enhanced control of electron transport in graphene-based devices. We also briefly discuss how our results could guide the design of graphene quantum dots for potential use in spin qubit devices.

</details>

**Main problem.** Understanding the mechanism behind the formation of large-scale, density-protected ring structures in active matter under virtual confinement, such as light-responsive patterns.

**Main result.** The system self-assembles into a high-density nematic ring at the boundary of illumination, which creates a protected disordered core with a density independent of the global particle density.

**Method.** The study combines microscopic agent-based simulations of a 2D Vicsek-style model with a hydrodynamic continuum theory and linear stability analysis.

**Summary.** This paper investigates how active nematic particles under light-induced confinement self-organize into dense rings. These rings act as a screen, creating a central core where the density is 'protected' and remains independent of the overall system density. The researchers use both particle simulations and hydrodynamic theory to show that this effect arises from the interplay between alignment and active currents. The findings provide a robust mechanism for pattern formation in active matter that is applicable to various experimental platforms.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** A 2D model of dry active nematics consisting of self-propelled particles with nematic symmetry, subject to virtual confinement via light-induced polarity reversals at a circular boundary.

**Key observables.** Local density, nematic amplitude (S), nematic angle (Phi), nematic tensor (Q), and radial density profiles.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Repulsion strength (beta), global particle density (rho_0), illumination radius (R), and topological charge (k).

**Assumptions / limitations.** Thin interface limit for the light-induced boundary, a minimal truncation of hydrodynamic equations, and small noise amplitude.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows an experimental reference of cyanobacteria forming rings and a simulation snapshot of a stationary ring configuration with nematic angle color-coding.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem via experimental motivation, presents a microscopic model and its hydrodynamic counterpart, details the emergence of ring formation and density protection, analyzes the scaling and stability of these states, and concludes with limitations.

**Why it may be interesting.** The concept of density protection and boundary-induced structure formation via localized driving provides insights into non-equilibrium phase transitions and the stability of patterns in driven-dissipative systems.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We investigate photo-responsive structure formation in a minimal model of dry active nematics. Combining microscopic simulations with the analysis of the corresponding hydrodynamic theory, we show that the system generically self-assembles into a dense, nematically ordered ring at the boundary of circular illumination patterns. Remarkably, these boundary structures give rise to a protected disordered core whose density is self-selected and independent of the global particle density. Our analysis reveals that these states emerge from a generic interplay between local nematic alignment and curvature-driven active currents. These results identify a robust route to boundary-induced structure formation in active matter and provide experimentally testable predictions.

</details>

**Main problem.** The paper addresses the 'dephasing time problem' in graphene HHG, specifically why experimental high-order harmonics above 3 eV are suppressed and why a very short phenomenological dephasing time is required in simulations.

**Main result.** Optical phonons cause 'phase scrambling' through destructive interference in the interband current, which suppresses high-energy harmonics and explains the experimental lack of emission above 3 eV. This effect is driven by zero-point motion and is largely temperature-independent.

**Method.** The authors use a 'static limit' formalism where the lattice is frozen during the electronic timescale, computing HHG by ensemble-averaging over thermally-occupied phonon snapshots using Semiconductor Bloch Equations.

**Summary.** This paper investigates how optical phonons in graphene influence high harmonic generation (HHG). It reveals that phonons induce phase variations that lead to destructive interference, effectively suppressing high-energy harmonics above 3 eV. The study finds that this suppression is primarily due to zero-point motion, making the effect robust even at low temperatures. Furthermore, the research identifies electron-phonon scattering as the dominant source of electronic dephasing in the strong-field regime.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The system is monolayer graphene modeled with a second-order nearest-neighbor tight-binding Hamiltonian. The model includes longitudinal and transverse optical (LO/TO) phonon modes at the Gamma point that perturb the hopping amplitudes.

**Key observables.** Time-dependent current (intraband and interband components), HHG spectral power, harmonic phase distributions, and HHG ellipticity-dependent curves.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Laser frequency, ellipticity, temperature, phonon frequency, and a calculated dephasing time of T2 ~ 5.7 fs.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The 'static limit' approximation assumes the lattice is frozen on the electronic timescale; phonons are treated as fixed snapshots that act on electrons without back-action.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 illustrates the phonon-induced lattice distortions and sampling; Figure 2 compares HHG spectra with and without phonons; Figure 4 shows interband coherence decay and dephasing fitting; Figure 5 demonstrates how phonons smooth the ellipticity dependence of harmonics.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the dephasing problem and the role of phonons, describes the tight-binding and phonon-sampling model, presents results on HHG suppression and phase scrambling, discusses temperature dependence and dephasing rates, and concludes with implications for other 2D materials.

**Why it may be interesting.** It provides a concrete physical mechanism (phononic phase scrambling) for electronic decoherence in a strong-field regime, which is highly relevant to the study of open quantum systems and ultrafast dynamics in solids.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

High harmonic generation (HHG) is a widely explored process in solids, where intense lasers drive attosecond-to-femtosecond electron dynamics within bands, causing high-energy emission. While electrons and photons are considered the main players in HHG, solids also host ubiquitous phonons that are typically assumed negligible in HHG due to their longer timescales. We theoretically study HHG in graphene with a formalism including optical phonons in the static limit, where the lattice is frozen on the electronic timescale and HHG is computed by sampling thermally-occupied phonons and ensemble-averaging. We show that in graphene: (i) Optical phonons strongly suppress HHG yields by coupling to interband currents and causing harmonic phase scrambling (destructive interference), explaining the lack of experimental HHG above ~3 eV. (ii) HHG yields become temperature-dependent due to phonon occupations, though in graphene this dependence is weak since phonon energy scales are dominated by zero-point motion. (iii) Optical phonons dephase interband coherences at a rate equivalent to T2~5.7 fs, substantially faster than e-e scattering, suggesting thermal phonons dominate electronic decoherence in strong fields. (iv) Phonons smoothen HHG ellipticity-dependent curves, yielding better agreement with experiments. Remarkably, all effects are timescale-independent, arising in the static picture of electron-phonon interactions, making results transferable to attosecond phenomena. Our results shed light on the dephasing time problem in HHG and the role of phonons on attosecond timescales, with implications for other systems and processes such as Floquet gaps and photocurrents in graphene.

</details>

### [Non-Hermitian corner skin effect in a two-dimensional photonic crystal](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23209v1)

**Authors:** Huyen Thanh Phan, Katsunori Wakabayashi  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.23209v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `analog quantum simulation` **2/5** · `correlated / nonlocal dissipation` **2/5** · `driven-dissipative phase transition` **2/5** · `methods for driven-dissipative` **2/5** · `non-equilibrium dynamics` **2/5** · `spintronics-quantum-optics interface` **2/5**

The green arrows denote the winding direction (clockwise or anti-clockwise) when kx is varied from −π/a0 to π/a0. Number 1 and 2 indicate band indices.The lower panels are field profile of the states labeled by green stars in the corresponding band structures. The states enclosed by the point gap exhibit skin effect, where EM waves are highly localized at the boundaries of structures.</figcaption>
</figure>

Red dots indicate real part of geometrical phase, blue dots denote imaginary part of geometrical phase. (b) The coordinate system in (a) is equivalent to a torus under periodic boundary condition. (c) The real and imaginary part of the complex geometrical phase are represented by red and blue lines on the torus.</figcaption>
</figure>

<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> FIG. 5. (a) Schematic of investigated finite structure, system size is 20 × 20 unit cells. PMC boundary condition is applied to all four boundaries. (b) Photonic dispersion in the complex plane of the finite structure (red) in (a) and semi-infinite (black) in Fig. 2(c). The inset shows enlarged figure of the region enclosed by the blue dot line. The topological edge states form loop in complex plane and encloses the complex eigenfrequencies of the corner modes. (c) Field profile of the corner skin modes labeled by the stars in (b) and the extended topological edge states labeled by the magenta circle in (b).</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23209_figures/2604.23209_fig6.jpg" alt="Figure 6">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 6.</b> FIG. 6. (a) Schematic of eigenfrequencies of the infinite structure (black) and eigenfrequencies of semi-infinite structure (red). From left to right panels, kim x is varied from 0 to the critical point, where eigenfrequencies of two structures become identical. (b) Band struc- ture of infinite structure with real kx (black), semi-infinite structure (red) and infinite structure with complex ˜kx.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** Investigating the second-order non-Hermitian skin effect (corner localization) in a continuous 2D photonic crystal, moving beyond traditional tight-binding models.

**Main result.** Demonstrated that a point gap in the complex eigenfrequency spectrum leads to the emergence of corner skin modes in truncated structures, distinguishing them from Hermitian topological corner states.

**Method.** Numerical simulations using the Finite Element Method (FEM) and extended plane-wave expansion, combined with non-Bloch band theory and complex Berry phase analysis.

**Summary.** This paper explores the non-Hermitian corner skin effect in a 2D photonic crystal made of lossy magneto-optical materials. The authors show that point gaps in the complex frequency spectrum cause electromagnetic waves to localize at the corners of finite structures. Unlike traditional topological corner states, these skin modes are driven by non-Hermitian topology and their number scales with the system size. This work is significant because it moves the study of non-Hermitian skin effects from simplified tight-binding models to more realistic, continuous photonic platforms.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** A 2D non-Hermitian photonic crystal composed of lossy magneto-optical materials arranged in a square unit cell, featuring broken inversion, mirror, and time-reversal symmetries.

**Key observables.** Complex eigenfrequencies, winding numbers, Chern numbers, and spatial field profiles of edge and corner states.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Point gaps in the complex spectrum, winding number (w), and the presence of nonreciprocity via the permittivity tensor.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The lattice constant is fixed at a=1, and the magnetization is assumed to be parallel to the z-direction.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows 1D vs 2D band structure topology; Figure 2 displays the unit cell, dispersion, and ribbon edge state profiles; Figure 3 shows complex eigenfrequency spectra; Figure 5 illustrates corner skin mode localization and field profiles; Figure 6 compares infinite and ribbon-based complex wave vector dispersions.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the non-Hermitian skin effect, describes the photonic crystal model and its symmetry breaking, presents numerical results for ribbon (edge) and finite (corner) structures, provides topological characterization via winding numbers, and concludes with a comparison to existing models.

**Why it may be interesting.** The study of non-Hermitian topology and skin effects is highly relevant to open quantum systems and quantum optics, as it provides a realistic continuous-system platform for studying how dissipation and non-reciprocity drive novel localization phenomena.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We numerically study topological effects of electromagnetic (EM) waves   in a two-dimensional (2D) non-Hermitian photonic crystal (PhC) composed   of lossy magneto-optical materials. In this system, not only the EM   wavefunctions but also the complex eigenfrequencies exhibit nontrivial   topological properties. We demonstrate that the non-Hermitian skin   effect, protected by point gaps in the complex eigenfrequency spectrum,   emerges at both the edges and corners of truncated structures. This   phenomenon has no counterpart in Hermitian systems. In addition, we identify non-Hermitian topological edge states   originating from the nontrivial topology of the bulk bands. While most   previous studies of non-Hermitian topology have focused on   tight-binding models, our work addresses a continuous photonic system,   providing a more realistic platform and offering a concrete route   toward experimental realization of non-Hermitian effects.

</details>

<a id="paper-2604.24387"></a>
### [A practicable method for the analysis of complex motion of biological and soft matter](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.24387v1)

**Authors:** Jun Ma  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.24387v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

**Main problem.** The lack of an analytical method to decipher the complex, stochastic 'fingerprint' trajectories of biological and soft matter to extract underlying dynamical information.

**Main result.** The development of a method that maps the Gaussian probability distribution of Brownian particle displacements onto a propagating wave packet, allowing for the extraction of microscopic dynamical information from irregular trajectories.

**Method.** A quantization-inspired approach that transforms the probability density of particle displacement into a wave function, using Fourier transforms and Taylor expansions of the angular frequency to relate ballistic motion to wave packet dynamics.

**Summary.** This paper presents a new analytical method for studying the complex, random motions found in biological and soft matter. By treating the stochastic trajectories of particles like Brownian motion as propagating wave packets, the author can extract hidden dynamical information from seemingly random paths. The method bridges the gap between microscopic ballistic steps and macroscopic diffusion. This approach allows for a systematic study of how spatio-temporal microstructures evolve in complex fluids.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** A 1D model of a spherical Brownian particle (e.g., proteins, bacteria, or silica beads) undergoing a series of random ballistic motion steps in a fluid, driven by a random thermal field.

**Key observables.** Mean-squared displacement, probability distribution P(x,t), group velocity (vg), mean displacement, and diffusion constant (D).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Ballistic step length (l), step time (tb), diffusion exponent (alpha), diffusion constant (D), and effective mass (m*).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The initial distribution of the wave function is Gaussian, the number of extra steps follows a Gaussian distribution, and the time intervals are integer multiples of the ballistic step time.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows irregular Brownian trajectories and a 1D model of a silica bead performing ballistic motion with direction flipping. Figure 2 illustrates the wave packet propagation mechanism, showing how oscillating motion and drift lead to net displacement.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of analyzing irregular trajectories, proposes a wave packet quantization model, derives the mathematical framework for the wave function, maps microscopic parameters to macroscopic observables, and concludes with the implications for studying soft matter dynamics.

**Why it may be interesting.** The paper uses a mathematical analogy between Brownian motion uncertainty and quantum mechanical wave packets, providing a framework for treating stochastic classical trajectories using tools similar to those used in open quantum systems and wave mechanics.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Biological function of living matter is fulfilled by complex motions of biological and soft matter. Unlike general motion is deterministic described by Newton's laws, these motions are mostly random and uncertain for the position in stochastic process, being characterized as irregular trajectories of movement without a defined velocity. Like human fingerprint, the trajectory is the identity of the motion containing fundamental dynamical information. Such irregular trajectories randomly inter-wind and twist to each other to produce a complicated turmoil configuration in which so far the unrealized mechanism of motion is hidden. Nowadays, the analytical method for this fingerprint trajectory is still missed. Here we develop a practicable method to decipher complicated trajectory configuration, which uncovers abundant dynamical information hiding in irregular trajectories, revealing the remarkable evolution of spatial-temporal micro-structure, thus leading to the novel systematic study of the dynamics of biological and soft matter.

</details>

**Main problem.** Investigating the generation and control of spin currents via the phonon-mediated Spin Seebeck Effect (SSE) in a normal-metal-chiral-insulator heterostructure.

**Main result.** The study identifies two nonlinear phenomena: negative differential SSE, where increasing thermal bias decreases spin current, and spin-current rectification, which enables a thermally controlled spin diode.

**Method.** The authors utilize the nonequilibrium Green's function (NEGF) formalism to calculate spin transport and solve for Green's functions and phonon self-energies self-consistently.

**Summary.** This paper explores how the angular momentum of chiral phonons can be converted into electron spin current in a metal-insulator heterostructure. By using the NEGF formalism, the authors demonstrate that this process can lead to unexpected nonlinear effects like negative differential spin Seebeck effect and spin-current rectification. These findings suggest that such structures could function as thermally controlled spin diodes. The work provides a theoretical foundation for developing new types of phonon-driven spintronic devices.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** A two-terminal heterostructure consisting of a normal metal (NM) and a chiral insulator (CI), where the central region is modeled as a quantum dot with spin-microrotation coupling between electrons and chiral phonons.

**Key observables.** Spin current (Is) in the left lead and the rectification ratio (eta).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Thermal bias (delta T), average temperature (T0), chemical potential (muL), on-site potential (epsilon_d), and phonon coupling strength (alpha).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The central region is simplified to a quantum dot model to capture essential physics, and the phonon reservoir is modeled using a super-Ohmic spectral density.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1 shows device schematics for phonon-to-spin conversion; Fig 2 illustrates spin current dependence on thermal bias, chemical potential, and interfacial potential; Fig 3 plots effective spectral and spin current densities; Fig 4 shows the rectification ratio.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the physical mechanism of phonon-mediated SSE, presents the theoretical NEGF framework, analyzes the effects of various parameters (bias, chemical potential, interface), identifies nonlinear transport phenomena, and concludes with implications for spintronic devices.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Phonons can carry angular momentum and exhibit chirality through the circular polarization of atomic motion. This enables a phonon-mediated spin Seebeck effect (SSE) via the conversion of phonon angular momentum into electron spin angular momentum. In this Letter, we develop a theoretical framework for calculating the spin current in a normal-metal (NM)-chiral-insulator (CI) heterostructure within the nonequilibrium Green's function formalism. We discuss the influence of (i) the thermal bias across the NM-CI interface, (ii) the chemical potential of the NM, and (iii) the insertion of an additional interfacial layer, on the spin transport properties. We identify two remarkable nonlinear spin transport phenomena: negative differential SSE and spin-current rectification. The negative differential SSE arises from the competition between the thermal bias and the thermally excited electron density. The spin-current rectification suggests the possibility of realizing a thermally controlled spin diode. We also find that the spin transport behavior is closely associated with an effective interfacial spectral density. This work provides a novel route toward thermally controlled spintronic devices using chiral phonons.

</details>

**Main problem.** Addressing the limitations of conventional polycrystalline metal films, such as high Ohmic losses and surface roughness, by utilizing crystalline metal flakes for advanced plasmonics and hybrid 2D material architectures.

**Main result.** Crystalline metal flakes provide superior structural definition and reduced scattering, enabling enhanced plasmonic performance, precise nanostructuring via FIB/EBL, and effective integration with 2D materials.

**Method.** The paper reviews chemical synthesis methods (polyol, Brust-Schiffrin, etc.), nanofabrication techniques (FIB, EBL, etching), and characterization tools (SEM, TEM, SNOM, ellipsometry).

**Summary.** This review examines the emergence of crystalline metal flakes as a superior alternative to traditional polycrystalline films for nanophotonics. It highlights how their high crystallinity and atomic flatness reduce optical losses and enable precise nanostructuring. The paper covers various chemical synthesis routes, fabrication methods like FIB and EBL, and the integration of these flakes with 2D materials. Ultimately, it positions these flakes as essential platforms for advanced applications in sensing, optoelectronics, and quantum plasmonics.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The primary system consists of quasi-monocrystalline noble metal flakes (Au, Ag, Al, Cu) characterized by atomically flat surfaces, well-defined {111} facets, and stacking faults.

**Key observables.** Surface Plasmon Polariton (SPP) propagation length, complex permittivity, surface roughness, aspect ratio, and nonlinear optical responses.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Thickness (from ~1.9 nm to >1 um), lateral size (up to ~250 um), and crystal facets ({111} vs {100}).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The flakes are treated as quasi-monocrystals due to the presence of twin planes/stacking faults required for anisotropic growth.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1: Morphology and crystal structure of Au flakes; Fig 2: Linear optical properties and SPP propagation; Fig 3: Comparison of Ga-FIB vs He-FIB milling quality; Fig 4: EBL fabrication flow and patterned nanostructures; Fig 5: Examples of micromanipulation techniques.

**Paper structure.** The review begins by contrasting polycrystalline vs. crystalline films, moves to the structural and chemical synthesis of flakes, discusses fabrication and nanostructuring techniques, explores integration with 2D materials, and concludes with future prospects.

**Why it may be interesting.** The paper is highly relevant to quantum optics and nanophotonics due to its discussion on quantum plasmonics, nonclassical light generation, and the manipulation of light-matter interactions in hybrid architectures.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Crystalline noble metal flakes are emerging as versatile platforms in nanophotonics, enabling a broad range of optical phenomena and applications. Their atomically flat surfaces, high crystallinity, and superior optical quality open new avenues in advanced plasmonics, quantum light generation, and hybrid photonic systems. In contrast to conventional polycrystalline metal films, which typically suffer from higher optical losses due to grain boundaries, surface roughness, and structural disorder, these monocrystalline flakes provide minimal scattering and enhanced performance. They serve as templates for precise nanostructuring through techniques like focused-ion beam (FIB) milling and are crucial for advanced applications in sensing and optoelectronics. Additionally, they facilitate frontier research in quantum plasmonics, enabling fundamental studies of nonlocal optical effects and the generation of nonclassical light. Furthermore, the well-defined $\{111\}$ facets of these flakes host Tamm--Shockley surface states that support 2D plasmons coexisting with bulk modes. At near-infrared wavelengths and beyond, crystalline flakes act as nearly ideal metallic mirrors, featuring surface roughness limited only to atomic terrace steps, making them highly suitable for integration with 2D materials in hybrid photonic architectures. This review surveys the key roles these flakes play, highlighting recent developments and discussing future prospects while emphasizing their unique benefits in addressing fundamental and applied challenges in modern nanophotonics.

</details>

**Authors:** William Ratcliff  
**Type:** both · **Category:** disordered systems and neural networks · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.23821v1>

<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> Figure 1: Hybrid TAS-AI workflow. The controller begins with agnostic Log-GP mapping to localize signal, hands off to physics-informed discrimina- tion/refinement once structure is detected, and uses motion-aware sequencing to optimize wall-clock time. An optional constrained audit layer can request targeted falsification probes under the same kinematic and safety constraints enforced by the numerical planners.</figcaption>

</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23821_figures/2604.23821_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> Figure 3: Analytic blind-reconstruction benchmarks. Agnostic methods are favored by the global reconstruction metric because they are optimized for dis- covery rather than for parameter inference.</figcaption>
</figure>

<img src="2604.23821_figures/2604.23821_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">

Panel (c) reports mean final weighted reconstruction error after 300 measurements, with individual seed values overlaid. Under this stricter realism-heavy whole- window benchmark, none of the methods reaches the 0.20 threshold within budget; enhanced Log-GP is competitive with grid on the gapless case and performs best on the gapped case, while physics-only TAS-AI performs poorly on this blind global-mapping objective.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23821_figures/2604.23821_fig5.jpg" alt="Figure 5">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> Figure 5: Controlled time-aware parameter-refinement study for the square- lattice ferromagnet. Once the model family is fixed, the motion-aware physics- informed planner reaches the RMS threshold earliest by prioritizing parameter- sensitive measurements with high information rate. Panel (a) shows RMS er- ror versus elapsed experiment time, panel (b) shows the TAS-AI path through (𝐻, 𝐸) space, and panel (c) separates science counting from motion overhead. In the representative run shown here, TAS-AI reaches threshold in 170 s while the random baseline does so only after 542 s; grid and Log-GP do not converge within the same budget.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23821_figures/2604.23821_fig6.jpg" alt="Figure 6">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 6.</b> Figure 6: In-loop Hamiltonian discrimination. The x-axis counts cumulative measurements, including the seeded points used to initialize the comparison; thus the decisive jump occurs within eight total measurements rather than eight post-seed updates. The AIC-derived evidence ratio becomes decisive in the representative run because the planner targets the regions where the competing dispersions diverge most strongly. In panel (d), the green vertical guides mark the measurement locations used for the corresponding discrimination trace.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23821_figures/2604.23821_fig7.jpg" alt="Figure 7">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 7.</b> Figure 7: Controlled motion-aware scheduling diagnostics on a fixed candidate set. Panel (a) shows the fixed candidate measurements colored by their posi- tion in the executed schedule, so the color scale encodes traversal order rather than intensity. Panels (b)-(d) then separate the corresponding cumulative path length, elapsed time, and per-point timing summaries. The figure is intended as a scheduling diagnostic, not as a second benchmark of adaptive discovery.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23821_figures/2604.23821_fig8.jpg" alt="Figure 8">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 8.</b> Figure 8: MCTS batch planning reduces path ineﬀiciency relative to one-step greedy ordering when motion dominates the cost budget.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23821_figures/2604.23821_fig9.jpg" alt="Figure 9">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 9.</b> Figure 9: Hybrid handoff from agnostic discovery to physics-informed inference. The agnostic front end uses the enhanced Log-GP policy (§3.1). In panel (d), the colored vertical bands mark the contiguous controller phases: coarse grid discovery, active Log-GP remapping, and physics-informed refinement. The figure makes the control transition explicit rather than mixing all points into a single trajectory.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23821_figures/2604.23821_fig10.jpg" alt="Figure 10">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 10.</b> Figure 10: Pilot LLM-audited closed-loop run (90 measurements). The left panel shows the executed measurements over the synthetic intensity map, with enhanced Log-GP grid, enhanced Log-GP active, and physics points shown sep- arately. The right panel shows the final model posteriors. The overseer operates only at the routing layer while preserving the same numerical posteriors, kine- matic constraints, and safety checks as the non-LLM loop.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** The challenge of automating neutron spectroscopy, specifically balancing the tasks of signal detection, Hamiltonian inference, and parameter refinement while avoiding 'algorithmic myopia' where a controller over-refines an incorrect model.

**Main result.** The TAS-AI framework successfully uses a hybrid agnostic-to-physics-informed workflow to accelerate characterization, reducing wall-clock time by 32% and using an LLM-based audit layer to prevent model lock-in.

**Method.** A hybrid active learning framework (TAS-AI) combining Log-Gaussian Process mapping for discovery, physics-informed planning for refinement, and an LLM-based 'audit committee' for strategic falsification.

**Summary.** This paper presents TAS-AI, an autonomous framework designed to accelerate the characterization of quantum materials via neutron spectroscopy. It addresses the inefficiency of using a single controller for all experimental tasks by proposing a hybrid approach: model-agnostic methods for initial signal discovery and physics-informed methods for parameter refinement. The authors also introduce an LLM-based audit layer to prevent the system from getting stuck on incorrect physical models. The framework demonstrates significant reductions in experimental wall-clock time and improved robustness in model selection.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The study uses a high-fidelity digital twin of a Triple-Axis Spectrometer (TAS) to simulate spin-wave spectroscopy on square-lattice magnetic systems, including ferromagnetic and antiferromagnetic Hamiltonians with J1-J1 and anisotropy parameters.

**Key observables.** Momentum-energy coordinates (Q, E), intensity (I), uncertainty (sigma), and AIC-derived evidence ratios for model selection.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Exchange constants (J1, J2), anisotropy (D), motor speeds (v_H, v_E), and counting time (t).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The framework relies on fast Laplace/Levenberg-Marquardt approximations for real-time decision-making and assumes the use of a digital twin for validation.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 illustrates the TAS-AI workflow stages; Figure S1 shows the effect of energy tapering on edge-locking; Figure 9 shows the transition between controller phases.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the TAS-AI framework, details the three-stage hybrid workflow (agnostic discovery, physics-informed refinement, and strategic audit), presents benchmarks on model discrimination and parameter accuracy, discusses the mitigation of algorithmic myopia via LLMs, and concludes with software implementation details.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Autonomous neutron spectroscopy must solve three distinct tasks: detection (where is the signal?), inference (which Hamiltonian governs it?), and refinement (what are the parameters?). No single controller solves all three equally well. We present TAS-AI, a hybrid agnostic-to-physics-informed framework for autonomous triple-axis spin-wave spectroscopy that separates these tasks explicitly. In blind reconstruction benchmarks, model-agnostic methods such as random sampling, coarse grids, and Gaussian-process mappers reach a global error threshold more reliably and with fewer measurements than physics-informed planning, supporting the claim that discovery and inference are distinct tasks requiring distinct controllers. Once signal structure is localized, the physics-informed stage performs in-loop Hamiltonian discrimination and parameter refinement: in a controlled square-lattice test between nearest-neighbor-only and J1-J2 Hamiltonians, TAS-AI reaches a decisive AIC-derived evidence ratio (>100) in fewer than 10 measurements, while motion-aware scheduling cuts wall-clock time by 32% at a fixed measurement budget. We also identify a failure mode of posterior-weighted design, algorithmic myopia, in which the planner over-refines the current leading model while under-sampling low-intensity falsification probes. A constrained falsification channel sharply reduces time spent committed to the wrong model and accelerates correct model selection without modifying the Bayesian inference engine. In controlled two-model ablations, both a deterministic top-two max-disagreement rule and an LLM-based audit committee achieve this gain under identical constraints. We demonstrate the full workflow in silico using a high-fidelity digital twin and provide an open-source Python implementation.

</details>

### [Emergence of dual axion response in condensed matter](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23186v1)

**Authors:** Elina Kokurina, Dmitry Vagin, Eduardo Barredo-Alamilla, Maxim A. Gorlach  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.23186v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `spintronics-quantum-optics interface` **2/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23186_figures/2604.23186_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> FIG. 1. Hierarchy of the models featuring nonreciprocal magneto-electric effect. (a) Multilayered metamaterial com- posed of gyrotropic layers with out-of-plane magnetization features axion [22] or dual axion [31] response depending on the properties of the layers. (b) Our simplified model of the spin lattice with antiferromagnetic exchange coupling between the spins in the neighboring layers. (c) Realistic condensed matter structure Cr2O3 featuring an antiferromag- netic arrangement of Cr spins [32] and promising for observing dual axion response.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23186_figures/2604.23186_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> FIG. 2. Geometry of antiferromagnetic spin lattice and choice of the material boundary in the effective medium description.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23186_figures/2604.23186_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> FIG. 3. Calculated material parameters of Cr2O3 at fre- quencies close to magnon resonance. The parameters are a = 1.359 nm, a⊥ = 0.496 nm, s = 3/2, g = 2.0, ℏKeff/(2π) = 21.9 GHz, ℏJ/(2π) = 58.1 GHz [40] and α = 2.2 · 10−4 [41]. Magnon resonance frequency is 165 GHz. (a) Permeability. (b) Dual axion response. Solid and dashed lines indicate real and imaginary parts, respectively.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23186_figures/2604.23186_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> FIG. 4. Reflection and transmission properties of a finite slab of model antiferromagnetic structure resembling Cr2O3 calculated in the effective medium approximation. The total number of periods N = 2.0 · 107, permittivity ε = 1. (a) Co- polarized reflection. (b) Cross-polarized reflection. (c) Co- polarized transmission. Cross-polarized transmission vanishes identically.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23186_figures/2604.23186_fig5.jpg" alt="Figure 5">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> FIG. 5. Reflection and transmission properties of a finite slab of model antiferromagnetic structure resembling Cr2O3 calculated via effective medium model (solid blue lines) and discrete dipole method (solid red lines). The total number of periods is N = 800, while the remaining parameters are the same as in Fig. 4.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23186_figures/2604.23186_fig6.jpg" alt="Figure 6">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 6.</b> FIG. 6. Probing the type of nonreciprocal magneto-electric effect by the external sources. (a) Geometry of excitation of antiferromagnetic spin lattice. (b,c) Co- and cross-polarized components of magnetic field produced by the planar cur- rent source. The magnetic field is presented in the arbitrary units. H0 denotes the magnetic field of a planar current in vacuum. Green and blue dashed lines correspond to the effec- tive medium calculation for axion and dual axion responses, respectively. Parameters of the simulation match those in Fig. 4.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23186_figures/2604.23186_fig7.jpg" alt="Figure 7">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 7.</b> FIG. 7. Cross-polarized component of magnetic field created by the current exciting our model antiferromagnetic structure consisting of N = 800 periods. Solid red line shows the re- sult of the discrete dipole model. Predictions of the effective medium model for the materials with axion or dual axion re- sponse are shown by the blue dashed and green solid lines, respectively. Parameters of the simulation match those in Fig. 5, magnetic field is shown in the arbitrary units having the same scale in all three cases.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** Investigating whether the recently predicted dual axion response, a nonreciprocal magneto-electric effect involving magnetic charge, can emerge in condensed matter systems like antiferromagnets rather than just in engineered metamaterials.

**Main result.** The authors prove that a periodic antiferromagnetic spin lattice exhibits an emergent dual axion response, which can be distinguished from the conventional axion response by analyzing the frequency dependence of polarization rotation from an internal current source.

**Method.** The study uses linear response theory, the semi-classical Landau-Lifshitz-Gilbert (LLG) equation, and the Discrete Dipole Approximation (DDA) for numerical validation, alongside an effective medium approach.

**Summary.** This paper investigates the emergence of a 'dual axion response' in condensed matter systems, specifically antiferromagnetic spin lattices. While previously predicted for metamaterials, the authors demonstrate that realistic materials like Cr2O3 can host this effect. They show that while standard scattering experiments cannot distinguish between conventional and dual axion responses, internal excitation probes can. This work proposes a new way to identify nonreciprocal magneto-electric coupling in solids.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** A periodic antiferromagnetic spin lattice (e.g., Cr2O3 or MnBi2Te4) with Heisenberg-type exchange coupling, magnetic anisotropy, and dipole-dipole interactions.

**Key observables.** Axion coefficients (chi and dual axion chi-tilde), effective permeability, reflection and transmission coefficients (r_xy, t_xy), and polarization rotation (Ey/Ex).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Lattice period-to-wavelength ratio (xi), magnon resonance frequency, exchange constants (J, J_perp), and anisotropy (K).

**Assumptions / limitations.** Effective medium limit (subwavelength regime), linearized spin dynamics, neglect of spatial dispersion, and neglect of transverse exchange coupling.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 compares metamaterials to spin lattices; Figure 2/3 show the lattice geometry and calculated material parameters for Cr2O3; Figure 4/5/6/7 show reflection/transmission properties and the diagnostic comparison between axion and dual axion models using simulated current sources.

**Paper structure.** The paper establishes a hierarchy from metamaterials to spin lattices, derives effective electromagnetic properties from the LLG equation, validates the dual axion emergence via numerical simulations, and provides a diagnostic recipe for experimental identification.

**Why it may be interesting.** The discovery of a dual axion response in natural solids suggests a new topological phase or invariant in condensed matter and provides a method to probe broken symmetries using internal radiation sources.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Recently, it was predicted that nonreciprocal magneto-electric effect in antiferromagnetic multilayered metamaterials occurs in two distinct versions. One is the conventional axion response, while another one is dual axion response captured by electrodynamics with magnetic charge. Here we investigate a model condensed matter system of spins coupled through antiferromagnetic exchange interaction and derive its effective electromagnetic properties. We predict that this system gives rise to the emergent dual axion field, support our conclusion by numerical simulations and put forward candidate materials.

</details>

<a id="paper-2604.23715"></a>
### [Modeling the Zero-Phonon Line of Strained SnV Centers in Diamond; Including Reflections on Computational Cost and Accuracy](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23715v1)

**Authors:** Danny E. P. Vanpoucke  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** quantum information and computing · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.23715v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `spintronics-quantum-optics interface` **2/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23715_figures/2604.23715_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> Figure 1. (a) Ball-and-stick representation of the local envi- ronment of the SnV color center. The Sn atom is shown in teal, and the six bonded C atoms are shown in dark blue. Together, these seven atoms are considered to represent the color center in diamond. (b) Schematic representation of the color center states according to group theory, and (c) their effective positioning as well as relevant states at the top of the valence band in practical DFT calculations.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23715_figures/2604.23715_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Figure 2. Schematic representation of the Franck–Codon ap- proximation of the ZPL of a color center.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** Predicting the Zero-Phonon Line (ZPL) position and pressure coefficient of SnV color centers in diamond while evaluating the trade-off between computational accuracy and cost.

**Main result.** The SnV0 ZPL is predicted to be redshifted by ~43 nm relative to SnV-, and the pressure coefficient is found to be a robust observable at approximately 1.4 nm/GPa across different methods.

**Method.** First-principles Density Functional Theory (DFT) using PBE and HSE06 functionals, comparing the delta-KS and Franck-Condon approximations.

**Summary.** This paper investigates the optical properties of SnV color centers in diamond using first-principles calculations. It compares different DFT functionals and approximations to determine the accuracy of predicting the Zero-Phonon Line and its response to pressure. The study finds that while absolute ZPL positions are sensitive to the chosen method, the relative shift between charge states and the pressure coefficient are robust. These findings are crucial for the development of SnV-based quantum photonic devices.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** Tin-Vacancy (SnV) color centers in diamond, specifically the neutral (SnV0) and negative (SnV-) charge states, modeled using large supercells (512 and 1000 atoms) to simulate dilute limits.

**Key observables.** Zero-Phonon Line (ZPL) position (nm/eV), pressure coefficient (nm/GPa), and Hirshfeld-I atomic charges.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Supercell size (0.195% and 0.100% concentrations), charge states (SnV0, SnV-), and hydrostatic strain levels (+/- 1%, +/- 2%).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The use of the delta-KS approximation assumes vertical excitations, while the Franck-Condon model assumes structural relaxation; absolute ZPL accuracy is limited by the inherent precision of DFT functionals.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the SnV local environment, symmetry-based energy level diagrams, and the positioning of Kohn-Sham states relative to the valence band.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the SnV system, details the DFT methodology and functional comparisons, presents results for ZPL positions and pressure coefficients, and concludes with a discussion on computational efficiency and accuracy.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work is highly relevant for quantum photonics and quantum information, as it provides essential spectroscopic parameters (ZPL and strain response) for SnV centers, which are promising candidates for scalable quantum networks.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Among the group-IV vacancy color centers in diamond, the SnV holds promise for photonics based quantum applications. In this work, the Tin-Vacancy (SnV) zero-phonon line (ZPL) and its pressure coefficient are calculated using first principles approaches. The predicted absolute ZPL position is shown to be strongly influenced by the method and supercell size used. The results are therefore extrapolated to the dilute limit allowing for direct comparison with experiments. The importance of identifying the color-center related Kohn--Sham states is highlighted, as well as the shifting of these states due to electron excitations as well as supercell size and k-point position. In contrast to the absolute ZPL positions, the relative position of the SnV$^0$ ZPL is consistently redshifted about $43$ nm compared to the SnV$^-$ ZPL. In addition, the pressure coefficient is shown to be very robust over different methods, always resulting in a value of about $1.4$ nm/GPa, for both SnV$^0$ and SnV$^-$. Finally, the computational accuracy and cost are put into perspective.

</details>

**Authors:** Baichen Lin, Shanquan Chen, Yubo Zhang, Yangyang Si, Haoliang Huang, Chuanrui Huo, Frans Munnik, Yongqi Dong, Lu You, Jian Shao, Yu-Chieh Ku, Nguyen Nhat Quyen, Aryan Keshri, Zhenlin Luo, Weiwei Zhao, Chun-Fu Chang, Chih-Wei Luo, Sujit Das, Shiqing Deng, Chang-Yang Kuo, Zuhuang Chen

Bottom: Local coordinate system defining the d-orbitals on V atoms. Isosurfaces denote the electron redistribution during the metallic-to-insulating transition, expressed as ∆𝒏= 𝒏𝒊𝒏𝒔𝒖𝒍𝒂𝒕𝒐𝒓−𝒏𝒎𝒆𝒕𝒂𝒍, where red and blue indicate electron depletion and accumulation, respectively. (B, C) Electronic density-of-states for the (B) high- and (C) zero-temperature phases. (D, E) Spectral functions for phases at (D) 350 K...</figcaption>
</figure>

**Main problem.** The metal-insulator transition (MIT) in VO2 is typically coupled to a symmetry-lowering structural phase transition, which limits the switching speed and endurance of Mottronic devices. The study aims to achieve an iso-symmetric MIT where the electronic transition occurs without changing the crystal symmetry.

**Main result.** Nitrogen doping in VO2 thin films suppresses V-V dimerization, enabling an iso-symmetric, tetragonal-to-tetragonal MIT driven by a Mott mechanism. This transition exhibits faster switching kinetics compared to undoped VO2.

**Method.** The researchers used reactive pulsed laser deposition (PLD) for nitrogen doping, combined with structural characterization (XRD, STEM, Raman), chemical analysis (XPS, ERDA), and ultrafast time-resolved optical reflectivity. Theoretical validation was performed using DFT (r2SCAN) and DFT+DMFT.

**Summary.** This paper reports the successful engineering of an iso-symmetric metal-insulator transition in VO2 thin films through nitrogen doping. By substituting nitrogen into oxygen sites, the researchers suppressed the structural dimerization that usually accompanies the transition, maintaining tetragonal symmetry throughout. This approach results in faster electronic switching speeds and provides evidence for a Mott-driven, rather than Peierls-driven, transition. These findings are significant for developing high-performance, high-speed electronic and photonic 'Mottronic' devices.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** Epitaxial VO2 thin films grown on (001) TiO2 substrates, where nitrogen ions (N3-) substitutionally replace oxygen (O2-) sites to induce p-type carrier density.

**Key observables.** Transition temperature (T_MIT), lattice parameters, carrier density, V-V dimerization, transient reflectivity (delta R/R), and orbital occupation (d_parallel and pi* bands).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Nitrogen doping concentration (~1%), transition temperature (~280 K), switching time (~207 ps for N-doped vs ~264 ps for pure VO2), and bandgap (~0.89 eV).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The study assumes the nitrogen is incorporated substitutionally at oxygen sites without significant interstitial presence or oxygen vacancies.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the fabrication strategy, N-doping schematic, and evidence of film quality via XRD and STEM. Figure 2 presents electrical resistivity, RSM, and Raman spectroscopy showing symmetry preservation. Figure 3 compares ultrafast transient reflectivity spectra and decay times.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins with the fabrication strategy and structural verification of N-doped VO2, moves to characterizing the electrical and structural properties of the phase transition, presents ultrafast dynamics of the switching process, and concludes with theoretical modeling (DFT/DMFT) of the orbital-driven Mott mechanism.

**Why it may be interesting.** The paper demonstrates a way to decouple electronic and structural phase transitions using chemical doping, providing a playground for studying pure Mott physics and orbital-driven transitions in a controlled, high-speed manner.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Metal-insulator transitions (MITs) in correlated oxides offer immense potential for next-generation Mottronic devices. However, their integration into practical applications is often hindered by the coupling of MITs with symmetry-lowering structural phase transitions, which limits switching speed and endurance. In this study, we engineered an iso-symmetric MIT on average in epitaxial rutile VO2 thin films via an in-situ nitrogen doping strategy. Nitrogen incorporation effectively suppresses V-V dimerization, enabling an iso-symmetric MIT, while preserving the original crystal symmetry. Furthermore, in-operando time-resolved optical reflectivity measurements revealed a shortened switching time in nitrogen-doped films, highlighting their enhanced performance. Our findings provide critical insights into the underlying mechanisms of MITs and introduce anion doping as a powerful tool for tailoring phase transitions in strongly correlated electron systems. This approach opens new avenues for the development of high-performance electronic and photonic devices.

</details>

**Authors:** Ida Juliane Bundgaard, Catarina G. Ferreira, Yonas Lebsir, Christos Tserkezis

(d) The colour difference, ∆ELab, between the average colour at each incidence angle with respect to the colour at normal incidence.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24411_figures/2604.24411_fig5.jpg" alt="Figure 5">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> Figure 5. Colours obtained from arrays with NPs of radius (a) 70 nm, (b) 100 nm and (c) the combined bipartite array at the same lattice constants varying from a1 = 260 nm to a12 = 480 nm in steps of 20 nm. An illustration of the configuration of particles in the bipartite array can be seen in (d).</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24411_figures/2604.24411_fig6.jpg" alt="Figure 6">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 6.</b> Figure 6. Plots of the CIE colour space, where each white dot represents a colour obtained by one of the arrays investigated. Subfigure (a) show arrays consisting of spherical WS2 NPs, where the radius of the particles vary from 30 nm to 150 nm in steps of 10 nm, while the lattice constant varies from the diameter of the particles to 800 nm in steps of 20 nm. In (b) arrays with intermediate radii in steps of two have been added as well as selected bipartite arrays. In subfigure (c) arrays of the same structure as the arrays in (a) but utilising several different TMDs, here MoS2[57], MoSe2[58], HfS2[59] and HfSe2[58].</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** Investigating the potential of Transition Metal Dichalcogenide (TMD) nanostructures, specifically nanoparticle arrays, as a platform for generating tunable structural colours.

**Main result.** TMD nanosphere arrays can achieve a wide range of colours by tailoring particle radius and lattice separation, with expanded colour space coverage possible through different TMD materials and bipartite array configurations.

**Method.** Semi-analytical calculations using the T-matrix approach, Mie theory, and lattice sums, combined with colorimetric analysis (CIE XYZ/Lab) to evaluate perceived colour.

**Summary.** This paper explores how TMD nanostructures can be engineered to produce a wide variety of structural colours. By adjusting the size and arrangement of TMD nanoparticles, researchers can manipulate Mie resonances and lattice modes to cover a large portion of the visible RGB spectrum. The study also highlights how material-specific features, like excitonic transitions, can be used to further tune the optical response. This work provides a roadmap for designing highly tunable, nanostructured optical surfaces.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** Arrays of spherical TMD nanoparticles (such as WS2, MoS2, and HfSe2) in square and bipartite lattices, as well as thin anisotropic TMD slabs.

**Key observables.** Reflectance spectra, scattering and absorption cross-sections, CIE XYZ tristimulus values, and color difference (Delta E_Lab).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Particle radius (30-150 nm), lattice constant (up to 800 nm), incidence angle, material type, and slab thickness.

**Assumptions / limitations.** Nanoparticles are treated as isotropic for small structures; relative permeability is assumed to be unity; spherical geometries are used as a first approximation for more complex morphologies.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows permittivity and cross-sections for different models; Figure 3 displays reflectance spectra and colours for various radii and lattice constants; Figure 4 shows angular dependence; Figure 5 compares different array configurations; Figure 6 presents CIE colour space plots showing coverage expansion.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the concept of TMD-based structural colours, details the electromagnetic modeling and colorimetric methods, analyzes the impact of particle geometry and material properties (including excitons and anisotropy), explores array configurations for maximizing colour gamut, and concludes with potential extensions.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We introduce transition metal-dichalcogenide (TMD) nanostructures as a promising platform for the realisation of structural colours. Processing of semianalytically calculated reflectance spectra of TMD nanosphere arrays shows a wide range of colours, which are obtained simply through tailoring the radius and separation of spheres in the array, with the size-dependent Mie modes of the nanoparticles being the primary contributor to the spectra. Additionally, it is demonstrated that further coverage of the colour space can be obtained by employing different materials or different lattice unit cells. Theoretical examination of the impact of the excitonic attributes of TMDs on the resulting structural colours indicates that self-hybridisation between nanoparticle modes and excitonic transitions may be employed for further tuneability. Moreover, the impact of TMD anisotropy on the structural colours is shown to be negligible for small structures at typical viewing angles, while the viewing angle itself may impact the colour. This work sets out to be a general investigation of TMD nanoarchitectures, with a focus on nanosphere arrays, for structural colours, by examining both inherent material features through the lens of colourimetry, and the ability of such structures to sustain a broad range of hues.

</details>

### [Ultrafast spectroscopy and role of interlayer coupling in high harmonic generation from layered solids](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23506v1)

**Authors:** Eyal Uzner, Ofer Neufeld  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.23506v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `non-equilibrium dynamics` **2/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23506_figures/2604.23506_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> FIG. 1: (a–b) Illustration of lattice geometry showing top view over xy planes (a), and side view showing AB stacking configuration (b). (c) Hexagonal BZ with high symmetry points for kz = 0 plane. (d,e) TB band structures for graphene/graphite and monolayer/bulk hBN, where bands are indicated by green dashed lines for the monolayers, and black solid lines for bulks. (f–g) Exemplary HHG spectra for (f) graphene/graphite and (g) hBN, calculated for linear driving along Γ-K for various values of interlayer couplings (legends are in eV).</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23506_figures/2604.23506_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> FIG. 2: Orientation dependence of normalized harmonic yields from layered solids for various interlayer coupling strengths. (a–f) Normalized HHG yields from graphite. (g–l) Normalized HHG yields from hBN. Select harmonics are presented that cover the full range of physical behaviors observed (all other harmonics orders are delegated to the SI).</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23506_figures/2604.23506_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> FIG. 3: Ellipticity dependence of normalized harmonic yields from layered solids for varying interlayer coupling strength. (a–f) Select harmonics from graphite. (g–l) Select harmonics from hBN. Other harmonic orders, as well as inter/intra-band separation, is delegated to the SI.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23506_figures/2604.23506_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> FIG. 4: Harmonic yield dependence on the interlayer hopping parameter up to 100 meV in the perturbative regime. Exact numerical results from SBE simulations are given by the empty circle black markers. Black solid lines and blue dash lines are: generic power law fitted function, 4’th-order polynomial fitted function, respectively. Note that the R2 values are given in the legend for each fit, and the sub-text in R2 indicates the number of points over which the fit was made (generally the power-law dependence was fitted over a shorter domain since it cannot capture curvature changes that appear towards larger tz (see discussion in text)). (a–f) HHG in graphite. (g–l) HHG in hBN.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** Investigating how interlayer coupling affects high harmonic generation (HHG) in layered solids, specifically addressing the gap where out-of-plane hopping is often neglected.

**Main result.** The authors show that interlayer coupling can significantly alter HHG angular and ellipticity dependence and develop an analytic theory showing HHG yields follow a 4th-order polynomial in the coupling parameter.

**Method.** Numerical solutions of the Semiconductor Bloch Equations (SBE) in the Houston basis and length gauge, combined with an analytic perturbative expansion of the laser-driven current.

**Summary.** This paper explores how the vertical coupling between layers in materials like graphite and hBN influences high harmonic generation. The authors demonstrate that even when a laser is polarized in-plane, the out-of-plane hopping can change the direction and shape of the emitted harmonics. They provide a new mathematical framework using a 4th-order polynomial to accurately predict these changes. This work suggests that HHG can be used as a powerful spectroscopic tool to measure interlayer interactions and potentially control ultrafast electron dynamics.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** A 4-band tight-binding model for AB-stacked honeycomb lattices, applied to materials including graphite, hexagonal boron nitride (hBN), and WS2, including interlayer hopping along the c-axis.

**Key observables.** HHG emission spectrum, time-dependent electric current, angular dependence of harmonics, and ellipticity dependence of harmonics.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Interlayer coupling strength (tz), laser polarization angle (theta), laser ellipticity (epsilon), and dephasing time (T2).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The interlayer hopping is treated as a perturbation; the model uses a phenomenological dephasing time; the laser is assumed to be off-resonant and femtosecond-scale.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1: Lattice geometry, Brillouin zone, and band structures. Fig 2: HHG yields vs. orientation for graphite and hBN. Fig 3: HHG yields vs. ellipticity. Fig 4: Dependence of HHG yield on the interlayer hopping parameter with comparison of power-law and 4th-order polynomial fits.

**Paper structure.** Introduction of the problem of interlayer coupling, description of the tight-binding model and SBE method, presentation of the analytic perturbative theory, numerical validation via HHG spectra and angular/ellipticity dependence, and discussion of implications for spectroscopy and control.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

High harmonic generation (HHG) in solids has recently emerged as a powerful all-optical approach for probing material properties and ultrafast electron dynamics in quantum systems. It has been widely applied for studying two-dimensional and layered solids of various kinds. In these studies, the laser is usually polarized within the layered planes, where most electron dynamics occurs, while out-of-plane hopping is commonly neglected. This is despite of interlayer hopping being ubiquitous in nano-systems. Here we develop theory for HHG in layered solids in presence of interlayer coupling and employ it for studying strong-field driven hexagonal BN, graphite, and the transition metal dichalcogenide WS$_2$. We show that sufficiently intense couplings can alter typical HHG emission characteristics such as angular or ellipticity dependence even when the driving laser is polarized in-plane. We develop an analytic perturbation theory for the laser-driven current expanded in the interlayer coupling parameter and explicitly show that HHG yields follow a 4'th order polynomial form, which is validated numerically. Our work should motivate experiments for probing interlayer coupling via HHG spectroscopy, as well as exploring its modulation as a control parameter for ultrafast dynamics and attopulse generation via laser driving and mechanical strain.

</details>

### [Campbell penetration depth in a single crystal of heavy fermion superconductor CeCoIn$_5$](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23657v1)

**Authors:** Hyunsoo Kim, Makariy A. Tanatar, Cedomir Petrovic, Ruslan Prozorov  
**Type:** experiment · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.23657v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `Kondo & dissipative impurity` **1/5**

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
