(a) Temperature-dependent in-plane magnetic penetration depth λm(T) in a single crystal CeCoIn5 sample with Hac and Hdc applied along the crystallographic c-axis. Different curves correspond to different values of the applied dc magnetic field indicated in the legend. (b) First temperature-derivative dλm/dT of λm(T) shown in panel (a). The sharp peaks correspond to superconducting phase transitions for each applied field. (c) The superconducting H-T phase diagram. The derivative maximum criteria was compared to bulk measurements determined at a discontinuous jump in dc magnetization measurements by T. Tayama et al. [51].</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23657_figures/2604.23657_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Figure 1(a) shows the magnetic field evolution of λm(T) from µ0Hdc = 0 to 5 T. In the absence of a magnetic field, the single crystal sample exhibits a step- like superconducting phase transition near T = 2.3 K where the London penetration depth, λL, diverges. The temperature dependence of λL in the Meissner state of unconventional superconductors is determined by the symmetry of the superconducting gap. In particular, a power-law behavior ∆λ(T) ∼T n best describes its low- temperature behavior below typically 0.3Tc. For a line- nodal superconductor, the exponent n varies between 1 and 2 depending on the impurity scattering [74] and the nature of the scattering center [75]. The...</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23657_figures/2604.23657_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> FIG. 2. (a) Temperature-variation of Campbell penetration depth λC at various fixed fields in CeCoIn5. (b) Isothermal field-variation of λC at various fixed temperatures.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23657_figures/2604.23657_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> FIG. 4. Temperature-dependent theoretical critical current density Jc(t) with a dc magnetic field µ0H0 = 0.1 T in CeCoIn5 where t is the reduced temperature T/Tc. Jc(T) in LiFeAs and SrPd2Ge2 are shown for comparison, which are taken in µ0H0 = 1 T and 0.02 T, respectively. The magnetic fields for each superconductor are the minimum applied field for the measurement of Jc.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** Investigating the superconducting pairing symmetry and vortex lattice properties of the heavy fermion superconductor CeCoIn5 by measuring the Campbell penetration depth.

**Main result.** The measured Campbell penetration depth deviates from conventional sqrt(H) behavior, signaling a vortex lattice symmetry change, and the critical current density Jc(T) shows a nearly T-linear dependence, providing new evidence for unconventional superconductivity.

**Method.** Frequency-domain tunnel diode resonator (TDR) technique used to measure temperature and magnetic field dependent magnetic penetration depth.

**Summary.** This paper uses the tunnel diode resonator technique to study the Campbell penetration depth in the heavy fermion superconductor CeCoIn5. The researchers found that the magnetic field dependence of the Campbell depth deviates from standard models, which they interpret as a change in the symmetry of the vortex lattice. Additionally, they discovered that the critical current density follows a T-linear dependence, which is highly unusual for conventional superconductors. These findings provide significant new evidence for the unconventional nature of superconductivity in this material.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** Single crystals of the heavy fermion superconductor CeCoIn5 in the clean limit, using a single-vortex pinning model with a quadratic pinning potential.

**Key observables.** Magnetic penetration depth (lambda_m), Campbell penetration depth (lambda_C), London penetration depth (lambda_L), and critical current density (Jc).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Temperature range 0.15 K to 2.5 K, magnetic fields up to 5 T, coherence length xi(0) = 24 nm, and London penetration depth lambda_L(0) approx 260 nm.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The pinning potential radius rp is assumed to be the coherence length xi, and the system is assumed to be in the clean limit.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1 shows temperature-dependent penetration depth, its derivative, and the resulting H-T phase diagram; Fig 4 compares the temperature dependence of Jc in CeCoIn5 to other superconductors like LiFeAs and SrPd2Ge2.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the scientific problem of CeCoIn5 symmetry, describes the TDR experimental setup and sample preparation, presents measurements of lambda_m and lambda_C, analyzes the vortex lattice symmetry changes and Jc temperature dependence, and concludes with a comparison to other superconducting systems.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

The temperature and magnetic field dependent magnetic penetration depth, $λ_m(T,H)$, was measured in a single crystal of a heavy fermion superconductor CeCoIn$_5$ using a frequency-domain tunnel diode resonator. In addition to the London penetration depth, which yields the superfluid density, measurements in a finite DC magnetic field provide Campbell penetration depth, $λ_C(T,H)$, which is directly linked to the true (unrelaxed) critical current density, $J_c$. The measured $λ_C(H)$ in CeCoIn$_5$ deviates significantly from the conventional $\sim \sqrt{H}$ behavior, and its slope changes abruptly at the characteristic magnetic field values. Considering that our sample is in the clean limit, we interpret this deviation as a fingerprint of the vortex lattice symmetry change. The temperature dependence $J_c(T)$ of CeCoIn$_5$ calculated from $λ_C(T)$ is nearly $T$-linear over the entire temperature range, also in stark contrast to expectations in a conventional type-II superconductor. Our results provide new evidence for unconventional superconductivity in CeCoIn$_5$ from the never-before-measured Campbell penetration depth.

</details>

### [Magnetic interactions and spin orders in Cr$_8$ and V$_8$ ring-shaped molecular magnets from non-collinear ab initio calculations](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23565v1)

**Authors:** Maria Barbara Maccioni, Elia Stocco, Luca Binci, Andrea Floris, Matteo Cococcioni  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.23565v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
**Topic relevance:** `spintronics-quantum-optics interface` **1/5**

The plots shows the magnetic susceptibility χ weighted by the temperature T as obtained from LDA+U (left) and LDA+U+V (right) by considering progressively only some of the computed interactions. The solid purple line correspond to a system of free spins and therefore the susceptibility recovers the Curie law. The curve ex(1) correspond to an anisotropic Heisenberg hamiltonian with only the 11 nearest neighbor interactions whose parameters are reported in Table II. The curve ex(1-4) includes also the 2nd, 3rd and 4th nearest neighbor interactions reported in Tables III and IV. Finally, ex(1-4)+Bq includes biquadratic and ex(1-4)+Bq+DM also DM...</figcaption>
</figure>

A single spin is being rotated from its reference orientation. Data points are the same shown in Fig. 6 of the main text.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** The study aims to refine the magnetic description of Cr8 and V8 molecular rings by investigating interactions beyond the standard Heisenberg exchange, specifically biquadratic coupling and Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya (DM) interactions.

**Main result.** The researchers found that biquadratic terms are essential for capturing low-energy excitations in V8, and that DM interactions in Cr8 have a geometric origin due to structural curvature, whereas in V8 they have an intrinsic electronic origin.

**Method.** Non-collinear Density Functional Theory (DFT) using LDA+U and LDA+U+V functionals, combined with spin rotation methods and exact diagonalization of an extended spin Hamiltonian.

**Summary.** This paper investigates the magnetic properties of Cr8 and V8 molecular rings using advanced non-collinear DFT calculations. It moves beyond the simple Heisenberg model to include biquadratic and Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya interactions. The study reveals that while Cr8's magnetic anisotropy is largely geometric, V8 possesses intrinsic, complex interactions that significantly alter its ground state. The use of extended Hubbard functionals (LDA+U+V) is shown to be crucial for accurate quantitative agreement with experimental data.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** Octanuclear ring-shaped molecular magnets Cr8 (antiferromagnetic) and V8 (ferromagnetic/antiferromagnetic transition), modeled using an extended spin Hamiltonian including Heisenberg, biquadratic, and DM terms.

**Key observables.** Magnetic susceptibility (chi and chiT), total magnetization, energy spectra, and exchange coupling constants (J1 through J4).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Exchange couplings (J), biquadratic parameters (BQ), DM interaction components (dr, dt, dz), and Hubbard parameters (U, V).

**Assumptions / limitations.** Use of hydrogen saturation to simplify organic ligands and the use of the Local Density Approximation (LDA) for exchange-correlation energy.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the polyhedral structure of the M8 rings; Figure 2 shows the linear chain models; Figure 7 illustrates spin configurations for DM extraction; Figure 13 compares magnetic susceptibility across different Hamiltonian approximations; Figure 14 shows spin-resolved energy spectra.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the molecular systems, describes the non-collinear DFT methodology, analyzes the magnetic interactions and energy profiles of Cr8, investigates the complex biquadratic and DM interactions in V8, and concludes with a comparison of different Hamiltonian models and their agreement with experimental susceptibility.

**Why it may be interesting.** The paper provides deep insights into how structural geometry and electronic correlations (via Hubbard U and V) generate complex, non-collinear magnetic interactions, which is highly relevant to understanding many-body dynamics and spin-spin coupling in molecular-scale systems.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We employ density functional theory within a non-collinear framework to investigate the magnetic properties of the octanuclear molecular rings Cr$_8$ and V$_8$. Our aim is to generalize the evaluation of the effective magnetic interactions by explicitly including non-collinear spin configurations, thereby refining our understanding of their dependence upon the underlying electronic structure and molecular geometry. By analyzing the energetics of a variety of magnetic configurations, particularly non-collinear arrangements with neighboring spins oriented along different directions, we move beyond the exchange-only Heisenberg Hamiltonian describing the low-energy sector of the excitation spectrum. This approach enables us to distinguish between in-plane and out-of-plane exchange interactions, and to incorporate biquadratic coupling terms into the effective spin Hamiltonian. We reveal significant antisymmetric exchange interactions of the Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya (DM) type whose dependence on the curvature of the annular structure is clarified by a comparison with the results obtained from linear chains of equal composition. Our work demonstrates that interactions beyond conventional exchange, particularly biquadratic anisotropic terms, in the spin Hamiltonian are essential for accurately capturing the low-energy excitations of these systems. The closest quantitative agreement with experimental results (particularly for the case of Cr$_8$) is achieved when extended Hubbard functionals are used for the evaluation of the effective magnetic couplings.

</details>

<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 6.</b> Figure 6: Evolution of magnetic texture in nanomagnets as a function of applied field for Case I (S1), Case II (S2), and Case III (S3). The top, middle, and bottom rows correspond to the simulated counterparts of S1, S2, and S3, respectively. The images show the field-driven reversal sequence from the negative reversal state toward positive saturation, highlighting the distinct intermediate configurations that emerge in each case, including S-domain states, mixed S + C states, and C-state-mediated reversal. The comparison illustrates how changes in geometry and interaction strength modify the reversal pathway and the complexity of the internal magnetic textures.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** Understanding how nanomagnet geometry and lattice pitch govern magnetization reversal pathways and the strength of dipolar interactions in artificial spin ice (ASI).

**Main result.** The study demonstrates that reducing aspect ratio induces complex C-type domain textures (boomerang FORC features), while reducing lattice pitch strengthens dipolar interactions, leading to a broad distribution of local bias fields.

**Method.** First-order reversal curve (FORC) measurements using the Magneto-optical Kerr effect (MOKE) combined with MuMax3 micromagnetic simulations.

**Summary.** This paper investigates how the geometry and spacing of nanomagnets in artificial spin ice control magnetic reversal and interaction strengths. By using FORC measurements and micromagnetic simulations, the authors show that changing the aspect ratio can trigger complex internal magnetization textures, while decreasing the lattice pitch enhances dipolar coupling. These findings provide a way to engineer interaction landscapes, which is crucial for developing new types of magnetic-based neuromorphic computing and reservoir computing architectures.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** Square artificial spin ice (ASI) arrays consisting of lithographically defined ferromagnetic Permalloy (Ni80Fe20) nano-islands on a Ta/AlOx multilayer stack.

**Key observables.** FORC diagrams (contour plots), magnetization traces (minor loops), interaction field (Hu), and coercive field (Hc), and magnetic textures (S-type, C-type, and mixed states).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Nanomagnet length, width, aspect ratio, lattice pitch, and demagnetization energy density.

**Figures summary.** FORC diagrams showing boomerang features for increased width (S2) and vertical elongation for reduced pitch (S3); magnetization reversal sequences through intermediate C and S states; and illustrations of C- and S-state magnetic domain textures.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of mapping interaction landscapes, describes the experimental and simulation setup, analyzes three different geometric configurations (S1, S2, S3) to isolate intra-island and inter-island effects, and concludes with implications for neuromorphic computing.

**Why it may be interesting.** The study explores how local interactions and geometric constraints drive collective behavior and complex switching landscapes, which is conceptually related to the study of glassy dynamics and reconfigurable networks in many-body systems.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

In artificial spin ice (ASI), magnetic interactions between nanomagnets determine both the stable states and the switching pathways under an applied field. Here, first-order reversal curve (FORC) measurements are used to map how these interactions govern magnetization reversal in square arrays as the element shape and spacing are varied. The FORC diagrams show that some geometries reverse more uniformly, whereas others exhibit broader, more asymmetric responses, indicating stronger interaction effects and more complex reversal pathways. Combined FORC analysis and micromagnetic simulations also capture subtle changes in internal magnetization textures during switching, linking local behavior within individual elements to collective behavior across the array. These results establish FORC as a practical tool for mapping and engineering interaction landscapes, with direct relevance to reconfigurable magnetic reservoirs and neuromorphic functionality.

</details>

**Authors:** Ryan A. Baker, William Z. Van Benschoten, James J. Shepherd  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** numerical methods · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.23405v1>

**Main problem.** The high computational cost of using twist-angle averaging (TA) to reduce finite-size errors in the electronic structure calculations of bilayer materials.

**Main result.** The development of 'paired sfTA' and 'binding sfTA' variants significantly improves the accuracy of interlayer binding correlation energies, with 'binding sfTA' achieving results approaching the true twist-averaged limit.

**Method.** The authors extend the Structure Factor Twist Averaging (sfTA) method by using low-level MP2 calculations to select a single 'special twist angle' for high-level CCSD calculations, specifically tailoring the selection process to include the binding transition structure factor.

**Summary.** This paper introduces new variants of the Structure Factor Twist Averaging (sfTA) method designed to efficiently calculate the electronic structure of bilayer materials. By using a 'binding' approach to select a single twist angle for both layers, the authors significantly reduce the error in interlayer binding energy calculations. The new method maintains the massive computational savings of the original sfTA while approaching the accuracy of much more expensive twist-averaging protocols. This is particularly important for studying the correlation energies of 2D materials like graphene and transition metal dichalcogenides.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The study focuses on low-dimensional bilayer systems, including various 2D materials such as graphene, BN, SiC, MoS2, and MoSe2, primarily in AB stacking configurations.

**Key observables.** Interlayer binding correlation energy, monolayer and bilayer correlation energies, and the transition structure factor S(G).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Twist angle/offset, k-mesh density, and the mean absolute error (MAE) relative to the twist-averaged (TA) reference.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The method assumes that the transition structure factor at the selected special twist angle accurately approximates the twist-averaged value, and that MP2 can serve as a reliable intermediate for CCSD calculations.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the construction of the binding transition structure factor; Figure 2 uses box plots to compare MAE across sfTA variants; Figure 5 compares binding correlation energies for various systems; Figure 6 provides contour plots of energy landscapes in graphene to demonstrate error cancellation.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of finite-size errors in bilayers, proposes two new variants of the sfTA method (paired and binding), evaluates their accuracy against TA benchmarks across multiple material systems, and provides a physical explanation for the improved accuracy via error cancellation in energy landscapes.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Structure factor twist averaging (sfTA) is a newer method that has been shown to reproduce twist-averaged (TA) CCSD energies for bulk systems at a low computational cost. In this work, we extend this method for the treatment of low-dimensional materials in the form of two variants: paired sfTA and binding sfTA. These variants affect which twist angles are used in the sfTA protocol, as well as how the special twist angle is selected, namely by using the binding structure factor. These changes are meant to incorporate the binding interaction into the twist-angle selection algorithm within sfTA. Both variants are tested on a variety of bilayer systems, and the resulting binding correlation energies are compared to original sfTA results. We show that the variants are able to produce results approaching TA, with binding sfTA producing the most accurate energies. We also use contour plots of the test systems to show that these improvements are most likely caused by a cancellation of errors.

</details>

### [A step-by-step workflow to extract the genuine circular dichroism of thin films](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.24653v1)

**Authors:** Franziska Schölzel, Arina Narudin, Aleksandra Ciesielska, Alexander Ehm, Dietrich R. T. Zahn, Wouter van Gompel, Simon Kahmann, Georgeta Salvan  
**Type:** experiment · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.24653v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>

</figure>

<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 6.</b> FIG. 6. 1D chiral perovskites thin films. (a) Crystal structures of (R)- and (S)-MBAPbBr3, featuring the</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** The difficulty of distinguishing genuine circular dichroism (CD) from artifacts caused by linear optical anisotropy (LD/LB) and instrumental imperfections in chiral thin films.

**Main result.** The authors present a reliable two-step workflow involving azimuthal sample rotation and sample flipping to isolate the orientation-invariant CD response from anisotropy-induced artifacts.

**Method.** A combination of azimuthal rotation (to remove orientation-dependent artifacts) and sample flipping (to remove propagation-dependent artifacts), supported by a custom-built rotation stage and a Python analysis script.

**Summary.** This paper provides a practical tutorial for researchers working with chiral thin films. It addresses the common problem where structural anisotropy mimics circular dichroism, leading to false signals. By using a specific rotation and flipping protocol, the authors show how to mathematically and experimentally cancel out these artifacts. The method is validated on both metallic and perovskite systems, providing a standardized way to extract intrinsic chiroptical properties.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The experimental platform uses a commercial CD spectrophotometer equipped with a custom-built automated rotation mount. The study applies this to chiral thin films, including molecules on metallic surfaces and chiral metal halide perovskites.

**Key observables.** Circular Dichroism (CD), ellipticity, rotatory strength, dissymmetry factor (g_CD), linear dichroism (LD), and linear birefringence (LB).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Azimuthal angle (theta), thickness non-uniformity, surface roughness, and the 50kHz signal from the Photoelastic Modulator (PEM).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The sample is assumed to be in a normal transmission geometry; the method may fail in the presence of significant scattering, reflections, or complex interference effects.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the microscopic origin of CD and LB; Figure 2 presents the workflow flowchart; Figure 3 shows the custom rotation hardware; Figure 4 demonstrates artifact identification in gold films using azimuthal dependence; Figure 5 shows CD extraction for Boc-Cysteine/Au films.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins with the physical origin of CD and artifacts, introduces a custom hardware setup and a two-step experimental workflow, validates the method using achiral gold films to identify artifacts, and demonstrates successful application to chiral molecular and perovskite thin films.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Circular Dichroism (CD) spectroscopy has evolved from a purely solution based method towards a powerful tool in the analysis of chiral thin films. Although a straightforward technique, the genuine CD signal is often accompanied by artifacts arising from optical anisotropy and instrumental imperfections. This tutorial presents a two-step workflow that reliably isolates the orientation invariant CD response for anisotropic thin films by combining azimuthal sample rotation with sample flipping. For this purpose, a home-built sample stage was developed, which enables systematic suppression of many anisotropy-induced artifacts in commercial CD spectrophotometers. Both a detailed description of the setup itself as well as the needed python script are provided. The reliability of the workflow is demonstrated on two selected samples from different research fields: chiral molecules attached to metallic surfaces as well as metal halide perovskites incorporating chiral spacer molecules

</details>

**Authors:** Chien-Ting Tung  
**Type:** both · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.23541v1>

**Main problem.** The need for a unified compact model for MOSFETs that smoothly and physically describes carrier transport across the transition from the diffusive (long-channel) to the ballistic (short-channel) regime while maintaining DC and AC symmetry.

**Main result.** The proposed Symmetric Unified Transport (UT) model successfully bridges the drift-diffusion and ballistic regimes, capturing velocity overshoot and capacitance reduction while passing both DC and AC symmetry tests.

**Method.** The model uses Matthiessen's rule to combine drift-diffusion and ballistic velocities and employs a weighted sum approach to connect charge distributions, incorporating quantum capacitance and a high-field scattering length.

**Summary.** This paper proposes a new mathematical framework for modeling MOSFETs that works across all channel lengths, from large-scale transistors to extremely small, ballistic ones. It solves a common problem in semiconductor modeling where models become discontinuous or physically inaccurate at very small scales. By unifying drift-diffusion and ballistic transport through a symmetric approach, the model accurately predicts current and capacitance. The model is validated using experimental data from advanced technologies like FinFETs.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The physical system is MOSFETs (including FinFET, FDSOI, and GAA architectures) with channel lengths ranging from micrometer to sub-20-nm scales.

**Key observables.** Drain-source current (Ids), gate capacitance (Cgg), threshold voltage (Vt), effective mobility, and charge distributions.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Transition from Drift-Diffusion (DD) to Ballistic Transport (BT) regimes; thermal injection velocity (vth); high-field scattering length (lambda_sat); channel lengths from 1 nm to 2 um.

**Assumptions / limitations.** Uses 1D Poisson and charge sheet approximations; assumes the scattering length L can be used in the transmission rate instead of a voltage-dependent critical length; GCA-based charge density is not applicable under fully ballistic conditions.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows model verification; Figure 8 compares model predictions to experimental data for Intel 3 FinFET Id-Vds and Id-Vgs; Figure 9 displays DC and AC symmetry test results.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the scientific problem of transport modeling, presents the three-part unified transport and charge model, details the mathematical formulation for velocity and charge, validates the model against theoretical limits and experimental data from various MOSFET technologies, and concludes with symmetry test results.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

This paper presents a symmetric unified transport (UT) compact model for MOSFETs that bridges drift-diffusion (DD) and ballistic transport (BT) regimes. The proposed model self consistently accounts for both current and charge across the DD-BT transition. Quantum capacitance and carrier transport are incorporated into the charge density formulation. Drain side velocity saturation and the source side thermal velocity limit are unified within a single framework using a physically motivated high field scattering length, enabling accurate modeling from DD square law behavior to the ballistic limit. In addition, a physical channel charge and capacitance model is developed to capture capacitance reduction in the quasi ballistic regime, which is not considered in standard compact models. The model is verified using theoretical analysis and experimental data from MOSFETs with multiple channel lengths, achieving accurate fitting using only physical transport parameters. The formulation is continuous and symmetric, and it passes both DC and AC symmetry tests.

</details>

**Main problem.** The challenge of accelerating materials discovery by moving from isolated deep learning models to an autonomous, agentic framework capable of orchestrating the full discovery pipeline, from screening to experimental validation.

**Main result.** The ElementsClaw framework successfully identified and experimentally synthesized four new superconductors, including Zr3ScRe8 and HfZrRe4, and screened 2.4 million crystals to find 68,000 high-confidence candidates.

**Method.** An agentic framework (ElementsClaw) that fuses Large Atomic Models (LAMs) for numerical computation with Large Language Models (LLMs) for high-level reasoning, literature retrieval, and tool orchestration.

**Summary.** This paper introduces ElementsClaw, an AI agentic framework that combines Large Atomic Models with Large Language Models to automate the materials discovery process. By orchestrating specialized tools for property prediction and structure generation, the system can autonomously navigate vast chemical spaces. The authors demonstrate the framework's power by discovering and experimentally validating several new superconductors. This approach represents a shift from using AI as a simple predictive tool to using it as an autonomous researcher capable of complex decision-making.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The system uses the Elements foundation model, a 1-billion-parameter EquiformerV2-based LAM pretrained on 125 million structures, integrated with specialized modules for property prediction (Elements-T), classification (Elements-C), stability (Elements-E), and generation (Elements-G).

**Key observables.** Superconducting transition temperature (Tc), magnetic susceptibility, electrical resistivity, energy above hull (E_hull), formation energy (E_form), and crystal structure parameters.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Critical temperature (Tc), 125 million pretraining structures, 2.4 million screened crystals, and 28 GPU hours for large-scale screening.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The framework assumes that the learned interatomic potentials and property predictions from the LAM are sufficiently accurate to guide experimental synthesis without exhaustive DFT verification for every candidate.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the ElementsClaw framework overview, the Elements model development, and large-scale discovery results. Figure 2 illustrates the agentic discovery workflow. Figure 3 compares Elements against SOTA methods across 22 tasks. Figure 4 shows Tc prediction performance and clustering. Figure 5 details the superconductor screening results.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the scientific problem of isolated AI models, describes the architecture of the ElementsClaw agentic framework and the Elements foundation model, details the four-stage discovery pipeline (screening, skill creation, pinpointing, and prioritization), presents large-scale screening results and experimental validation of new superconductors, and concludes with performance benchmarks.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

The discovery of novel materials is critical for global energy and quantum technology transitions. While deep learning has fundamentally reshaped this landscape, existing predictive or generative models typically operate in isolation, lacking the autonomous orchestration required to execute the full discovery process. Here we present ElementsClaw, an agentic framework for materials discovery that synergizes Large Atomic Models (LAMs) with Large Language Models (LLMs). In response to varied human requirements, ElementsClaw dynamically orchestrates a suite of LAM tools finetuned from our proposed model Elements for atomic-scale numerical computation, while leveraging LLMs for high-level semantic reasoning. This shift moves AI-driven materials science from isolated processes toward integrated and human interactive discovery. In the demanding domain of superconductors, our agentic system guides the experimental synthesis of four new superconductors, including Zr3ScRe8 with a transition temperature of 6.8 K and HfZrRe4 at 6.7 K. At scale, ElementsClaw screens more than 2.4 million stable crystals within only 28 GPU hours, identifying 68,000 high-confidence superconducting candidates and vastly expanding the known superconducting space. These results demonstrate how our agent accelerates materials discovery with high physical fidelity.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

<a id="paper-2604.23491"></a>
### [Amorphous High Density Plutonium](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23491v1)

**Authors:** J. K. Katz, A. Rollett, R. J. Hemley  
**Type:** experiment · **Category:** disordered systems and neural networks · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.23491v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23491_figures/2604.23491_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> Figure 1: Left (from [18]): The increase of the lattice constant of Ga-stabilized δ-Pu under self</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** Explaining the anomalous, rapid density changes and lattice parameter discrepancies in alpha and delta plutonium phases under self-irradiation at cryogenic and ambient temperatures.

**Main result.** The authors propose that radiation damage induces the formation of a metastable, high-density amorphous (glassy) phase with an intermediate density between the alpha and delta phases.

**Method.** Experimental analysis using X-ray diffraction (XRD), dilatometry (bulk dimension measurements), and comparison with accelerated aging using 238-Pu spikes.

**Summary.** This paper investigates why plutonium undergoes rapid, unexpected swelling or shrinkage at very low temperatures under self-irradiation. The researchers found that the lattice expansion measured by X-ray diffraction does not match the bulk density changes, suggesting the creation of a new phase. They propose that radiation damage creates a high-density amorphous or glassy phase that acts as an intermediate state between the alpha and delta phases of plutonium. This discovery helps explain how the material recovers its original structure upon heating.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The physical system consists of plutonium phases, specifically high-density alpha-Pu and low-density, aluminum-alloyed metastable delta-Pu, subject to self-irradiation from alpha and beta decay.

**Key observables.** Lattice parameter (a), bulk density, linear dilation (length changes), and volumetric shrinkage/swelling rates.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Temperature regimes (4 K vs. room temperature), radiation dose (displacements per atom, dpa), and annealing temperature (~100 K).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The assumption that accelerated aging via 238-Pu enrichment is equivalent to natural aging, and that the recrystallization of the amorphous phase is nucleated by the existing crystalline template.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 compares XRD data and dilatometry for Ga-stabilated delta-Pu, showing that the lattice constant increases more rapidly than the bulk sample dimension.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of anomalous density changes, presents experimental observations of cryogenic and ambient temperature behavior, rejects the phase conversion hypothesis in favor of an amorphous phase model, and discusses the implications for room-temperature dynamics.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Metastable aluminum-alloyed $δ$-plutonium shrinks rapidly and pure $α$-plutonium swells rapidly at 4 K. At ambient temperature alloyed $δ$-plutonium swells about $10^{-3}$ as fast as it shrinks at 4 K, but its bulk density decreases more slowly than would be inferred from the increase in its lattice parameter determined by X-ray diffraction. These results might be explained as the result of ingrowth of the opposite phases, but they have not been found in X-ray diffraction. The cryogenic results may be explained by ingrowth of an amorphous phase with density intermediate between those of $α$ and $δ$. This phase, when formed from alloyed $δ$-plutonium, rapidly but not instantaneously anneals to $δ$-plutonium at temperatures $\gtrapprox 100\,$K; when formed from pure $α$-plutonium it anneals to $α$ at similar temperatures. The room temperature discrepancy between growth of lattice parameter and length of $δ$-plutonium is also explained by ingrowth of a denser amorphous phase that is continuously formed and annealed out.

</details>

<a id="paper-2604.24288"></a>
### [Atomistic Mechanisms of Temperature-Dependent Ion Track Formation in Gallium Nitride under Swift Heavy Ion Irradiation](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.24288v1)

**Authors:** Jiayu Liang, Shaowei He, Wenlong Liao, Tan Shi, Hang Zang, Yonghong Li, Xiaojun Fu, Chuanjian Yao, Chaohui He, Jianan Wei, Huan He  
**Type:** both · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.24288v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24288_figures/2604.24288_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> FIG. 1. The cross-sectional schematics (25 nm thickness) of GaN at 200 nm depth</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24288_figures/2604.24288_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> FIG. 2. The cross and longitudinal diagrams (25 nm thickness) of final ion tracks</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24288_figures/2604.24288_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> FIG. 3. Under 1171 MeV Ta irradiation: (a) Total radial distribution function (RDF)</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24288_figures/2604.24288_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> FIG. 4. (a) Total dislocation density as a function of temperature. (b)(c) The</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** Understanding the atomistic mechanisms of temperature-dependent ion track formation and structural degradation in Gallium Nitride (GaN) under swift heavy ion irradiation.

**Main result.** The study reveals a temperature-driven transition from discontinuous tracks to continuous channels, driven by the decomposition of GaN into Ga clusters and N2 molecules, and identifies that zincblende nanodomains and screw dislocations create pathways for leakage current.

**Method.** A multi-scale approach combining the Two-Temperature Model (TTM) with Molecular Dynamics (MD) simulations.

**Summary.** This paper investigates how high-energy ions damage GaN crystals at different temperatures using advanced simulations. It finds that increasing temperature causes ion tracks to evolve from broken segments into continuous channels filled with nitrogen bubbles. The process involves the chemical decomposition of GaN into gallium and nitrogen gas. These structural changes, including the formation of new crystal phases and dislocations, are critical because they can lead to electrical leakage and device failure in next-generation electronics.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** Wurtzite Gallium Nitride (GaN) subjected to irradiation by 430 MeV Kr and 1171 MeV Ta ions at temperatures ranging from 300 K to 1800 K.

**Key observables.** Ion track morphology (radius and continuity), radial density profiles, atomic displacement radius, phase fractions (wurtzite vs. zincblende), dislocation density, and spatial distribution of N2 and Ga clusters.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Electronic stopping power (Se), initial simulation temperature (300 K - 1800 K), and ion species (Kr and Ta).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The melting-quenching process deviates from thermodynamic equilibrium due to extremely high cooling rates.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows cross-sectional schematics and radial density profiles of the ion track evolution; Figure 2 displays final ion track morphology; Figure 3 illustrates the spatial distribution and radial distribution functions of N2 and Ga; Figure 4 quantifies dislocation density and morphology.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of radiation tolerance in GaN, describes the TTM-MD methodology, presents results on temperature-dependent track morphology and atomic-scale decomposition, analyzes defect generation and phase transformations, and concludes with implications for device failure.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

The radiation tolerance of gallium nitride under extreme conditions is critical for its deployment in next-generation electronic and optoelectronic devices, yet the microscopic mechanisms governing swift heavy ion induced damage at elevated temperatures remain poorly understood. Therefore, this study employs a coupled approach including the two-temperature model and molecular dynamics simulations to resolve the entire processes of ion track generation induced by swift heavy ions irradiation across a wide temperature range. A temperature-driven morphological transition of ion tracks, evolving from discontinuous segments to continuous tracks composed of isolated nanobubbles, and ultimately to fully continuous channels is observed. Under lower electronic stopping loss of 430 MeV Kr irradiation, increasing temperature significantly enhances track visibility, enlarges track radii and promotes nanobubble formation. For higher electronic stopping conditions of 1171 MeV Ta irradiation, continuous ion tracks consisting of discontinuous nanobubbles (~1.5 nm radius) emerge already at 300 K, followed by a thermally activated transition into continuous channels with further radial expansion. At the atomic scale, SHI irradiation induces decomposition of wurtzite GaN into Ga clusters and N2 molecules along the ion trajectory, with Ga-rich regions and recrystallized wurtzite phases accumulating near bubble interfaces, while N2 preferentially segregates within bubble cores. Additionally, zincblende nanodomains nucleate around ion tracks and exhibit strong spatial correlation with radiation-induced dislocation networks, particularly screw dislocations, providing potential pathways for leakage current and increased susceptibility to single-event burnout.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

### [Attention Is Not All You Need for Diffraction](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23811v1)

**Authors:** Elizabeth J. Baggett, Edward G. Friedman, Abhishek Shetty, Derrick Chan-Sew, Vanellsa Acha, Harshita Dwarcherla, Paul Kienzle, William Ratcliff  
**Type:** both · **Category:** disordered systems and neural networks · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.23811v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>

</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23811_figures/2604.23811_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> FIG. 2. Effect of curriculum stages on real RRUFF holdout Top-1 accuracy. RRUFF domain fine-tuning (Phase 2) is the dominant factor in bridging the sim-to-real gap.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23811_figures/2604.23811_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> FIG. 3. Decoder comparison on the RRUFF-473 benchmark.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23811_figures/2604.23811_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> FIG. 4. Post-hoc calibration restores ranking accuracy on RRUFF-325. Results shown for the final Stage-2c checkpoint. Softening the auxiliary logits (T = 5) before adding the ge- ological prior decouples structural evidence from geological overconfidence.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23811_figures/2604.23811_fig5.jpg" alt="Figure 5">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> FIG. 5. Topological structure of Top-1 errors on the RRUFF-325 benchmark. Left: error-distance distribution on the condensed extinction-group subgroup graph. Right: directionality. Calibration and preferred-orientation-aware training shift errors toward shorter graph distances, reduce the strong descendant bias of the non-PO model, and reveal that many residual failures are local lateral hops rather than distant hallucinations.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23811_figures/2604.23811_fig6.jpg" alt="Figure 6">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 6.</b> FIG. 6. The catastrophic paradox. Classical profile-fit qual- ity and neural classification difficulty do not align monotoni- cally; extinction-group reweighting removes about 45% of the recoverable-versus-catastrophic gap.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** Automating the determination of crystal symmetry (extinction groups) from powder X-ray diffraction patterns, which is an ill-posed inverse problem due to overlapping peaks and experimental noise.

**Main result.** A physics-informed transformer architecture, combined with a three-stage training curriculum and post-hoc temperature scaling, significantly improves the accuracy and reliability of symmetry classification on real-world mineral data.

**Method.** The authors use a transformer-based model with a physics-aware positional encoding and a dual-head decoder, trained via a curriculum of synthetic pretraining, realistic fine-tuning with preferred orientation, and Bayesian prior injection.

**Summary.** This paper addresses the difficulty of identifying crystal symmetry from X-ray diffraction patterns using machine learning. By incorporating crystallographic physics—such as reciprocal-space coordinates and symmetry-based decoding—into a transformer architecture, the authors overcome the 'synthetic-to-real' domain gap. They demonstrate that a structured training curriculum and proper model calibration are just as vital as model capacity. The resulting model provides a fast, robust alternative to computationally expensive classical refinement methods.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The physical system is powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) of crystals/minerals. The model is a physics-informed transformer featuring an explicit sin^2(theta) coordinate channel and a split-head decoder for crystallographic rule learning.

**Key observables.** Top-1/Top-k accuracy, Expected Calibration Error (ECE), Negative Log-Likelihood (NLL), and topological error metrics (mean DAG distance, error directionality).

**Important parameters / regimes.** 99 extinction groups (classification targets), 230 space groups (baseline), temperature scaling factor (T=5), and the alpha weight for fusion decoding.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The primary limitation is an information-theoretic ceiling on highly degraded mixtures; the model assumes that symmetry-defining features can be recovered despite noise and texture.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows geological class imbalance in the ICSD; Figure 2 illustrates the impact of the training curriculum on RRUFF accuracy; PCA analysis shows the learned physics embedding aligns with the reciprocal-space ruler.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the symmetry problem, describes the physics-informed transformer architecture, details the three-stage training curriculum, presents performance benchmarks on RRUFF datasets, and concludes with a topological error analysis and efficiency comparison with classical methods.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Determining crystal symmetry from powder X-ray diffraction is a central problem in materials characterization, yet multiple space groups can produce indistinguishable patterns, making automated classification difficult. We show that attention-based architectures, while superior to convolutional networks for this task, are insufficient on their own: reliable symmetry extraction requires encoding crystallographic knowledge into both the network architecture and the training curriculum. We introduce a physics-informed transformer that classifies powder patterns into 99 extinction groups, the most specific symmetry classification accessible from diffraction data alone, using an explicit sin^2(theta) coordinate channel, physics-aware positional encoding, and a structured multi-task decoder that separates geometric rule learning from holistic pattern recognition. A three-stage curriculum of balanced synthetic pretraining, realistic fine-tuning with explicit preferred-orientation modeling, and Bayesian prior injection proves essential for bridging the synthetic-to-real domain gap, while post-hoc temperature scaling rather than additional training is the key remaining ingredient for robust real-data transfer. By mapping predictions onto the directed acyclic graph of maximal translationengleiche subgroups, we show that the calibrated model's errors are not random but physically structured: they remain local on the subgroup hierarchy and flow predominantly toward lower-symmetry descendants, consistent with the physical erasure of systematic-absence cues by real-world noise. These results establish that physics-informed target design, curriculum, and calibrated inference matter as much as model capacity for scientific machine learning on diffraction data.

</details>

### [Bottom-up realization of a type-II organic-TMD heterointerface: Pentacene on monolayer WS2](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.24671v1)

**Authors:** Michele Capra, Christian S. Kern, Mira S. Arndt, Karl J. Schiller, Max Niederreiter, Francesco Presel, Iolanda Di Bernardo, Marco Gruenewald, Torsten Fritz, Stefan Tappertzhofen, Martin Sterrer, Peter Puschnig, Mirko Cinchetti, Giovanni Zamborlini

To enhance pentacene features, the difference between the UPS spectra, before and after 5A deposition, is plotted, red line; b)-c) Symmetrized experimental pentacene POT averaged in the energy range associated only to 5A/WS2 interface and VBM of pristine WS2/Au(111); d)-e) DFT calculated 5A/WS2/Au(111) POT and WS2/Au(111) VBM. All spectrum and k-maps are acquired at ħ𝜔= 26.4 𝑒𝑉, 𝑇= 77𝐾.</figcaption>
</figure>

**Main problem.** The challenge of creating high-quality, electronically homogeneous interfaces between organic semiconductors and transition metal dichalcogenides (TMDs) using bottom-up synthesis to enable efficient charge separation.

**Main result.** The researchers successfully demonstrated the bottom-up growth of a highly ordered pentacene monolayer on WS2, establishing a Type-II (staggered) band alignment critical for optoelectronic applications.

**Method.** The study combines reactive molecular beam epitaxy (MBE) for synthesis with STM-STS, photoemission orbital tomography (POT), and G0W0 electronic structure calculations for characterization.

**Summary.** This paper reports the successful bottom-up synthesis of a pentacene/WS2 heterostructure using molecular beam epitaxy. By using a non-toxic sulfur source (DMDS), the authors achieved atomically flat WS2 layers that allow for a highly ordered organic molecular overlayer. Using advanced spectroscopy and many-body calculations, they confirmed a Type-II band alignment, which is essential for separating charges in future solar cells and spintronic devices. This system serves as a model platform for studying charge transfer and energy-level renormalization in 2D hybrid interfaces.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** A hybrid van der Waals heterostructure consisting of a self-assembled monolayer of pentacene (5A) on a monolayer of WS2, which is grown epitaxially on an Au(111) substrate.

**Key observables.** Band alignment (Type-II), HOMO-LUMO gap, WS2 transport gap, moiré periodicity, and molecular orbital momentum maps.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Temperature regimes of 4K, 77K, and 950K; WS2 lattice constant of ~0.314 nm; moiré periodicity of ~3.43 nm.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The computational model uses a supercell approach that ignores the Au substrate to manage lattice mismatch.

**Figures summary.** Figure 7 shows UPS spectra comparisons for pristine WS2 and pentacene, experimental and DFT-calculated momentum maps for orbital identification, and symmetrized POT data.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of interface quality, describes the MBE synthesis of WS2 using DMDS, details the structural and chemical characterization (XPS, LEED, STM), presents the electronic structure analysis (STS, POT, ARPES), validates results with G0W0 calculations, and concludes with the implications for hybrid heterostructures.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Stacked van der Waals heterostructures based on transition metal dichalcogenides (TMDs) exhibit a rich variety of exotic interfacial phenomena. Substituting one component with an organic semiconductor (OSC) enables the design of hybrid heterostructures with tunable functionalities for optoelectronic, photovoltaic, and spintronic applications. In this work, exploiting scanning tunneling spectroscopy (STS), photoemission orbital tomography (POT) and G0W0 electronic structure calculations, we experimentally and theoretically demonstrate the self-assembly of an ordered single layer of pentacene (5A) above monolayer WS2, exhibiting a type-II (staggered) band alignment in the hybrid 5A/WS2 interface. Central to this result is the synthesis of extended, atomically flat WS2 - an essential prerequisite for a highly ordered and electronically homogeneous OSC/TMD interface - which can only be reliably achieved via bottom-up growth, most notably molecular beam epitaxy (MBE). We realize this by leveraging Au(111) as an atomically clean and conductive sample for epitaxial growth - a necessary requirement for reliable and comparable STS/POT characterizations. The high quality of the synthesized heterostructure, together with its type-II band alignment, establishes pentacene/WS2 as a model system for orbital-resolved studies of charge transfer, energy-level renormalization, and non-equilibrium interfacial processes in hybrid organic-inorganic-2D heterostructures.

</details>

<a id="paper-2604.24098"></a>
### [Characterizing Fill Factor Limitations in Perovskite-Silicon Tandem Solar Cells](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.24098v1)

**Authors:** Yueming Wang, Nan Sun, Chris Dreessen, Gaosheng Huang, Alexander Eberst, Kaining Ding, Thomas Kirchartz  
**Type:** both · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.24098v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>

<figure class="paper-figure-card">

<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> Figure 1. The relative losses of photovoltaic parameters of a selected group of high-efficiency single- junction and tandem solar cells are presented, normalized to their respective values within the framework of the SQ model. As the product of each ratio gives the normalized efficiency according to equation (1), and because multiplicative factors preserve their area on a logarithmic scale, the y-axis is a logarithmic axis in % of losses, whereby the lowest edge of each column represents the normalized efficiency 𝜂real 𝜂SQ ⁄ .</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">

Higher mobilities generally lead to higher shunt resistances. The exception are extremely low mobilities, where the series resistance becomes so high that the whole current-voltage curve becomes purely ohmic. In this case, the increasing series resistance with lower mobilities also drives up the measured parallel resistance. Simulated (c) efficiencies and (d) FFs of perovskite-Si tandem solar cells vs. charge transport layer mobility µTL (from 10-6 to 1 cm2V-1s-1). The bandgap of perovskite is 1.74 eV.</figcaption>

</div>

**Main problem.** Identifying and characterizing the mechanisms responsible for Fill Factor (FF) losses in perovskite-silicon monolithic tandem solar cells, specifically distinguishing between series resistance and the 'photoshunt' effect.

**Main result.** The study identifies 'photoshunt'—an illumination-dependent reduction in parallel resistance due to low charge transport layer mobility—as a critical FF loss mechanism, and shows that operating in a 'bottom-cell limited' regime can mitigate this effect.

**Method.** The authors combine experimental characterization (electroluminescence spectroscopy, EQE, and J-V measurements) with equivalent circuit modeling and analytical derivations to quantify resistive losses.

**Summary.** This paper investigates why perovskite-silicon tandem solar cells fail to reach their theoretical efficiency limits, focusing on the Fill Factor. The researchers identify a phenomenon called 'photoshunt,' where low mobility in the charge transport layers causes an apparent drop in parallel resistance under light. They demonstrate that this loss can be minimized by designing cells that are slightly limited by the bottom silicon cell's current. This work provides a roadmap for improving the efficiency of next-generation tandem photovoltaics.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** A monolithic two-terminal perovskite-silicon tandem solar cell consisting of a perovskite top cell (approx. 1.7 eV) and a silicon heterojunction bottom cell.

**Key observables.** Fill Factor (FF), short-circuit current density (Jsc), open-circuit voltage (Voc), series resistance (Rs), and photoshunt resistance (Rp,photo).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Charge transport layer mobility (mu_TL), bandgap (Eg), current matching conditions, and the radiative limit (Shockley-Queisser limit).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The Shockley-Queisser model assumes radiative recombination is the only recombination mechanism and assumes a standard AM1.5G solar spectrum.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows efficiency losses relative to the SQ limit; Figure 2 shows SQ limits for varying bandgaps; Figure 3 shows EL spectra and reconstructed J-V curves; Figure 4 shows tandem efficiency and FF as functions of top-cell bandgap and photoshunt resistance; Figure 5 shows simulated J-V curves.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by defining the efficiency gap relative to the SQ limit, introduces the experimental characterization via EL, presents the discovery of the photoshunt mechanism through analytical modeling, and concludes with design strategies for high-efficiency tandem cells.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Perovskite-silicon tandem technology has exceeded the single junction theoretical efficiency limit. However, there is still distance to the thermodynamic limit mainly caused by the fill factor. This work presents a methodology to illustrate the mechanisms of FF loss in perovskite-Si monolithic tandem solar cells. Apart from the series resistance related loss characterized by electroluminescence, another loss factor is from the photoshunt, a phenomenon in which the parallel resistance apparently reduces under illumination in perovskite solar cells due to the moderate charge transport layer mobility. In addoition, the two-diode property of the Si cell can also influence the FF of tandem devices. The photoshunt can be hidden when the bottom cell is over illuminated, which explains highly efficient tandem solar cells are usually bottom cell limited. This work outlines strategies that overcoming the photoshunt issue can move the perovskite top cell closer to low FF losses in tandem solar cells.

</details>

### [Chirality Transfer to the Centrosymmetric Magnetic Sublattice in the Hybrid Perovskite (R)-/(S)-3-Fluoropyrrolidinium Copper(II) Chloride](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.22952v1)

**Authors:** Zheng Zhang, Mingyu Xu, Jose L. Gonzalez Jimenez, Stephen Zhang, Weiwei Xie, Xianghan Xu, Daniel B. Straus  
**Type:** experiment · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.22952v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>

</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.22952_figures/2604.22952_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Figure 2. (a) UV-Vis absorption and (b) CD spectra of (R)-, (S)-(C4H9FN)2CuCl4 and racemic (C4H9FN)2CuCl4 thin films.</figcaption>
</figure>

<img src="2604.22952_figures/2604.22952_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> Figure 3. Plot of the inverse susceptibility (𝜒−𝜒0)−1 versus temperature for powder samples of (R)-, (S)-, and racemic (C4H9FN)2CuCl4. Curie-Weiss fits are shown as solid lines with extrapolations of the fits as dashed lines.</figcaption>
</figure>

<img src="2604.22952_figures/2604.22952_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> Figure 4. Plots of the magnetic susceptibility and magnetic specific heat capacity data for (a), (c), (e) chiral (C4H9FN)2CuCl4 and (b), (d), (f) racemic (C4H9FN)2CuCl4 single crystals.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.22952_figures/2604.22952_fig5.jpg" alt="Figure 5">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> Figure 5. Plots of the magnetic moment vs. magnetic field H (at T=1.8 K) for (a) chiral and (b) racemic (C4H9FN)2CuCl4 single crystals. The blue circle in panel a is likely a spin-flop transition.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.22952_figures/2604.22952_fig6.jpg" alt="Figure 6">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 6.</b> Figure 6. In-phase (χ’) component of AC susceptibility for (a) (R)-(C4H9FN)2CuCl4 and (b) racemic (C4H9FN)2CuCl4. Out-of-phase (χ’’) component of AC susceptibility component for (c) (R)-(C4H9FN)2CuCl4 and (d) racemic (C4H9FN)2CuCl4.</figcaption>
</figure>

<img src="2604.22952_figures/2604.22952_fig7.jpg" alt="Figure 7">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 7.</b> Figure 7. Magnetoelectric data collected at 2 K on (a) chiral and (b) racemic (C4H9FN)2CuCl4 crystals. The magnetic field is applied along the in-plane (110) direction and the out-of-plane magnetoelectric current is collected. 3. Conclusion</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** Investigating the mechanism of chirality transfer from chiral organic cations to a structurally centrosymmetric (achiral) inorganic magnetic sublattice in a hybrid perovskite.

**Main result.** The chiral organic cations induce chiral magnetic order in the achiral Cu-Cl sublattice, evidenced by a second-order magnetoelectric effect present only in the enantiopure forms.

**Method.** Single-crystal X-ray diffraction, Circular Dichroism (CD) spectroscopy, magnetic susceptibility, specific heat capacity, and magnetoelectric current measurements.

**Summary.** This paper demonstrates that chirality can be transferred from organic molecules to an inorganic magnetic lattice in a hybrid perovskite. While the copper-chloride sublattice is structurally centrosymmetric, the presence of chiral cations induces chiral magnetic order. This is experimentally verified through the observation of a second-order magnetoelectric effect in the enantiopure crystals, which is absent in the racemic mixture. This finding provides a method for designing new materials with tailored chiral magnetic and optical properties.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** A 2D magnetic S=1/2 metal halide perovskite, (R)- and (S)-3-fluoropyrrolidinium copper(II) chloride, consisting of Cu-Cl inorganic layers separated by chiral organic cations.

**Key observables.** Néel temperature (TN), magnetoelectric signal, circular dichroism (CD) spectra, magnetic susceptibility (DC and AC), and specific heat capacity.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Néel temperature TN = 2.23 K, spin S=1/2, space groups P212121 (chiral) and P21/c (racemic).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The study assumes the observed magnetoelectric effect is a direct consequence of the induced toroidal moment from structural chirality.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the crystal structures of the (R), (S), and racemic variants; Figure 6 displays AC susceptibility components; Figure 7 shows the magnetoelectric signal in chiral vs. racemic samples.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the chirality transfer problem, describes the synthesis and structural characterization of the hybrid perovskite, presents magnetic and optical measurements to demonstrate chirality transfer, and concludes with the observation of the magnetoelectric effect.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Incorporating chiral organic cations into organic-inorganic hybrid materials has been shown to enable the inorganic sublattice to display chiroptical properties. We report a new two-dimensional magnetic (S=1/2) chiral metal halide material, (R)- and (S)-$(C_4H_9FN)_2CuCl_4$ (where $(C_4H_9FN)^+$ is 3-fluoropyrrolidinium), which consists of Cu-Cl inorganic layers separated by $(C_4H_9FN)^+$ organic cations. The presence of the chiral $(C_4H_9FN)^+$ organic cation induces formation of chiral magnetic order, even though the inorganic sublattice itself is structurally centrosymmetric. We also report the racemic variant, containing an equal amount of (R)- and (S)- cations, which shows no evidence of chiral magnetic order. When the magnetic susceptibility is measured perpendicular to inorganic Cu-Cl layer propagation direction, an antiferromagnetic phase transition at Néel temperature $T_N = 2.23~K$ is observed in both the chiral and racemic materials, and the existence of the magnetic phase transition is supported by specific heat capacity measurements. Field-induced magnetic chirality is observed through the existence of a second-order magnetoelectric effect in the chiral variant, while no magnetoelectric signal is observed for the racemic material, indicating the absence of magnetic chirality. Our findings demonstrate that materials exhibiting chiral magnetic order can be created through the incorporation of a chiral cation into an organic-inorganic hybrid magnetic material, potentially allowing for the design of tailored materials that combine chiral magnetism with other desirable optical and electronic properties that come from structural chirality.

</details>

### [Comparative analysis of nonlinear elastic moduli of polystyrene, polycarbonate and PMMA](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23244v1)

**Authors:** A. V. Belashov, A. A. Zhikhoreva, Y. M. Beltukov, I. V. Semenova  
**Type:** experiment · **Category:** disordered systems and neural networks · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.23244v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

</figure>

</figure>

<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 6.</b> Figure 6: Fitting of the frequency dependencies of nonlinear elastic moduli 𝑙(a), 𝑚(b) and 𝑛(c) under assumption of a single dominating relaxation process.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** The study addresses the lack of experimental data regarding the frequency and temperature dependence of nonlinear viscoelastic properties in glassy amorphous polymers.

**Main result.** Nonlinear elastic moduli exhibit much more profound frequency dependence (1.5-2.5x) than linear moduli, with significant variations occurring below 1 MHz due to relaxation processes. PMMA showed higher absolute values for both linear and nonlinear moduli compared to PC and PS.

**Method.** The researchers used the acousto-elastic effect to measure variations in longitudinal and shear ultrasonic wave velocities under applied static pressure (0-12 MPa) in the frequency range of 0.4-3 MHz.

**Summary.** This paper provides a comparative experimental analysis of the linear and nonlinear elastic properties of three common glassy polymers: PS, PC, and PMMA. Using ultrasonic wave measurements, the authors demonstrate that nonlinear moduli are much more sensitive to frequency changes than linear moduli, particularly below 1 MHz. The study identifies that PMMA's polar groups lead to higher moduli values and highlights how molecular structures influence relaxation processes. The findings help validate nonlinear viscoelastic models for amorphous materials.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The physical system consists of three glassy amorphous polymers: Polystyrene (PS), Polycarbonate (PC), and Poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA). The mechanical behavior is modeled using Murnaghan's model for nonlinear elasticity, which includes five parameters (two Lamé and three Murnaghan moduli) derived from the energy density function.

**Key observables.** Longitudinal and shear ultrasonic wave velocities, wave packet transition times, and phase shifts under static pressure.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Lamé moduli (lambda, mu), Murnaghan moduli (l, m, n), frequency range (0.4-3 MHz), and the relaxation exponent (gamma between 2.4 and 2.9).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The polymers are assumed to have a broad spectrum of relaxation times; the applied pressure range is assumed to be within the elastic regime without irreversible deformation; and the effective elastic moduli are assumed to depend linearly on static pressure.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the experimental setup; Figure 2 is a flowchart of data processing; Figure 3 displays frequency dependence of wave velocities; Figure 4 shows molecular structures of the polymers; Figure 5 shows frequency dependence of moduli.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of nonlinear viscoelasticity, describes the experimental setup and materials, details the mathematical models for calculating moduli, presents the frequency-dependent results and material comparisons, and concludes with a discussion on the molecular origins of the observed nonlinearities.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We present the comparative experimental analysis of frequency dependencies of linear (Lamé) and nonlinear (Murnaghan) elastic moduli of polystyrene, PMMA and polycarbonate. The measurement methodology, based on the acousto-elastic effect, provided data on variations of these moduli in block samples of the polymers in the frequency range of 0.45-3 MHz. In all the three polymers the linear Lamé moduli demonstrated moderate rise with frequency, most pronounced rise was observed in modulus $λ$ of PMMA in about 35%. The frequency dependencies of Murnaghan moduli were considerably nonlinear. At higher frequencies above ~1 MHz no significant variations of the Murnaghan moduli occurred, while at lower frequencies the absolute values of the moduli $l$ and $m$ demonstrated rapid rise, more pronounced for the modulus $l$. At the same time the absolute values of the modulus $n$ decreased and demonstrated a tendency to become positive at lower frequencies. Both linear and nonlinear moduli of PMMA had higher values than those of PC and PS, with the latter two demonstrating close values of both types of moduli. The potential origins of the differences in nonlinear elastic properties of the three polymers are discussed.

</details>

### [Complementary-polarity double-layer LiTaO3 resonators for symmetry-selective SH2 excitation with ultrahigh electromechanical coupling (kt^2 = 25.7%)](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.24309v1)

**Authors:** Hao Yan, Zhen-hui Qin, Zhi-Wen Wang, Shu-Mao Wu, Chen-Bei Hao, Hua-Yang Chen, Sheng-Nan Liang, Ke Chen, Si-Yuan Yu, Yan-Feng Chen  
**Type:** both · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.24309v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>

<img src="2604.24309_figures/2604.24309_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> FIG. 1. (a) Three-dimensional diagram of the double-layer lithium tantalate structure resonator.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24309_figures/2604.24309_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> FIG. 2. (a) Piezoelectric coefficient e34 of the LiTaO3 at different rotation angles for four cuts.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24309_figures/2604.24309_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> FIG. 3. (a) Fabrication process schematic of the device. (b) Device micrograph and structural</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24309_figures/2604.24309_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> FIG. 4. (a) Effect of varying the single-layer film thickness. (b) Simulated and measured</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24309_figures/2604.24309_fig5.jpg" alt="Figure 5">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> FIG. 5. (a) Effect of varying the relative rotation angle between the top and bottom films.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** The need to increase the electromechanical coupling coefficient (kt^2) in LiTaO3 resonators for wider bandwidth applications while simultaneously suppressing unwanted spurious modes and maintaining temperature stability.

**Main result.** The development of a double-layer LiTaO3 resonator with complementary polarization that achieves an ultrahigh electromechanical coupling of 25.7% and suppresses parasitic modes through symmetry-selective excitation.

**Method.** The researchers used rotation-bonding of two 31 deg Y-oriented LiTaO3 films with complementary (+X/-X) polarization, followed by finite-element analysis (FEA) to predict frequency scaling and temperature compensation.

**Summary.** This paper presents a new way to build lithium tantalate (LiTaO3) acoustic resonators by bonding two layers with opposite polarities. This specific arrangement allows for the selective excitation of a desired mode (SH2) while canceling out unwanted interference modes. The result is a record-high electromechanical coupling of 25.7%, which is crucial for creating wideband ultrasonic filters and sensors. The design is also scalable to much higher frequencies and can be made temperature-stable using a compensation layer.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** A double-layer bulk acoustic resonator consisting of two 365 um thick LiTaO3 plates rotation-bonded with an in-plane relative rotation of 121 degrees, featuring aluminum electrodes for longitudinal electric field driving.

**Key observables.** Electromechanical coupling coefficient (kt^2), resonance frequency (fs), quality factors (Qs and Qp), and temperature coefficient of frequency (TCF).

**Important parameters / regimes.** kt^2 = 25.7%, resonance frequency = 5.24 MHz, rotation angle = 121 degrees, and predicted TCF of -25 ppm/deg C with SiO2 compensation.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The frequency scaling assumes the ability to reduce film and electrode thickness while preserving the coupling coefficient; temperature modeling assumes known temperature-dependent material constants.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1 shows the 3D and cross-sectional diagrams of the resonator; Fig 2 displays piezoelectric coefficients at different rotation angles, admittance spectra for single and double-layer resonators, and displacement-field distributions for primary and spurious modes.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of coupling and spurious modes, describes the fabrication and structural design of the bilayer resonator, presents experimental measurements of coupling and frequency, provides FEA simulations for scaling and temperature stability, and concludes with potential applications.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We report a structurally simple double-layer lithium tantalate (LiTaO3) bulk acoustic resonator that enables symmetry-selective excitation of the second-order thickness-shear (SH2) mode with ultrahigh electromechanical coupling. Two 31 deg Y-oriented single-crystal LiTaO3 films are rotation-bonded with complementary polarization (+X/-X) and driven by a longitudinal electric field. Matching between the effective piezoelectric symmetry and the SH2 mode yields an effective electromechanical coupling coefficient of kt^2 = 25.7% at 5.24 MHz. To our knowledge, this is the highest kt^2 reported for a LiTaO3 resonator architecture to date. The measured response is dominated by the target SH2 mode, with only weak parasitic features in the operating band. The structure is also tunable: the resonance frequency and coupling can be adjusted through geometric parameters while maintaining stable modal behavior, indicating good process tolerance. Finite-element analysis further suggests straightforward frequency scaling beyond 5 GHz by reducing the film and electrode thickness while preserving approximately 25% kt^2. In addition, introducing a SiO2 compensation layer is predicted to improve the temperature coefficient of frequency to approximately -25 ppm/deg C. These results establish complementary-polarity double-layer LiTaO3 as a practical platform for high-coupling, spurious-suppressed acoustic resonators and provide a scalable route toward wideband ultrasonic resonators, filters, and related transducers.

</details>

**Authors:** Saikat Roy, Yezaz Ahmed Gadi Man  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** disordered systems and neural networks · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.23642v1>
