(b) The same distribution is plotted for the frictional gel at identical conditions. Isotropy in contact orientations is also maintained at higher values of attraction strengths and vol- ume fractions (see [19]). (c)-(d) show the universality in an- gular modulation of the normal forces irrespective of the fric- tional interactions or lack thereof. (e) depicts the azimuthally symmetric sinusoidal modulation of ftθ(θ, ϕ) (f) Negligible isotropic background value as observed for ftϕ(θ, ϕ).</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23642_figures/2604.23642_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> FIG. 2. (a) Evolution of contact anisotropy, a, and normal force anisotropy parameter, an, is shown as a function of volume fraction for gels with central and non-central inter- actions. In addition, the evolution of the tangential force anisotropy, at, is presented for the frictional gel. Here, βU0 = 50. Similar values are observed for higher attraction strengths. (b) Comparison of dimensionless NSD values as obtained in the simulation with those of the model predic- tion. Here, βU0 = 50. Model comparison is also remarkable for a higher βU0 (not shown).</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23642_figures/2604.23642_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> FIG. 3. </figure>

(b) The gel network for the fric- tional case at the identical conditions. (c)-(d) shows the gel structure in a thin slice for the depletion and frictional gels, respectively.</figcaption>

</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** Existing continuum theories for colloidal gels fail because they assume a stress-free reference state and affine deformation, which do not account for the inherent quenched internal stresses and structural inhomogeneities in amorphous gels.

**Main result.** The authors propose new constitutive relations that relate macroscopic stress to internal state parameters (force and contact anisotropy), successfully capturing the evolution of Normal Stress Difference (NSD) and demonstrating that stress anisotropy originates from the force network rather than the contact network.

**Method.** Large-scale 3D Langevin dynamics simulations of depletion and frictional gels using the LAMMPS code, combined with a mathematical framework involving spherical harmonic expansions of contact and force distributions.

**Summary.** This paper addresses the failure of traditional continuum mechanics to describe the mechanical response of colloidal gels. By accounting for the quenched internal stresses present in amorphous materials, the authors develop new constitutive relations that accurately predict macroscopic stress components and Normal Stress Differences. Through large-scale simulations, they show that stress anisotropy is driven by the force network rather than the contact network. This work provides a more robust framework for understanding the rheology of complex, disordered soft matter.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The study uses two models of colloidal gels: depletion gels (modeled with a Morse potential for short-range attraction) and frictional gels (modeled via the Discrete Element Method with non-hydrodynamic contact friction).

**Key observables.** Shear modulus (Gc), axial and transverse stresses (sigma_zz, sigma_xx, sigma_yy), Normal Stress Difference (N1, N2), contact orientation distribution, and anisotropy parameters (a, an, at).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Volume fraction (phi), attraction strength (beta U0), coordination number (zc), and the distinction between central (depletion) and non-central (frictional) interactions.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The contact network is assumed to be nearly isotropic; hydrodynamic forces are neglected in the quasistatic limit; and pairwise interaction forces are assumed to be statistically independent of branch vectors.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows contact orientation and force angular modulation; Figure 2 compares anisotropy parameter evolution; Figures 4-7 show gel structure snapshots, static structure factors, and probability density functions for bond orientations.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by critiquing traditional continuum theories, introduces the proposed constitutive relations based on stressed reference states, describes the simulation models for depletion and frictional gels, presents results on stress anisotropy and force network modulation, and concludes with the validation of the new relations.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

The theoretical treatment of depletion gels with central interactions often involves expanding the free energy around a stress-free reference state to derive a constitutive relation between global stress and strain. The premise upon which the previous continuum theories are based, i.e., the stress-free reference state and the affine deformation, both of which do not hold in the context of amorphous gel materials. Gels never reach a true global minimum in the potential energy landscape and contain local regions of significant compressive and tensile stress, interspersed with zero-stress regions. Hence, expansion of free energy around a stressed reference state will produce scalar terms in harmonic expansion, the effects of which are qualitatively different from the terms appearing in the expansion around an unstressed reference state. In this study, we demonstrate the limitations of traditional continuum theories and propose simple constitutive relations that better capture the mechanical response of gel materials. The robustness of the proposed relations is established through large-scale numerical simulations of depletion and frictional gels across a vast parameter space.

</details>

### [Cosine bands, flat bands and superconductivity in orthorhombic iron selenide](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.24298v1)

**Authors:** Ian D R Mackinnon, Jose A Alarco  
**Type:** both · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.24298v1>

</figure>

</figure>

<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 8.</b> Figure 7: EDD plots for orthorhombic β–FeSe1-x calculated for a 2c P1 lattice at (a) 1.6 GPa,</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** Investigating how pressure-induced changes in the electronic band structure, specifically the interaction between flat bands and cosine-shaped bands, influence superconductivity in orthorhombic beta-FeSe1-x.

**Main result.** The study finds that the energy convergence of flat bands and cosine bands at approximately 9.0 GPa coincides with the experimental peak in Tc, suggesting that flat bands help enhance and sustain superconductivity up to 23 GPa.

**Method.** Ab initio Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculations using LDA and GGA functionals, combined with a P1 2c superlattice approach and COOP analysis.

**Summary.** This paper explores the relationship between electronic band topology and superconductivity in iron selenide under high pressure. It identifies that the interaction between flat bands (originating from Selenium lone pairs) and cosine-shaped bands is a key driver for the enhancement of the superconducting transition temperature. The researchers demonstrate that the peak in Tc at 9 GPa corresponds to specific band energy convergences. The study provides a microscopic view of how pressure-induced structural changes and charge transfer mechanisms sustain superconductivity up to 23 GPa.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The physical system is orthorhombic beta-FeSe1-x (specifically x=0.01 and x=0.03) under high pressure (up to 23 GPa), focusing on the role of Selenium lone pair electrons and their impact on band topology.

**Key observables.** Superconducting transition temperature (Tc), superconducting gap, electronic band structures (EBS), Fermi surface topology, and electron density difference (EDD).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Pressure regimes from 0 to 23 GPa; temperature regimes from 5 K to 16 K; energy scales in meV.

**Assumptions / limitations.** Use of a P1 superlattice to moderate band intersections; reliance on experimentally determined cell dimensions for DFT input.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows Tc vs pressure and diffraction data; Figure 2 shows crystal structure schematics; Figure 3 displays calculated band structures and FB energy shifts; Figure 4 tracks energy changes; Figure 5 compares calculated and experimental Tc; Figure 6 illustrates band intersections and Fermi surface orientations; Figure 7 shows electron density difference plots.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of pressure-induced superconductivity, describes the structural and computational models (DFT/superlattice), analyzes the evolution of band structures and Fermi surfaces under pressure, discusses the role of Selenium lone pairs in bonding and charge transfer, and concludes by correlating band topology changes with experimental Tc trends.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Electronic band structures (EBSs) for orthorhombic beta FeSe1-x at less than 16 K and up to 23 GPa using experimentally determined cell dimensions are evaluated for cosine-shaped bands near, or crossing, EF. Cosine shaped bands are present in reciprocal directions parallel to the c axis at all pressures. Calculations using a P1 cell derived from Cmma symmetry with a 2c superlattice moderates the effect of intersecting bands to 9.0 GPa. This approach enables determination of a superconducting gap consistent with experimentally determined values. Key influences on charge distribution and transfer in the interplanar region of beta FeSe1-x are lone pair electrons which feature as flat bands (FBs) near EF along GZ in an EBS. FBs also influence the topology of Fermi surfaces as pressure increases and in directions parallel to the c* direction (i.e. offset along ky) within the Brillouin zone. At the Fermi surface along b*, cosine bands split and align favorably for electron-hole pairing with nodal inflection points located at EF. For P greater than 12.0 GPa, FBs interact with folded cosine bands invoking additional band dispersions. These calculations suggest that FBs participate in, and with increased pressure, enhance and sustain the superconducting properties of beta FeSe1-x to 23 GPa.

</details>

### [Cosolvency response in polymer brushes](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23521v1)

**Authors:** Huaisong Yong, Binyu Zhao  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.23521v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>

</figure>

</figure>

</figure>

<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 6.</b> Figure 6. An example of the free energy per unit volume as a function of polymer concentration for the</figcaption>
</figure>

<img src="2604.23521_figures/2604.23521_fig7.jpg" alt="Figure 7">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 7.</b> Figure 7. Definition of the soluble transition and reentry transition. In the figure, μA denotes the cosolvent</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** The paper addresses the lack of an analytic theory to explain the cosolvency effect in polymer brushes, specifically how polymer chains that are poorly soluble in two pure solvents become soluble in certain mixtures.

**Main result.** The authors develop the first analytic theory with closed-form solutions, revealing that preferential adsorption of a cosolvent induces an effective repulsion between different types of solvated monomers, driving a discontinuous soluble transition and subsequent re-collapse.

**Method.** A mean-field free-energy model based on an extension of Flory-Huggins theory is used, employing perturbation expansions and Maxwell construction to determine phase boundaries.

**Summary.** This paper provides the first analytical framework to describe the cosolvency effect in polymer brushes. It demonstrates that the phenomenon is driven by an effective repulsion between monomers solvated by different solvents, triggered by preferential adsorption. The theory predicts a complex phase behavior including a discontinuous swelling transition followed by a re-collapse. Notably, it shows that repulsive interactions between the two solvents in the bulk are not a prerequisite for cosolvency. This work offers a theoretical foundation for designing smart, stimulus-responsive materials.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The system consists of polymer brushes (chains end-anchored to a surface) immersed in a binary mixture of two poor solvents. The model incorporates preferential adsorption, effective monomer-monomer repulsion, and a concentration-dependent chi-function.

**Key observables.** Brush thickness, monomer volume fraction, effective Flory-Huggins parameter (chi-function), and phase transition boundaries (spinodal and binodal).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Preferential-solvation strength (epsilon_1), repulsion coupling (epsilon_2), grafting density (sigma), solvent demixing tendency (chi_s), and chemical potential (mu).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The theory assumes a homogeneous density profile (Alexander-de Gennes approach), an incompressible system, and identical sizes for monomers and solvent molecules.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows a schematic of preferential solvation; Figure 2 plots the square of the external adsorption field vs. monomer density to show the effect of repulsion and grafting density; Figure 3 shows the phase transition conditions; Figure 5 presents the derived phase diagram; Figure 6 illustrates the free energy density and the role of the third virial coefficient.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the cosolvency problem, presents a mean-field free-energy framework, derives analytical solutions for the solvation and phase transitions, explores specific regimes like low grafting density and symmetric solvents, and concludes with a phase diagram and discussion of implications for material design.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We present the first analytic theory with elegant and closed-form analytical solutions to explore the cosolvency effect in polymer brushes, where polymer chains that are poorly soluble in two pure solvents become fully soluble in certain mixtures thereof. This effect is key to designing stimulus-responsive smart materials but has not previously been addressed by analytic theory for polymer brushes. Our theoretical framework reveals that preferential adsorption of cosolvent induces an effective repulsion between monomers solvated by cosolvent and those solvated by solvent. The equilibrium solvation of polymer chains by cosolvent gives rise to a concentration-dependent $χ$-function, which captures the effective interactions within the brush and reproduces the reentrant behavior characteristic of the cosolvency effect. The model predicts a discontinuous soluble transition followed by a re-collapse transition at higher cosolvent concentrations. Analytical treatment within a minimal free-energy model for the case of two symmetric poor solvents shows that the swelling and re-collapse transitions share the same thermodynamic origin. For low-density brushes, we derive an analytical approximation and delineate the phase diagram of parameter space in which discontinuous transitions occur. For cosolvency to take place, the theory specifies a minimum strength for preferential solvation and the associated repulsive coupling. Furthermore, it demonstrates that, contrary to previous models, repulsive interactions between cosolvent and solvent in the bulk are not required. This work lays the groundwork for the rational design of smart stimulus-responsive materials based on the cosolvency effect in polymer brushes, a capability which was not previously established.

</details>

### [DNA melting: intra base-pair dynamics and a vector generalization of the Peyrard-Bishop-Dauxois model](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23771v1)

**Authors:** Nikos Theodorakopoulos  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** statistical mechanics · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.23771v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>

Attached to each base at a distance l = 2R sin(π/8) are two point masses, one smaller, mimicking the carbonyl and exocyclic amino groups, and another approximating endocyclic nitrogen and its sur- rounding unit. The angle between the bond connecting the mass mi to its neighboring base core and the horizontal line is ϕi; at equilibrium, ϕ(0) 2 = ϕ(0) 1 −π = π/8. The dashed lines represent the effective couplings between the complementary bases; they are...</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23771_figures/2604.23771_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">

The onset shows that the curves for the entropy s1 (right y-axis, red, dashed) and the melting fraction (left y-axis, solid, blue) θ coincide when appropriately rescaled. The main plot shows the temperature derivative of the anharmonic part of the en- tropy vs the temperature derivative of the melting fraction (open circles). The linear fit to the data through the origin yields the slope ∆s∗. The absence of scattering in the data points (which originate in different regions of the melting pro- file) confirms the validity of the Ansatz (6) and, hence, that the melting entropy of a base pair is largely independent of chemical composition and/or...</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23771_figures/2604.23771_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> FIG. 3: The eigenfunction ψ0 in the homogeneous case. Note the purely exponential asymptotics which allow the extraction of ξ⊥for GC and AT. Parameters are the same as in Section III.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23771_figures/2604.23771_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> FIG. 4: Extraction of single chain PBD (N=1000) average energies and entropies per site (cf Fig. 1); left panel: pure AT: ds/dT as a function of the temperature derivative of the melting fraction (squares, left y-axis); dϵ/dT as a function of the temperature derivative of the melting fraction (cir- cles, right y-axis); the lines represent least square fits and their slopes provide estimates for melting entropy and en- ergy, respectively; the onset shows the temperature derivative of the melting fraction as a function of temperature. right panel: same for pure GC. The Morse depth parameters used, DAT = 0.1291 eV , DGC = 0.1681, correspond to a salt con- centration 0.2M.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** The original scalar Peyrard-Bishop-Dauxois (PBD) model fails to accurately predict DNA melting entropies, unzipping forces, and DNA hairpin opening rates.

**Main result.** By generalizing the PBD model from a 1D scalar to a 2D vector order parameter, the model achieves much better agreement with experimental values for melting entropy, unzipping force, and hairpin kinetics.

**Method.** The author uses an atomistic toy model to derive a vector-based Hamiltonian and employs the Transfer Integral (TI) operator method to calculate partition functions and thermodynamic quantities.

**Summary.** This paper proposes a new 'vector' version of the PBD model to better describe DNA melting. By treating base-pair separation as a 2D vector rather than a 1D scalar, the model accounts for the internal degrees of freedom within the base plane. This modification successfully corrects major discrepancies in predicted melting entropy and unzipping forces. Most significantly, it reduces the error in DNA hairpin opening rates from 17 orders of magnitude to a much more reasonable factor of 20.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The system is DNA denaturation (melting) modeled as a chain of nonlinear oscillators. The new model treats each base pair as a planar vector with two independent 1D oscillators representing intra base-pair motion.

**Key observables.** Melting entropy, unzipping force, hairpin opening rates, melting fraction, and imino proton exchange equilibrium constants.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Morse potential depth (D) and width (1/alpha), stacking interaction parameters (b, k, rho), temperature (T), and salt concentration.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The model assumes the dynamics are restricted to a planar geometry and that the mass of the base core is much larger than the attachment masses, allowing the core to be treated as fixed.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the atomistic toy model of a single base plane; Figure 2 shows the melting entropy and fraction for a single PBD chain; Figure 4 shows thermodynamic integration results for AT and GC sequences.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the failure of the scalar PBD model, presents an atomistic toy model for a vector generalization, derives the new Hamiltonian, calculates thermodynamic and kinetic observables, and compares results to experimental data.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

The Peyrard-Bishop-Dauxois (PBD) model of DNA denaturation, although successful in the description of melting profiles, fails to predict melting entropies, unzipping forces and dynamical properties, e.g. hairpin dynamics. The paper presents an atomistic "toy model" of the intra base-pair motion which suggests that the thermodynamics may be better described by a planar vector - rather than a scalar - order parameter. This leads to correct estimates of melting entropy, unzipping force, hairpin opening rates, and the equilibrium constant of open/closed base pair states during imino proton exchange.

</details>

<a id="paper-2604.23109"></a>
### [Effective phonon models based on symmetry-adapted multipole basis -- Hidden chiral phonon angular momentum splitting in ferroaxial systems](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23109v1)

**Authors:** Yu Xie, Rikuto Oiwa, Satoru Hayami  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.23109v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23109_figures/2604.23109_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> FIG. 1: (a) Schematic illustration of the force constant for the diagonal isotropic interaction, corresponding to the electric monopole Q(a) 0 at the bond center, and (b) that for the oﬀ-diagonal anisotropic interaction, corre- sponding to the electric quadrupole Q(a) zx at the bond cen- ter.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23109_figures/2604.23109_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> FIG. 2: (a) Schematic illustration of the one-dimensional zigzag chain. (b) Phonon energy dispersion of the model.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23109_figures/2604.23109_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> FIG. 4: (a) Schematic representation of the electric dipole ˆQ(inter) z deﬁned in Eq. (22), and (b) the corre- sponding phonon energy dispersion with the phonon AM distribution. (c) Combined order of ˆQ(inter) z and ˆG(inter) z and (d) the corresponding phonon energy dispersion with phonon AM distribution.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23109_figures/2604.23109_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> FIG. 3: (a) Schematic representation of the electric toroidal dipole ˆG(inter) z deﬁned in Eq. (21), and (b) the corresponding phonon energy dispersion and AM dis- tribution. (c) Sublattice-resolved phonon AM distribu- tion under ferroaxial order described by ˆG(inter) z . (d) Antiferro-type chiral order of ET quadrupoles G± u cor- responding to Fig. 4(a).</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** The microscopic origin of phonon angular momentum (AM) splitting in ferroaxial systems and the mechanism by which ferroaxiality and polarity combine to create global chirality are unclear.

**Main result.** The authors identify that ferroaxial order creates 'hidden' sublattice-resolved chiral phonons that cancel globally, while adding polar order lifts this cancellation to produce observable global chirality.

**Method.** A symmetry-based framework using a Symmetry-Adapted Multipole Basis (SAMB) to decompose the force-constant matrix into bond-centered electric multipoles.

**Summary.** This paper proposes a new theoretical framework to understand how different types of crystal symmetry-breaking (ferroaxial and polar) affect phonon angular momentum. By decomposing force constants into electric multipoles, the authors show that ferroaxial systems possess a 'hidden' chirality that is only revealed as a global property when polar order is present. This provides a unified way to predict and control phonon properties through electronic ordering and external fields.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** A minimal 1D zigzag-chain model with two sublattices (A and B) in space group No. 51 (point group D2h), using a harmonic approximation for the potential energy.

**Key observables.** Phonon energy dispersion, phonon angular momentum (AM) distribution, and sublattice-resolved AM.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Electric and electric toroidal multipoles, isotropic and anisotropic force constants (k1, k2, k'1, k'2), and ferroaxial/polar order parameters.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The study is restricted to the harmonic approximation (second-order expansion) and assumes a bond-based formulation where energy depends on relative displacements.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1 shows schematics of isotropic and anisotropic interactions via multipoles; Fig 2 displays the 1D zigzag chain and its phonon dispersion; Fig 3 illustrates electric toroidal dipoles and the distribution of sublattice-resolved AM.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces a new symmetry-based framework (SAMB), applies it to a 1D zigzag chain model, analyzes the emergence of hidden chirality in ferroaxial states, and demonstrates how polar contributions lead to global chirality.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We propose a symmetry-based framework for constructing effective harmonic phonon models using a symmetry-adapted multipole basis. By decomposing the force-constant matrix into bond-centered electric multipoles, we identify the minimal microscopic ingredients responsible for phonon angular-momentum splitting. Applying this framework to a minimal zigzag-chain model, we show that ferroaxial order gives rise to a hidden sublattice-resolved chiral phonons, while an additional polar contribution leads to finite global chirality. Our results provide a unified symmetry-based description of hidden and emergent phonon phenomena and suggest a route to control phonon properties via electronic orderings and external fields.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

<a id="paper-2604.24651"></a>
### [Electrical tunability of terahertz nonlinearity in graphene](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.24651v1)

**Authors:** Sergey Kovalev, Hassan A. Hafez, Klaas-Jan Tielrooij, Jan-Christoph Deinert, Igor Ilyakov, Nilesh Awari, David Alcaraz, Karuppasamy Soundarapandian, David Saleta, Semyon Germanskiy, Min Chen, Mohammed Bawatna, Bertram Green, Frank H. L. Koppens, Martin Mittendorff, Mischa Bonn, Michael Gensch, Dmitry Turchinovich  
**Type:** both · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.24651v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

</figure>

<img src="2604.24651_figures/2604.24651_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> Figure 4. Gating dependence of THz higher harmonics generation. (a) The THz amplitude spectra of the THz</figcaption>
</figure>

<img src="2604.24651_figures/2604.24651_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> Fig. 5(c). We see a good agreement between the experiment and the model calculations for</figcaption>
</figure>

<img src="2604.24651_figures/2604.24651_fig5.jpg" alt="Figure 5">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> Figure 5: The thermodynamic model calculations. (a &amp; b) the thermodynamic model calculations (solid lines)</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** Achieving efficient, electrical control over the large nonlinear terahertz (THz) response in graphene for applications in high-frequency electronics.

**Main result.** Graphene's THz nonlinearity can be tuned by a factor of ~70 using low gating voltages (a few volts), with optimal gating enhancing third-harmonic generation efficiency by approximately two orders of magnitude.

**Method.** The study uses nonlinear THz spectroscopy, including THz pump-probe and multi-cycle THz time-domain spectroscopy, combined with a thermodynamic model of carrier heating and cooling.

**Summary.** This paper demonstrates that the massive nonlinear optical response of graphene in the terahertz regime can be electrically tuned using very low voltages. By adjusting the Fermi energy through gating, the researchers achieved a 70-fold modulation in THz transmission and a 100-fold increase in third-harmonic generation efficiency. The mechanism is driven by the ultrafast heating and subsequent cooling of the electronic population. This work provides a foundation for developing highly efficient, voltage-controlled graphene-based components for ultra-high-frequency signal processing.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The system is CVD-grown monolayer graphene on a quartz substrate, controlled via a polymer electrolyte gate. The physical model is a single-band thermodynamic approach describing the interplay of THz-induced electronic heating and phonon-mediated cooling.

**Key observables.** THz third-harmonic generation (THG) efficiency, THz saturable absorption, differential transmission (delta T/T0), and carrier temperature (Te).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Fermi energy (E_F) between 50 and 200 meV, peak electric field strengths up to 80 kV/cm, and momentum scattering time (tau) dominated by long-range Coulomb scattering.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The single-band model assumes carrier density conservation and is valid when E_F > k_B Te; the modulation speed is currently limited by the polymer electrolyte gate.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows THz waveforms and the temporal modulation of conductivity/absorbance; Figure 3 illustrates harmonic electric field enhancement; Supplementary figures show conductivity spectra, electronic temperature evolution, and Hall measurement validation.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of THz nonlinearity in graphene, describes the experimental setup and gating mechanism, presents results for both single-cycle and multi-cycle pulses, validates the findings with a thermodynamic model, and concludes with implications for high-frequency device design.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Graphene is conceivably the most nonlinear optoelectronic material. Its nonlinear optical coefficients in the terahertz (THz) frequency range surpass those of other materials by many orders of magnitude. This, in particular, allows one to use graphene for extremely efficient up-conversion of sub-THz electronic input signals into the THz frequency range at room temperature and under ambient conditions, thus paving the way for practical graphene-based ultrahigh-frequency electronic technology. Here, we show that the THz nonlinearity of graphene can be efficiently controlled using electrical gating, with gating voltages as low as a few volts. For example, optimal electrical gating enhances the power conversion efficiency in THz third-harmonic generation in graphene by about two orders of magnitude. This essentially converts graphene from an almost perfectly linear, inert electronic material to a material with the highest possible THz nonlinearity. We demonstrate gating control of THz nonlinearity of graphene for both ultrashort single-cycle and quasi-monochromatic multi-cycle input signals. Our experimental results are in quantitative agreement with a physical model of graphene nonlinearity, describing the time-dependent thermodynamic balance maintained within the electronic population of graphene during interaction with ultrafast electric fields. Our results can serve as a basis for straightforward and accurate design of devices and applications for efficient electronic signal processing in graphene at ultra-high frequencies.

</details>

### [Electronic and optical properties of arsenic monolayers: from planar honeycomb to the puckered phase](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.24585v1)

**Authors:** Niloufar Dadkhah, Walter R. L. Lambrecht  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.24585v1>

After includ- ing quasiparticle corrections, both QSGW and QSG ˆW predict an indirect gap, with the VBM located between Γ and Y and the CBM at Γ. The independent-particle approxima- tion (IPA) spectrum is compared with the corresponding BSE result, where electron–hole interactions and local- field effects are explicitly included. As expected for the puckered structure, the optical response is strongly anisotropic: the x and y polarizations show different onset energies and different distributions of oscillator strength. In both polarizations the BSE spectrum is red- shifted relative to the IPA result, reflecting the binding of electron–hole pairs. The first bright exciton appears near 1.8 eV for x...</figcaption>
</figure>

</figure>

<img src="2604.24585_figures/2604.24585_fig10.jpg" alt="Figure 10">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 10.</b> FIG. 8. Guide to the structural parameters that vary under strain, as shown in Fig. 9. Orange circles indicate (a) the in-plane bond length d1, (b) the out-of-plane bond length d2, (c) the in-plane bond angle θ1, and (d) the out-of-plane bond angle θ2. Upon going from the relaxed puckered structure to the flat limit, d1 changes from 2.509 to 2.521 ˚A, d2 from 2.490 to 2.523 ˚A, θ1 from 94.783◦to 120.057◦, and θ2 from 100.367◦</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** The study investigates the evolution of electronic and optical properties of arsenic monolayers as they undergo a structural transition from a puckered alpha-phase to a flat honeycomb phase under biaxial strain.

**Main result.** The researchers found that quasiparticle corrections reveal an indirect band gap and significant p-d orbital hybridization, and that biaxial strain induces a transition where the lowest exciton shifts from bright to dark.

**Method.** The study uses Density Functional Theory (DFT) with LDA, many-body perturbation theory via the quasi-particle self-consistent GW (QSGW) and QSW methods, and the Bethe-Salpeter Equation (BSE) for optical response.

**Summary.** This paper explores how the electronic and optical properties of arsenic monolayers change when they are flattened by biaxial strain. Using advanced many-body methods, the authors show that while simple models predict a direct gap, more accurate calculations reveal an indirect gap and significant orbital hybridization. They also demonstrate that strain can turn bright excitons into dark ones, providing insight into the tunable optoelectronic properties of 2D materials.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The physical system is a monolayer of arsenic (As), a Group-V element, studied in two structural phases: a puckered alpha-phase (similar to black phosphorus) and a flat honeycomb structure.

**Key observables.** Band gaps (direct and indirect), orbital composition (p and d character), exciton binding energies, optical anisotropy (x vs y polarization), and exciton brightness.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Biaxial strain levels, spin-orbit coupling (SOC), and in-plane/out-of-plane bond lengths and angles.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The study assumes the validity of the LDA and QSGW approximations for electronic structure and the BSE for electron-hole interactions.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the crystal structure and Brillouin zone; Figure 2 compares LDA, QSGW, and QSW band structures; Figure 3 shows SOC effects; Figure 4 shows orbital-resolved bands; Figure 5 compares dielectric functions (IPA vs BSE); Figures 6-7 show k-space distribution of excitons; Figures 10-13 show band structure evolution under strain and Brillouin zone folding.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins with structural characterization, moves to electronic structure calculations (LDA, QSGW, and SOC effects), analyzes orbital hybridization, investigates optical properties and excitonic effects, and concludes with a study of the phase transition under biaxial strain.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Group-V monolayer materials exhibit intriguing electronic and optical properties, influenced by their unique crystal symmetries and structural phases. In this work, we study arsenic monolayers, investigating their electronic and optical properties across different phases, including planar, and puckered forms, using density functional theory (DFT) and quasi-particle self-consistent $GW$ (QS$GW$) methods, with and without vertex contributions (ladder diagrams) and examine the effects of spin-orbit coupling and the orbital composition of the bands. The Bethe-Salpeter equation (BSE) method is used to study the optical response and the band origin of the low lying excitons is determined. The gradual transformation from the puckered $α$-phase to the flat honeycomb structure is studied under biaxial strain and the evolution of the band structure and optical response is described in terms of band inversions of bands of different orbital character.

</details>

### [Electronic Spectroscopy of Atomic Defects in Molybdenum Disulfide under Ambient Conditions](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23391v1)

**Authors:** Joshua R. Evans, Diego A. Garibay, Aiden N. Kuhls, Mehmet Z. Baykara  
**Type:** experiment · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.23391v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

</figure>

<img src="2604.23391_figures/2604.23391_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">

</figure>

**Main problem.** The difficulty of identifying the chemical identity and electronic nature of atomic-scale defects in MoS2 under realistic, ambient conditions without the need for ultra-high vacuum environments.

**Main result.** The researchers successfully categorized and chemically identified various defects (n-type/p-type metal substitutions and oxygen substitutions) using a new C-AFM spectroscopy method, specifically identifying point defects as oxygen substitutions rather than sulfur vacancies.

**Method.** A new 'Discrete I-V spectroscopy' approach using Conductive Atomic Force Microscopy (C-AFM) under ambient conditions, involving high-speed scanning of current maps at discrete bias voltages and polynomial fitting to extract differential conductance.

**Summary.** This paper presents a new method for performing electronic spectroscopy on individual atomic defects in MoS2 under ambient conditions using C-AFM. By using a 'discrete I-V spectroscopy' technique, the authors can identify the chemical nature of defects, such as distinguishing between n-type/p-type metal substitutions and oxygen substitutions. This approach avoids the need for ultra-high vacuum environments, making it highly relevant for studying 2D materials in practical device-operating conditions. The study provides a way to map the electronic density of states of defects with high spatial resolution.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The physical system is molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) thin films, specifically focusing on atomic-scale defects including transition metal substitutions (Re, V, Nb) and oxygen substitutions at sulfur sites.

**Key observables.** Local conductivity, differential conductance (dI/dV-V), current maps, and spatial morphology of electronic perturbations.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Ambient conditions (22-23 C, 20-50% humidity), scan sizes from 10x10 nm2 to 150x150 nm2, and defect densities ranging from 10^11 to 10^12 cm-2.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The study assumes that the electronic signatures (dI/dV-V) can be reliably compared to existing STM and DFT data to achieve chemical identification.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1 shows atomic-resolution current maps of single-atom and larger defects; Fig 2 displays current maps at different bias polarities showing changes in spatial morphology; Fig 3 presents I-V curves and derived differential conductance spectra for identified defects; Fig 4 demonstrates the identification of oxygen substitutions via current attenuation and lack of in-gap states.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of defect characterization, describes the new C-AFM discrete I-V spectroscopy methodology, presents the categorization of substitution defects, details the identification of oxygen-based point defects, and concludes with a comparison of findings to existing literature.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Transition metal dichalcogenides (TMDs) attract significant attention as potential building blocks in next-generation electronic devices. On the other hand, a comprehensive understanding of how various defects affect local electronic properties under realistic operational conditions is yet to be formed. Here, we present results of electronic spectroscopy experiments performed on individual defects in the prototypical TMD molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) under ambient conditions, by way of conductive atomic force microscopy (C-AFM). Data acquired in the form of consecutive, high-resolution current maps at various bias voltages allow the assessment of local conductivity and differential conductance as a function of bias voltage for individual defects, the effects of which range from single atomic sites to several nanometers in lateral size. Characteristic behavior in spectroscopy data allows the categorization of observed defects into distinct groups, and their chemical identification as n-type and p-type transition metal substitutions of molybdenum atoms, as well as oxygen substitutions of sulfur atoms.

</details>

### [Errors that matter: Uncertainty-aware universal machine-learning potentials calibrated on experiments](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.24607v1)

**Authors:** Matthias Kellner, Teitur Hansen, Thomas Bligaard, Karsten Wedel Jacobsen, Michele Ceriotti  
**Type:** both · **Category:** disordered systems and neural networks · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.24607v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>

<img src="2604.24607_figures/2604.24607_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> FIG. 1. Normalized errors vs. uncertainty estimates from cross-validated data. UAFD is 5-fold cross-validated on atom- ization energies, cohesive energies, lattice constants, and bulk moduli. Running root-mean-square normalized error (RM- SNE) (red) is shown in each plot averaged over 30 points in the atomization energies and averaged over 10 points in the rest. Running RMSNE shows that uncertainty estimates are good for atomization energies, lattice constants, and bulk moduli. Cohesive energies have too low uncertainty estimates compared to errors.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24607_figures/2604.24607_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> FIG. 2. PET-UAFD predicted densities against experimental reference densities. In subplot a) we plot reference densi- ties against predicted PET-UAFD densities and indicate the PET-UAFD uncertainties as error bars. The dashed line in- dicates the identity line. In subplot b) we plot PET-UAFD predicted uncertainties σ(ρpred.) against unsigned prediction errors |∆ρ|. The dashed line indicates the unit line, and gray lines from bottom to top are the 0.5, 5, 15, 85, 95, and 99.5 % quantile lines of the log folded normal distribution, indicating that between the lowermost and uppermost line 99 % of the samples should be found.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24607_figures/2604.24607_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> FIG. 3. Radial distribution function of selected liquids at ambient pressures ordered alphabetically. PET-UAFD predicted pair correlation functions are drawn in blue and the PET-UAFD predicted ±1 standard deviation uncertainties are shaded in blue. Data points that are marked with an asterisk (*) are affected by reported or potentially large measurement uncertainties. More details are given in the main text and the appendix section S9.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24607_figures/2604.24607_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> FIG. 4. Demonstration of the origin of invariant points in the pair correlation functions generated by an MLIP ensemble for liquid Ga at 326 K. (a) Principal components of the atom- resolved pair-correlation function ˜g(r, i). The color-coded rectangles correspond to the coloring of atoms in panel b and the average correlation functions in panel c. (b) Representa- tive snapshots extracted from simulations using the weighted potentials w4, wbest, and w2, color-coded according to the first PCA component.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24607_figures/2604.24607_fig5.jpg" alt="Figure 5">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> FIG. 5. Comparing simulated and experimentally obtained RDFs for liquid gallium at 326 K: a) PET-UAFD predicted RDF±1σ compared with experiment; b) PET-UAFD multihead model predicted RDF±1σ compared to experiment; c) RDF±1σ obtained by the full PET-EXP protocol (combining thermodynamic reweighting and PET-UAFD multihead models) compared to experiment.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** Machine-learning interatomic potentials (MLIPs) accurately mimic their DFT training references but fail to account for the inherent errors in the underlying electronic structure approximations relative to experimental reality.

**Main result.** The authors developed PET-UAFD, an ensemble of MLIPs calibrated against experimental data, which provides accurate predictions for liquid densities and structural properties while using the ensemble spread to quantify uncertainty relative to experiment.

**Method.** The framework uses an uncertainty-aware functional distribution approach (UAFD) to train an ensemble of MLIPs on various DFT functionals and employs the PET-EXP protocol (statistical reweighting) for efficient uncertainty quantification.

**Summary.** This paper presents a new way to make machine-learning potentials truly predictive by calibrating them against experimental data rather than just approximate DFT calculations. By using an ensemble of models representing different levels of DFT theory, the authors can estimate how much a prediction might deviate from physical reality. They introduce an efficient protocol (PET-EXP) that allows for this uncertainty quantification at almost no extra computational cost. The method successfully predicts the properties of various liquids and provides a reliable way to assess the trustworthiness of atomistic simulations.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The study utilizes the Point Edge Transformer (PET) architecture to create universal MLIPs trained on an ensemble of DFT functionals (PBE, PBEsol, PBE+U, r2SCAN, r2SCAN-D3) applied to various solids and liquids.

**Key observables.** Atomization energies, cohesive energies, lattice constants, bulk moduli, liquid densities, and radial distribution functions (RDFs).

**Important parameters / regimes.** A fixed local cutoff radius of 4.5 Angstroms; ensemble weights (w0 to w4); temperature and pressure in NPT ensembles.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The error in the MLIP is primarily driven by the variation in the underlying DFT functional space; the predictive distribution can be estimated by sampling specific unit vector points.

**Figures summary.** Plots of normalized errors vs. predicted uncertainties; radial distribution functions (RDFs) for various liquids with shaded uncertainty bands; PCA analysis of atomic environments; and parity plots comparing predictions to experimental values.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of DFT-inherent error, describes the PET-UAFD and PET-EXP frameworks, presents calibration results on static solid properties, demonstrates transferability to liquid structures and densities, and discusses the computational efficiency and physical origin of prediction errors.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Machine-learning models of atomic-scale interactions achieve the accuracy of the quantum mechanical calculations on which they are trained, but at a dramatically lower computational cost. Their predictions can be made trustworthy by uncertainty quantification techniques that estimate the residual error relative to their reference. These errors, however, do not include uncertainty contributions from the approximations inherent in the electronic structure calculations, which are often the main source of discrepancy with empirical observations. We construct an ensemble of ML potentials trained on multiple electronic-structure references and calibrate it against experimental data on cohesive energies, atomization energies, lattice constants and bulk moduli of simple materials and molecules, similar to the uncertainty-aware functional distribution approach. The resulting ensemble of models, which we call PET-UAFD, can be used to simulate matter across a wide range of compositions and thermodynamic conditions. By comparison with experimental measurements of the density and structure of liquids, we demonstrate that, even outside the static properties on which it was calibrated, PET-UAFD enables predictions that are as accurate against experiments as the best available electronic-structure reference and that the spread in the ensemble can be used to assess the reliability of such predictions. We also introduce the PET-EXP protocol that uses shallow ensembles and statistical reweighting techniques to provide accurate estimates of uncertainty relative to experimental measurements at virtually no additional cost over a simulation based on a single conventional ML potential. Ultimately, this approach provides a practical and inexpensive approach to elevate machine-learning potentials from faithful interpolators of approximate theories to genuinely predictive tools anchored in experimental reality.

</details>

### [Evidence of Micron-Scale Ion Damage in (010), (110), and (011) $β-Ga_2O_3$ Epitaxial Layers](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.24190v1)

**Authors:** Carl Peterson, Chinmoy Nath Saha, Yizheng Liu, James S. Speck, Sriram Krishnamoorthy  
**Type:** experiment · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.24190v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 7.</b> FIG. 5. β-Ga2O3 crystal structure models for the (a, e) (001), (b, f) (010), (c, g) (110), and (d, h) (011) planes, showing the large channeling pathways present in (010) plane when compared to the (001) plane. Row (a-d) depicts a crystal structure 1 unit cell thick, whereas row (e-h) depicts a crystal structure 4 unit cells thick into the page. The (110) and (011) planes are rotated from the (010) plane by an angle of (i) 14.34° and (j) 28.35° respectively, with the normal vectors 𝒏̂ =</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** Investigating the origin and extent of anisotropic, micron-scale ion damage in beta-Ga2O3 epitaxial layers caused by sputtering and ICP etching processes.

**Main result.** Ion damage is highly orientation-dependent, causing significant charge depletion (up to 11.5 um deep) and increased on-resistance in (010), (110), and (011) orientations, while the (001) orientation remains largely unaffected due to ion channeling effects.

**Method.** Experimental characterization using J-V and high-voltage C-V measurements on NiOx heterojunction p-n diodes, Schottky barrier diodes, and MOSCAPs subjected to RF sputtering and BCl3-based ICP etching.

**Summary.** This paper demonstrates that common fabrication processes like sputtering and plasma etching cause significant, deep-seated damage in specific crystal orientations of beta-Ga2O3. While the (001) orientation is resistant, the (010), (110), and (011) planes suffer from massive charge depletion and increased resistance. The authors suggest this is due to ions channeling through open paths in the crystal structure. This finding is critical for developing reliable power electronics using beta-Ga2O3.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** Beta-Ga2O3 (ultra-wide bandgap semiconductor) epitaxial layers with various crystallographic orientations: (001), (010), (110), and (011).

**Key observables.** Specific on-resistance (Ron,sp), net donor concentration (ND - NA), charge depletion depth, and Schottky barrier height (Von).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Depletion depth up to 11.5 um, reduction in net donor concentration by 85-91%, and increase in Ron,sp by up to 9.4x.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The damage is attributed to energetic ions traveling through open crystal channels along the [010] direction and creating compensating point defects.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of anisotropic damage, details the fabrication and etching processes, presents electrical characterization results (J-V and C-V) for different orientations, and concludes with a proposed mechanism involving ion channeling and defect creation.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We report on the experimental observation of up to 11.5 $μm$ deep charge depletion in (010), (110), and (011) $β-Ga_2O_3$ epitaxial layers due to ion damage from sputtering and inductively coupled plasma (ICP) etching processes whereas charge depletion in (001) $β-Ga_2O_3$ epitaxial layers was minimal. The orientation-dependent reduction in CV-measured charge density was first observed in $NiO_x$ reactively sputtered heterojunction p-n diodes (HJDs). When compared to reference low-damage Schottky barrier diodes (SBDs), the sputtered HJDs showed a $9.4{\times}$ increase in the specific on resistance $(R_{on,sp})$ and 85% reduction in net donor concentration $(N_D - N_A)$ at zero bias for sputter-damaged HJDs on (010) epitaxial layers whereas HJDs on (001) remained unchanged. Similarly, sputtered SiO2 caused a reduction of $N_D - N_A$ 11.5 $μm$ deep into the (010) material. Next, SBDs were fabricated on $β-Ga_2O_3$ surfaces previously etched via a BCl3 based ICP process and compared to SBDs on un-etched surfaces. The (010) SBDs on etched surfaces exhibited a $7.7{\times}$ increase in $R_{on,sp}$ and a 91% reduction in $N_D - N_A$ at zero bias where the (001) etched diodes exhibited little change. Additionally, (110) and (011) diodes fabricated on ICP damaged surfaces also saw a ~82% reduction in $N_D - N_A$ at zero bias, indicating (110) and (011) are also susceptible to ion damage. Damage in the (010), (110), and (011) diodes is potentially caused by energetic ions that travel into the open channels present along the [010] direction and create compensating point defects which could potentially diffuse further.

</details>

### [From Data-Driven Models to Physical Insight: Vibrational Entropy Governed by Atomic Volume](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23369v1)

**Authors:** Shivam Tripathi, Jatin Kawatra, Varun Malviya, Krishna Mehta  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** disordered systems and neural networks · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.23369v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>

</figure>

<img src="2604.23369_figures/2604.23369_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Figure 2: Top five features ranked by mean absolute SHAP values, demonstrating that atomic</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23369_figures/2604.23369_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> Figure 3: (a) Dependence of vibrational entropy (Svib) on atomic volume, showing fitted linear,</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23369_figures/2604.23369_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> Figure 4: Parity plot comparing predicted and actual vibrational entropy (Svib) for the temperature-</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** Calculating vibrational entropy using first-principles phonon methods is computationally expensive, creating a bottleneck for high-throughput materials discovery.

**Main result.** The authors developed a highly accurate, interpretable log-linear model and a temperature-dependent formulation that predicts vibrational entropy using atomic volume as the primary descriptor.

**Method.** The study combines a feedforward neural network trained on Materials Project and PhononDB descriptors with SHAP analysis and least-squares regression to derive simplified analytical models.

**Summary.** This paper presents a way to bypass expensive phonon calculations by using machine learning and simple analytical models to predict vibrational entropy. By analyzing a large dataset, the authors found that atomic volume is the most important factor governing this property. They developed a new log-linear model and a temperature-dependent framework that captures both low-temperature Debye-like and high-temperature Einstein-like behavior. This approach enables much faster, large-scale screening of materials for thermodynamic stability.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The study analyzes a dataset of 9,866 crystalline materials, utilizing a single-hidden-layer neural network and a log-linear model relating vibrational entropy to atomic volume.

**Key observables.** Vibrational entropy (S_vib)

**Important parameters / regimes.** Atomic volume, temperature (100-500 K), transition temperature (T* = 200 K), and Debye/Einstein temperature regimes.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The study operates within the harmonic approximation and assumes atomic volume serves as a sufficient effective descriptor for mass and bonding strength.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows a parity plot of predicted vs actual S_vib; Figure 2 ranks feature importance via SHAP; Figure 3 compares linear, logarithmic, and log-linear models; Figure 4 shows parity plots for the temperature-dependent model.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the computational bottleneck of phonon calculations, describes the dataset and neural network architecture, uses SHAP analysis to identify atomic volume as the key driver, presents the derivation of a simplified log-linear model, introduces a temperature-dependent formulation, and validates the models via parity plots and error analysis.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Vibrational entropy plays a central role in determining phase stability and temperature dependent behavior in materials, yet its calculation from first-principles phonon methods remains computationally demanding. In this work, we combine data-driven modeling with physically motivated analysis to develop an efficient and interpretable framework for predicting vibrational entropy. Using a dataset derived from PhononDB, a feedforward neural network trained on Materials Project and composition based descriptors achieves high predictive accuracy, while SHAP analysis identifies atomic volume as the dominant factor governing vibrational entropy. Guided by this insight, simplified analytical models are constructed, revealing a logarithmic dependence of vibrational entropy on atomic volume consistent with lattice dynamical considerations. A logarithmic linear model is shown to provide an accurate and physically interpretable description across the full range of materials.   To extend the analysis to finite temperatures, a temperature dependent formulation is introduced that incorporates T3 scaling at low temperatures and logarithmic dependence at higher temperatures, consistent with Debye and Einstein type behavior. This unified model captures both structural and thermal contributions to vibrational entropy with good accuracy. Overall, the proposed framework demonstrates that vibrational entropy can be predicted using simple, physically meaningful relationships, offering a computationally efficient alternative to full phonon calculations and enabling entropy informed materials screening.

</details>

**Authors:** Sourav Sarkar, Kalyan Mandal  
**Type:** experiment · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.24124v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

A solid black horizontal line at ′ = 0 is drawn for a guide to the eye.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24124_figures/2604.24124_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> Fig. 4: Schematic interpretation of the corresponding polarization mechanism for (a) NSS and (b) NHS. The black and red arrows indicate the direction of the applied electric field and the polarization vector developed in the material. The larger arrow for polarization in central cavity (𝑃𝑐⃗⃗⃗ ) than that of the shell region (𝑃𝑠⃗⃗⃗ ) indicates stronger polarization in the cavity than the shell area for NHS.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24124_figures/2604.24124_fig5.jpg" alt="Figure 5">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> Fig. 5: Frequency variation of phase shift angle, , of CaFO (a) NSS, and (b) NHS at different temperatures, T. For clarity,  corresponding to some select T only are shown, as indicated by the colour code. A solid black horizontal line at  = 0 is drawn for a guide to the eye.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** Achieving epsilon-negative (ENG) behavior in single-phase materials without the use of conductive metallic fillers, which are traditionally required in multi-phase composites.

**Main result.** The study demonstrates that nano hollow spherical (NHS) morphology of Calcium Ferrite (CaFe2O4) induces colossal negative permittivity due to a phase inversion of polarization within the hollow cavity, a feature absent in solid nano spheres.

**Method.** The researchers used a template-free solvothermal method for synthesis and characterized the morphology using XRD, TEM, and SAED, while analyzing dielectric properties via frequency and temperature-dependent measurements.

**Summary.** This paper reports a method to achieve colossal negative permittivity in a single-phase material, CaFe2O4, by simply changing its nanostructure from solid to hollow spheres. Unlike traditional metamaterials that require adding metal fillers, the hollow geometry itself induces a phase inversion of polarization. This effect is driven by charge accumulation at the inner cavity boundary, creating a net polarization opposite to the applied electric field. This discovery suggests that morphology engineering can be a powerful tool for designing novel dielectric materials for applications like superlenses and antennas.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The physical system consists of Calcium Ferrite (CaFe2O4) nanostructures in two morphologies: Nano Solid Spheres (NSS) and Nano Hollow Spheres (NHS), studied in the temperature range of 298 K to 573 K and frequency range up to several MHz.

**Key observables.** Relative permittivity (epsilon'), AC conductivity, phase shift angle (Phi), and activation energy (Ea).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Temperature (298 K–570 K), frequency (kHz–MHz range), and morphology (hollow vs. solid).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The mechanism assumes that the polarization within the air-filled cavity partially offsets the polarization in the shell, leading to a net polarization opposite to the applied electric field.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1 shows TEM and SAED for morphology and crystallinity; Fig 2 shows frequency dependence of AC conductivity and Arrhenius plots for activation energy; Fig 3 shows frequency dependence of permittivity for NSS and NHS; Fig 4 provides a schematic of the polarization inversion mechanism; Fig 5 shows the frequency variation of the phase shift angle.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of ENG materials, describes the synthesis and characterization of the two morphologies, presents the comparative dielectric and conductivity measurements, proposes a physical mechanism based on polarization inversion in the hollow cavity, and concludes with the significance of morphology engineering.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Negative permittivity metamaterial is a scientifically rich avenue due to its tremendous application in several arena of materials research including novel superlens, band-gap materials, invisibility cloaks, antenna and filter design. Traditionally, epsilon negative (ENG) behaviour is achieved in multi-phase composites with the addition of conducting metal fillers. However, this study reports colossal ENG feature in a single phase Calcium Ferrite for a particular nano hollow spherical (NHS) morphology, without the use of any filler. On the contrary, the same material synthesized in a different morphology, namely, nano solid sphere (NSS) shows conventional dielectric behaviour. Occurrence of ENG is successfully interpreted with the phase inversion of dominant polarization within the hollow cavity of NHS. This report marks a significant step in realizing colossal ENG in a single phase material just by restructuring the nanoscale morphology.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

### [Improved Electrochemical Performance and Diffusion kinetics by Boron-doping in Na$_{0.66}$Mn$_{0.8}$Fe$_{0.2}$O$_{2}$ Layered Cathodes for Sodium-Ion Batteries](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.24683v1)

**Authors:** Jayashree Pati, P. Senthilkumar, Deepak Seth, Riya Gulati, Manish Kr. Singh, Madhav Sharma, Anita Dhaka, M. Ali Haider, Rajendra S. Dhaka  
**Type:** both · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.24683v1>

<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 9.</b> FIG. 8. (a) The DFT calculated formation energies for B-NMFO system for possible boron occupancy at seven distinct sites for comparison. (b) The Bader charge distribution, (c) the Na-ion trajectory density maps (from green to blue) showing diffusion pathways in the bulk NMFO structure (color scheme: O–red, B–orange, Fe–black, Mn–purple). (d) The mean squared displacement (MSD) versus time plot for the Na-ion diffusion.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** Improving the electrochemical performance, structural stability, and sodium-ion diffusion kinetics of Mn-rich Na0.66Mn0.8Fe0.2O2 (NMFO) layered cathodes for sodium-ion batteries.

**Main result.** Boron doping enhances specific capacity (from 133 to 163 mAh/g) and cycling stability (from 60% to 70% retention) by stabilizing the lattice oxygen via strong B-O bonds and facilitating a reversible P2 to O'P4 phase transition.

**Method.** The study combines experimental techniques (sol-gel synthesis, XRD, Raman, XPS, EIS, GITT) with computational methods (DFT for energetics/electronic properties and classical MD simulations for Na-ion transport).

**Summary.** This paper investigates how Boron doping can optimize Mn-rich layered cathodes for sodium-ion batteries. By introducing Boron, the researchers improved both the capacity and the long-term cycling stability of the material. The enhancement is attributed to stronger B-O bonds that stabilize the oxygen framework and a more efficient sodium-ion diffusion pathway. Computational modeling via DFT and MD simulations confirms that Boron prefers specific interstitial sites that promote better ion transport and structural reversibility.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The physical system is a P2-type hexagonal layered transition metal oxide, specifically Na0.66Mn0.8Fe0.2O2 (NMFO) and its Boron-doped counterpart (B-NMFO), used as a cathode in sodium-ion battery coin cells.

**Key observables.** Specific capacity, capacity retention, Na-ion diffusion coefficient (D_Na), voltage polarization, redox potentials, and B-O vibrational modes.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Diffusion coefficient range (10^-8 to 10^-10 cm^2/s), B-O bond energy (809 kJ/mol), and b-parameter for capacitive vs. diffusive contributions (0.65 to 0.74).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The study assumes the effectiveness of the sol-gel process for stoichiometry and uses a classical pair-potential model for MD simulations.

**Figures summary.** Figures include density maps of Na-ion diffusion pathways, Mean Square Displacement (MSD) plots demonstrating enhanced diffusivity in B-NMFO, and electrochemical/structural characterization data.

**Paper structure.** The paper follows a logic of: problem identification (instability in NMFO) -> synthesis and structural characterization (XRD, Raman, TEM) -> electrochemical performance testing (capacity, cycling, rate capability) -> kinetic analysis (GITT, CV, DRT) -> theoretical investigation (DFT and MD simulations) -> conclusion.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We report the electrochemical investigation and study the diffusion kinetics of boron doped Na$_{0.66}$Mn$_{0.8}$Fe$_{0.2}$O$_{2}$ (B-NMFO) cathode materials for sodium-ion batteries. Notably, the B-NMFO cathode exhibits improved specific capacity of 163 mAh g$^{-1}$ as compared to 133 mAhg$^{-1}$ at 0.1~C for the NMFO cathode. Further, we observe better capacity retention of 70\% for B-NMFO as compared to the NMFO (60\%) at 1 C after 200 cycles, indicating high structural stability due to the presence of strong B-O bonds. The diffusion coefficient evaluation through galvanostatic intermittent titration technique and cyclic voltammetry, which is found to be in the range of 10$^{-8}$--10$^{-10}$ cm$^{2}$s$^{-1}$. Interestingly, the temperature dependent distribution of relaxation time (DRT) analysis provides a clear understanding about the individual physical processes occurring at different time domains during the electro-chemical testing. Moreover, density functional theory is employed to determine the energetics and the electronic properties of B-NMFO, which suggests that the interstitial tetrahedral sites, especially those next to vacancies, are the dominant incorporation path ways for B in the host structure. Additionally, classical molecular dynamics (MD) simulations are applied to gain insights into the Na-ion transport properties in the bulk structures cathode materials.

</details>
