### [Interfacial breathing as a dynamic failure law in all-solid-state batteries: amplitude, phase lag and dual-timescale memory as design principles](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.24075v1)

**Authors:** Changdeuck Bae  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.24075v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24075_figures/2604.24075_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> Figure 1 | Multiscale failure hierarchy in all-solid-state batteries. Bulk transport establishes the initial operating window, but the decisive losses emerge from decomposition-layer growth, interfacial void evolution, grain-boundary transport and chemo-mechanical contact loss. In this review these five levels are treated not as independent mechanisms but as the coupled components of a single breathing system: a chemo-electro-mechanical oscillation of the Li | SE contact whose four signatures {Aφ, Bψ, BR, Sσ} ride on a slow reactive memory Mdec.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24075_figures/2604.24075_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Figure 2 | Reduced-order benchmark demonstrates the weakness of bulk-transport levers. a, Levels 1 (liquid) and 2 (single-ion sulfide-style) discharge curves at 0.5 C are nearly indistinguishable; the delivered-capacity difference is only 1.4 × 10⁻⁴ Ah (~3 ppm). b, Once the interphase terms of Levels 3 and 4 are activated, the initial resistance budget is dominated by the SEI contribution, which enters at ≈ 200× the base ohmic value, and the model collapses immediately into an interface-limited state.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24075_figures/2604.24075_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> Figure 3 | Interfacial resistance dominates over bulk conductivity across realistic parameter ranges. a, Sweeping the ionic conductivity κ from 0.3 to 1.2 S m⁻¹ — a four-fold range that spans most competitive solid electrolytes — shifts the mean discharge voltage by only 2.1 mV. b, Sweeping the cathode-electrolyte interphase resistance RCEI from 0 to 50 mΩ, in contrast, shifts the mean discharge voltage by 126 mV and the delivered energy by 0.725 Wh.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24075_figures/2604.24075_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> Figure 4 | Phase-field breathing calculations: pressure reshapes the reactive interface. a, b, Low-pressure (5 MPa) and high-pressure (200 MPa) snapshots of the void field in a representative sulfide ASSB on a 60 × 40 grid. c, The interfacial void fraction ⟨φ⟩interface follows the SOC-driven breathing cycles, with markedly larger oscillation at low pressure. d, Increasing pressure compresses voids, lowers Rcontact and reduces the SEI / CEI order parameters. e, The mechanical safety window differs strongly by SE family: oxide and halide systems are intrinsically safe at any pressure, whereas sulfides require 200 MPa, LGPS 700 MPa, and polymer electrolytes 3.2 GPa to reach the Monroe–Newman...</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24075_figures/2604.24075_fig5.jpg" alt="Figure 5">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> Figure 6 | Breathing-regime map: three regions of interfacial dynamics. a, The forcing–healing plane with three labelled regions and the four-pressure sweep overlaid as a vertical trajectory. b, Chemistry placement on the same plane: oxides lie in the healing- dominant region, halides in the intermediate interphase-memory region, and sulfide / LGPS / anode-free systems in the void-growth region.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24075_figures/2604.24075_fig6.jpg" alt="Figure 6">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 6.</b> Figure 8 | Ragone crossover and its origin in breathing + memory. a, Ragone plot (specific energy E versus specific power P) for five solid-electrolyte chemistries on log–log axes. Dotted diagonals indicate iso-C-rate loci (0.1 C, 1 C, 10 C); the shaded band highlights the breathing-controlled regime in which rank inversions concentrate. b, The same data replotted as E versus C-rate, so that crossovers appear as explicit intersections. AF-ASSB loses to sulfide argyrodite at ≈ 0.65 C, AF-ASSB loses to oxide at ≈ 1.3 C, and sulfide argyrodite loses to oxide at ≈ 2.1 C. The crossover C-rates are direct measurements of the dominant breathing descriptor in each chemistry.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** The failure of all-solid-state batteries (ASSBs) is traditionally analyzed using static mean quantities, which fails to capture the dynamic, cycle-scale instabilities of the interface.

**Main result.** The paper identifies that battery degradation is driven by a dual-timescale process of 'interfacial breathing' (fast oscillations) and 'reactive memory' (slow accumulation), and demonstrates that stack pressure can suppress breathing but cannot undo chemical memory.

**Method.** A coupled chemo-electro-mechanical phase-field model using Cahn-Hilliard and Allen-Cahn equations, supplemented by a reduced-order electrochemical benchmark.

**Summary.** This paper proposes a new framework for understanding the degradation of all-solid-state batteries by focusing on dynamic interfacial oscillations rather than static resistance. It distinguishes between fast, cycle-scale 'breathing' of the lithium contact and the slow, irreversible 'memory' of chemical decomposition. The study shows that while mechanical pressure can effectively control contact breathing, it cannot mitigate the accumulation of interphase products. This provides a roadmap for designing more stable batteries through both pressure programming and interphase chemistry engineering.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** A stratified 2D model of a Li | Solid Electrolyte interface (including sulfide, oxide, and halide chemistries) featuring evolving void fields, interphase thickness, and hydrostatic stress.

**Key observables.** Void amplitude (A_phi), interphase breathing (B_psi), resistance breathing (B_R), stress signature (S_sigma), and reactive memory (M_dec).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Stack pressure (P_stack), ionic conductivity (kappa), C-rate, and the forcing-healing regime map.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The mechanical description is purely elastic and does not yet include lithium creep or viscoelasticity.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows a hierarchy of multiscale failure; Figure 2 compares electrochemical levels; Figure 3 shows the effect of varying ionic conductivity; Figure 5 demonstrates pressure-induced suppression of breathing signatures but invariance of memory; Figure 6 presents a regime map of battery chemistries.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the dynamic failure problem, presents a phase-field modeling framework, establishes a static baseline via electrochemical benchmarks, analyzes the effects of pressure and conductivity, proposes a regime map for different chemistries, and concludes with experimental design principles.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

All-solid-state batteries fail not only by bulk transport limits, but by a reactive interface that evolves during cycling. We show that degradation is governed by two coupled processes: interfacial breathing, the cycle-scale oscillation of lithium contact, and reactive memory, the slow accumulation of electrolyte decomposition. Four descriptors capture breathing, together with a memory metric based on decomposed interphase thickness. A reduced electrochemical benchmark shows that ionic conductivity has little effect on mean discharge voltage, whereas cathode electrolyte interphase resistance causes major voltage and energy losses. A phase-field model of a sulfide-based cell shows that higher stack pressure strongly suppresses breathing-related fluctuations, but leaves reactive memory nearly unchanged. Thus, pressure controls breathing, not memory. The resulting regime map identifies void-growth-dominant, healing-dominant, and interphase-memory-dominant regions. The theory also predicts energy-density rank inversion with C rate, where an initially superior architecture loses advantage at higher rate as breathing intensifies and interphase memory locks in. The design target is therefore not merely higher conductivity or lower resistance, but simultaneous suppression of breathing and independent control of reactive memory through interphase chemistry.

</details>

### [Large language model-enabled automated data extraction for concrete materials informatics](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.22938v1)

**Authors:** Zhanzhao Li, Kengran Yang, Qiyao He, Kai Gong  
**Type:** both · **Category:** disordered systems and neural networks · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.22938v1>

Panel b shows representative models and their category-wise performance (F1 score), while panel c summarizes the overall F1 score versus extraction time for all...</figcaption>
</figure>

The detailed survey methodology and the comparison with the ten largest datasets are provided in Supplementary Note 2 and Supplementary Note 5, respectively. b, Two-dimensional Uniform Manifold Approximation and Projection (UMAP)72</figcaption>
</figure>

The base input features comprise mixture proportions, curing conditions, and specimen dimensions. Each violin includes individual data points from 10 runs with different random seeds, with horizontal dashed lines indicating the median and interquartile range. The inset shows a parity plot of predicted versus measured compressive strength for XGBoost (XGB) using both chemical and physical descriptors as input features, with marginal kernel density estimates shown along each...</figcaption>
</figure>

Results for mixture proportion and mixture property are based on the outcome of article screening, while those for binder property, curing condition, and specimen dimension are derived from the curated database (before missing data imputation).</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** The scarcity of large, high-quality, and accessible experimental datasets for concrete materials informatics due to the labor-intensive nature of manual data extraction from unstructured scientific literature.

**Main result.** The development of an LLM-powered pipeline that achieved F1 scores up to 0.97, enabling the creation of the largest open laboratory database for blended cement concrete with nearly 9,000 high-quality records.

**Method.** A modular, sequential chain of specialized Large Language Model (LLM) agents (extraction and processing) used to automate the extraction, cleaning, and structuring of data from XML/HTML scientific publications.

**Summary.** This paper presents an automated pipeline using Large Language Models to extract complex materials data from scientific literature. By using specialized LLM agents, the authors successfully built a massive database of nearly 9,000 records for blended cement concrete. This approach overcomes the bottleneck of manual data collection and demonstrates that large-scale, automated curation can significantly improve the accuracy and generalization of machine learning models in materials science.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The physical system is blended cement concrete, involving mixtures of Portland cement and supplementary cementitious materials (SCMs) like fly ash, slag, and silica fume. The computational platform is an automated LLM-based data extraction and machine learning pipeline.

**Key observables.** Compressive strength, binder chemical/physical properties (oxide compositions, Blaine fineness), mixture proportions, and curing conditions.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Water-to-binder ratio, CaO, SiO2, Al2O3, Fe2O3, MgO, curing age, temperature, and humidity.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The pipeline performs significantly better with XML/HTML formats than PDF; the study focuses on concrete-level formulations and excludes mineral-level characteristics like particle shape.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows dataset size gaps in existing studies; Figure 2 compares LLM performance (F1 score vs. time); Figure 3 illustrates the scale and diversity of the new database via UMAP and ternary diagrams; Figure 4 evaluates ML model performance (ID vs. OOD) and feature importance via SHAP; Figure 5 quantifies missing data in literature.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the data scarcity problem, describes the LLM-agent pipeline architecture, benchmarks various LLMs, presents the newly constructed large-scale concrete database, evaluates machine learning models for strength prediction, and discusses future directions for multimodal extraction.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

The promise of data-driven materials discovery remains constrained by the scarcity of large, high-quality, and accessible experimental datasets. Here, we introduce a generalizable large language model (LLM)-powered pipeline for automated extraction and structuring of materials data from unstructured scientific literature, using concrete materials as a representative and particularly challenging example. The pipeline exhibits robust performance across a broad range of LLMs and achieves an $F_1$ score of up to 0.97 for diverse composition--process--property attributes. Within one hour, it extracts nearly 9,000 high-quality records with over 100 attributes screened from more than 27,000 publications, enabling the construction of the largest open laboratory database for blended cement concrete. Machine learning analyses underscore the importance of large, diverse, and information-rich datasets for enhancing both in-distribution accuracy and out-of-distribution generalization to unseen materials. The proposed pipeline is readily adaptable to other materials domains and accelerates the development of scalable data infrastructures for materials informatics.

</details>

### [Mesoscopic Josephson effect in graphene disk at magnetic field](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23470v1)

**Authors:** Adam Rycerz  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.23470v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>

<img src="2604.23470_figures/2604.23470_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> Figure 1: (a) Schematic of a graphene disk with inner radius r1 and outer radius r2, contacted by two circular superconducting elec- trodes (yellow areas). A current source drives a dissipationless su- percurrent through the annular region. The uniform magnetic field B = (0, 0, B) is perpendicular to the sample. A separate gate electrode (not shown) allows one to tune the carrier concentration. (b) At low field but high doping, such that the cyclotron diameter 2rc &gt; r2 −r1, incoherent scattering along the classical trajectory governs the transport. (c) Current-phase relation for high doping (|µ| ≫ℏvF /r1) and the narrow-disk limit (r2/r1 →1). Red line correspond to the B = 0 case (see Ref....</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23470_figures/2604.23470_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> Figure 2: (a) Normal-state conductance 1/RN, (b) product IcRN, and (c) skewness of the current-phase relation S as functions of the chemical potential for different magnetic fields, B⋆≡eBr2 1/2ℏ, specified for each line in (a); same line-color encoding is used in (a)–(c). The radii ratio is r2/r1 = 2 (a = 0.5). Dashed-black lines correspond to the B →0, |µ| ≫ℏvF /r1 limit (see Ref. [26]).</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23470_figures/2604.23470_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> Figure 3: (a) Chemical potential corresponding to the activation voltage (µon ≡eUon/2) for the normal state displayed as function of the magnetic field for the two selected threshold conductances: Gmin(Uon) = ηg0, with η = 0.5, 1 [solid lines]. Additional line [dashed] marks 2rc = r2−r1 (or µ⋆ on = B⋆). (b) Product IcRN and (c) skewness S calculated at µ = µon [solid lines]. Thin horizontal lines in (b,c) mark the predictions for incoherent scattering (see also Table I). Insets in (b,c) depict the averages taken for the consecutive intervals between the maxima, i.e., (1 . . . 2), . . . , (5 . . . 6).</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** Investigating the mesoscopic Josephson effect in a graphene Corbino disk under a perpendicular magnetic field, specifically looking for deviations from the standard tunneling-limit current-phase relation.

**Main result.** In the limit of vanishing conductance, the system exhibits an enhanced I_cR_N product (~1.85 Delta_0/e) and positive skewness (S ~ 0.14) compared to the standard tunneling limit.

**Method.** Quantum-mechanical mode-matching analysis using the Dirac-Bogoliubov-De-Gennes equation, compared against an incoherent scattering approximation.

**Summary.** This paper explores how the geometry and magnetic field of a graphene Corbino disk influence the Josephson effect. It demonstrates that even in the limit of very high resistance, the junction maintains mesoscopic features like an enhanced I_cR_N product and a skewed current-phase relation. The study provides a detailed comparison between exact quantum-mechanical calculations and simpler scattering models. These findings highlight the persistence of non-trivial transport properties in graphene-based superconducting junctions.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** A superconductor-graphene-superconductor (S-g-S) junction in a Corbino (disk) geometry, featuring an annular graphene sample with two circular superconducting electrodes.

**Key observables.** Critical current (I_c), normal-state resistance (R_N), current-phase relation (CPR), skewness (S), and conductance (G).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Magnetic field (B), chemical potential (mu), radii ratio (a), and the short-junction limit (junction width much smaller than coherence length).

**Assumptions / limitations.** Short-junction limit (width << coherence length); use of an incoherent scattering approximation for comparison; averaging datasets to mitigate Landau level resonance effects.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1 shows the Corbino geometry and the current-phase relation; Fig 2 displays conductance, I_cR_N, and skewness as functions of chemical potential; Fig 3 shows the relationship between chemical potential and magnetic field-induced resonances and compares averaged results to incoherent scattering predictions.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the Corbino geometry, presents the theoretical framework (DBdG equation), details the numerical mode-matching method, compares results with an incoherent scattering model, and analyzes the evolution of Josephson observables under varying magnetic fields and doping.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Unlike for tunneling Josephson junctions, for which the current-phase relation is given by the sine function, with the critical current ($I_c$) and normal-state resistance ($R_N$) following the relation $I_cR_N=(π/2)\,Δ_0/e$ (where $Δ_0$ is the superconducting gap and electron charge is $-e$), mesoscopic Josephson junctions show more complex current-phase relations, with the skewness $S>0$, what is related to the presence -- in case the leads are in the normal state -- of transmission probabilities taking the values comparable to $1$. Here, we show that these features also appear for a superconductor-graphene-superconductor (S-g-S) junction in the disk-shaped (Corbino) geometry, when the magnetic field is adjusted such that $I_c\rightarrow{}0$ and $R_N\rightarrow{}\infty$. In such a case, the product $I_cR_N\approx{}1.85\,Δ_0/e$, and the skewness $S\approx{}0.14$. The results obtained from quantum-mechanical mode-matching analysis for the Dirac-Bogoliubov-De-Gennes equation are compared with simpler model assuming incoherent scattering between two circular interfaces separating the sample and the leads.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

<a id="paper-2604.23429"></a>
### [Multi-photon schemes for mid-infrared detection](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23429v1)

**Authors:** Alistair H. Duff, J. E. Sipe  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.23429v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

The larger ovals on the right indicate constructive interference for the electron-hole transition at positive k, versus destructive interference at −k.</figcaption>
</figure>

The bold red arrows represent the pump photon energy, and the narrow blue arrows the signal photon energy.</figcaption>
</figure>

</figure>

<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 9.</b> FIG. 9: The three independent components of the D-2PA tensor of GaAs as a function of incident photon energy ℏω, calculated with the 30-band k · p model.</figcaption>
</figure>

<img src="2604.23429_figures/2604.23429_fig10.jpg" alt="Figure 10">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 10.</b> FIG. 10: The spectral dependence of the anisotropy and linear-circular dichroism for D-2PA and ND-2PA with a pump at ℏωp = 1.3 eV, calculated with the 30 band k · p GaAs model. σ plotted in black uses the left axis, while δ plotted in red uses the right axis.</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** Developing efficient mid-infrared (MIR) detection methods that avoid cryogenic temperatures by comparing two multi-photon absorption schemes using non-degenerate two-photon absorption (ND-2PA).

**Main result.** The study finds that Ge(1-x)Sn(x) alloys (Scheme II) exhibit a substantially larger nonlinear response and three-color injected current compared to GaAs (Scheme I) in the mid-infrared window.

**Method.** Theoretical calculations using first- and second-order time-dependent perturbation theory and a 30-band k.p model.

**Summary.** This paper investigates theoretical methods for detecting mid-infrared light using multi-photon processes in semiconductors. It compares a scheme using wide-gap GaAs to one using narrow-gap Germanium-Tin alloys. The researchers demonstrate that the GeSn-based approach provides much higher sensitivity and larger nonlinear current generation. This work is significant for developing room-temperature infrared detectors.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The physical systems studied are bulk GaAs and Ge(1-x)Sn(x) alloys, modeled using a 30-band k.p Hamiltonian to account for the entire Brillouin zone and anisotropic nonlinear responses.

**Key observables.** Nonlinear absorption coefficient (alpha), three-color injected current response tensor, linear electric susceptibility, and anisotropy/dichroism parameters.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Photon energies (signal and pump), alloy composition (x), band gap energy, and pump intensities up to 1 TW/cm2.

**Assumptions / limitations.** Independent particle approximation, continuous wave limit, neglect of many-body effects, neglect of phonon-mediated indirect absorption, and neglect of local inversion symmetry breaking in GeSn alloys.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows a schematic of current generation via interference between degenerate 2PA and 1PA; Figure 2 shows schematics of the two ND-2PA schemes (Scheme I and Scheme II).

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of MIR detection, defines two multi-photon schemes, details the 30-band k.p modeling approach, compares the nonlinear responses and current injection of GaAs and GeSn, and provides technical parameters for the alloys.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We calculate the theoretical non-degenerate two photon absorption and three color injected current response tensors for bulk GaAs and Ge$_{1-x}$Sn$_x$ for a range of alloy compositions. In particular, by including a ''pump'' beam we compare two ''schemes'' that are sensitive to mid-infrared photons. In ''scheme I'' we consider GaAs and a pump photon with energy greater than half the band gap, and in ''scheme II'' we consider Ge$_{1-x}$Sn$_x$ with a pump photon with energy less than half the band gap. We find that for certain pump and alloy concentrations Ge$_{1-x}$Sn$_x$ has a substantially larger nonlinear response and three-color injected current than GaAs in the mid-infrared frequency window where both materials can absorb photons via non-degenerate two-photon absorption.

</details>

<a id="paper-2604.23943"></a>
### [Nearly Isotropic Magnon Transport in Epitaxial Lithium Aluminum Ferrite Thin Films](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23943v1)

**Authors:** Yiming Li, Katya Mikhailova, Lerato Takana, Daisy O'Mahoney, Sauviz P. Alaei, Guanxiong Qu, Dominic Petruzzi, Samuel Crossley, Harold Y. Hwang, Ian R. Fisher, Clare C. Yu, Yuri Suzuki  
**Type:** experiment · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.23943v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>

(b) Frequency dependence of HFMR at 250 K can be fit to the Kittel equation to deduce the Land´e g-factor, effective magnetization Meff and in-plane anisotropy field H4,∥along the [100] and [110] axes.</figcaption>
</figure>

<img src="2604.23943_figures/2604.23943_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> FIG. 1. Field dependence of the magnetization for a (001) LAFO film on (001) MAO along the in-plane [100] and [110] directions at 250 K. (inset) Schematic diagram of the LAFO film showing the [001] out-of-plane axis and the [100] and [110] in-plane axes.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23943_figures/2604.23943_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> FIG. 4. Amplitudes of the SHE nonlocal resistance ∆RSHE (upper panel) and amplitudes of the SSE nonlocal resistance ∆RSSE (lower panel) as a function of the magnon propaga- tion distance d along the [100] and [110] directions at 250 K. Measurements were taken with an injection charge current of I0 = 0.4 mA, 0.5 mA and 0.6 mA on each device and one or two devices at the same distance d. A single best-fit curve is plotted for each orientation and for each type of nonlocal resistance.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23943_figures/2604.23943_fig4.jpg" alt="Figure 4">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 4.</b> FIG. 3. Nonlocal spin transport measurements along the [100] directions show (a) SHE nonlocal resistance RSHE and (b) SSE nonlocal resistance RSSE as a function of the angle γ between the magnetization and the magnon propagation di- rection. Similar SHE and SSE measurements are shown for magnon propagation along the [110] direction in (c) and (d). These measurements were taken on a device with Pt elec- trodes a distance d = 0.5 µm apart at 250 K and 300 mT with 3 different injection currents I0: 0.4 mA (yellow), 0.5 mA (grey) and 0.6 mA (blue).</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** The study aims to determine if the anisotropic magnon transport observed in magnesium aluminum ferrite (MAFO) is a general feature of spinel ferrites or if isotropic in-plane magnon propagation can be achieved for information transport.

**Main result.** The researchers found that epitaxial lithium aluminum ferrite (LAFO) thin films exhibit nearly isotropic magnon diffusion lengths along the [100] and [110] directions at 250 K, despite having pronounced fourfold in-plane magnetic anisotropy.

**Method.** Nonlocal transport measurements using platinum electrodes were used to detect electrically (Spin Hall Effect) and thermally (Spin Seebeck Effect) generated magnons, combined with SQUID magnetometry and broadband Ferromagnetic Resonance (FMR).

**Summary.** This paper investigates magnon transport in lithium aluminum ferrite (LAFO) thin films to address the need for low-loss, isotropic magnetic waveguides. While previous spinel ferrites showed anisotropic transport, LAFO demonstrates nearly identical magnon diffusion lengths along different in-plane directions. This isotropy is attributed to isotropic exchange stiffness and potential angular averaging. These findings suggest that LAFO is a promising platform for developing efficient, isotropic magnon-based information interconnects.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The physical system consists of epitaxial (001) lithium aluminum ferrite (LAFO) thin films grown on MgAl2O4 substrates, characterized by a spinel structure with significant in-plane magnetic anisotropy.

**Key observables.** Magnon diffusion length (lambda), nonlocal resistance (R_SHE and R_SSE), saturation magnetization (Ms), anisotropy field (H4,parallel), and Gilbert damping (alpha).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Temperature of 250 K, magnetic field of 300 mT, and film thickness of approximately 12 nm.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes a 1D magnon diffusion model and attributes the observed isotropy to nearly isotropic exchange stiffness and effective angular averaging due to inhomogeneous broadening.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1 shows magnetization field dependence and hysteresis loops; Fig 2 shows FMR frequency dependence; Fig 3 demonstrates the angular dependence of nonlocal resistance (cos^2 and cos dependence).

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of magnon anisotropy, describes the growth and structural characterization of LAFO films, presents magnetic property measurements (SQUID and FMR), details the nonlocal transport experiments, and concludes with a discussion on the mechanism of isotropic transport.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Low-loss magnetic insulating thin films are promising for information transport via magnons, where isotropic in-plane magnon propagation is desirable. We report nonlocal measurements of electrically and thermally generated magnons in epitaxial (001) lithium aluminum ferrite Li$_{0.5}$Al$_{0.7}$Fe$_{1.8}$O$_4$ thin films with pronounced fourfold in-plane magnetic anisotropy. By measuring the inverse spin Hall signal as a function of the magnon diffusion distance, we deduce magnon diffusion lengths that are nearly identical along the [100] and [110] directions at 250~K. This isotropy is consistent with a nearly isotropic exchange stiffness. These results highlight spinel ferrites as viable platforms for isotropic magnon transport.

</details>

### [Non-Bloch band theory of nonlinear eigenvalue problems](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.24284v1)

**Authors:** Kota Otsuka, Kazuki Yokomizo  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.24284v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">

<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> FIG. 1. Eigenvalues of the nonreciprocal hopping system. The black and gray curves represent the non-Bloch and Bloch bands, respectively. The red and orange dots indicate the spectra of the open-boundary and periodic-boundary systems with L = 10, respectively. The parameters are set to be tR = 1 and tL = 0.5.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24284_figures/2604.24284_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> FIG. 2. Eigenvalues, generalized Brillouin zones, and eigen- states of the model with the nonlinear pseudo-Hermiticity. (a) Bloch and non-Bloch bands. The winding number (15) takes +1, −1, and 0 in the red, blue, and white regions, re- spectively. (b) Spectra of the open-boundary and periodic- boundary systems. (c) Generalized Brillouin zones. The gray curve represents the unit circle. (d) Spatial profiles of the eigenstates defined in Eq. (28). The corresponding eigenval- ues are shown in the same colors in (b). The parameters are m = 0.9, t = 1, and v = 0.5, and the system size is L = 50 in (b) and (d).</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24284_figures/2604.24284_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> FIG. 3. Eigenvalues and Chern number of the nonlinear Chern insulator with nonreciprocal hopping amplitudes. (a) Continuum bands with m = 1. (b) Spectra with m = 1 un- der a full open boundary condition. The gray and red points represent the spectra of the bulk eigenstates and the edge states, respectively. (c) and (d) Spectra under the cylindrical geometry (open boundary conditions in the x direction). The color bar means the biorthogonal inverse participation ratio defined in Eq. (46). (e) Generalized Brillouin zones of the auxiliary bands n = + and n = −. (f) Chern number (50) for the lower band n = −. The Chern number is −1 and 0 in the gray and white regions, respectively. The...</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** The breakdown of conventional Bloch band theory and topological characterization in nonlinear eigenvalue problems due to extreme sensitivity to boundary conditions.

**Main result.** The establishment of a non-Bloch band theory framework that uses Generalized Brillouin Zones (GBZs) to accurately reproduce open boundary condition spectra and predict topological edge states in nonlinear systems.

**Method.** Development of a non-Bloch framework using complex-valued Bloch wavenumbers and the construction of Generalized Brillouin Zones (GBZs) via the characteristic equation of the nonlinear system.

**Summary.** This paper introduces a non-Bloch band theory specifically designed for nonlinear eigenvalue problems. It addresses the failure of standard Bloch theory in systems where parameters depend on the eigenvalue, which typically causes a discrepancy between periodic and open boundary spectra. By using Generalized Brillouin Zones, the authors can accurately predict the spectra and the emergence of the nonlinear skin effect. The framework is successfully extended to 2D to establish a bulk-boundary correspondence for nonlinear Chern insulators.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** 1D and 2D tight-binding models featuring nonlinear eigenvalue equations where system parameters depend on the eigenvalue, including nonreciprocal hopping models and nonlinear Chern insulators.

**Key observables.** Complex eigenvalue spectra, winding numbers, Chern numbers, and the biorthogonal inverse participation ratio (bi-IPR).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Nonreciprocity strength (tL/tR, gamma_x), nonlinearity strength (delta), and mass parameter (m).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The nonlinearity follows specific analytical forms (e.g., f(omega) = omega or -omega^2), and the 2D extension assumes mirror symmetry to suppress skin effects in one direction.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1 compares Bloch vs. non-Bloch bands and PBC vs. OBC spectra; Fig 3 shows the reproduction of bulk and edge spectra in 2D, the disappearance of edge states, and the calculation of Chern number transitions.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of nonlinear eigenvalue sensitivity, develops the 1D non-Bloch framework and GBZ construction, investigates the nonlinearity-induced skin effect and pseudo-Hermiticity, and extends the theory to 2D topological Chern insulators.

**Why it may be interesting.** This work provides a critical theoretical tool for understanding nonlinear dynamics in optics, mechanical oscillators, and weakly interacting bosonic systems where boundary-induced spectral shifts are prevalent.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Nonlinear eigenvalue problems arise in a wide range of physical systems, in which system parameters depend on the eigenvalue. Such systems have been proposed to exhibit an extreme sensitivity of their spectra to boundary conditions, which leads to the breakdown of conventional topological characterizations. In this work, we establish a non-Bloch framework for calculating continuum bands that reproduce the spectra of the nonlinear system with open boundary conditions. This non-Bloch band theory enables us not only to calculate the eigenvalues but also to reveal phenomena unique to the nonlinear system. We further investigate the topological bulk-boundary correspondence in a nonlinear Chern insulator within an extended version of this framework.

</details>

### [Nonintegral Flux Trapping in Frustrated Josephson Networks of Triplet Superconductors](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.24734v1)

**Authors:** Grayson R. Frazier, Colton Lelievre, Yi Li  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** strongly correlated electrons · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.24734v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>
<div class="paper-figures-scroll">
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24734_figures/2604.24734_fig1.jpg" alt="Figure 1">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 1.</b> FIG. 1. Schematic of Josephson junction network in a spin triplet superconductor. At each superconducting grain, the pairing correlation is given by ˆ∆n(k), which can be expressed in terms of its d vector, ˆdn. Between neighbouring grains, there is Josephson coupling, which is dependent on the relative orientation of d vectors and U(1) phase difference. At the intersection of three grains, there is a three-grain ring, as shown on the right. If the Josephson network is frustrated, this can lead to spontaneous trapped flux, Φ.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24734_figures/2604.24734_fig2.jpg" alt="Figure 2">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 2.</b> FIG. 2. Example of minimal-energy d vector configurations in the three-grain Josephson ring. (a) For DM-like coupling |D0| &lt; |J0|/ √</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.24734_figures/2604.24734_fig3.jpg" alt="Figure 3">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 3.</b> FIG. 3. Flux trapping and frustration in three-grain network of triplet superconductors. (a) Contour plot of the free energy FJ in Eq. (8) as a function of the relative d vector orientation angles β1,2 and β2,3 for D0 = −0.7J0, which is above the critical value D∗= |J0|/ √</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** Investigating how anisotropic Josephson coupling between spin-triplet Cooper pairs in a junction network leads to frustrated d-vector textures and spontaneous nonintegral flux trapping.

**Main result.** The authors identify a critical coupling strength D* = |J0|/sqrt(3) above which the system enters a frustrated regime characterized by chiral 120-degree d-vector textures and the spontaneous trapping of pi-flux.

**Method.** The study uses a theoretical framework involving the minimization of the Josephson free energy and the analytical derivation of an emergent geometric phase arising from d-vector orientations.

**Summary.** This paper explores how the internal spin structure of triplet superconductors can induce frustration in Josephson junction networks. By analyzing the competition between different types of anisotropic coupling, the authors show that a transition occurs to a state with chiral d-vector textures. This transition leads to the spontaneous trapping of fractional (pi) magnetic flux. The findings suggest new ways to engineer frustrated superconducting states using magnetic textures.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** A Josephson junction network of superconducting grains (such as polycrystals or single-crystal networks) with spin-triplet pairing, modeled via a three-grain ring with Heisenberg-like and Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya-type interactions.

**Key observables.** Nonintegral/fractional flux trapping (specifically pi-flux), spontaneous Josephson currents, and chiral d-vector textures.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Anisotropic coupling amplitudes (J0, D0, Gamma), the dimensionless ratio x = D0/J0, and the critical value D* = |J0|/sqrt(3).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes unitary triplet pairing (d x d* = 0) and neglects the self-inductance of the network.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 provides a schematic of a Josephson junction network illustrating superconducting grains, d-vector orientations, and a three-grain ring demonstrating flux trapping.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the problem of flux trapping in triplet networks, defines the Hamiltonian and coupling terms, performs an analytical derivation of the critical coupling regime, compares the results to half-quantum vortices, and discusses experimental candidates and implications for glassy phases.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

In a Josephson junction network, anisotropic coupling between spin triplet pairing correlations can lead to frustrated $d$ vector textures that support spontaneous Josephson currents and nonintegral flux trapping. Such networks can appear in superconducting polycrystals, as well as single-crystal superconductors. In analogy to classical spin systems, in which the presence of geometric frustration and anisotropic superexchange can lead to nontrivial spin textures, Josephson networks with anisotropic Josephson couplings cannot simultaneously optimize their $\mathrm{U}(1)$ superconducting phase difference and relative $d$ vector orientations. The internal pairing structure of Cooper pairs twists as they tunnel across the Josephson junction, and the $d$ vector texture enters as an emergent geometric phase which can spontaneously trap fractional flux. For unitary triplet pairing order, this mechanism can support $π$-flux trapping above a critical value of antisymmetric Josephson coupling, and is distinct from usual half-quantum vortices. The results of this work reveal new routes to engineer frustrated Josephson networks from the interplay of magnetic textures and spin triplet superconducting pairing order.

</details>

**Main problem.** Investigating the tunable optical properties and the onset of phase separation in (InxGa1-x)2O3 microcrystalline alloy films.

**Main result.** The study identifies the onset of incipient phase separation at x ~ 0.3, where phase-segregated precipitates act as a major defect type, and finds that large Urbach energies are driven by strong hole-phonon coupling.

**Method.** Wet chemical synthesis followed by UV-Vis transmission spectroscopy, spatial photoluminescence mapping, AFM, and EDS analysis.

**Summary.** This paper explores how changing the indium content in (InxGa1-x)2O3 films tunes their optical properties from the deep-UV to the visible spectrum. The researchers discovered that at a composition of x ~ 0.3, the material begins to undergo phase separation, creating defects that significantly impact the optical properties. They also found that the material exhibits much larger Urbach energies than similar alloy systems due to strong coupling between holes and phonons. This work is important for designing transparent conducting oxides and wide-bandgap semiconductors.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** Microcrystalline (InxGa1-x)2O3 alloy films ranging from beta-Ga2O3 to In2O3, characterized using a saturation model for the optical gap and an Urbach model for absorption band tails.

**Key observables.** Optical gap (E_OG), Urbach energy (E_u), self-trapped hole (STH) photoluminescence emission, and compositional-dependent absorption tails.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Indium composition (x), solubility limit/saturation composition (x0 ~ 0.3), and hole-phonon coupling strength.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The use of a tanh-based saturation model to identify the onset of phase separation.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows normalized transmission spectra; Figure 3 and 5 include transmission spectra showing absorption features and the In2O3 optical gap.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the alloy system, describes the synthesis and characterization methods, presents the compositional dependence of the optical gap and STH PL, analyzes phase separation via saturation modeling, compares Urbach energies with the MgxZn1-xO system, and concludes with the impact of hole-phonon coupling.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

The tailored optical properties of $(In_xGa_{1-x})_2O_3$ microcrystalline films were studied as a function of composition x via transmission, Urbach energy analysis, and spatial photoluminescence (PL) mapping of the self-trapped hole (STH) emission, with the objective of addressing material characteristics specific to this alloy system. Up to x = 0.46, the optical gap exhibited a redshift of 1 eV from the deep to the near-UV range, while the STH PL was redshifted by 0.5 eV in the visible range. For higher composition, x = 0.63, the transmission spectra indicated the co-existence of two optical gaps attributed to Ga-rich and to In-rich domains, implying that this sample is phase-separated. However, the saturation behavior of the optical gap and that of the STH PL showed that incipient phase separation occurs at a lower composition: x ~ 0.3. This is consistent with the compositional trend found for Urbach energy, implying that phase segregation in the alloys is a major defect even at its incipient stages. Additionally, Urbach analysis of $(In_xGa_{1-x})_2O_3$ was compared to that of $Mg_xZn_{1-x}O$. Both systems were found to have similar compositional dependence: at lower range, Urbach energies exhibited a negligible increase, while at the higher range a significant dependence on the composition was found. The main difference between the two alloy systems is in their Urbach energy: those for $(In_xGa_{1-x})_2O_3$ were significantly larger than those of $Mg_xZn_{1-x}O$. This stems from the strong hole coupling to phonons of $(In_xGa_{1-x})_2O_3$, which provides a dynamic transition additionally to that of defect-type.

</details>

If originally all the particles are located at the radii to the left of point A on the graph, the initial polydispersity of the particles does not cause the particles to diverge more in size, as there is a restoring force to bring them back to equilibrium; this is caused by the positive value of derivative of the chemical...</figcaption>

</div>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates how the addition of a sparingly soluble component affects the kinetics and particle size distribution of Ostwald ripening in dispersed systems.

**Main result.** The author derives a generalized growth law that accounts for molar volume differences and non-ideality, showing that high concentrations of the additive follow classical LSW theory while low concentrations lead to bimodal distributions.

**Method.** The study uses an extension of the Lifshits-Slezov-Wagner (LSW) theory, employing mass flux analysis, the Kelvin equation, and numerical simulations of particle size evolution.

**Summary.** This paper extends the classical LSW theory of Ostwald ripening to systems containing a sparingly soluble additive. It demonstrates that at high additive concentrations, the system maintains classical cubic growth and distribution patterns, but at low concentrations, the distribution splits into a bimodal pattern consisting of 'fines' and ripening particles. The author provides a new mathematical framework to account for non-ideal solution behavior and differences in molar volumes, offering practical insights for stabilizing emulsions.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** A two-component dispersed phase (e.g., oil-in-water emulsions) consisting of spherical liquid drops in a medium, where the second component is sparingly soluble.

**Key observables.** Particle size distribution (PSD), ripening rate (cubic law), critical radius, and the lock-in number.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Lock-in number (L1), molar fraction of the second component (x2,0), interfacial tension, and molar volume differences.

**Assumptions / limitations.** Quasi-steady-state mode, negligible solubility of the medium in the dispersed phase, and small-scale expansion of the Kelvin equation.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows the LSW particle size distribution attractor; Figure 2 illustrates chemical potential vs. particle size regimes; Figure 3 shows the evolution of bimodal distributions; Figures 4-11 show the evolution of various initial distributions (uniform, log-normal, gamma) toward the lock-in state.

**Paper structure.** The paper revisits and extends a previous model, establishes the applicability of LSW theory in high-concentration regimes, derives an improved extrapolatory equation for ripening rates, analyzes the transition to bimodal distributions at low concentrations, and concludes with practical stabilization strategies.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Additives of sparingly soluble components are known to slow down or completely inhibit Ostwald ripening in dispersed systems. In this paper, our earlier model of stabilization against Ostwald ripening is revisited and extended. In a quasi-steady-state mode, the process is shown to be controlled by the diffusion of the less soluble component, and the whole machinery of the classical Lifshits-Slezov-Wagner (LSW) theory can be leveraged almost without any change. The particle size distribution is predicted to follow the same distribution function pattern as in the classic LSW theory. The rate of ripening follows the classic cubic law. To extend our earlier result, an improved extrapolatory equation for the ripening rate is derived, that covers the whole formulation range, accounts for the difference in molar volumes of the components and for the solution non-ideality. The behavior described above is observed over the range of high concentrations of the poorly soluble component, with the cutoff determined by the lock-in number described in the previous paper of this series. When the concentration of the additive is low, the kinetics no longer follows the LSW pattern; instead, the particle size distribution becomes bimodal, with the fraction of 'fines' enriched by the poorly soluble component and the fraction of the large particles to ripen as if no additive were present. The lock-in parameter L1 can be used to characterize for the transition from one mode to another. In the end, some practical stabilization approaches for emulsions are discussed.

</details>

**Main problem.** Understanding the kinetic arrest of the metastable T' phase in monolayer MoS2 and determining why it persists despite a strong thermodynamic driving force toward the stable H phase.

**Main result.** The phase transformation is kinetically hindered by repulsive S-S interactions at grain boundaries that create high energy barriers for kink generation. The study also reveals that sulfur vacancies fail to accelerate transformation at the most stable interface because they are thermodynamically unstable there and migrate away.

**Method.** The researchers used machine learning-accelerated molecular dynamics (via DeepMD-kit) and first-principles DFT calculations to study nucleation and interface propagation.

**Summary.** This paper investigates why the metastable T' phase in MoS2 persists despite thermodynamic pressure to transform into the H phase. By using machine learning-accelerated molecular dynamics, the authors show that repulsive interactions between sulfur atoms at grain boundaries create significant energy barriers. They demonstrate that while defects can sometimes help, sulfur vacancies actually migrate away from the most stable interfaces, failing to catalyze the transition. This work provides a new paradigm for controlling phase transitions in 2D materials through interface-specific design.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The physical system is monolayer MoS2, specifically focusing on the phase transition between the metastable metallic T' phase and the stable semiconducting H phase, including various grain boundary configurations (ZZ and AC) and the role of sulfur vacancies.

**Key observables.** Nucleation barriers, S-S pair counts, interface propagation velocity, kink density, and vacancy formation energies.

**Important parameters / regimes.** The system operates in a low-density kink regime (Nk << L/a) where the rate-limiting step is kink generation rather than migration; S-S distance and COHP-derived antibonding character are critical.

**Assumptions / limitations.** The kinetic modeling of interface propagation assumes steady-state conditions and a low-density limit of kinks where kink-kink interactions are negligible.

**Figures summary.** Fig 1: DFT analysis of S-S repulsion and COHP curves; Fig 2: MLFF training details; Fig 3: Energy profiles of nucleation pathways and the effect of vacancies; Fig 4: Analysis of H-T' interface propagation and energy barriers for different grain boundaries.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins with a DFT-based investigation of atomic-scale S-S repulsion, moves to the development and validation of a machine learning force field, presents large-scale MD simulations of nucleation and growth, and concludes with a kinetic model of interface propagation and the role of defects.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

The metastable T' phase in monolayer MoS2 exhibits remarkable persistence despite a strong thermodynamic driving force toward the stable H phase. Using machine learning-accelerated molecular dynamics and first-principles calculations, we reveal that this kinetic arrest originates from repulsive S-S interactions, which impose high energy barriers during both nucleation and grain boundary propagation. While sulfur vacancies can alleviate these barriers in certain interfaces, they fail to accelerate transformation at the most stable interface, ZZ-Mo|-, due to their thermodynamic instability there. Instead, vacancies migrate into the T' phase, leaving the advancing front defect-free. Direct simulations of nanostructures confirm that H-phase nucleation initiates at corners or edges, and all observed growth fronts adopt the ZZ-Mo|- configuration, consistent with its low interfacial energy but slow kinetics. Our work establishes that phase transformation in 2D materials is governed not by global defect concentration, but by the local compatibility between defects and moving interfaces, offering a new paradigm for controlling structural transitions through interface-specific design.

</details>

</div>

**Main problem.** The paper seeks to establish a consistent physical basis for charge transport and dimensionality in amorphous oxide semiconductor (AOS) FETs, addressing uncertainties regarding the role of crystalline order, high trap density, and the validity of band transport models in nominally amorphous materials.

**Main result.** The authors demonstrate that high-mobility AOS FETs are best described by a trap-influenced band transport model in a quasi-2D channel, supported by evidence of nanoscale crystalline domains and delocalized carrier transport.

**Method.** The study combines structural analysis (FEM, TEM, STEM) with electronic transport measurements (Hall effect, carrier velocity) and theoretical modeling (Schrödinger-Poisson analysis, MTR and percolation models).

**Summary.** This paper provides a conceptual framework to justify using band-transport models in amorphous oxide semiconductors. By analyzing structural and electronic data, the authors show that despite the amorphous nature, the presence of nanoscale crystalline domains and high carrier mobilities allow for a quasi-2D band transport description. The work reconciles the Multiple Trapping and Release (MTR) model with percolation effects caused by spatial disorder. This is crucial for the accurate design and scaling of advanced AOS-based transistors.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The system consists of Amorphous Oxide Semiconductor (AOS) FETs, such as IGZO and ZTO. The transport is modeled using an extended Multiple Trapping and Release (MTR) framework combined with percolation theory to account for spatial inhomogeneities.

**Key observables.** Carrier mobility, carrier density, Hall voltage, carrier velocity, mean free path, and subband energies.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Effective mass (m*), De Broglie wavelength, nanocrystalline domain size (1-5 nm), mean free path (> 1 nm), and the metal-insulator transition threshold (e^2/h).

**Assumptions / limitations.** Use of effective medium approximation, parabolic band assumption, and the assumption that the accumulation layer can be treated as a quasi-2D system.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 shows crystalline domain sizes and the relationship between De Broglie wavelength and carrier density; Figure 2 displays the Density of States and Fermi level positions; Figure 3 plots the temperature dependence of various scattering mechanisms and mobility; Figure 4 illustrates the impact of spatial non-uniformity and velocity saturation.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by defining the problem of transport interpretation in AOS, moves to structural evidence of nanocrystalline domains, presents electronic evidence for band transport and 2D confinement, develops a mathematical framework for MTR and percolation, and concludes by integrating these models to describe high-field transport.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

A consistent and widely accepted physical basis for interpretation of charge transport in amorphous oxide semiconductor (AOS) field-effect transistors (FETs), and more generally device physics, has been hampered by uncertainties in crystalline order, dimensionality, and the effects of a significant density of traps. The overarching theme of this paper is to build and justify a much-needed conceptual framework for describing advanced AOS transistors, particularly those with very small channel lengths. Combining new work and selecting prior research results on charge transport and device physics together with literature reports from various groups on morphology, physical properties, electronic structure and percolation effects, the main evidence that is available in support of a trap-influenced band transport picture in quasi-2-dimensional channels in high mobility AOS FETs is presented.

</details>

### [Pulsed Vertical Electric Dipole Over a Lossy Halfspace: On the Time-Domain Zenneck Wave](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23254v1)

**Authors:** Giampiero Lovat  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.23254v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

<div class="figure-strip-hint">↔ Scroll figures horizontally</div>

Dashed red line: k0-SDP for SDP0 and SDP1 (when τ &lt; ρ), k0-SDP for SDP1 (when τ &lt; ρ√εr ). Dashed green line: k0-SDP for SDP0 (when τ &gt; ρ) and SDP1 (when τ &gt; ρ√εr ).</figcaption>

<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 5.</b> Fig. 6 we report the dispersion curves k(1,m) 0 (q) obtained from D0(k0, q) = 0. Although three distinct trajectories exist for each q, the modal-pole response is accurately represented by a single dominant modal pole, which we denote as kZW 0 ≡k(1,1) 0 (q). This pole is generated by the second (frequency-plane) deformation and, as clarified next, provides a compact and physically interpretable TD footprint of the ZW: as it can be seen, kZW 0 (q) is complex for small q and becomes purely imaginary for larger q. In practice, a very accurate approximation of the field is obtained as</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23254_figures/2604.23254_fig6.jpg" alt="Figure 6">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 6.</b> Fig. 4: TD field hϕ calculated through the proposed DDT and through a double inverse transform (DIT) for two representative distances: ρ = 5 m (a) and ρ = 100 m (b).</figcaption>
</figure>

<img src="2604.23254_figures/2604.23254_fig7.jpg" alt="Figure 7">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 7.</b> Fig. 6: Modal-pole dispersion curves: the three roots k(1,m) 0 (q) as functions of the real parameter q ≥0. Parameters: εr = 3.2 and σ = 0.02 S/m. Real part (a) and imaginary part (b).</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23254_figures/2604.23254_fig8.jpg" alt="Figure 8">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 8.</b> Fig. 7: TD field hϕ(ρ, t) for ρ = 5 m (a) and ρ = 100 m (b): total field hϕ calculated through the proposed DDT, its approximation hs ϕ0 + hZW ϕ , and the single components hs ϕ0 and hZW ϕ .</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23254_figures/2604.23254_fig9.jpg" alt="Figure 9">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 9.</b> Fig. 8: Modal contribution hZW ϕ as a function of the reduced time τρ for different values of ρ.</figcaption>
</figure>
<figure class="paper-figure-card">
<img src="2604.23254_figures/2604.23254_fig10.jpg" alt="Figure 10">
<figcaption class="paper-figure-caption"><b>Fig 10.</b> Fig. 9: Spatial attenuation of the modal contribution hZW ϕ .</figcaption>
</figure>
</div>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates the transient electromagnetic field signatures of the Zenneck wave excited by a pulsed vertical electric dipole over a lossy half-space, addressing whether the Zenneck wave is a physical phenomenon or a mathematical artifact.

**Main result.** The study identifies a specific modal contribution that acts as a time-domain footprint of the Zenneck wave, exhibiting waveform invariance in reduced time and spatial attenuation consistent with a surface wave.

**Method.** The author employs the Double-Deformation Technique (DDT), involving successive contour deformations in both the transverse-wavenumber and frequency planes, to decompose the field into source-pole, loss-pole, modal-pole, and residual contributions.

**Summary.** This paper provides a rigorous mathematical framework to identify the Zenneck wave in the time domain. By using a double-deformation technique, the author decomposes the electromagnetic field into distinct physical components, proving that a specific modal contribution represents the Zenneck wave. The study shows that this wave can dominate the field during a finite late-time interval and exhibits characteristic spatial attenuation. This work helps resolve long-standing debates regarding the physical reality of the Zenneck wave in pulsed excitation scenarios.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** A vertical electric dipole (VED) is located at the interface between free space and a lossy, homogeneous, isotropic half-space characterized by complex permittivity.

**Key observables.** The transient azimuthal magnetic field (h_phi) and the radiated TM-polarized field.

**Important parameters / regimes.** Relative permittivity (epsilon_r), conductivity (sigma), central frequency (f0), spectral damping (alpha_0), and observation distance (rho).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The source is a causal, damped-sinusoidal pulsed current; the media are homogeneous and isotropic; the analysis assumes the validity of the Sommerfeld representation.

**Figures summary.** Figures illustrate the physical geometry, the complex integration paths for wavenumber and frequency deformations, the decomposition of field contributions, dispersion curves, and the validation of the DDT method against standard inverse transforms.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins with the physical model and geometry, moves to the mathematical derivation using the double-deformation technique, presents the field decomposition and validation, analyzes temporal and spatial regimes (including waveform invariance and attenuation), and concludes with asymptotic analysis of the late-time tail.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We investigate the transient electromagnetic field radiated by a pulsed vertical electric dipole above a lossy half-space and identify its time-domain signatures associated with the Zenneck wave. Starting from the classical Sommerfeld representation, we derive a causal time-domain formulation based on the double-deformation technique, with successive contour deformations in the transverse-wavenumber and frequency planes. This yields an explicit decomposition of the field into source-pole, loss-pole, modal-pole, and residual steepest-descent contributions. The resulting expressions exactly satisfy causality and are validated against a reference solution obtained through a standard double inverse transform. The analysis shows that one modal contribution, generated by the frequency-plane deformation and related to the frequency-domain Zenneck pole, exhibits reduced-time invariance and a spatial attenuation consistent with a surface-wave component. Under suitable source and observation conditions, this term can dominate the field over a broad and physically relevant finite late-time interval. At the same time, for the considered damped-sinusoidal excitation, the strict asymptotic tail at fixed distance remains algebraic of order $t^{-5/2}$, with contributions from both the residual continuous spectrum and the modal-pole family. These results provide a rigorous and physically interpretable time-domain manifestation of the frequency-domain Zenneck wave in the pulsed half-space problem.

</details>

<sub>[↑ back to top](#top)</sub>

<a id="paper-2604.23868"></a>
### [Quenched Dipole Pairs in Viscous Fluid Membranes across the Saffman Crossover: Integrable Hamiltonian Dynamics](http://arxiv.org/abs/2604.23868v1)

**Authors:** Satyagni Bhattacharya, Debdatta Dey, Samyak Jain, Yassir Khan, Tirthankar Mazumder, Aryaman Mihir Seth, Nikhil Mogalapalli, Divyansh Tiwari, Pravallika Vemparala, Rickmoy Samanta  
**Type:** theory · **Category:** other · **PDF:** <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2604.23868v1>  
**Analysis basis:** full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

</figure>

</figure>

**Main problem.** The paper investigates how the Saffman crossover (the transition from near-field to far-field hydrodynamic flow) reorganizes the interaction and phase-space structure of quenched force-dipole pairs in viscous membranes.

**Main result.** The Saffman crossover induces a transition from effectively 1D dynamics (where angular mismatch is conserved) to 2D dynamics (with coupled radial and angular evolution), leading to a universal cubic collapse law for pullers in the far-field.

**Method.** The authors use an analytical approach involving 2D Fourier transforms, derivation of the membrane Green's function, and asymptotic analysis of the resulting hydrodynamic equations within a Hamiltonian framework.

**Summary.** This paper explores how hydrodynamic screening in fluid membranes changes the way active particles interact. It shows that the Saffman crossover fundamentally changes the nature of the interaction from a simple 1D-like radial motion to a complex 2D coupled motion. For 'puller' type particles, this leads to a predictable, universal way that particles aggregate over time. This work provides a minimal mathematical framework for understanding how momentum leakage into a surrounding fluid can reorganize the dynamics of embedded inclusions.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** A 2D incompressible, viscous membrane coupled to an infinite 3D surrounding fluid, containing two identical, orientation-fixed (quenched) force-dipoles (either pullers or pushers).

**Key observables.** Velocity fields, vorticity fields, relative separation (R), angular evolution (psi), and the scaling of the collapse time (tc).

**Important parameters / regimes.** Saffman length (lambda), dipole strength (sigma), membrane viscosity (eta_s), and the distinction between pullers (sigma > 0) and pushers (sigma < 0).

**Assumptions / limitations.** Flat membrane geometry, infinite subphase, low Reynolds number (Stokes flow), incompressible 2D flow, and fixed dipole orientations.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 visualizes the velocity fields of a single puller dipole, comparing the purely radial near-field flow to the rotational far-field flow. Figure 2 provides a schematic of the two-dipole system and their relative coordinates.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the physical problem of dipolar interactions, derives the hydrodynamic Green's function for the membrane, analyzes the near-field and far-field asymptotic regimes, constructs a Hamiltonian description of the two-body problem, and concludes with the derivation of universal scaling laws for particle aggregation.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

We investigate an analytic theory of force-dipole hydrodynamics in a viscous membrane coupled to an infinite surrounding fluid, focusing on quenched (orientation-fixed) dipoles. While the single-dipole flow exhibits the known Saffman crossover from a near-field \(v\sim r^{-1}\) to a screened far-field \(v\sim r^{-2}\), we show that this crossover induces a qualitatively new reorganization of dipole--dipole interactions. For two identical quenched dipoles, the near-field dynamics is exactly solvable and effectively one-dimensional, with a fixed line of centers and linear evolution of the squared separation. In the far field, the system remains integrable but becomes intrinsically two-dimensional, with coupled radial and angular dynamics and an exact first integral. For pullers, the angular dynamics drives alignment toward an attracting manifold, leading to universal late-time collapse \(R\sim (t_c-t)^{1/3}\), in contrast to the near-field scaling \(R\sim (t_c-t)^{1/2}\). The Saffman crossover thus reorganizes the Hamiltonian phase-space structure of dipolar interactions and produces a transition from effectively one-dimensional to fully coupled dynamics, providing a minimal framework for aggregation in viscous fluid membranes.

</details>

**Main problem.** The electronic properties of WTe2 are highly sensitive to crystal disorder, which obscures intrinsic phenomena like quantum oscillations and superconductivity.

**Main result.** Using a new Horizontal Flux Transport growth technique, the authors achieved record-high crystal purity, leading to the largest reported magnetoresistance in a metal, enhanced superconductivity (Tc up to 1.8 K), and the first observation of quantum oscillations in WTe2 monolayers.

**Method.** The study employs Horizontal Flux Transport (HFT) for crystal growth, Scanning Tunnelability Microscopy (STM) for defect characterization, and low-temperature electrical transport measurements (including Shubnikov-de Haas oscillations) in dual-gate field-effect transistors.

**Summary.** This paper reports a breakthrough in the growth of WTe2 using a Horizontal Flux Transport technique, which significantly reduces crystal disorder. This improved quality allows for the observation of unprecedentedly large magnetoresistance and the first quantum oscillations in WTe2 monolayers. Furthermore, the researchers demonstrate an enhanced superconducting dome with a critical temperature reaching 1.8 K. These findings provide a new platform for studying topological electronics and gated superconducting devices.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** The physical system consists of high-purity bulk and monolayer WTe2, a topological semimetal, studied in both bulk crystals and electrostatically doped monolayer field-effect transistors encapsulated by hBN.

**Key observables.** Residual Resistivity Ratio (RRR), magnetoresistance (MR), transport and quantum mobility, critical temperature (Tc), valley splitting, and Landau fan diagrams.

**Important parameters / regimes.** RRR up to 12,200; magnetoresistance of 7.8 x 10^5 at 13 T; Tc approaching 1.8 K; electron density (ne) and perpendicular displacement field (D_perp).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The analysis assumes the superconductivity follows BCS clean-limit theory and that the discrepancy between transport and quantum mobility is due to persistent long-range scattering mechanisms.

**Figures summary.** Figure 1 compares HFT vs conventional growth and shows STM images; Figure 2 displays resistance maps showing Landau fans and valley splitting; Figure 3 presents evidence of the superconducting dome and scaling of critical current.

**Paper structure.** The paper begins by introducing the HFT growth method and demonstrating improved crystal quality, followed by bulk transport properties (magnetoresistance), then moves to monolayer studies (quantum oscillations and valley splitting), and concludes with the characterization of the enhanced superconducting state and defect analysis.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

The diverse electronic properties of transition metal chalcogenides can be very sensitive to crystal imperfections. A new crystal growth technique, known as horizontal flux transport, offers a route to improved crystal quality. By refining this technique and applying it to the topological semimetal WTe2, we achieved crystals with an order of magnitude less disorder as determined by electrical transport and scanning tunneling microscopy measurements. At low temperatures these crystals exhibit the largest magnetoresistance reported in a metal. Exfoliated monolayers show quantum oscillations for the first time in the electrostatically doped metallic states, enabling determination of band degeneracies and the valley splitting induced by an electric field. Moreover, they exhibit a gated superconducting dome with a greatly enhanced critical temperature approaching 1.8 K. This advance opens up new avenues for employing WTe2 in topological electronics and gated superconducting devices, and promises comparable breakthroughs with other chalcogenides.

</details>

**Authors:** M. Menshawy, D. Sanz-Hernández, L. Mazza, V. Puliafito, G. Finocchio, A. Jenkins, R. Ferreira, L. Benetti, J. Grollier, F. A. Mizrahi

**Main problem.** The challenge of selectively programming a large number of non-volatile synaptic weights in dense in-memory computing architectures without the scalability bottlenecks of individual electrical access lines or complex selector devices.

**Main result.** Demonstrated a scalable method for remote, frequency-selective programming of 22 spintronic synapses using a single broadcast RF signal, enabling the same hardware to be reconfigured for distinct tasks like digit classification and drone signature identification.

**Method.** The researchers used high-power RF pulses to induce frequency-selective vortex-core polarity reversal in MTJs and low-power RF signals for readout via the spin-diode effect, utilizing frequency multiplexing to address individual junctions.

**Summary.** This paper presents a way to program a spintronic neural network remotely using radiofrequency signals. By using different frequencies to target specific magnetic tunnel junctions, the researchers can change the weights of the network without needing individual wires for every synapse. This allows a single piece of hardware to be reconfigured 'on the fly' to perform different tasks, such as recognizing handwritten digits or identifying drone RF signatures. This approach offers a highly scalable and compact route for building next-generation edge intelligence hardware.

<details markdown="1"><summary>Detailed structure</summary>

**Model / system.** A spintronic neural network consisting of two series-connected chains of 11 vortex-based magnetic tunnel junctions (MTJs) with varying diameters, all coupled to a shared metallic strip line for RF delivery.

**Key observables.** DC voltage (Vdc) across the MTJ chains, switching probability, spectral transfer function, and classification accuracy.

**Important parameters / regimes.** RF power (ranging from -20 to 13 dBm), frequency range (240-600 MHz), magnetic field strength (-300 to -20 mT), and MTJ diameters (300-800 nm).

**Assumptions / limitations.** The model assumes the microwave field from the strip line is proportional to the RF current amplitude and neglects certain logarithmic terms in the switching field calculation.

**Figures summary.** Figures show the experimental setup, the frequency-dependent voltage response and spectral reshaping of the MTJ chains, the mapping of pixel intensities to RF waveforms, and the performance of the network on digit and drone identification tasks.

**Paper structure.** The paper introduces the scalability problem in neuromorphic computing, describes the experimental MTJ-based platform and RF programming mechanism, presents the results of frequency-selective weight programming, demonstrates task-specific reconfigability, and provides an analytical scaling law for switching energy.

</details>

<details markdown="1"><summary>Abstract</summary>

Selectively programming large number of non-volatile synaptic weights without compromising scalability is a key challenge for in-memory computing. Here, we demonstrate remote programming of synaptic weights in series-connected chains of 11 vortex-based magnetic tunnel junctions using broadcast radiofrequency signals applied through a shared strip line. The programming relies on frequency-selective reversal of the vortex-core polarity and therefore does not require individual access lines or selector devices. By reconfiguring the binary states of these chains, we reshape the weighted sums they perform on frequency-multiplexed RF inputs. Using a 22-synapse network composed of two such chains, we remotely reconfigure the same hardware to perform two distinct tasks: handwritten-digit classification and drone RF-signature identification. The digit-optimized configuration reaches 94.91 +/- 0.26% accuracy on handwritten digits but only 13.17 +/- 0.47% on drone RF signatures, whereas the drone-optimized configuration reaches 97.33 +/- 0.62% on drones but only 47.59 +/- 1.5% on digits. Broadcast RF programming thus provides a compact and scalable route to rapidly reconfigurable spintronic neuromorphic hardware.

</details>

For vanishing RSOC (α = 0), the RKKY interactions are pre- dominantly isotropic, with the in-plane Heisenberg and out- of-plane Ising terms showing small amplitude oscillations as a function of λ. Increasing α enhances anisotropic effects, leading to larger amplitudes and pronounced oscillations in all components, especially in the off-diagonal Jxy and DM terms, indicating that RSOC significantly modulates the spin texture of the indirect exchange.</figcaption>
